title: Trump’s new tariffs ‘a sales tax that will make life harder for Americans’, say Democrats – US politics live
excerpt:
Even some Republicans apparently expressing concern over measures against 85 countries

What to know about Trump’s new tariffs on more than 80 countries

Hello and welcome to the US politics live blog...
body_text:
Even some Republicans apparently expressing concern over measures against 85 countries

What to know about Trump’s new tariffs on more than 80 countries

Hello and welcome to the US politics live blog.

Donald Trump’s move to impose a fresh round of tariffs on more than 80 countries has drawn criticism from both Democratic politicians and his own allies.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, announced that the Trump administration “is imposing tariffs on more than 80 countries for their failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor”.

The tariffs will replace a 10% global duty that was due to expire for supposedly failing to crack down on forced labor. However, congresswoman Linda Sánchez, a California Democrat, denounced the pretext for the new tariffs as a sham.

“President Trump isn’t serious about combating forced labor. He’s trying to make an end-run around the Supreme Court and Congress to reimpose his illegal tariffs,” Sánchez wrote on social media.

double quotation markIf he was serious, he would not be applying the same tariff rate to China, one of the worst forced labor abusers in the world, as he does to countries like Australia. Then again, unlike China, Australia, doesn’t manufacture any Trump corporation products.

This is the same administration that gutted funding to combat forced labor through cuts to the Bureau of International Labor Affairs and rolled back worker protections here at home, while encouraging companies to profit off some of the world’s worst forced labor abusers through his proposed US-China Board of Trade.

We need to take forced labor seriously. But that includes addressing our problems here at home, including by taking on the export of goods made with American prison labor. Slapping more tariffs on every country that upsets the president isn’t the way to do it.

“Trump is hell-bent on raising your prices,” congressman Brendan Boyle, a Pennsylvania Democrat said in a statement on Trump’s lastest tariffs. “His new tariffs are a nationwide sales tax that will only serve to make life harder for Americans just trying to make ends meet.”

And even the president’s own allies have raised concerns over the latest round of tariffs, Politico is reporting.

One person close to the White House, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said:

double quotation markEverything that you do that’s not focused on affordability is not helpful. This might not be a negative five, but it’s a negative 0.5. Anything that you’re doing that is not on the plus side of the scale is a problem.

When you announce new tariffs, it’s not like, okay, well, let’s see what they do. It’s, ‘Oh crap, those are the bad things.’

Another White House source told Politico:

double quotation markI think Republicans are comfortable with the threat, even though that has some market impacts. But actual revenue coming away from people’s pocketbooks is not what Republicans are happy with.

Any conversation about tariffs, unless it’s reducing them, is a bad conversation for Republicans.

The government of Brazil has rejected the Trump administration’s claim that it, and 84 other nations, failed to enforce a ban on forced labor, and called the new 12.5% US tariff on imports from Brazil “completely arbitrary and unjustified”.

The Pentagon’s official online list of service members killed in the ongoing Iran war does not include the four soldiers who died during renewed fighting over the weekend, despite the fact that their names were included in a Pentagon press release on the formal transfer of their remains back to the United States on Wednesday.

The premier of Ontario, Doug Ford, said “it’s very hard to deal with President Trump when he changes his mind every single day. One day the prime minister [Canada’s Mark Carney] is with him side by side, and next thing you know, President Trump turns on him like a rabid dog.”

President Donald Trump ⁠will address the White House Correspondents’ Association at a rescheduled dinner later today, nearly three months after a shooting at the Washington Hilton hotel interrupted the event.

The black-tie gathering of journalists, politicians and administration officials ⁠was called off on 25 ⁠April after a ​man tried to force his way through a security checkpoint and fired a shotgun outside the ballroom where Trump and members of his cabinet were taking their seats.

The suspect, Cole Allen, pleaded ⁠not guilty in May to charges including attempting to assassinate the president. The dinner has been moved to the Waldorf Astoria hotel and will take place under heightened security.

Secret Service director Sean Curran told reporters that an ⁠advance team was implementing a comprehensive security plan at the hotel, Reuters reported.

“We do expect bad people to show up,” Curran said. “It’s just a reality ​of where we are, but we’re treating it no differently ‌than any other site that the ‌president goes to.”

The April incident occurred during Trump’s first presidential appearance at the correspondents’ dinner. He had boycotted ​the event in his previous years in office.

Just before a temporary levy on global imports was set to expire, the Trump administration issued a fresh wave of tariffs on more than 80 countries late on Thursday, ranging from 10% to 12.5%. The new tariffs take effect Friday morning.

It’s Donald Trump’s latest effort to continue imposing global levies without congressional approval, despite the supreme court ruling in February that found the US president had illegally used his executive power to implement his global trade policies. Trump has long maintained that the US has been paying more than its fair share in tariffs, and that increased duties are necessary to boost American manufacturing and jobs.

Here’s what to know about Trump’s latest round of tariffs:

Donald Trump has once again inspired anger and confusion in US allies and trading partners as he imposed a wave of tariffs on more than 80 countries, replacing an expiring global duty introduced earlier this year.

The new measures effectively replace blanket 10% tariffs that Trump imposed in February. The US supreme court ruled many of those were illegal. The new levies come in at between 10% and 12.5% on countries including the UK, Mexico, Canada, Australia, India, China and the 27 countries that make up the EU.

They are imposed under section 301 of the US Trade Act of 1974, and the Trump administration has said the new measures are due to the dozens of countries failing to enforce bans on goods produced by forced labor.

The imposition of the new tariffs led to Asian stock markets taking a hammering overnight. The Japanese Nikkei 225 fell 3.1%, while the Chinese SSE Composite dropped 1.4%.

Hong Kong’s Hang Seng index was the most heavily hit, down 11.4%, while the South Korean Kospi, which is dominated by its major semiconductor companies, slumped 6.2%.

It was more of a mixed picture in Europe as trading began on Friday, with the Stoxx 600, which tracks the biggest companies on the continent, dropping 0.7% before steadying to a fall of 0.1%. France’s Cac 40 initially fell almost 1% before moving into positive territory, up 0.12%.

Similarly, Germany’s Dax fell 8% before erasing losses to climb 0.51%. In the UK the FTSE 100 index rose 0.28% in early trading. For many officials digesting the news on Friday, the US rationale was hard to swallow.

“You can’t say that ⁠for the European Union,” its foreign policy chief, Kaja Kallas, said ​on the sidelines ‌of the Asean ‌meeting in Manila. “If you compare our ‌labour laws to the ones of the United States, I mean, we have paid vacations, we have very good labour conditions for our employees, so ‌it’s not really grounded.”

title: Trump’s latest tariffs put UK at disadvantage to EU, say experts
excerpt:
While overall levy for UK is unchanged, sectors not covered by Turnberry deal face tougher competition

Donald Trump’s latest round of tariffs has put UK businesses at a disadvantage against the EU ev...
body_text:
While overall levy for UK is unchanged, sectors not covered by Turnberry deal face tougher competition

Donald Trump’s latest round of tariffs has put UK businesses at a disadvantage against the EU even though the overall tariff for British goods has not changed, experts have said.

In the levies, announced late on Thursday and said to be aimed against countries that engage in forced labour, the EU’s previous 15% near blanket tariff is reduced to 10%, the same level as the tariff deal struck by Keir Starmer and Peter Mandelson last year.

The UK welcomed the deal, announced by the US trade representative, Jamieson Greer, and said there was “no change” to the headline 10% tariff or preferential rates in the car, pharmaceutical and aerospace sectors concluded in the UK’s economic prosperity deal (EPD).

The new measures for the EU will replace the tariff in the Turnberry deal concluded at the US president’s Scottish golf course last year.

An EU spokesperson said the deal “establishes an all-inclusive tariff rate of 10% for the EU, and reintroduces the additional tariff exemptions for the EU, such as cork and diamonds, on top of those on aircrafts and parts, generic medicines, and active ingredients”.

The Turnberry deal meant no tariffs could be added to the 15% blanket levy. The reduction to 10% effectively puts the EU at an advantage for sectors not specified in Starmer’s deal, such as bikes, clothing, chemicals, beverages or gifts.

However, in a fillip for Andy Burnham, a deal cutting US tariffs on Scottish whisky to zero is expected to be announced later on Friday, giving the UK an advantage over Irish and French spirits rivals, which attract a 10% duty.

William Bain, the trade policy director for the British Chambers of Commerce, welcomed the fact that there was no change on the headline 10% rate for the UK but said some sectors, such as clothing, have lost their comparative advantage against the EU.

A British knitted jumper, for example, which attracted a 12.5% tariff, would be imported with an additional EPD tariff of 10%, bringing total levies to 22.5%. An EU jumper could now be sold into the US at an all-inclusive rate of 10%.

“Clearly there is a difference between how the UK has been treated today and how the EU has been treated today. And that is undeniable,” Bain said.

“Negotiations have to go on between the UK and the US over this. At the chambers network we want to see a path to lower tariffs,” he added.

The GMB trade union said the new tariffs were “ill-judged, potentially catastrophic for business and likely to utterly fail in their stated aim”.

Charlotte Brumpton-Childs, the GMB national secretary, said: “The EU now has a better trading relationship with the US than we do – which undermines the so-called ‘special relationship’.”A government spokesperson said: “There is no negative change to the tariff rate facing UK businesses as a result of this announcement. Our agreement with the US remains in place, and today we see an improvement to our trading terms with zero tariffs on whisky and medical technology.

“We take forced labour very seriously to ensure that in global supply chains UK businesses are not complicit. The US has recognised the steps the UK is taking, which is why there are no additional tariffs for the UK under this announcement.”

An EU spokesperson said: “We did not agree with the premise of this investigation on forced labour. And we had communicated this, also to our US counterparts.”

Trump is expected to announce more tariffs under section 301 of the 1974 Trade Act. With hints of retaliation over the EU’s decision to impose a €890m (£760m) fine on Google earlier this week and a threat to impose 100% tariffs on pharma, this would hit Ireland, Germany and Belgium hard.

title: Jennifer Sey reveals alleged communication with Sophie Cunningham amid recruitment pitch for XX-XY Athletics
excerpt: Jennifer Sey, the founder of the activist sportswear brand XX-XY Athletics, says she first received a DM from Sophie Cunningham in 2024, as she is now trying to court the WNBA star as a brand ambassad...
body_text:
Jennifer Sey, the founder of the activist sportswear brand XX-XY Athletics, says she first received a DM from Sophie Cunningham in 2024, as she is now trying to court the WNBA star as a brand ambassador.

Jennifer Sey is ready to make Sophie Cunningham the face of XX-XY Athletics — and says she would "give my right arm" to convince the WNBA star to leave Adidas for her brand.

Sey, the founder and CEO of the pro-women’s sportswear brand, told Fox News Digital she has wanted to sign Cunningham since the Indiana Fever star privately praised XX-XY Athletics’s viral "Dear Nike" campaign in 2024.

"She’s the perfect person for us," Sey said. "We have wanted to work with her for such a long time."

WHITE HOUSE DEFENDS WNBA STAR SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM AFTER SHE SPEAKS OUT IN SUPPORT OF PROTECTING WOMEN'S SPORTS

"It’s not just that she’s a fantastic athlete and she’s a currently competing professional athlete," she added. "She’s just got such an incredible personality. She’s fun and she’s funny and she’s tough and she says what she thinks, and that’s what we’re all about."

Sey revealed that she first opened communication with Cunningham after XX-XY Athletics released its "Dear Nike" in October 2024. The ad challenged the sportswear giant to take a public stand against biological males competing in women’s sports, and generated more than 10 million views.

Sey claims Cunningham liked and commented on the original ad, then sent Sey a private direct message on social media praising it. But Sey said she did not see the message for months because it landed in a folder for messages from accounts she did not follow.

FEVER GUARD SOPHIE CUNNINGHAM'S VIRAL FINGER-POINTING MEME REACHES WWE

"She liked it and commented on it. She actually wrote me a DM on Instagram, which I didn't see for months because I'm a moron and I never check the little tab from unknown people," Sey claims.

"Cause there's just so many in there, I just never look, and I didn't follow her. I didn't know who she was. And then a few months later, and she was kind of taking off at this point as Caitlin [Clark]'s defender... I checked that random tab from unknown DMs and she had DMed me about the ad, that she really liked the Dear Nike, and I was like, 'Oh my gosh, I should have... How did I miss this? I should have responded months ago.'"

Fox News Digital has reached out to Cunningham's representatives for comment.

Cunningham is already under contract with Adidas, which is preparing to release her first publicly available player-exclusive shoe Friday. The Adidas Crazy Energy "Sophie Cunningham" PE will retail for $120. It is a Cunningham colorway of an existing Adidas model, not a signature shoe created exclusively for her. Adidas recently unveiled the Crazy Energy line.

But Sey hopes Cunningham will eventually defect from Adidas.

"I would do anything within my power to get her to defect from Adidas and come to work with us," Sey said. "I don’t know that Sophie is likely to do that, but I would give my right arm to have her do it."

Sey criticized Adidas for not publicly endorsing Cunningham’s position in the days since the WNBA star made her position public.

"Adidas is not a brand that supports women," Sey said.

Sey also pointed to Adidas’ 2023 Pride campaign, which featured a male-presenting model wearing a swimsuit sold in the women’s section. Adidas said at the time that the collection promoted LGBTQIA+ advocacy and self-acceptance. Sey also criticized the company’s sponsorship of the Boston Marathon, whose rules permit transgender athletes to qualify in their gender category, although eligibility for prize money is governed by World Athletics standards.

"Even now, when she’s getting a lot of heat, it’s not like Adidas is coming out and saying, ‘We stand with Sophie. We agree women’s sports must be protected,’" Sey said. "They’re silent."

"They just want to capitalize on sassy Sophie who points," she added. "They don’t want to actually stand with her when she says something that makes people angry."

Still, Sey praised Adidas for not dropping Cunningham as a client since she's made her statements.

"It says something is shifting in the culture about this issue. They’re not dropping her," Sey said.

Sey conceded that XX-XY could not match the financial resources of Adidas. But she said her brand could offer Cunningham something the global sportswear company could not: top billing and unconditional support.

Sey previously served as Levi Strauss & Co.’s chief marketing officer and global brand president. She said she personally negotiated celebrity partnerships while at Levi’s and often sold potential ambassadors on the relationship rather than the biggest possible check.

"I wasn’t offering the most money," Sey said. "I was offering the best partnership."

Asked how much she would offer Cunningham, Sey declined to name a figure.

"I would pull together as much money as I could to pay her, to make her a favorable deal, but I’m not going to pretend like I can pay what Adidas can pay," Sey said. "We’re going to be more fun to work with."

"She’s not going to be a third-rate athlete on our roster. She’s going to be number one," Sey added. "And she’s going to be able to say whatever she wants, and we’re going to have her back."

Sey is also pitching Cunningham on something bigger than a traditional endorsement.

After the WNBA Players Association declined to allow its group licensees to commercialize Cunningham’s viral pointing image, Sey posted a silhouette modeled on the pose. The union called the image "derogatory," according to an initial report, but later explained that it generally avoids monetizing confrontations between its members. It clarified that Cunningham remains free to license or create her own products.

Sey offered to produce a hoodie featuring the silhouette and give Cunningham all the proceeds. She now envisions using it as the logo for a Cunningham line under XX-XY, similar to how Michael Jordan’s Jumpman pose became the symbol of Jordan Brand.

"Sophie can have her own brand like Jordan with us, and that’s the logo," Sey said.

"That should be the logo for the XX-XY Cunningham collection."

Cunningham’s profile has risen sharply since she joined Caitlin Clark and the Fever in 2025. She became known as Clark’s on-court defender, then turned into one of the WNBA’s most recognizable personalities after her prolonged finger-pointing exchange with Phoenix Mercury veteran DeWanna Bonner went viral in June.

The 29-year-old guard is also enjoying one of her most efficient seasons. Cunningham entered the All-Star break averaging 9.3 points while shooting 47.4% from the field and 42.9% from 3-point range.

Her growing popularity has extended beyond basketball. Cunningham recently appeared as a guest ring girl at UFC 329 and has built her image around an outspoken, humorous and combative personality.

She then waded into one of the most divisive issues in women’s sports.

Cunningham told ESPN this week that she wanted to protect girls who "shouldn’t have to go against biological men" in sports or share locker rooms with them. She later doubled down, calling her position "common sense" while insisting she does not hate transgender people.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"I said what I said," Cunningham told reporters Wednesday. "I think it’s really important to protect children, and that’s little girls who are also involved in that category. I stand on what I said."

The Fever responded by saying its players have the right to their own views.

"Our players are thoughtful adults with their own perspectives and voices, and those views are their own," the organization said. "We are committed to welcoming fans from every background and treating everyone with respect, and that’s what guides us as an organization."

Jackson Thompson is a sports reporter for Fox News Digital covering critical political and cultural issues in sports, with an investigative lens. Jackson's reporting has been cited in federal government actions related to the enforcement of Title IX, and in legacy media outlets including The New York Times, The Los Angeles Times, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Associated Press and ESPN.com.

title: Australia World Cup star Volpato faces speeding, cocaine charges
excerpt:
Cristian Volpato, who represented Australia at the World Cup, has allegedly tested positive for cocaine and was also charged with speeding, New South Wales state police have said.

They said the 22-ye...
body_text:
Cristian Volpato, who represented Australia at the World Cup, has allegedly tested positive for cocaine and was also charged with speeding, New South Wales state police have said.

They said the 22-year-old Volpato, who switched allegiances from Italy to Australia before the World Cup, was allegedly clocked at 50km (30 miles) an hour over the designated speed limit when police stopped him on Sydney’s Anzac Bridge early on Friday morning.

He was subjected to a roadside drug test which returned a positive result for cocaine, police said in a statement.

Volpato made three appearances for the Socceroos at the latest World Cup.

The forward represented Italy at youth level but never appeared for the senior national side.

He began his youth career with Sydney United, Sydney FC and Western Sydney Wanderers.

Volpato’s big break came when Roma signed him in 2020. He moved to Sassuolo in 2023, where he established himself as a Serie A player.

His final appearance for Italy came at under-21 level in 2025. Volpato had previously been linked with a switch to Australia ahead of the 2022 World Cup in Qatar.

title: Citizens Bank cuts ties with ICE prison contractors after months of activist pressure
excerpt: Fox News legal analyst Gregg Jarrett tells Lawrence Jones that banking discrimination for political or religious purposes is illegal. Jarrett asserts that federal and state laws protect consumers, mak...
body_text:
Fox News legal analyst Gregg Jarrett tells Lawrence Jones that banking discrimination for political or religious purposes is illegal. Jarrett asserts that federal and state laws protect consumers, making it unlawful to close accounts based on political or religious beliefs, unless criminal activity is suspected. He warns of civil enforcement actions, including significant financial penalties, for banks and their officers.

Citizens Bank is ending its financial relationships with two of the nation's largest private prison companies following months of protests over their role in operating ICE detention facilities, though the bank insists the decision was driven by changing business conditions rather than political pressure.

The Rhode Island-based lender said it will wind down its lending relationships with CoreCivic and GEO Group, companies that have operated immigration detention centers under federal contracts. Activists who had urged customers and municipalities to cut ties with the bank hailed the move as a victory, while Citizens said the companies' financing needs had changed because the federal government plans to acquire some detention facilities.

"Over the past year, we have been targeted by several activist groups regarding our relationships with two private prison operators, CoreCivic and The GEO Group," Citizens Bank said in a July 17 announcement. "To date, we have refrained from commenting directly given our commitment to maintaining client privacy. However, given recent developments, we feel it is appropriate to comment at this time."

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION PLANS NEW ICE HOLDING FACILITY NEAR LOUISIANA DEPORTATION FLIGHT HUB

This Wednesday, Aug. 28, 2019, photo shows the Adelanto U.S. Immigration and Enforcement Processing Center operated by GEO Group, Inc. (GEO) a Florida-based company specializing in privatized corrections in Adelanto, Calif.

"GEO has been a client of the bank since 2018, and CoreCivic has been a client since 2011," the announcement continued. "Among the services these companies offer is the operation of prisons under service contracts with the federal and various state governments. This includes contracting with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to operate detention facilities for those individuals scheduled for deportation. Both companies also own various facilities in addition to their service contracts."

Citizens said it decided to end its lending relationship with CoreCivic and GEO Group because recent federal purchases of correctional facilities have reduced the companies' capital needs and changed how they finance their operations. The bank said the decision was driven by those business changes, not by any concerns about the companies or their business practices.

"Citizens has been disappointed that the activists have dragged it into what is largely a political matter," the bank’s announcement continued. "Public characterizations of Citizens by activist groups, including as it relates to our commitment to immigrants and to communities, do not reflect who we are or the record we have built. Citizens is deeply committed to serving our customers, colleagues, and communities."

SOROS-BACKED NONPROFIT ACCUSES NJ GOV SHERRILL OF SPREADING 'MAGA PROPAGANDA' ON ICE DETAINEES

An exterior view of a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) administrative office and processing center on June 28, 2025. (Paul Weaver/SOPA Images/LightRocket via Getty Images)

The bank also highlighted its broader community investments, saying it supports 140 nonprofit organizations that serve immigrant communities, logged more than 265,000 employee volunteer hours last year, has staff serving on more than 1,000 nonprofit boards, and provided $2 billion in community development financing for affordable housing and local economic development.

In a statement to Fox News Digital, a spokesman for CoreCivic said that the company "is a lawful business that has served government partners at the federal, state and local levels, under administrations of both parties, for more than 40 years. Our financial position is the strongest it has been in the company's history and we value very much our diverse set of financial partners that help support the company's capital needs."

NEW YORK TIMES TORCHED FOR ‘FALSE’ REPORTING ON ICE INVESTIGATIONS, OUTLET FIRES BACK

Police officers patrol outside of Delany Hall, an immigrant detention center in Newark, N.J., on May 13, 2025. (Stephanie Keith for Fox News Digital)

It added, "We remain focused on delivering safe, high-quality services for our government partners and delivering quality outcomes which is where we believe our emphasis should be."

Fox News Digital contacted Citizens Bank, which referred to its July 17 statement. Fox News Digital also reached out to ICE and GEO Group for comment.

Rachel del Guidice is a culture reporter for Fox News covering the intersection of politics, faith, family, and American culture.

title: Alleged body cover-up, frantic texts to hide wanted Florida murder suspect exposed in escape plot: affidavits
excerpt: Criminal defense attorney Donna Rotunno analyzes the manhunt for a woman accused of murdering a 43-year-old man in Florida. Rotunno details how the suspect has allegedly used aliases, altered appearan...
body_text:
Criminal defense attorney Donna Rotunno analyzes the manhunt for a woman accused of murdering a 43-year-old man in Florida. Rotunno details how the suspect has allegedly used aliases, altered appearances and manipulation of accomplices to evade the Walton County Sheriff's Department.

Newly released arrest reports detail how a network of suspected accomplices allegedly helped a wanted fugitive hide a body and hide from law enforcement.

These alleged accomplices are accused of helping deputy-dubbed "master manipulator" Isabelle Rose Johnson, 38, steer clear of cops; she's been on the run for several weeks.

Johnson, of Florida, is accused of killing Jason Coulthart, 43, whose body was found buried in a shallow grave on a Florida property on June 25. He was last seen alive on May 24.

Jason Christopher Coulthart, 43, was found buried in a shallow grave at a property in Niceville, Florida. He leaves behind a son. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

FLORIDA WOMAN ON THE RUN AND WANTED FOR MURDER RELIED ON NETWORK OF ALLEGED ACCOMPLICES TO EVADE CAPTURE: COPS

Isabelle Johnson, who is wanted in connection with the murder of Jason Coulthart, according to the Walton County Sheriff's Office. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

Alleged accomplice No. 1: In one of the arrest reports from the Walton County Sheriff's Office, a witness detailed to investigators how Johnson allegedly murdered Coulthart and disposed of his body.

The report alleges Johnson's reported roommate, Bobbie Gean Wagstaff, 31, helped her dig a hole to bury Coulthart's body.

Bobbie Jean Wagstaff, Isabelle Johnson's roommate, is charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder and possession of methamphetamine after investigators alleged she helped conceal the killing of Jason Coulthart. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

📩 Send me a story idea: kelsie.cairns@fox.com

A witness in the same report said, "She killed the victim by placing a zip tie around his neck." The same witness went on to say, "She covered the body with cement so that the dog would not dig it up again."

ALABAMA MOTHER SENTENCED TO LIFE FOR HIRING HITMAN TO KILL HER CHILD'S FATHER OVER CUSTODY DISPUTE

Johnson's possible motive for the alleged murder? The report says the witness told investigators "he was aware of her 'getting rid' of a boy because he was 'aggravating and irritating her.'"

Isabelle Johnson, 38, was captured on security video days after the remains of Jason Christopher Coulthart, 43, were discovered last month. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

This witness also said that "the 'boy' was also threatening to interfere with Isabelle's custody of her children."

Wagstaff is charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder and possession of methamphetamine.

'AMERICA'S MOST WANTED' FUGITIVE LAID BLUEPRINT FOR VANISHING BEFORE 20-YEAR ESCAPE: AFFIDAVIT

Alleged accomplices 2 & 3: In another report, husband and wife Martin and Sandra Leaverton, of Fort Walton Beach, allegedly admitted driving Johnson multiple times after Coulthart's body was discovered and helping move her between homes while she was wanted by authorities.

Martin Leaverton is charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder after investigators alleged he knowingly helped murder suspect Isabelle Rose Johnson evade capture. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

Investigators say the couple tried to help Johnson leave the state and retrieved her backpacks after she fled from deputies, giving her rides to locations where she could avoid capture.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Sandra Leaverton was charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder after investigators alleged she knowingly helped murder suspect Isabelle Rose Johnson evade capture. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

"Martin Leaverton agreed to call associates and attempt to arrange safe passage and a place to stay for Johnson outside of Florida," the report said.

Deputies say the Leavertons provided "rides, supplies, and false information to law enforcement about her location."

Authorities charged the Leavertons with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder.

Alleged accomplices 4 & 5: In another report, more alleged helpers were arrested for assisting Johnson.

Michael White and Kathleen Morris of Santa Rosa, Florida allegedly helped Johnson evade law enforcement after the murder by giving her a place to stay.

Michael White was charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder and obstruction without violence after investigators alleged he knowingly sheltered murder suspect Isabelle Rose Johnson, provided her with supplies and lied to law enforcement about her whereabouts. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

Kathleen Morris was charged with accessory after the fact to first-degree murder and obstruction without violence after investigators alleged she knowingly sheltered murder suspect Isabelle Rose Johnson, provided her with supplies and lied to law enforcement about her whereabouts. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

After initially denying Johnson had ever been at their home, investigators say the pair later admitted they sheltered her at two properties and provided supplies while she was on the run.

(Walton County Sheriff's Office, Florida) (Walton County Sheriff's Office, Florida)

The report also includes a string of text messages between Morris, White and another suspect, aforementioned Leaverton, that investigators say show them coordinating Johnson's whereabouts, discussing her backpacks and referencing efforts to help her.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

In one alleged text exchange, Morris texted Leaverton, "Why is Harley on our couch?" before following up with, "More importantly, when are you coming to get her?"

"Harley" is one of the aliases that Johnson has been using, according to the sheriff's office, along with "Jessica Elaine Bowman" and "Denise."

Isabelle Johnson is wanted for murder and investigators say she has been on the run for weeks, relying on help from alleged accomplices to stay hidden from cops. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

Morris later allegedly texted White, "WTF is going on Michael?! Why did Marty leave his young female friend here??? And when is he coming back to get her??????"

Prosecutors also point to messages they say show Morris offering assistance. After Leaverton replied, "I'm headed there now," Morris allegedly responded, "We talked — anything I can do to help I will. Don't forget the backpacks please."

The couple is charged with accessory to a capital felony after the fact and obstruction.

title: We need a truth commission to show the world the insanity of the Biden border crisis
excerpt:
Rep. Seth Moulton, D-Mass., criticized his party for lacking a clear immigration plan, relying on opposition to President Donald Trump and downplaying inflation under former President Joe Biden.

Near...
body_text:
Rep. Seth Moulton, D-Mass., criticized his party for lacking a clear immigration plan, relying on opposition to President Donald Trump and downplaying inflation under former President Joe Biden.

Nearly 18 months since President Donald Trump ended the worst mass migration border crisis in American history, terabytes of government emails and records that could teach future generations why and how it happened await liberation.

The secrets in this trove beg discovery, exploitation and analysis about the great "Biden Border Crisis" that had millions of foreign nationals (at least 8 million) enter through the U.S. Southwest Border for 45 months between January 2021 and January 2025. Their value to the American national interest? Crucial knowledge that can prevent history from repeating.

But the referenced government material and the testimonies of involved government workers remain unstudied, the preventative value uncollected. That’s why Trump must appoint a presidential commission now that will establish a comprehensive official public record as to what happened.

THE BORDER IS CLOSED THANKS TO PRESIDENT TRUMP. IN VIRGINIA, THAT DOESN’T MATTER

The Trump administration is, of course, busy repairing the damage with mass deportations and policies to secure the southern border. But it must pause for the longer-range benefit of establishing this border crisis history before the opportunity to prevent the next big one is gone.

Former President Joe Biden oversaw the largest immigration surge in American history. (Biden photo by Drew Angerer/Getty Images, Migrant caravan photo by Jose Torres/Anadolu via Getty Images)

The great mass migration crisis at the southwest border stands as a transformational event of lasting generational consequence. It affects all Americans in one form or another, beginning with having, by most accounts, decided the 2024 presidential election.

DEM REP SETH MOULTON SAYS PARTY HAS NO IMMIGRATION PLAN, IS ONLY ‘OPPOSITION TO TRUMP’

Competing narratives about who carried out which border policies, as well as the ensuing crisis’s magnitude, range of costs and consequences, still fester without a foreseeable end along partisan lines, a permanent state of national dispute. That’s a major problem because history repeats itself when it is not studied, written, absorbed and referenced when needed.

The reason a commission must form now, before Trump leaves office in January 2029, is to ensure there is enough time for records excavators, analysts, researchers and writers to complete the job before a less amenable president enters office and the preventative learning opportunity is lost forever.

For the last 150 years, relatively trusted presidential and congressional commissions have cleared the national air of damaging conspiracy theories and public disputes over consequential events in American life. Policymakers often found they could justify action when facts were supported by evidence and generally accepted.

Ideally for the Biden border crisis, Congress would establish a bipartisan commission such as the Warren Commission that examined the assassination of President John F. Kennedy. Another example was the National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States, which produced the famed report in 2004 that Congress followed as a guideline for major homeland security legislation.

Congress alone has ordered over 170 such commissions since 1989, according to a 2017 Congressional Research Service (CRS) report.

The chambers of Congress are too hopelessly fractured for bipartisan study today, so a presidential commission must be the option for this job. Presidents have ordered 45 such commissions over the decades.

It’s true that commissions can be wasteful, their suggestions sometimes ignored. The Hart-Rudman Commission released a report in 1998 that fruitlessly warned about the potential for a major terrorist attack on U.S. soil three years before 9/11.

But other commissions have mattered. They are still invoked as final authoritative reference guides about the controversial events of high public interest that birthed them. The U.S. presidential Tower Commission, for instance, investigated the Iran-Contra Affair, laying bare a vast secret foreign military venture during the Ronald Reagan administration.

The great mass migration crisis at the southwest border stands as a transformational event of lasting generational consequence. It affects all Americans in one form or another, beginning with having, by most accounts, decided the 2024 presidential election.

A presidential truth commission is warranted for other sound reasons. The American public agrees on little about the crisis because the Biden administration frequently obstructed good information flow.

Throughout the administration’s four years, President Joe Biden, his staff, media outlets and prominent partisan pundits denied that anything was amiss at the border.

Official White House communications policy required Biden’s top talkers to say that nothing extraordinary was happening at the border. They’d call record-smashing border-crossing numbers a mere "challenge" and, in the face of video evidence and credible reporting, say, "The border is closed. The border is secure!" No top administration official, including Biden, would go anywhere near the border as 200,000 inadmissible aliens a month were pouring over it.

"It’s a seasonal thing … earthquakes, floods … lack of food. It’s because of gang violence and because of a whole range of things," Biden once told reporters with a straight face in March 2021 when all-time national crossing records had just been broken. Such claims found traction in mainstream media outlets for all four years. When they eventually did acknowledge the crisis, Biden officials insisted it was disconnected from administration policies.

From beginning to end, the Biden administration sandbagged the release of data and information that would illuminate impacts on local public safety and national security.

Or the expense of it all. What did the crisis end up costing the American people to care for the millions who crossed and were released by design? Where did all the immigrants go afterward?

Lawmakers never got answers to information requests about how U.S. tax money helped United Nations agencies and migrant advocacy nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) aid the illegal immigrant flows. How much did we give the resettlement industry, and what were our bureaucrats thinking as they cut checks?

The administration ignored barrages of information requests from Congress as well as Freedom of Information Act requests from reporters and public policy think tanks.

Maybe a presidential commission can fill many of these requests as part of its work and use the data in a final report.

Migrants seen after getting off a plane in Westchester, New York, in October 2021. (New York Post/Christopher Sadowski)

Most Americans understand that Trump is busy trying to achieve priorities – including some he may regard as central to his presidential legacy – that seem more urgent than forming a commission to study an event already in the rearview mirror.

But because none of its lessons were learned, yesterday’s crisis is very relevant to tomorrow. Appointing a commission before it’s too late is legacy stuff, because it might just spare America another one or two border catastrophes long after Trump is gone.

Todd Bensman is a senior research fellow in the Heritage Foundation’s Border Security and Immigration Center. Prior to joining Heritage, he served in the Trump administration as a senior adviser to the Department of Homeland Security, Border Czar Tom Homan and the National Counterterrorism Center.

title: Sharon Stone's terrifying weed withdrawal sparks warning about today's potent marijuana
excerpt: Fox News senior medical analyst Dr. Marc Siegel discusses new research that casts doubt on cannabis benefits and highlights increased mental health risks, especially for teenagers. Modern marijuana, w...
body_text:
Fox News senior medical analyst Dr. Marc Siegel discusses new research that casts doubt on cannabis benefits and highlights increased mental health risks, especially for teenagers. Modern marijuana, with 70-90% THC potency, is linked to higher rates of PTSD, anxiety, depression, ADHD, and bipolar disorder. Usage among Americans aged 12 and older has skyrocketed by 180% since the early 2000s, leading to concerns about addiction and health issues.

Hollywood icon Sharon Stone recently opened up about a health scare after quitting marijuana, revealing her symptoms were so intense that she turned to a neurologist.

During an interview with Variety, the actress said her decision to quit was driven by a desire for a drug-free life.

"And because I think they put so much stuff in it now — they really do — when I stopped smoking pot, I also got sick," she said.

CANNABIS COMPOUNDS COULD REVERSE DISEASE AFFECTING ONE-THIRD OF ADULTS

"I got pretty sick for a month. I went to my neurologist, and he said, ‘Sharon, I’ve had people dying in the emergency room from marijuana.'"

Stone attributed her ordeal to the potency of modern weed, saying, "It’s not like you’re pulling it out of the ground anymore ... It’s not like when we were kids."

American actress Sharon Stone quit "smoking pot" and got so sick that she consulted a neurologist, she revealed in a recent interview. (Getty Images, iStock)

Experts note that cannabis withdrawal itself is generally not considered life-threatening, although severe symptoms, underlying medical conditions or interactions with other substances can complicate recovery.

FLYING WITH CANNABIS? TSA'S NEW GUIDANCE COMES WITH A MAJOR HEALTH WARNING

Dr. Chris Tuell, clinical director of addiction services at the Lindner Center in Cincinnati, confirmed that today's cannabis products are vastly different from the strains of decades past.

THC levels in marijuana have surged from about 3% to 5% in the 1980s to as high as 30% in today's cannabis products, while concentrates can exceed 90% THC, he told Fox News Digital.

"I do believe cannabis is addicting, especially with the high levels of THC."

Higher THC concentrations have been linked to greater risks of dependence, cannabis use disorder (CUD), psychosis in some users, and more severe withdrawal symptoms, according to the National Institute on Drug Abuse.

As today's cannabis products become increasingly potent, experts say there are several risks and warning signs that daily users should know before deciding whether to continue — or quit — using marijuana.

According to Cleveland Clinic, CUD occurs when marijuana takes over the brain's reward pathways, amplifying compulsive behavior.

"I do believe cannabis is addicting, especially with the high levels of THC," Tuell told Fox News Digital.

The American Psychiatric Association’s DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition) outlines the following diagnostic criteria signaling that casual use may have crossed into dependency.

Today’s cannabis flower and concentrates contain up to 90% THC, making modern products exponentially stronger than those from decades past, experts say. (iStock)

Quitting heavy, long-term cannabis use can trigger withdrawal symptoms within 24 to 48 hours, Cleveland Clinic notes.

Users frequently experience severe insomnia, intense irritability, anxiety, appetite loss, sweating, headaches and stomach upset. While acute physical symptoms typically resolve within a month, mood swings and sleep issues can linger much longer, according to Tuell.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

While cannabis withdrawal alone rarely requires hospitalization, Cleveland Clinic notes that people with significant medical or mental health conditions — or those stopping cannabis while also withdrawing from alcohol, opioids or certain other medications — may benefit from medical supervision.

Addiction experts strongly advise stepping down THC intake slowly to cushion the impact on the nervous system and avoid severe rebound symptoms. (iStock)

Mental health conditions often occur alongside cannabis use disorder, and treating those underlying issues may help reduce problematic marijuana use, according to the National Institute on Drug Abuse and the Cleveland Clinic.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"I strongly believe that CUD and mental health issues co-exist, and that mental health counseling can address the issues driving the addictive behaviors," Tuell said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Stone's representatives requesting comment.

Khloe Quill is a lifestyle writer with Fox News Digital. She and the lifestyle team cover a range of story topics including food and drink, travel, and health.

title: Trump administration drive to seize election powers sparking ‘chaos’ despite pushback
excerpt:
US courts, state officials and Democrats warn that moves are endangering voting rights and the rule of law

The Trump administration continues to expand its authoritarian and radical drive to revamp v...
body_text:
US courts, state officials and Democrats warn that moves are endangering voting rights and the rule of law

The Trump administration continues to expand its authoritarian and radical drive to revamp voting rules and seize state election powers, though it faces growing resistance from US courts, state officials and Democrats, who warn the moves are sparking “chaos” and endangering voting rights and the rule of law.

Donald Trump’s unprecedented drive to change election rules, which states legally set, is fueled by his obsession with his 2020 election loss to Joe Biden, which he falsely attributes to voting fraud, coupled with his fear that Democrats will “find a reason to impeach me” if they take control of the House in November’s midterms elections, say former election officials and legal experts.

Given Trump’s dark mindset, his administration has used justice department legal moves and threats, FBI raids and investigations, and other federal agencies to coerce states to change voting rules in key ways, including curtailing voting by mail and machines that Trump and Maga allies have vilified, baselessly, as riddled with fraud.

Legal experts say the administration is intimidating election workers and voters before the November elections, and laying the groundwork for potentially challenging election results where Republican candidates lose to ensure the GOP keeps control of Congress.

In a conspiratorial 25-minute televised talk on 16 July, Trump presented a hodgepodge of selective intelligence and misleading allegations about Chinese efforts to influence the 2020 elections to hurt him, plus debunked claims of increased voting by noncitizens, flawed voting machines and other threats, to argue that the US elections system needs drastic changes.

Trump asserted without hard evidence that American elections were rife with “hacking, manipulation and corruption. Great damage has been done to our country.”

In a sharp rebuttal, the nation’s 24 Democratic governors said: “It’s deeply alarming that President Trump continues to try to undermine free and fair elections. No amount of lies and conspiracy theories can change the fact that our country’s elections have repeatedly been proven to be safe and secure.”

Trump’s goal, say critics, is to instill fear in voters’ minds, and push for radical measures pre-election including congressional passage of the Save America Act, which would sharply limit voting by mail and require voters to present proof of US citizenship. It lacks the votes in the Senate to pass.

Over the past few months, for example, the Department of Justice has demanded that 30 US states provide unredacted voter lists, which contain personal and sensitive information. In July, top justice department official Harmeet Dhillon threatened state election officials with criminal investigations and prosecutions if they knowingly permitted noncitizens to register to vote.

The FBI has taken extraordinary steps to again investigate the Georgia election results in 2020 that Trump bitterly contested after his loss there: early in 2026, the FBI seized hundreds of boxes of 2020 ballots from Fulton county, and in July sent 200 agents there to investigate the 2020 results and election workers.

The administration’s hardball tactics have sparked strong criticism from experts, and resistance including legal rebuffs from courts nationwide as well as state officials and Democrats.

Voting rights advocates and former election officials point out that courts have overwhelmingly rebuked justice department lawyers for their claims seeking to justify administration moves to obtain unredacted voting lists from the states and information about election workers in Georgia and other states.

Eileen O’Connor, who spent eight years as an attorney in the justice department’s voting section in the civil rights division and is now with the Brennan Center for Justice, said: “[The] DoJ has had case after case seeking unredacted voter rolls from the states rejected by judges. The DoJ has filed 30 of these cases and has now lost 15 of them.”

In another election-related matter where the Department of Justice sought detailed information on every election worker in Fulton county, Georgia, in the 2020 election, O’Connor noted the judge “openly excoriated the DoJ” and called the subpoena an “arbitrary fishing expedition”.

Although the justice department is not winning in courts, O’Connor stressed, “they’re injecting chaos” into this year’s elections. More broadly, O’Connor said, the administration’s strategy was “to decrease confidence in the elections and possibly lay the groundwork for possible challenges” post-election.

Former election officials are also raising red flags about Trump’s multiple moves to undermine confidence in the fall elections and intimidate voters and election workers.

“Trump is trying to intimidate state election officials and threatening actions aimed at suppressing the Democratic vote,” said Larry Noble, a former general counsel at the Federal Election Commission who teaches law at the American University. “It’s also likely he thinks he’s laying the groundwork to claim that any Republican loss is due to fraud.”

Noble said several of Trump’s recent actions – including his “firing the members of the non-partisan Election Assistance Commission, and suggesting he is open to possibly sending ICE agents and national guard troops to polling places” – were “a continuation of his efforts to exert control over the midterm elections”.

He added: “It’s not clear what, if any, limits he has on how far he will go to prevent Democrats from gaining back power. I fear it’s even possible he will order actions to try to disrupt voting or vote counting during the election to avoid Republican losses.”

Democratic and Republican election officials and congressional Democrats have been pushing back hard on the Trump administration’s multifront fight to change election rules.

In a social media post, for instance, Deidre Henderson, Utah’s top election official and lieutenant governor, condemned the tone and tactics used by Dhillon in her threatening letter to states.

“Got another love letter this morning from the DoJ, sprinkled throughout with threats of criminal prosecution,” Henderson wrote. “This is truly bizarre behavior by the federal agency that is supposed to be protecting civil rights.”

In another pressure move, Dhillon told Michigan officials this month that the justice department plans to send federal monitors to three cities, including Lansing, that are Democratic-leaning while the state’s 4 August primaries are held.

Likewise, the Federal Emergency Management Agency promulgated new rules late last month that demand states change how they conduct elections to avoid losing federal counter-terrorism funding from the Department of Homeland Security, Fema’s parent agency.

Markwayne Mullin, in a press briefing the day after Trump’s speech, upped the ante by threatening local election officials with jail time if they failed to comply with the administration’s moves to change election policies. The DHS secretary reiterated several of Trump’s unsubstantiated assertions about the security of American elections.

Voting experts and former justice department officials say the administration’s multifaceted blitz to overhaul voting rules is a dangerous political exercise aimed at boosting Trump’s powers and keeping the GOP in control of Congress.

“Six years after the 2020 election, after audits, recounts, litigation and now internal investigation, Donald Trump has failed to produce any evidence of election tampering that changed votes,” said Barbara McQuade, a former US attorney who teaches law at the University of Michigan.

“While foreign adversaries have conducted propaganda campaigns to influence public opinion, those operations have favored Trump as much as his opponents. Reducing foreign influence in our elections is a valid goal, but stopping voting by mail will not advance that goal. With an estimated one in four Democrats and one in five Republicans voting by mail, ending voting by mail has only one effect, and that is to favor Republican candidates.”

McQuade added: “Trump’s speech revealed no new evidence and merely served to lay the groundwork for baseless claims of election fraud in November, when election outcomes will almost certainly be disastrous for a president with a dismal 37% approval rating.”

Justin Levitt, a professor at Loyola Law School and a former top official in the justice department’s civil rights division, said: “The administration is trying desperately to project power that it simply doesn’t have. That includes trying to intimidate state and local officials who aren’t going to be intimidated.”

Still, that intimidation drive is especially acute in the FBI’s efforts in Georgia.

“The assignment of hundreds of FBI personnel to look at the 2020 election is doing nothing more than allowing a puppet FBI director to be dangled about by the president,” said Michael Moore, a former US attorney in Georgia.

Some Republicans also worry that Trump’s obsession with his 2020 loss and his non-stop attacks on election rules and voting rights will backfire and hurt GOP candidates.

“Trump simply has an unhealthy obsession with the 2020 election that he lost,” said Charlie Dent, the former Republican House member of Pennsylvania. “He’s using the levers of the federal government to investigate an election that’s already been well investigated.

“Trump seems very determined to prevent losing congressional control by the GOP. But he’s doing so much that makes it difficult for the GOP to win.”

In historical terms, some voting rights experts say the Trump administration’s attacks on voting rules are unprecedented and foresee other dangerous moves ahead.

“The Trump administration is doing everything they can to stack the deck in their favor. This is the most hostile administration towards voting rights and democracy we’ve seen in our lifetimes,” said Jonathan Diaz, director of voting advocacy at the non-partisan Campaign Legal Center.

Looking ahead, Diaz warned: “We have very real concerns that they’ll try to weaponize law enforcement either to disrupt or interfere with the elections while they’re in progress, or after ballots have been cast before they’re certified.”

title: ‘Forever chemicals’ linked to gut inflammation in children after early exposure
excerpt:
Researchers tracked mother-child pairs for up to 11 years, finding Pfas mixtures may affect developing guts

Prenatal and early life exposure to Pfas mixtures is probably linked to higher levels of in...
body_text:
Researchers tracked mother-child pairs for up to 11 years, finding Pfas mixtures may affect developing guts

Prenatal and early life exposure to Pfas mixtures is probably linked to higher levels of intestinal inflammation in children, new first-of-its-kind research shows. Intestinal inflammation can cause irritable bowel diseases, like ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s disease and colorectal cancer.

While Pfas has been linked to irritable bowel disease and intestinal inflammation in adults, the new peer-reviewed Mt Sinai study is the first to look at how the chemicals impact children during a critical developmental phase.

The authors checked and followed mother-child pairs from during prenatal development, or just after birth, through up to 11 years of age. The findings showed a strong correlation between elevated Pfas levels around birth and markers of inflammation across the children in the cohort.

“The idea that the mom-baby dyads are exposed to these very toxic chemicals this early in the children’s’ life, and that that leads to later intestinal inflammation, is a very important finding,” said Vishal Midya, a study co-author and researcher with Mt Sinai’s Icahn School of Medicine. “There is so much of a knowledge gap in this area, and a gap in recognizing how important the early life period is to later onset of inflammatory bowel disease.”

Pfas are a class of at least 16,000 compounds often used to help products resist water, stains and heat. They are called “forever chemicals” because they do not naturally break down and accumulate in the environment, and they are linked to serious health problems such as cancer, kidney disease, liver problems, immune disorders and birth defects.

Pfas are known to be particularly dangerous for developing children, and prenatal or early childhood exposure is linked to a range of diseases, from reproductive harms to ADHD.

The new study checked for Pfas either in maternal blood collected during pregnancy, umbilical cord blood, or newborn dried blood spots across 107 mother-child pairs in the United States and Mexico. They found as many as 955 Pfas metabolites, but focused on 46 compounds that were most prevalent.

These include Pfas compounds like PFOA and PFOS, which are considered the most common and dangerous, though they have been partially phased out of production. The authors also found smaller, more recently developed Pfas for which there is much less public health data, and which industry has claimed are safer.

Research often focuses on how an individual Pfas compound affects human health, but humans are typically exposed to multiple at a time, so the authors looked at mixtures of chemicals.

“We’re exposed to thousands of thousands of chemicals to tease out the one or two 10 chemicals. It doesn’t mimic the real world by putting it together as a mixture,” Midya said.

The authors then looked for fecal calprotectin, which is a biomarker of intestinal inflammation commonly used to monitor irritable bowel syndrome, when the children were older. The fecal calprotectin does not confirm the children are suffering from a specific disease, but it is a marker of inflammation that frequently leads to disease.

Though the study is the largest to investigate early life Pfas exposures for intestinal inflammation, the authors say one of its limitations is the small sample size.

Mothers can take some steps to protect themselves, including using water filtration systems that remove Pfas, buying organic, avoiding waterproof clothing, and avoiding stain guards. However, the findings more broadly should be used to help shape policy that protects mothers and children, said Manasi Agrawal, a study co-author with Mt Sinai.

“We are showing that there is an early life pathway to this, and understanding the disease and its origin makes the prevention part – the ‘What can we do?’ part – much more viable,” Agrawal said.

title: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana is manipulating system, victims’ families say
excerpt:
Axel Rudakubana moved to psychiatric hospital after it was concluded he could not be safely managed in prison

The Southport killer Axel Rudakubana has been accused of attempting to “manipulate the sy...
body_text:
Axel Rudakubana moved to psychiatric hospital after it was concluded he could not be safely managed in prison

The Southport killer Axel Rudakubana has been accused of attempting to “manipulate the system” by the families of his victims, after it emerged he had been transferred from prison to a psychiatric hospital.

Rubakubana, 19, was jailed for life in January last year, with a minimum sentence of 52 years, for the murders of Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and nine-year-old Alice da Silva Aguiar.

The Guardian revealed on Thursday that Rudakubana was transferred to the high-security hospital Broadmoor from the category A prison Belmarsh after it was concluded that he could not be managed safely in jail.

Next Wednesday marks two years since he carried out the attack at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class on 29 July 2024.

The transfer does not alter the killer’s 52-year sentence.

Representing the three bereaved families, Chris Walker, of the law firm Bond Turner, said: “The three families we represent have been made aware of this development in the last 48 hours and it is safe to say that whilst this news would devastate them at any time, the fact that this has occurred as we approach the second anniversary in our view is another move by AR who would appear to be attempting, once again, to manipulate the system.

“We have been given the barest of details and whilst we are supporting the families through this we are urgently seeking a meeting with the appropriate authorities to understand the situation before commenting further.”

Prison officers levelled similar accusations against the convicted murderer. The chair of the Prison Officers’ Association, Mark Fairhurst, said in a statement: “This vile offender has played the system and will now enjoy the freedoms that are afforded mentally unwell patients in secure psychiatric hospitals, including benefits that law-abiding taxpayers will fund.

“My members will be placed at risk without the protections afforded to staff in prisons because he is now classed as a patient. He should spend his entire sentence in a prison in solitary confinement.

“My thoughts are with the families of the victims and all those affected by his heinous crimes.”

Rudakubana, from Banks, Lancashire, attacked eight other children as well as the class instructor, Leanne Lucas, and a businessman, John Hayes, who had tried to disarm him. He was also found guilty of producing the biological toxin ricin and of possessing an al-Qaida training manual, an offence under the Terrorism Act.

Alder Hey children’s NHS foundation trust previously confirmed Rudakubana had been under its care as a mental health patient between 2019 and 2023, when he “stopped engaging”. He was referred to them after taking a knife to school and later saying he had done so “to use it”.

He was also referred to the UK’s counter-extremism programme, Prevent, because he had been researching school massacres on a computer at his next school, the Acorns.

There were two other referrals to Prevent, but none were progressed as Rudakubana had no clear ideology behind his obsession with violence.

Broadmoor, in Crowthorne, Berkshire, is the oldest of England’s three high-security psychiatric hospitals. It has been used to hold some of the UK’s most notorious murderers, including Peter Sutcliffe, the Yorkshire Ripper.

Patients remain locked up, with conditions heavily regulated by strict physical security including locked wards, monitored by staff and 24-hour CCTV. They receive mandatory therapeutic and rehabilitative programmes. If offenders no longer require treatment in hospital, the 1983 Mental Health Act provides for a swift return to prison.

title: Nancy Armour's disastrous Caitlin Clark article exemplifies why sportswriting and sports media is failing
excerpt:
Fox News contributor Clay Travis reveals why he thinks the basketball pro would be successful branching out on 'The Ingraham Angle.'

Caitlin Clark is the most popular player in the WNBA as a result o...
body_text:
Fox News contributor Clay Travis reveals why he thinks the basketball pro would be successful branching out on 'The Ingraham Angle.'

Caitlin Clark is the most popular player in the WNBA as a result of her talent, ability and flair for making shots few of her peers would think of attempting. She's also White. And for that reason alone, the vast majority of modern sportswriters or sports media personalities appear to hate her.

The latest example is Nancy Armour, a columnist for USA Today Sports, who put together one of the most bizarre, inexplicable, unacceptable articles you'll ever see attacking Clark. Some of her complaints about Clark's penchant for sometimes flopping or going after the officials are legitimate issues that the 24-year-old should address.

But other complaints? Well, let's just say Armour left the world of rationality far, far behind.

DAVE PORTNOY BLASTS USA TODAY COLUMNIST FOR CLAIMING CAITLIN CLARK’S REFEREE COMPLAINTS FUEL RACISM

Caitlin Clark #22 of the Indiana Fever during the game against the Phoenix Mercury at Gainbridge Fieldhouse on June 24, 2026 in Indianapolis, Indiana. (Andy Lyons / Getty Images)

She stated that Clark arguing calls puts "Black" and "queer" WNBA players "in danger." Even though Clark herself has often been aggressively fouled or mistreated, with little protection from the league's officials. She brought up examples of contact that she claims Clark exaggerated, then blames her for certain fans sending inappropriate and offensive messages to WNBA players. Even though Clark has never, and would never, encourage that behavior. In fact, she did the opposite, saying in 2024 that fans should not use her to push racism or harass opposing players.

But all that pales in comparison to the profoundly absurd comparison to Emmett Till. What does Till, a 14-year-old Black teen who was abducted, tortured and eventually lynched in 1955 Mississippi after an accusation by a White woman, have to do with Caitlin Clark? Well, because Clark's "actions" are supposedly influencing, uh, lynching? Seriously.

"The WNBA's All-Star Game this weekend is in Chicago, Emmett Till's hometown, and a local art gallery is currently hosting an exhibit imagining his life if he hadn't been lynched. While it’s incumbent on the WNBA to do more to protect its players, there also should be an expectation on Clark to recognize the wider consequences of her actions," Armour wrote.

Caitlin Clark watches the action during the Indiana Fever's highly anticipated matchup with the Las Vegas Aces. (David Becker/NBAE via Getty Images)

Caitlin Clark telling officials she's been fouled on a basketball court can lead to "consequences" like Emmett Till, is the implication. This paragraph grabbed so much attention for its idiocy and offensive premise that Armour issued an apology at the ancestral home of sportswriter liberalism, BlueSky, saying it was an "inartful comparison."

USA TODAY REPORTER APOLOGIZES FOR INVOKING EMMETT TILL IN COLUMN ABOUT CAITLIN CLARK AFTER UPSETTING THE WOKE

No kidding. OutKick reached out to the Emmett Till Foundation to see if they had any comment about Armour trying to tie Caitlin Clark to a lynch mob, but as of time of publishing, had not received a response.

Armour has a long history of ludicrous columns, including one just before the start of the 2026 World Cup claiming that the U.S. had already "lost" because of President Donald Trump, or something. But she's just a symptom of a larger problem. Sportswriting and sports media is broken. Consumed by politics. Unable to cover things objectively, fairly, or without injecting their own personal beliefs where it doesn't belong.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

There are countless examples, and it's cost employers ratings, revenue and prestige. Ryan Clark, now formerly of ESPN. Mina Kimes. Jemele Hill. Essentially the entire staff of The Athletic. Los Angeles Times writers who have pushed far-left ideology on Dodgers fans and demanded their local team avoid visiting the White House. The Ringer. Remember the reaction and outrage from many sports media personalities when the US Men's National Hockey Team won the gold medal and laughed at a joke made by President Trump? They "failed to meet the cultural moment." Or didn't "read the room."

USA Today sports columnist Nancy Armour smiles after an interview during a 2025 WNBA Draft watch party at Studio IX. (Lucas Boland-USA Today)

How many media figures asked Steve Kerr about his disregard for the truth with ICE-involved shootings in Minnesota, other than OutKick? Or Doc Rivers? How many in sports media criticized teams visiting the Biden White House to celebrate their titles? What about Sophie Cunningham? After she bravely spoke out to defend women's sports, she was immediately peppered with questions from a furious WNBA press unable to comprehend someone telling the truth about biology.

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

Nancy Armour is just one in a long, long line of sportswriters who are so consumed with progressive politics and injecting it into every story, every situation, every athlete, that they can't stop even when audiences or readers beg them to. Comparing Caitlin Clark to Emmett Till might sound unreasonable or even insane for most readers. But that's how Armour thinks. And the worst part is, it's not just her. It's all of them.

title: Four Palestinians and one Israeli killed during West Bank settler attack
excerpt:
Several others seriously injured in attack that residents of Palestinian village say also involved Israeli military

Four Palestinians and one Israeli have been killed in a shooting in the northern We...
body_text:
Several others seriously injured in attack that residents of Palestinian village say also involved Israeli military

Four Palestinians and one Israeli have been killed in a shooting in the northern West Bank, health officials have said, during what residents described as a settler attack also involving the Israeli military. Several more people were seriously injured.

The serious incident in the Palestinian village of Tell, near Nablus, is the deadliest attack in the West Bank this year and has the potential to lead to a series of reprisals between Israeli-backed settlers and Palestinian communities.

A Palestinian resident of the village said Israeli settlers had come to the village early on Friday morning and that they were soon joined by the military. The Israeli military said it had come to the aid of Israeli hikers.

Walid Zidan, the head of Tel village council, told Agence France-Presse that about 20 Israeli settlers had entered the village at about 8.30am local time. “Villagers came out to defend their homes and property. Clashes and physical altercations broke out between the residents and the settlers,” Zidan said.

“Israeli soldiers were present and together with the settlers began opening fire … It was the settlers who stormed the village, with the intention of killing, vandalising and setting property on fire,” he said.

The Palestinian foreign ministry condemned what it described as a “massacre committed by settlers in partnership with the occupation forces in the village of Tell”.

The circumstances of the shooting were not immediately clear, but video of the attack posted online has shown several people who appear to be Palestinians on the ground in a field near the village who appear to have been shot.

The Israeli military said it had deployed soldiers after reports of an attack against Israeli civilians who were hiking near Havat Gilad, a West Bank settlement near Tell. According to the military, the attackers seized the weapon of a member of the security forces at the scene and opened fire.

The Palestinian health ministry in Ramallah said the four Palestinians were killed by Israeli forces. All four were from the same family and have the same last name, according to people from the village.

Israel’s Magen David Adom rescue services did not say who had killed the Israeli, a man in his 30s. Social media channels run by settler communities said he was from Havat Gilad.

Violence in the Israeli-occupied West Bank has flared in recent days.

The Israeli prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, will meet his defence minister and chief of staff to discuss a response, his office said. A statement said Israel would “act forcefully against the terrorists”, although the incident has not been investigated.

Israeli settlers have called for a wave of attacks to “erase” the village.

All Israeli settlements in the West Bank are illegal under international law.

Palestinian health officials said they were treating four injured Palestinians, three in critical condition. Israel’s rescue service said two injured Israelis were being airlifted out of the West Bank.

The Israeli military said it had imposed a “temporary cordon” around Nablus and Tell.

title: First Thing: Trump imposes fresh tariffs on UK, EU and dozens of other trading partners
excerpt:
Measures replace 10% global duty and come after supreme court declared many of the earlier tariffs illegal. Plus, how secondhand fashion got so expensive

Donald Trump has imposed a fresh round of tar...
body_text:
Measures replace 10% global duty and come after supreme court declared many of the earlier tariffs illegal. Plus, how secondhand fashion got so expensive

Donald Trump has imposed a fresh round of tariffs on more than 80 countries to replace a 10% global duty that was due to expire, provoking a wave of criticism and protests from US allies and trading partners.

In the latest attempt to instate aggressive trade policies despite challenges by supreme court, the president has imposed tariffs of between 10% and 12.5% on dozens of countries, including the UK, Mexico, Canada, Australia, India, China and the 27 member states that make up the EU. In effect, it replaces the blanket 10% tariff that Trump imposed in February, after the supreme court declared that many of his earlier tariffs were illegal.

What is Trump’s justification for the tariffs? The newest tariffs, announced late last night by the US trade representative, Jamieson Greer, would fall under section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, aimed against countries that engage in forced labor. Trump had said his administration would investigate unfair trading practices to impose permanent tariffs as soon as the February supreme court decision was announced.

How have the affected countries reacted to the news? The ‘forced labour’ rationale for Trump tariffs has been met with bewilderment, with some saying their labor laws are stronger than those in the US.

Concerns are growing in southern California over a cluster of “exceptionally rare” cancer cases that have affected several children in a suburban community.

Families living in the Orange county neighborhood of Ladera Ranch, home to about 26,000 people, reported that six children had been diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma, according to Katrina Foley, the vice-chair of the county board of supervisors.

Ewing sarcoma, a rare cancer that primarily affects bone and soft tissue, typically affects young adults and children.

How are the authorities responding? Last week, Bill Essayli, an assistant US attorney, asked the Environmental Protection Agency to investigate the cluster to determine if there is an environmental cause.

Trump has said he is close to deciding whether to mount “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes launched during the months-long conflict with Tehran.

Speaking to the Axios news site, the president said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.”

Trump made his remarks on Thursday, as oil prices once again soared past the symbolic $100 for a barrel of Brent crude, the international benchmark, and amid indications the price could continue to climb.

What else did he say? Trump acknowledged that such a move would have consequences but said he had not yet made a final decision. Israel “would join in two minutes if I ask them to”, he claimed, adding: “We don’t need anybody.”

The Minnesota man who pleaded guilty to killing a senior Democratic lawmaker and her husband was sentenced to spend the rest of his life in prison yesterday, a little over a year after the political attacks.

A panel of federal health advisers yesterday narrowly recommended easing access to several peptides popular with wellness influencers and celebrities, despite warnings from government scientists that the chemicals have not been shown to be safe or effective.

Four Palestinians and one Israeli have been killed in a shooting in the northern West Bank, according to health officials. Several more people were seriously wounded.

The Indian “cockroach” activist Sonam Wangchuk has ended a 26-day hunger strike over an exam paper scandal that has spiralled into a wide-ranging, youth-led wave of demonstrations against Narendra Modi’s government.
