After 10 years and much complaining by Ryan Gosling, a “minor adjustment”to correct his famously bent wrist has been made to the La La Land poster. The actor has previously said the pose “haunts” him because he ignored the choreographers and bent his wrist during filming, not realising the image would become the film’s poster. But many fans have criticised the change, arguing the quirky pose had become an celebrated part of the movie’s identity.

When the much-maligned Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) was slashing federal spending in February 2025, the defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, ordered officials to find annual cuts of 8% for five years – a plan that briefly sent Palantir and other defense stocks tumbling before quietly disappearing. He is now seeking a $454bn increase for 2027 military spending, taking the budget to almost $1.5tn, calling it a “generational investment”.

Janelle Best, the owner of a vintage boutique, has been rummaging through thrift shop bins since the 1990s. When she started, shopping for secondhand clothes carried a stigma. “It was either you were a freak or you were poor – and I was a freak.” Today, thanks to gen Z resellers and platforms such as Depop, thrifting has ballooned into a $350bn industry.

Recent hot, dry weather in Canada has triggered hundreds of wildfires across the country. As of Wednesday, more than 900 were burning, scorching about 3m hectares of land. The greatest concern is in the Northwest Territories and Ontario, where several remain out of control. The risk of further blazes remains high as North America enters the peak of fire season.

Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman at the helm of Doge.

First Thing is delivered to thousands of inboxes every weekday. If you’re not already signed up, subscribe now.

If you have any questions or comments about any of our newsletters please email newsletters@theguardian.com

title: Four Palestinians killed as Israeli settlers raid occupied West Bank town
excerpt:
Four Palestinians have been killed in an attack by Israeli forces and settlers southwest of Nablus in the occupied West Bank.

Four other Palestinians were injured in the attack on the town of Tal, “t...
body_text:
Four Palestinians have been killed in an attack by Israeli forces and settlers southwest of Nablus in the occupied West Bank.

Four other Palestinians were injured in the attack on the town of Tal, “three of them critically”, the Palestinian Ministry of Health said in a statement on Friday.

Israeli health officials separately reported that one Israeli settler was killed and others injured in the area.

The town of Tal, which is close to the illegal Israeli settlement of Havat Gilad, was attacked by Israeli settlers earlier on Friday, local and security sources said. The settlers opened live fire before clashes broke out, as Palestinians tried to protect their homes.

Essam Saifi, a local leader in the Palestinian Fatah party from Tal, told the Reuters news agency ⁠in a phone call that about 25 to 30 Israeli settlers initially attacked the eastern ⁠area of the town and tried to break into ⁠two houses there. The residents came out to confront them and the settlers opened fire.

Half an hour later, Saifi said, the Israelis returned ‌and attacked the western part of the town, striking a child with a weapon. This caused chaos, and the Israeli military ‌arrived and ‌started shooting, along with the settlers, he added.

The Israeli army, however, said it had dispatched troops to the area near the Havat Gilad following what it called “an attack on Israelis hiking in the area”.

The military claimed a Palestinian had stolen a weapon from local security personnel and fired at the Israeli settlers.

According to Israel’s Magen David Adom (MDA) rescue services, one Israeli was killed in the area and two wounded people were evacuated by helicopter.

Reporting from Nablus, Al Jazeera’s Nida Ibrahim said Friday’s incident took place after Israeli settlers stormed Tal under the “pretext that they were hiking in the area”.

“Given what we’ve been seeing time and again in these attacks, it’s the settlers who are armed, who are provoking Palestinians and stealing their land. Sometimes we see these settlers pull up in caravans and start putting up temporary tents that later become illegal Israeli settlements,” Ibrahim said.

After the incident, Israeli forces imposed a security cordon around Nablus and Tal.

“In these situations, the Israeli army generally prevents the injured Palestinians from accessing medical help by setting up siege-like conditions around the city,” Ibrahim said. “And that’s what we’re seeing right now, as all checkpoints leading to Tal have been closed.”

According to the Palestinian health ministry, the killings in Tal bring the toll to 86 Palestinians killed by Israelis across the West Bank since the beginning of the year, including 21 shot by settlers.

The Palestinian foreign ministry condemned the killing of Palestinians by settlers in the “strongest terms”.

“The Ministry affirms that this massacre represents a renewed image of the ongoing Nakba to which our Palestinian people are subjected, and reflects the integration of crimes and roles and the deliberate escalation of these coordinated crimes between the occupation authorities and their forces and settler gangs,” it said in a post on X on Friday.

Meanwhile in Israel, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu pledged to respond firmly after an Israeli was killed near Tal, and said he was ⁠convening an emergency meeting to discuss a response.

Far-right Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich also called on the army to act with an “iron fist” against the Palestinian town and its surroundings where the Israeli died.

“The people of the farms and the heroic settlement pioneers are the defensive wall of the State of Israel. We will continue to strengthen and honour them for their steadfast stand for our existence,” he said in a post on X.

title: France evacuates Cap Ferret peninsula and Spain’s Sanchez warns of ‘dramatic situation’ as wildfires spread – Europe live
excerpt:
Evacuees have been told to leave using hundreds of mobilised boats or the only road linking the area with the mainland

Thousands of people have been forced to flee parts of south-west France amid wor...
body_text:
Evacuees have been told to leave using hundreds of mobilised boats or the only road linking the area with the mainland

Thousands of people have been forced to flee parts of south-west France amid worsening wildfires that prompted the authorities to order people to evacuate from the Cap Ferret peninsula along the Atlantic coast.

Evacuees have been told to leave the area before 10am, using hundreds of mobilised boats or the only road linking the area with the mainland.

More than 10,000 hectares are affected by the two concurrent wildfires, which last night crossed the firebreak established by firefighters.

The French authorities formally requested the activation of the European Union’s Civil Protection Mechanism to tap into the bloc’s resources as they battle the fires, with Emmanuel Macron saying the situation remained “very intense.”

The EU’s crisis commissioner, Hadja Lahbib, confirmed that “following France’s activation of the EU’s civil protection mechanism, the EU is providing assistance and deploying 3 water bomber aircraft.”

Dangerous situation is also unfolding in Spain, with the Spanish government declaring a national emergency over wildfires near Madrid and Avila, forcing more than 10,000 people to evacuate.

It’s Friday, 24 July 2026, it’s Jakub Krupa here, and this is Europe Live.

Authorities in south-western France have ordered the total evacuation of the Cap Ferret peninsula on the Atlantic coast by land and sea as an “intense and highly unpredictable” wildfire advances on one of the country’s best-known holiday spots.

The fire had burned more than 10,000 hectares (24,700 acres) by Friday morning, the French interior minister, Laurent Nuñez, said, with 40,000 people already or about to be evacuated, including thousands of tourists from campsites and rental homes.

Boats began ferrying holidaymakers and residents off four piers on the peninsula early on Thursday after the Gironde prefecture ordered its progressive evacuation, “village by village”, by sea or by the only road linking the area with the mainland.

Brutal heat, as climate breakdown triggers more frequent and extreme weather events – causing thousands of excess deaths, evaporating soils and fuelling wildfires – has also prompted Spain to declare a national emergency, with blazes burning out of control.

Worth stressing that the declaration of a national emergency in Spain is a big deal.

The move – which allows the central government to assume overall control of powers and responsibilities that are usually the domain of Spain’s self-governing regions – has only happened once before, which was during the nationwide blackout in April 2025.

Spain’s regional governments are sometimes reluctant to cede their powers to the central government when emergencies occur.

The deadly floods that hit Valencia at the end of October 2024, killing more than 230 people, were classified as a level 2 emergency, meaning that the regional government was in charge of the response effort.

Had the disaster been declared a level 3 emergency at the request of the Valencian government, then the central government would have stepped in – as has happened with the current wildfires.

Over in France, it’s not just the south-west that gets affected, but the country’s south-east regions are also being hard hit, AFP noted.

Dozens of fires flared up and are being fanned by the mistral wind around the historic village of Cotignac – officials saying around 25 houses had been destroyed since Tuesday.

In an update issued on Friday morning, Spain’s environment ministry said 114,796.42 hectares of land had been burned by forest fires so far this year, compared with 25,133.26 by the same time last year.

It also said that 29 big forest fires had been recorded so far in 2026. That figure represents a huge increase from the average of nine big forest fires between January and the end of July per year over the past decade.

Spain’s prime minister Pedro Sánchez responded to the wildfires ravaging parts of country and neighbouring France, amid heightened warnings about their further spread.

“We’re living through a dramatic situation, not just in various Spanish provinces but also in regions of neighbouring countries. Forest fires are devastating thousands of hectares and affecting numerous communities.

“I want to express my deepest solidarity with all those who are watching the fire burn their forests and threaten their homes. The government has deployed all available resources. It will be by your side during the emergency and the recovery. Thank you to the BRIF (Forest Fire Brigades), the UME (Military Emergency Unit), the Security Forces, the Civil Protection System, and all the emergency services that are fighting day and night to stop the advance of the flames and extinguish the fire.”

On Thursday night, authorities in the Madrid region asked the central government to step in and declare the fires around the region a national emergency.

Madrid’s regional president, Isabel Díaz Ayuso, commended the people of the region for their response to the fires and thanked the emergency services for their efforts.

“We remain attentive minute by minute, deeply grateful for the work, solidarity, and patience of everyone,” she said. “As soon as the magnitude of this disaster is known, the Community of Madrid will announce aid and plans to mitigate its consequences.”

In a video posted on social media, the shift commander for Madrid’s emergency medical services urged people to be extra vigilant as the fires continued to burn.

“If people notice smoke or ashes in the air, we recommend that they do not take part in any outdoor activities,” said Ester Armela. “If people have any chronic breathing problems of allergies, they should use facemasks when going outside.” If people developed serious symptoms, she added, people they should ring the 112 emergency line.

Spain’s prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, chaired an emergency response meeting at the Moncloa palace on Friday morning as the state meteorological office warned that the danger of more fires remained high.

“Today, the fire danger will remain very high or extreme across most of the territory despite the drop in temperatures,” it said. “Conditions will improve slightly over the weekend, but the danger will rise again in the following days.

Spain’s interior ministry has also requested for fire-fighting aircraft through the European Union’s Civil Protection Mechanism (10:53).

Meanwhile, Spain has also requested the support of four firefighting aircraft through the European Union’s Civil Protection Mechanism, the bloc’s system for coordinating disaster assistance among member states, to help tackle multiple wildfires near Madrid, the interior ministry said.

Overnight, France did the same, as reported earlier (9:48).

There are also two other emerging news lines in Europe this morning.

Romania’s military just shot down a drone breaching its airspace, the country’s president Nicușor Dan said. He said the drone was spotted over an uninhabited area of the country, allowing an F-16 fighter jet to shoot it down “without any risks”.

Elsewhere, the Ukrainian capital Kyiv is under a ballistic missile warning with explosions reported across the city, after Ukraine struck warehouses supplying Russian army overnight.

I will keep an eye on both of these stories for you.

Nuñez also warned about a second fire in Biscarrosse, which he said was also “significant” and “somewhat similar” to the main fire in Gironde since it broke out yesterday afternoon.

It has burned over 2,500 hectares, prompting the evacuation of just over 20,000 people, he said.

The minister said that the weather conditions were “highly unfavourable,” making it difficult for more than 700 firefighters deployed there to tackle the blaze, and “the fire continues to spread.”

Speaking at a press conference this morning, France’s interior minister Laurent Nuñez said that the largest wildfire in Gironde – which started in Somos, but now moved to the Cap Ferret peninsula – has already burned more than 10,000 hectares.

“As I speak, a total of 40,000 people have been or are currently being evacuated due to this fire,” he said.

Nuñez warned that “the fire is currently not under control and continues to spread,” with around 1,000 firefighters involved in battling the blaze.

In total, more than 50,000 hectares have been burned in wildfires since the start of the year.

Thousands of people have been forced to flee parts of south-west France amid worsening wildfires that prompted the authorities to order people to evacuate from the Cap Ferret peninsula along the Atlantic coast.

Evacuees have been told to leave the area before 10am, using hundreds of mobilised boats or the only road linking the area with the mainland.

More than 10,000 hectares are affected by the two concurrent wildfires, which last night crossed the firebreak established by firefighters.

The French authorities formally requested the activation of the European Union’s Civil Protection Mechanism to tap into the bloc’s resources as they battle the fires, with Emmanuel Macron saying the situation remained “very intense.”

The EU’s crisis commissioner, Hadja Lahbib, confirmed that “following France’s activation of the EU’s civil protection mechanism, the EU is providing assistance and deploying 3 water bomber aircraft.”

Dangerous situation is also unfolding in Spain, with the Spanish government declaring a national emergency over wildfires near Madrid and Avila, forcing more than 10,000 people to evacuate.

It’s Friday, 24 July 2026, it’s Jakub Krupa here, and this is Europe Live.

title: Andy Burnham to spend first working day at new No 10 North in Manchester – UK politics live
excerpt:
PM will chair the inaugural meeting of the national economic council that will aim to shift economic decision-making away ‌from London

Andy Burnham will hold his first meeting in No 10 North in Manch...
body_text:
PM will chair the inaugural meeting of the national economic council that will aim to shift economic decision-making away ‌from London

Andy Burnham will hold his first meeting in No 10 North in Manchester today, the new branch of Downing Street in the north-west that the prime minister has described as “the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen”.

He will chair the inaugural meeting of the national economic council [NEC], a cabinet committee first launched in 2008 by Gordon Brown in response to the financial crash. It will bring together senior ministers and regional mayors to shift economic decision-making away ‌from London.

Writing for the Guardian, Burnham said: “It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.”

double quotation markNo 10 North will be responsible for accelerating devolution. It will drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK. It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council housebuilding to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way. Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.”

Based in Heron House in central Manchester, in the same building which houses GCHQ in the north-west, Burnham is expected to work from No 10 North once a week, bringing him closer to his Makerfield constituents and to the community where he grew up. Other ministers including Angela Rayner and Lucy Powell, who hail from the north-west, will also regularly use the office, the government said.

Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch accused the prime minister of governing “like he’s still ​the mayor of ⁠Manchester”, saying he needed to focus on broader national challenges.

The Welsh first minister said he has a “Celtic alliance” with his counterpart in Scotland, as he visited Glasgow for the Commonwealth Games, PA reports.

Rhun ap Iorwerth said Plaid Cymru worked closely with John Swinney’s SNP, and that he was “optimistic” after meeting prime minister Andy Burnham yesterday.

Iorwerth’s party won the May election in Wales and, with an SNP win in Scotland and Sinn Féin holding the first minister role in Northern Ireland, all of the UK’s devolved nations have independence-supporting parties in power.

Speaking to BBC Radio Scotland’s Breakfast programme this morning, where he was asked about the relationship between nationalist parties in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland, Iorwerth said: “I think we have a Celtic alliance. I work closely with John Swinney …

“I have a good relationship. My party and the SNP were often referred to as sister parties.

“We have worked together at Westminster for many years prior to devolution, and we work closely now.”

The Times has reported this morning that Andy Burnham is expected to “move quickly” to scale up North Sea oil and gas production as part of his policy blitz to tackle the cost of living.

Industry sources suggested to the newspaper that the prime minister and his new energy secretary, Miatta Fahnbulleh, are considering approving oil and gas production at the Jackdaw and Rosebank sites off the coast of Scotland to help drive down costs this winter.

A source said: “Once we are past the consultation phase, the ball is in their court. From the sounds of it, there is willingness to move quickly this time around.

“An August approval would allow production to commence by October 1. Everything is constructed, the platform is in place, it’s just a piece of paper standing in the way.”

As the Guardian’s environment correspondent Sandra Laville writes in her profile of the new energy secretary, Fahnbulleh was a surprising choice for the role, especially given her previous apparent opposition to new drilling in the North Sea while she was head of the New Economics Foundation (NEF) thinktank.

double quotation markIn a joint report with Friends of the Earth in 2021, her organisation called for a total end to new drilling in the North Sea basin, the cancellation of all future licensing rounds, the revocation of all undeveloped licences and the rejection of any applications for development consent. Today, those who collaborated with her in the past are watching her first steps closely.

“She must hold the line against harmful fossil fuel expansion – including the highly contentious Jackdaw and Rosebank fields – make cheap, clean energy available to all and build on Ed Miliband’s efforts to scale up our renewable power capacity,” said Imogen Dow, energy lead for Friends of the Earth.”

Heavy police presence this morning outside Heron House in central Manchester, where the prime minister will hold his first meeting at No 10 North:

Consumer confidence in the UK jumped this month by its biggest margin for nearly three years as the prospect of Andy Burnham becoming prime minister became almost certain.

The data company GfK said a “Burnham bounce” along with a strong run by the men’s England football team at the World Cup and sunny weather had lifted the mood of consumers in its long-running monthly survey.

Burnham’s byelection victory on 18 June, which put the former mayor of Greater Manchester in pole position to take over from Keir Starmer, was credited with helping to improve consumers’ outlook for the economy over the next year by eight points.

Their view of the economy’s performance over the previous year also improved by 10 points. The overall score in the GfK index increased by six points to -17 in July, the highest level since January and the biggest rise since November 2023.

Neil Bellamy, consumer insights director at GfK, said July had delivered “a wave of optimism”.

A review of the early prisoner release scheme ordered by Andy Burnham could be conducted “in the coming days and weeks”, a minister has said.

The prime minister hit pause on the scheme that would allow thousands of prisoners in England and Wales to be released early from September after criticism from survivors and victims’ families.

Burnham ordered an urgent review into the scheme, saying he wanted to “minimise risk to the public”.

Speaking to Sky News this morning, Sally Jameson, a junior housing minister and former prison officer, said: “I think the justice ministers have said that the urgent review will be conducted in the coming days and weeks to make sure that we have a timely response to that, so that the prison system and the criminal justice system can react and cope accordingly.”

The housing secretary, Angela Rayner, has said the new Andy Burnham administration is on “borrowed time” to turn the country around.

Appearing on BBC Radio 4’s Today programme, Rayner said the flurry of announcements made by the new prime minister – cap on bus fares, VAT cut on electricity bills and support for pubs and clubs – showed he understood the frustration over the cost of living.

“We’re on borrowed time if we don’t get a move on and I think Andy acknowledged that,” she said. “As prime minister he stood on the streets of Downing Street without notes and spoke directly to the public and said ‘we get it, I get the frustration’.”

Turning to housing policy, Rayner said the Government’s goal to build 1.5m homes in this parliament is “a really difficult stretched target” but insisted she is not dropping it.

She said: “It certainly got more challenging, and we’ve seen things like construction costs go up etc, which has had a real negative impact on that.

“But I’m not dropping it. I’m just saying that it is a really difficult stretched target.”

Earlier on BBC Breakfast, Rayner said she is not looking at bringing in rent controls.

She said changes made through renters’ rights legislation brought in under Labour so far “are already having a significant impact on the market”.

Speaking to BBC Breakfast, she said: “We won’t be looking at rent controls if that’s what you’re getting at at the moment.

“That is not something that we’re looking to do because we’ve seen what’s happened in areas like Scotland. It hasn’t necessarily brought rents down.”

On Monday, while the cameras were fixed on that famous black door in Downing Street, something important was happening 165 miles away. A small team of officials and advisers were walking into an office building in Manchester to set about the remaking of the British state.

This was the beginning of No 10 North: the first major act of this government and what I hope will be seen as its most defining.

Over the course of the past 40 years, Britain has taken a series of wrong turns. In the 1980s, political power was centralised, economic power was privatised and communities were deindustrialised. Many of the places most affected have not recovered since and are still in decline. They have been unable to turn things around because they simply do not have the necessary power to do so. This feeling of neglect has lain underneath the increasingly fractious politics that has bedevilled Britain in recent times.

Solving this problem is crucial for the cohesion of our country. It is something with which it has wrestled for a long time but never got right. We have seen gimmicks, such as levelling up. We have seen the shifting of bits of the British state around the map to no great effect.

Why has none of it worked? It is partly because, even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London. It is also because, in the very centre of government itself, Nos 10 and 11 have not been sufficiently aligned – particularly on growth.

This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run.

Andy Burnham will hold his first meeting in No 10 North in Manchester today, the new branch of Downing Street in the north-west that the prime minister has described as “the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen”.

He will chair the inaugural meeting of the national economic council [NEC], a cabinet committee first launched in 2008 by Gordon Brown in response to the financial crash. It will bring together senior ministers and regional mayors to shift economic decision-making away ‌from London.

Writing for the Guardian, Burnham said: “It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.”

double quotation markNo 10 North will be responsible for accelerating devolution. It will drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK. It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council housebuilding to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way. Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.”

Based in Heron House in central Manchester, in the same building which houses GCHQ in the north-west, Burnham is expected to work from No 10 North once a week, bringing him closer to his Makerfield constituents and to the community where he grew up. Other ministers including Angela Rayner and Lucy Powell, who hail from the north-west, will also regularly use the office, the government said.

Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch accused the prime minister of governing “like he’s still ​the mayor of ⁠Manchester”, saying he needed to focus on broader national challenges.

title: Samsung Galaxy Z Fold8 lineup adds three choices
excerpt:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

Samsung is giving those of you who are looking to buy a new phone more choices this week with three new Galaxy Z folda...
body_text:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

Samsung is giving those of you who are looking to buy a new phone more choices this week with three new Galaxy Z foldable models built for very different kinds of use.

The Galaxy Z Fold8 Ultra focuses on productivity, cameras and a larger workspace. The regular Galaxy Z Fold8 takes a wider approach for reading, gaming and watching content. Meanwhile, the Galaxy Z Flip8 puts more apps and AI tools on its outer screen.

Here is how the three new models compare and what you should know before deciding whether one belongs in your pocket.

CyberGuy Live: Missed "Sick of Spam?" Get the replay and checklist

Our free CyberGuy Live class, "Sick of Spam?" , has ended, but you can still watch the full replay and download our spam-stopping checklist. Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson walks you step by step through simple ways to reduce robocalls, spam texts, junk email and unwanted messages. You’ll also learn how to curb political texts, clean up your inbox and spot messages that could put your personal information at risk.

Get the free replay and checklist now at CyberGuyLive.com.

AMAZON LEO SATELLITE INTERNET TAKES ON STARLINK

Samsung’s new Galaxy Z lineup includes the Fold8 Ultra, wider Fold8 and compact Flip8, giving buyers three very different ways to use a foldable phone. (Samsung)

The Galaxy Z Fold8 Ultra is the model for someone who wants a phone that can also serve as a small tablet. It opens to an 8-inch main screen, giving you room to view two apps at once. You could keep your email open beside your calendar or watch a video while looking up related information. The 6.5-inch outer screen also works more like the display on a traditional smartphone, so you do not need to open the phone for every task.

Samsung says the Fold8 Ultra measures about 0.16 inches thick when opened and weighs about 7.6 ounces. That is fairly light for a phone with such a large folding screen. Still, you will notice more bulk than you would with a traditional phone. The Fold8 Ultra also has the most capable camera system in the lineup. Its 200 MP main camera should capture more detail, especially when you crop or enlarge a photo. A 50 MP ultra-wide camera helps with landscapes and group pictures. You also get a separate telephoto camera with 3x optical zoom.

That zoom lens could be useful when you are photographing a child onstage, an animal across the yard or a landmark that you cannot approach. The other two phones lack a dedicated telephoto camera.

A 5,000 mAh battery powers the phone. Samsung supports 45-watt wired charging, although you need to buy a compatible charger separately. The phone also includes extra cooling to help it stay responsive while you use several apps or record high-resolution video.

The Galaxy Z Fold8 Ultra starts at $2,099.99 with 256 GB of storage. Samsung also sells 512 GB and 1 TB versions.

The regular Galaxy Z Fold8 takes a different approach. Instead of following the taller design of previous Fold phones, it uses a shorter and wider shape. The 5.5-inch cover screen handles quick tasks while the phone is closed. Open it and you get a 7.6-inch main display with a shape closer to a small tablet.

That wider screen could make articles, e-books and websites more comfortable to read. It should also give movies and games more room without leaving as much unused space around the picture. At about 7.1 ounces, Samsung calls this its lightest Galaxy Z Fold so far. The phone measures about 0.18 inches thick when opened and 0.38 inches when closed. That lower weight could make it easier to hold during a long reading session or video call.

The Fold8 has two 50 MP rear cameras. One handles everyday photos while the other captures a wider view. You can still take detailed pictures and record video in up to 8K resolution. However, there is no dedicated telephoto camera. Digital zoom reaches 10x, but it will not provide the same clarity as the Ultra model's optical zoom. The Fold8 uses the same Snapdragon processor found in the Ultra and carries a 4,800 mAh battery. It also supports 45-watt wired charging with a compatible charger sold separately.

The Galaxy Z Fold8 starts at $1,899.99 with 256 GB of storage. You can also choose 512 GB or 1 TB.

The Galaxy Z Flip8 is the most compact model. It looks like a traditional smartphone when open, then folds in half so it takes up less room in a pocket or purse. The main screen measures 6.9 inches. When you close the phone, a 4.1-inch FlexWindow remains available on the outside.

You can use that smaller screen to check the weather, view your calendar or respond to supported messages. You may also access some apps and AI tools without opening the phone. This could help when you only need one piece of information. Instead of opening your phone and becoming distracted by other alerts, you can check what you need from the outside screen.

The Flip8 weighs about 6.3 ounces and measures about 0.24 inches thick when opened. It is the lightest of Samsung's three new foldables. The Flip8 includes a 50 MP main camera and a 12 MP ultra-wide camera. Its folding design also allows the phone to stand on a table by itself.

That can make hands-free video calls or group photos easier. You can place the phone on a table, adjust the angle and step into the picture without finding someone to hold it. The outer screen can show a preview when you take a selfie with the main camera. Samsung also added tools that help keep video level while you move. The 4,300 mAh battery supports 25-watt wired charging. Samsung says the phone can reach up to 55% in about 30 minutes under its laboratory conditions. The required charger comes separately.

The Galaxy Z Flip8 starts at $1,199.99 with 256 GB of storage. A 512 GB version is also available.

HOW TO SHARE VACATION PHOTOS ON ANY SCREEN

The Galaxy Z Flip8’s 4.1-inch FlexWindow lets you check apps, view updates and use supported AI tools without opening the phone. (Samsung)

All three phones run Android 17 with Samsung’s One UI 9 software. They also include two Galaxy AI features that serve different purposes.

Now Brief works like a personalized daily summary. It can gather the weather, upcoming appointments and reminders in one place, saving you from opening several apps. You can choose which types of information appear in the summary.

Now Nudge suggests a helpful next step based on what is happening on your screen. For example, if someone mentions a date while you are exchanging messages, the phone may offer to open your calendar. On the Fold models, it can place the calendar beside the conversation so you can check your schedule without leaving the message.

Gemini Intelligence can handle broader requests across supported apps. You might ask it to find a restaurant or help with a reservation. I would still review any important action involving travel, appointments or purchases before approving it.

Samsung has also added an AI Assistant Activity dashboard. It gives you one place to review supported actions the phone completed on your behalf. Privacy Alerts can warn you when an app tries to use certain permissions in the background, while Knox and Knox Vault help protect sensitive information.

The Flip8 can stand on its own for hands-free photos and video calls while the outer screen helps you frame the shot. (Samsung)

Samsung uses a new Flex Titanium structure beneath the folding displays. The company says this helps support the screens and reduce the visibility of the crease. The two Fold screens can reach up to 3,000 nits of brightness. Samsung also uses a low-reflection finish to make the displays easier to see outdoors. All three phones carry an IP48 water resistance rating.

Samsung has updated Smart Switch for people moving from an iPhone. You can scan a QR code and transfer supported information wirelessly without installing another app on the iPhone. Samsung says the transfer can include passwords, passkeys and call history. Some accessibility settings and eSIM information may also move, depending on your device and carrier.

Quick Share also gains compatibility with AirDrop. That means supported Galaxy and Apple devices can exchange files in both directions. Moving years of photos and account information can make switching phones feel like a chore. These updates could make the move to Galaxy much easier.

Preorders are open through Samsung, major U.S. carriers and participating retailers. General availability begins Aug. 7, 2026. The Fold8 Ultra comes in Graphite, Cream and Violet Shadow. Green Shadow is an online-exclusive color.

The Fold8 comes in Graphite, Cream and Lavender. Samsung offers Pistachio as an online-exclusive choice. The Flip8 comes in Graphite, Cream and Pink. Mint is the online-exclusive option.

Samsung advertises up to $1,200 in savings with an eligible trade-in during the preorder period. Buyers who choose no trade-in may receive up to $200 in Samsung credit for eligible add-ons. Offers depend on the phone and trade-in condition. Each model also includes a six-month Google AI Pro trial with 5 TB of cloud storage. The service costs $19.99 per month after the trial unless you cancel.

Samsung has made the differences between its foldables much easier to understand. The Fold8 Ultra focuses on a large workspace and stronger cameras. The regular Fold8 favors reading and entertainment, while the Flip8 gives you a phone that becomes smaller when you close it. I like that Samsung now offers two versions of the Fold for different types of use. The new AI activity dashboard also gives you more visibility when the phone takes action on your behalf. The biggest question is whether one of these designs would truly make your phone easier to use every day. That answer will depend on how much you value a larger screen, a more compact shape or Samsung's newest AI tools.

Do you think a larger folding screen would make your phone easier to use? Let us know by writing to us at Cyberguy.com

Copyright 2026 CyberGuy.com. All rights reserved.

Kurt "CyberGuy" Knutsson is an award-winning tech journalist who has a deep love of technology, gear and gadgets that make life better with his contributions for Fox News & FOX Business beginning mornings on "FOX & Friends." Got a tech question? Get Kurt’s free CyberGuy Newsletter, share your voice, a story idea or comment at CyberGuy.com.

title: Wisconsin protester brands Madison as 'corrupt city' during anti-police tirade following deadly shooting
excerpt: Madison Police Chief John Patterson said "I'll step aside" and allowed a protester to take over the microphone during an update on the fatal police shooting of Corey Ruiz. Police said Ruiz was armed w...
body_text:
Madison Police Chief John Patterson said "I'll step aside" and allowed a protester to take over the microphone during an update on the fatal police shooting of Corey Ruiz. Police said Ruiz was armed with a knife.

A Wisconsin police briefing descended into chaos Thursday when a protester seized the podium during a heated demonstration that interrupted officials discussing the fatal police shooting of a homeless man.

The briefing came one day after law enforcement officers shot and killed a homeless man who authorities said wounded an officer with a knife during an encounter Wednesday.

According to officials, officers were responding to reports of a person stealing bicycles and checking vehicle doors. Police identified the man as Corey Durell Ruiz, a 38-year-old homeless resident.

Police said Ruiz was armed with a knife and injured an officer, but protesters accused law enforcement of using excessive force and invoked the 2020 death of George Floyd.

FBI ARRESTS PROTESTER WHO THREATENED TO KILL ICE OFFICER'S FAMILY AT NJ DETENTION CENTER PROTEST, BLANCHE SAYS

A protester took over a police briefing in Madison, Wisconsin, on July 23, 2026, following a police-involved shooting incident.  (City of Madison)

One demonstrator then grabbed the microphone from Police Chief John Patterson.

"We don’t want any of that talk," a protester said.

The protester then delivered a lengthy denunciation of police, capitalism, imperialism and Zionism, accusing Patterson of participating in a system that he said had oppressed his family for generations.

While the disruption continued, another demonstrator held up a sign that read, "Corrupt cops deserve to die too!!!"

Once the protesters quieted down, the chief resumed his presentation, promising his department’s full cooperation with the state investigation, according to The Associated Press.

VIDEO SHOWS UNDERCOVER COPS SHOOTING MAN THREATENING SHOPPERS WITH FAKE GUN IN ‘JUSTIFIED’ DEFENSE: OFFICIALS

Officials said a man pulled out a fixed-blade knife and injured an officer in Madison, Wisconsin, on July 22, 2026.  (Madison Police Department)

Court records show Ruiz had a lengthy criminal history spanning nearly three decades. He repeatedly cycled in and out of jail in cases involving failure to pay child support, vehicle theft, bail jumping, threats to authorities, resisting an officer, theft, attempted invasion of privacy and cocaine possession.

Earlier in the briefing, city officials also called for accountability, questioning the circumstances surrounding the shooting.

Madison Mayor Satya Rhodes-Conway said the shooting would be "rigorously investigated" and that no one should lose their life from a law enforcement encounter.

"It does not matter who you are or what your past is, you should not lose your life as a result of an encounter with law enforcement," Rhodes-Conway said, according to the AP.

Madison Common Council President Sabrina Madison went further, questioning why officers involved in fatal shootings should not be taken into custody.

"My first thought is, when the officer’s walking away, why isn’t he walking away in cuffs," Madison said, according to the AP. "I’m angry, there’s no way around it."

Protesters attend a police briefing in Madison, Wisconsin, on July 23, 2026.  (City of Madison)

Gov. Tony Evers also called for transparency and accountability as the investigation moves forward in a statement released Wednesday night.

According to police, Ruiz fled the area on a bicycle and rode through backyards as officers responded to theft reports.

When officers caught up with Ruiz, officials said he "either fell or was pulled off the bicycle," leading to a struggle with officers in the middle of an intersection.

"During the struggle, the man pulled out a fixed-blade knife and injured an officer," the Madison Police Department said.

After unsuccessful attempts to detain Ruiz with a Taser, the officer injured during the encounter reportedly opened fire. Authorities described him as a veteran officer.

A second officer was also injured during the encounter, but it is unclear whether the injury was caused by the knife, according to police.

Officers reportedly attempted to provide life-saving measures at the scene before Ruiz was transported to a hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

In total, four officers were involved in or witnessed the shooting, and all officers involved will be placed on administrative leave, authorities said.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

Bonny Chu is a Breaking and Trending News Writer for Fox News Digital

title: Germany appoint Klopp as ex-Liverpool coach sought to lift World Cup blues
excerpt:
Germany has hired Jurgen Klopp to coach its national team, giving the ex-Liverpool manager a daunting task to turn around the fortunes of a squad which failed yet again at the World Cup.

Klopp, famed...
body_text:
Germany has hired Jurgen Klopp to coach its national team, giving the ex-Liverpool manager a daunting task to turn around the fortunes of a squad which failed yet again at the World Cup.

Klopp, famed for his success during stints as coach at Borussia Dortmund and especially Liverpool, has been in talks with the German Football Association (DFB) about trying to lead Germany, long a football powerhouse, back to prominence.

Citing fatigue, Klopp stepped down from the Liverpool job in 2024. He was “recharged” in comments while in talks over the Germany job.

The 59-year-old is among the most decorated football coaches of his generation and is expected to sign a contract with Germany that runs until 2030, the date of the next World Cup.

Since 2025, Klopp has been under contract with Austrian energy drink maker Red Bull as “Head of Global Soccer”, where he oversees the football clubs affiliated with the company such as RB Leipzig, Red Bull Salzburg and the New York Red Bulls.

According to German media reports, Red Bull released Klopp from his deal without a transfer fee, instead accepting a million-euro ($1.1m) donation to Red Bull’s Wings for Life charity foundation, which supports research into spinal cord injuries.

The DFB dismissed former head coach Julian Nagelsmann after Germany crashed out of this summer’s World Cup in disappointing fashion, losing to Paraguay on penalties in the round of 32.

That means the German men’s team has suffered three straight World Cup debacles since winning their fourth title in 2014.

Germany made first-round exits in both 2018 and 2022. They have won just one knockout match at the past five major tournaments, reaching the quarterfinal as hosts at Euro 2024.

Klopp had successful coaching tenures at German clubs Mainz, where he was a player for more than a decade, and then Dortmund.

He then became a legend at Liverpool, where he coached the English side to a league title in 2020, ending a 30-year drought. He also led the club to a Champions League triumph, their sixth European crown.

title: France orders evacuation of tourist spot as hundreds flee wildfires by boat
excerpt:
Hundreds have been evacuated from the peninsula by boat while others have been told to leave via road

French authorities have ordered the evacuation of the entirety of the Cap Ferret peninsula on the...
body_text:
Hundreds have been evacuated from the peninsula by boat while others have been told to leave via road

French authorities have ordered the evacuation of the entirety of the Cap Ferret peninsula on the south-west coast, with hundreds of people escaping the area by boat.

Tens of thousands of people are being evacuated from the tourism destination west of Bordeaux, as firefighters continue to battle wildfires that have raged in the Gironde region for days.

The fires, which began on Tuesday in Saumos, continued to burn overnight with 8,700 hectares (around 21,500 acres) consumed by the flames.

Meanwhile, the government in Spain has declared a national emergency as wildfires burn out of control in several areas close to the capital Madrid.

Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez spoke of a "dramatic situation" and urged people to take great care. Three fires were burning west of Madrid, at Villa del Prado and San Martín de Valdeiglesias.

French Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez said 40,000 people had either been or were in the process of being evacuated from the Cap Ferret peninsula, which has the Atlantic on one side and the Bay of Arcachon on the other.

"A total of 80 homes have burnt, around 50 of which have been completely destroyed," he told reporters.

The mayor of Arès, which lies south-east of Lège-Cap Ferret on the Bay of Arcachon, said his town was being evacuated too. A local councillor said 2,000 evacuees from the peninsula had already arrived by boat in the port of Arcachon on the south side of the bay.

Further down the Atlantic coast, around the town of Biscarosse in France's Landes region, another major fire that began on Thursday afternoon has prompted the evacuations of more than 23,000 people.

About 18,000 people in Biscarrosse and 5,000 in Parentis-en-Born had to leave their homes, campsites, a nursing home and a summer camp.

No further casualties have been reported overnight in south-west France, although two firefighters died on Tuesday while tackling a blaze near Bordeaux airport.

French President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday night the wildfire situation in the country was "very tense" - especially in the Gironde region.

Macron said he had asked for help from the European Union, and that support would soon come in the form of aircraft from Croatia, Portugal, the Czech Republic and Slovakia.

Gironde prefect Sophie Brocas ordered the "final phase" of the peninsula evacuation early on Friday morning.

People are being told to leave via the main D106 road towards Bordeaux while boats were being made available for others between 07:00 and 10:00 local time (06:00 BST and 09:00 BST) from four piers.

The road runs along the east side of the peninsula and is the only land route out.

BBC journalist told to evacuate on holiday due to France wildfires

The Cap Ferret peninsula has a year-round population of just under 8,000 but that can swell by 10 times that number in peak season.

Around 20,000 people within the Gironde region had been evacuated on Thursday many of whom were visitors to campsites on the peninsula.

Further evacuations also took place from La Porge on Thursday night which Ms Brocas said had taken place "calmly".

She added that homes in evacuated areas would be monitored to prevent any vandalism.

"Significant resources" remain on the ground tackling the blaze while additional ground and air reinforcements are due in the coming hours.

The public has been told not to go to the affected areas.

In Spain, firefighters are tackling wildfires in several areas not far from Madrid and Interior Minister Fernando Grande-Marlaska declared a national emergency as fires burned at Villa del Prado and San Martín de Valdeiglesias in the Madrid region and at Burgohondo in Ávila.

Pedro Sánchez said forest fires were "ravaging thousands of hectares" and affecting numerous communities.

"The government has deployed all available resources. It will stand by your side during the emergency and in the recovery."

Local emergency services said more than 10,000 people had been evacuated.

An update on Friday morning described both fires as "out of control" although their progression in recent hours had been "limited".

Aldea del Fresno and some other areas in the region have been evacuated.

Are you in an affected region?  If it's safe to do so, get in touch by using this link or the form below.

title: Philippines accuses China of using water cannon on vessels for second day
excerpt: Tensions between China and the Philippines are continuing after Manila’s coastguard accused its Chinese counterpart of firing a water cannon at government vessels near a disputed shoal for the second ...
body_text:
Tensions between China and the Philippines are continuing after Manila’s coastguard accused its Chinese counterpart of firing a water cannon at government vessels near a disputed shoal for the second day.

The Philippine Coast Guard said on Friday that a Chinese vessel at the Scarborough Shoal, in the Philippines’ exclusive economic zone, came within about seven metres (23 feet) of a Philippine fisheries boat, creating a “serious risk of collision”.

“The PCG strongly condemns the dangerous and unprofessional actions of the China Coast Guard,” spokesperson Jay Tarriela said in a statement.

Tarriela added that the shoal was a traditional fishing ground of Filipinos, “whose rights cannot be lawfully impeded by any foreign power”.

The coastguard added that 12 China Coast Guard vessels and three Chinese maritime militia vessels were involved in the incident, and water cannon hit two Philippine ships, while two others had to make dangerous manoeuvres, risking collisions.

The vessels were delivering fuel subsidies to Filipino fishermen and conducting routine patrols at the Scarborough Shoal, also known to locals as Bajo de Masinloc, which lies within the Philippines’ exclusive economic zone.

Earlier on Friday, the China Coast Guard said it had imposed “control measures” on several Philippine vessels it claimed were operating illegally in waters around the shoal.

In a more detailed statement, it accused the Philippines of organising several government and coastguard vessels, along with a large number of fishing vessels, to “illegally gather” in the waters.

It said it had taken legal measures to warn, block and deploy water cannon after the official vessels from the Philippines ignored those measures from the coast guard and “insisted on intruding.”

A similar incident took place on Thursday.

On Friday, China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said the coastguard’s actions were “reasonable, lawful ⁠and beyond reproach.”

“The Philippine side should immediately stop the infringement, provocation and hype,” ministry ⁠spokesperson Lin Jian said at a regular briefing.

The two incidents at Scarborough Shoal, the South China Sea’s most contested area, came after a confrontation on Monday at the disputed Second Thomas Shoal, 650km (400 miles) away from where Manila said a Filipino sailor was injured after being hit with a baton by Chinese coastguard personnel.

Beijing had accused the Philippine Navy of ignoring warnings and of trying to ram a Chinese patrol boat. Both countries have summoned each other’s ambassadors in protest.

But China has long occupied the Scarborough Shoal, deploying an armada of coastguard ships throughout the South China Sea.

Beijing also claims sovereignty over nearly all of the waterway, including parts of the Exclusive Economic Zone of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam.

The tensions also come as Southeast Asian foreign ministers finished regional meetings in Manila that were attended by Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi and United States Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who called Beijing’s actions in the sea “disturbing”, reaffirming support for the Philippines, with which the US has a defence treaty.

title: US and Iran trade more strikes in Gulf as fears of escalation mount
excerpt:
Vessels sighted in the Strait of Hormuz from Oman on 23 July

Iran says it has carried out drone strikes on US military facilities and bases across the Middle East in retaliation for the 13th consecut...
body_text:
Vessels sighted in the Strait of Hormuz from Oman on 23 July

Iran says it has carried out drone strikes on US military facilities and bases across the Middle East in retaliation for the 13th consecutive night of American attacks on the country.

Explosions were reported along Iran's Gulf coast, and in western, northern and central areas. Four people were killed and five injured in an attack in the south-west, Iranian state media said.

Iran later announced strikes on US-allied Kuwait, while sirens sounded in Bahrain and Jordan, and flights were suspended at a major Iraqi airport after a reported drone crash.

It comes as Iran condemned the US plan to use frozen Iranian assets to pay for war damages, in a sign of further escalation between the two countries.

On Thursday, US President Donald Trump said damages to ships and cargo in the Strait of Hormuz would be paid for by "Iranian Money that the United States has in its possession, and controls".

"These damages may be very substantial but, nevertheless, this is the fair and equitable thing to do," he posted on his Truth Social platform.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi responded by saying that seizing another country's assets to "pay for unrelated future claims is an incendiary precedent".

"Ensuing chaos will not be pretty or peaceful," he added.

In June, the US and Iran agreed on a 14-point Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days.

But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in the strait, and US strikes in response.

Point 11 of the MoU detailed that the "United States of America undertakes to make fully available for use the frozen or restricted funds and assets of the Islamic Republic of Iran".

As the US pushes to regain control over the Strait of Hormuz, there's growing concern over new threats to shipping in the Red Sea from Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis.

That has helped push the price of crude oil back over $100 (£74.96) a barrel - the highest level since May.

Trump separately on Thursday vowed "major military punishment" against Iran for Houthi attacks.

He later told the Axios news website he was considering restarting major combat operations in Iran and launching strikes "bigger than ever before".

Tensions have been escalating between the US and Iran since the start of July.

On Thursday night, the US military says it carried out strikes to degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian shipping in regional waters.

US Central Command (Centcom) said it had targeted "Iranian military command centers, drone storage facilities, communication networks, coastal surveillance sites and maritime capabilities".

Iran's army claimed it had launched attacks on US bases in Kuwait, while Bahrain's Interior Ministry reported that its air sirens had been sounded and Iranian state media also said alarms had been activated in Jordan.

The AFP and Reuters news agencies both reported a drone crash at Iraq's Erbil International Airport, causing a temporary stoppage. Flights have since resumed.

US-Iran memorandum of understanding in full

title: Trump’s latest ‘forced labour tariffs’: Who’s been hit, how badly?
excerpt:
US President Donald Trump has imposed new double-digit tariffs on dozens of trading partners of the United States.

This announcement came just as the clock ran out on Friday on stopgap levies he anno...
body_text:
US President Donald Trump has imposed new double-digit tariffs on dozens of trading partners of the United States.

This announcement came just as the clock ran out on Friday on stopgap levies he announced after a stinging defeat at the Supreme Court.

The US has slapped levies of 10 to 12.5 percent on imports from 60 countries accounting for 99 percent of US imports, accusing them of inadequately enforcing bans on goods produced by forced labour.

“The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it’s well past time for our trading partners to do the same,” US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a statement on Thursday.

“Today’s action will begin to correct what is both a human rights abuse and distortive trade practice to improve the welfare of workers everywhere.”

The new tariffs took effect just as the temporary 10 percent worldwide tariffs expired at 12:01am on Friday in Washington, DC (04:01 GMT). Trump had turned to the temporary levies after the Supreme Court struck down his biggest and boldest tariffs in February.

In the Federal Register notice, where the tariffs were announced, there were details of the countries hit by the tariffs.

These countries and economies are: Algeria; Angola; Argentina; Australia; the Bahamas; Bahrain; Bangladesh; Brazil; Cambodia; Chile; China, People’s Republic of; Colombia; Costa Rica; Dominican Republic; Egypt; El Salvador; Guatemala; Guyana; Honduras; Hong Kong, China; India; Iraq; Israel; Japan; Jordan; Kazakhstan; Kuwait; Libya; Malaysia; Morocco; New Zealand; Nicaragua; Nigeria; Norway; Oman; Peru; the Philippines; Qatar; Russia; Saudi Arabia; Singapore; South Africa; South Korea; Sri Lanka; Switzerland; Taiwan; Thailand; Trinidad and Tobago; Turkiye; United Arab Emirates; United Kingdom; Uruguay; Venezuela; Vietnam; Canada; Ecuador; the European Union; Indonesia; Mexico; and Pakistan.

Trump is tapping more durable tariffs under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, which permits the president to impose import taxes and other sanctions against countries found to engage in “unjustifiable”, “unreasonable”, or “discriminatory” trade practices.

Trump used Section 301 to impose major tariffs on China in his first term, and they survived court challenges.

Trump, who argues that high tariffs will revive US manufacturing, last year overturned decades of US policy that favoured lower tariffs and freer trade.

Invoking the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), he imposed double-digit tariffs on imports from almost every country, saying that the US’s longstanding trade deficit amounted to a national emergency.

But the Supreme Court ruled that IEEPA did not authorise tariffs. The decision forced the Trump administration to pay refunds to importers that had paid the levies.

In response, Trump announced 10 percent worldwide tariffs under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. But he can only use Section 122 levies for 150 days, and the time ran out at the start of Friday.

John Diamond, director of the Center for Tax and Budget Policy at the Baker Institute, said the administration was looking for a new authority to replace the expiring tariffs and decided to use Section 301.

“It’s a little bit ridiculous to think that over 60 major trading partners, including countries in the EU, are really relying on that much forced labour. It’s kind of hard to believe,” Diamond told Al Jazeera. “But I don’t think the courts will want to overrule these tariffs as they did the IEEPA tariffs.”

Countries that have laws against the import of products made using forced labour, including India, Pakistan and Argentina, have been hit with a 10 percent tariff.

Nations without such laws, including China and the United Kingdom, have been slapped with a 12.5 percent tariff.

Additional tariffs on the basis of Section 301 are likely coming: Greer’s office has launched a probe into whether 16 countries, accounting for 70 percent of US imports, have overproduced goods, pushing down prices and putting US companies at a disadvantage in global markets.

The administration has yet to complete that investigation.

title: Progressives want to tax billionaires first, and you're next on the list
excerpt:
Fox News anchor Jon Scott reports on a major economic shift as Americans leave coastal cities on 'Fox Report.'

If you ever wished you could be counted among billionaires, your wish might come true so...
body_text:
Fox News anchor Jon Scott reports on a major economic shift as Americans leave coastal cities on 'Fox Report.'

If you ever wished you could be counted among billionaires, your wish might come true soon if the socialists get their way. Of course, it’s not that you’ll have a billion-dollar net worth, but rather you’ll be subject to the same wealth tax that progressives say they want to foist onto billionaires.

What Russian revolutionary Leon Trotsky said of war applies to the wealth tax: you may not care about it, but it cares about you. Recently, California Democrat Rep. Ro Khanna published his own personal manifesto, "Why I Support a Billionaire Wealth Tax." But he quickly pulled the mask back even further, revealing the true targets of his tax scheme.

Khanna started arguing to tax billionaires, then halfway through the manifesto decided it should be centimillionaires — $100-million net worth. By the end, he was going after for $50-million-aires. At that rate, give it a year or two, and the wealth tax will be $50 thousand, not $50 million.

NEWSOM BEGS CALIFORNIANS TO VOTE 'NO' ON BILLIONAIRE'S TAX IN FACE OF MASS EXODUS, PITCHES NATIONWIDE TAX HIKE

This wouldn’t be the first time progressives pulled this kind of bait and switch — it’s what they did with the income tax. Billionaires are the cheese in the mousetrap, a marketing prop to pass the tax before going after the real target. Once the tax is in place, the communists can get to work knocking zeros off the threshold until it hits pretty much anybody with a job or savings or a home.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani is one of many politicians pushing a wealth tax. (Anna Connors/Pool via REUTERS)

This just happened in New York. Comrade Zohran Mamdani, the Democratic Socialist mayor, campaigned on enacting new taxes for billionaires, but he's already pushing tax hikes on millionaires.

Again, this is nothing new. It’s the same play progressives ran with the income tax. It was just a tax on the rich, they promised at the time — for annual incomes equivalent to $15 million today. Back in 1908, Democrat nominee William Jennings Bryan called for taxing "swollen fortunes" of men of large incomes, so that, as the platform explained, "wealth may bear its proportionate share of the burdens of government."

NEW YORK CITY MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI STRUGGLES TO DEFINE 'WORKING CLASS'

Of course, the rich were the bait to get the middle class into the trap. Today, the income tax hits two-thirds of Americans, starting at $15,000, not $15 million. So much for swollen fortunes.

Meanwhile, the tax that started at a rate of 1% to 7% on millionaires now hits the middle class at a 35% marginal rate. Throw in additional taxes on income for Social Security, Medicare and state income tax, and the marginal rate is 50% in places like California.

That’s just the dollars that people lose. It doesn’t include the billions of hours Americans spend in an annual month-long humiliation ritual, financial colonoscopy, under threat of audit and imprisonment.

CALIFORNIA ‘BASIC INCOME’ EXPERIMENT FAILS TO PROVIDE ‘FINANCIAL INDEPENDENCE,’ STUDY FINDS

So, it's nice of progressives to spring the trap early by already removing zeros from the original target of their wealth tax. But even if the tax were limited to billionaires, wealth taxes are catastrophically destructive.

Progressives can’t grasp this because their understanding of billionaires seems to be informed by a cartoon duck who swims around in a vault of gold coins. In reality, billionaires keep nearly all their wealth invested in businesses — Walmart, to Chick-fil-A, Tesla, etc.

So, every dollar taken in a wealth tax isn't scooped out of the gold pool but is liquidated along with the jobs and innovation built by that investment. The companies fade, replaced with generous funding for Somali "Learing" Centers, left-wing NGOs to riot for social justice and starting new and interesting wars.

This wouldn’t be the first time progressives pulled this kind of bait and switch — it’s what they did with the income tax. Billionaires are the cheese in the mousetrap, a marketing prop to pass the tax before going after the real target.

And the businesses fade fast — at Khanna's 5% annual wealth tax, you essentially cancel the investment returns. As in, billionaires should just spend the money on yachts and supermodels instead of parking it in jobs and innovation.

Even if progressives grasped this reality, they’d probably keep pushing a billionaire tax because it's one of their few schticks that polls well. Of course, it’s just the first act of a tragedy: Khanna's already pushing for the proceeds to seed a one-year universal basic income that would pay millions to sit on the couch, including illegal aliens.

It’s essentially a venture investment for trillions of dollars in vote-buying bread and circuses forever. The great economist Frédéric Bastiat once said government is the fiction through which everybody endeavors to live at the expense of everybody else. Taxing billionaires — and now millionaires — is the cheese in the mousetrap.

E.J. Antoni, Ph.D., is Chief Economist and the Richard F. Aster fellow at the Heritage Foundation and a senior fellow at Unleash Prosperity.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM PETER ST. ONGE

Peter St. Onge is the Heritage Foundation’s Mark A. Kolokotrones Fellow in Economic Freedom.

title: SEN MIKE LEE: Trump proves America can stand up to a power-mad foreign court
excerpt:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani says protests will be respected and calls on the U.S. to join the ICC and pursue accountability for accused 'war criminals' like Benjamin Netanyahu.

At the very cor...
body_text:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani says protests will be respected and calls on the U.S. to join the ICC and pursue accountability for accused 'war criminals' like Benjamin Netanyahu.

At the very core of the American project has always been a fierce defiance of far-off powers, a stubborn opposition to those who would seek to undermine our right to self-rule or impose their will on a people determined to decide their own destiny.

This spirit not only brought America into being but is essential for our nation to preserve its sovereignty into the future.

America again defied those who would undermine our right to self-rule on July 13 when Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced that the Trump administration would "dismantle" the International Criminal Court and teach them "the full meaning of American resolve."

CNN LEGAL ANALYST CALLS OUT 'UTTERLY RIDICULOUS' CLAIM BY MAMDANI ABOUT NETANYAHU ARREST

This will include diplomatic pressure on nations that partner with American law enforcement or our military and enjoy the benefits of our protection, coordination with allies to join us in rejecting International Criminal Court (ICC) authority, sanctions against the ICC and its affiliates, and visa bans for ICC personnel. I have pushed for similar policies legislatively, including the " ICC Out of NYC Act ," legislation that would forbid United Nations properties in New York City from being used, leased or occupied by representatives or employees of the ICC.

President Donald Trump is the first president to really stand up to the International Criminal Court. (AP Photo/Peter Dejong, File)

The ICC, however, remains convinced of its own power to pierce the veil of American sovereignty and wage lawfare against American citizens, soldiers and public servants alike. A global court that attempts to wield ultimate authority – above and beyond the authority of any nation state – poses a direct and dire threat to the continued sovereignty of the United States that can no longer be tolerated.

As Americans, we wholeheartedly reject the notion that a supranational entity, especially one formed by a statute that was never ratified by our country, has legitimate jurisdiction over our citizens and our leaders. The U.S. Constitution is our law and guide and none other.

To forfeit the legal authority of the United States government to an unaccountable transnational organization would amount to a betrayal of the American people – and an abdication of our right to self-rule.

An active and emboldened ICC means that American sovereignty is contingent on the whims of a globalist entity run by those who have never stood for election in front of the American people, never represented America’s interests, and never sworn loyalty to America’s Constitution. No self-respecting nation, certainly not one founded in rebellion against the tyranny of a far-off power, can allow unelected globalists to wield unchecked power against its citizens, soldiers and leaders.

The United States has worked to impose limits on the ICC for decades, with presidents from both parties warning against the threat posed by the court to American sovereignty. No president, from Bill Clinton to George W. Bush to Barack Obama, has been willing to ratify the Rome Statute, which would surrender American sovereignty into the hands of globalist bureaucrats empowered to launder their political grievances through the language of unenforceable international law.

WORLD COURT PROSECUTOR WHO WENT AFTER NETANYAHU FOR WAR CRIMES SUSPENDED OVER SEXUAL MISCONDUCT

But despite past presidents’ reluctance to fully legitimize the court, none were willing to take decisive action to prevent the severe breaches of sovereignty that the ICC remains capable of committing today. Tepid half-measures are not sufficient in the fight to defend America’s sovereignty.

As Americans, we wholeheartedly reject the notion that a supranational entity, especially one formed by a statute that was never ratified by our country, has legitimate jurisdiction over our citizens and our leaders. The U.S. Constitution is our law and guide and none other.

Only President Donald Trump is proving bold enough to take decisive action against the capabilities of the ICC, necessary to ensure that American citizens and servicemembers are never subjected to tribunals, and so America’s elected leaders are never threatened with arrest for acting in accordance with our country’s national interest.

No nation is truly independent when it allows an unaccountable court to sit above it and claim jurisdiction over its people and elected officials. Meeker countries may be content handing veto power over their foreign and domestic policy to an unaccountable supranational body, but such a blatant departure from national self-rule is entirely alien to the American tradition, mind and spirit.

It’s why the Trump administration is right to take on the ICC and ensure it never has the capability to threaten the United States and her people.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM SEN. MIKE LEE

Republican Mike Lee represents Utah in the United States Senate. He is a member of the Judiciary Committee and serves as chairman of the Antitrust, Competition Policy and Consumer Rights Subcommittee.

title: Canada’s wildfire smoke is choking America — Congress must clear the air
excerpt:
Dr. Marc Siegel, Fox News senior medical analyst, discusses health risks associated with Canadian wildfire smoke and the Cyclospora parasite found on lettuce.

This week, millions of Americans woke up...
body_text:
Dr. Marc Siegel, Fox News senior medical analyst, discusses health risks associated with Canadian wildfire smoke and the Cyclospora parasite found on lettuce.

This week, millions of Americans woke up to hazy skies and scratchy throats under air quality alerts, not because of anything happening in their own local communities or states, but because smoke from the hundreds of wildfires burning in Canada drifted across the border. Across the Atlantic seaboard to the Midwest, Americans were told to stay indoors. Seniors and people with respiratory conditions faced increased health risks. Kids enjoying their summer break watched their outdoor plans get cancelled. Families on vacation were stuck inside.

America’s own regulatory system allows forests to grow dangerously overgrown, letting dead trees and dense underbrush pile up for years while environmental reviews drag on before a single chainsaw ever starts. Meanwhile, drought intensifies conditions and one lightning strike, or a careless spark, can turn into an inferno. This is the opposite of environmental stewardship and neglect dressed up as caution.

Americans deserve policies that help prevent catastrophic fires before they start, protect our communities when fires do occur, and ensure our environmental laws actually improve the environment instead of burying commonsense solutions in years of bureaucratic delay.

TRUMP BLAMES CANADA FOR WILDFIRE SMOKE, SAYS POLLUTION COSTS SHOULD BE ADDED TO TARIFFS

Under the Clean Air Act (CAA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has established regulations for six critical air pollutants, the majority of which are found in wildfire smoke.

We passed two pieces of legislation on a bipartisan basis to support states in their efforts to mitigate, respond to, and recover from wildfires.

The Fire Improvement and Reforming Exceptional Events (FIRE) Act  rests on a simple truth: preventing catastrophic wildfires is itself an environmental win. Prevention beats response every time, and waiting for disaster to strike is almost always the most expensive, and most dangerous option on the table. The FIRE Act allows states to perform prescribed burns, the most effective tool to mitigate wildfires and their emissions, without being penalized for those mitigation efforts. It also directs the EPA to conduct regional analyses when multiple states are affected by the same wildfire or event, which will reduce regulatory burden and streamline federal action. This bill rewards proactive forest management while protecting public health, and is a commonsense solution to a problem that shouldn’t have existed in the first place.

AIR QUALITY CONCERNS FROM CANADIAN WILDFIRES FORCE RESCHEDULING OF TWO PROFESSIONAL SPORTS GAMES

The Foreign Emissions and Nonattainment Clarification for Economic Stability (FENCES) Act  clarifies that states should not be held accountable for  foreign emissions beyond their control and excludes foreign emissions when the EPA determines whether a state complies with air quality standards to ensure states aren’t penalized for emissions originating outside of the United States.  The FENCES Act protects states, energy producers, and manufacturers from costly and unnecessary compliance requirements. When Canadian wildfire smoke degrades air quality in American communities, it’s an injustice to hold those states accountable for outside pollution and does nothing to solve the underlying cause of the air emission.

The Senate already has what it needs. The FIRE Act clears the way for states to manage their own forests, the best tool we have to address wildfires, without years of bureaucratic sign-off and fear of facing extreme economic sanctions. The FENCES Act makes sure no state foots a federal penalty for emissions it never created. Passing both isn't optional anymore.

Canadian researchers themselves have noted  that wildfire smoke is becoming a recurring feature of Canadian summers. It is simply unacceptable for American communities and businesses to continue being punished and burdened by wildfires raging outside of our own borders.

POOR AIR QUALITY DUE TO WILDFIRES FORCES LOGISITCAL CHANGES AT SEVERAL MLB GAMES, INCLUDING POSTPONEMENT

Our states know the cost of catastrophic wildfires firsthand. We've watched communities lose homes, businesses, treasured public lands, and, tragically, lives. We've seen tourism suffer, infrastructure damaged, watersheds contaminated, and local economies disrupted. Even after the flames are extinguished, the impacts linger for years through erosion, flooding, degraded wildlife habitat, and diminished water quality. To hold states accountable for pollution originating from another country is just salt in the wound.

The smoke currently blanketing much of the United States offers a powerful reminder that poor forest management doesn't stay contained within political boundaries. Air pollution doesn't ask whether you're a Democrat or Republican before affecting your family. It doesn't stop at state lines or international borders. It impacts millions of Americans regardless of where the fire started.

The best way to improve air quality across the country is to prevent these catastrophic events from happening, and legislation like the FIRE Act and FENCES Act ensure that a future without these disasters and their lasting negative impact to our environment and communities is possible. That's why these bills matter, and why we’re urging our colleagues in the Senate to pass this legislation quickly. Every day we wait is another day that preventable risks are allowed to grow. We can't control every wildfire, but we can make it easier to reduce their frequency, severity, protect our communities, and restore healthier forests.

We didn't start the fire. We're just done punishing American communities for our neighbors to the north's mess.

Gabe Evans represents Colorado's eight congressional district.

Republican August Pfluger represents Texas's 11th Congressional District in the United States House of Representatives. He sits on the House Committee on Energy & Commerce and is the Chairman of the Homeland Security Subcommittee on Intelligence and Counterterrorism.

title: China’s routing of Biden in chip war with US created a communist tech giant
