Just as significantly, Aiyar believes the movement has already outgrown the organisation that sparked it and Wangchuk's hunger strike.

"The catalyst may have been the CJP," she says, "but it became bigger than them."

Students came not only because of the state's handling of the protests, she argues, but because they wanted "a space and a site to express the deep frustration" they had been carrying for some time. "The memes, posters and slogans suggested grievances extending well beyond exam paper leaks."

After initially leaving the response to ministers and party spokespersons, the  ruling BJP party's top leadership stepped in on Wednesday. Defence Minister Rajnath Singh accused the opposition of using students "as a political tool" while insisting the government remained committed to addressing their concerns through parliament.

Modi addressed the issue publicly for the first time on Thursday.

"Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth," he wrote on X, announcing fast-track courts to ensure "swift and stringent punishment" for those involved in paper leaks. "Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared."

Whether those assurances will be enough to defuse the protests remains uncertain.

Aiyar believes the movement has tapped into broader anxieties over a slowing economy, scarce jobs and a deepening sense that the future offers diminishing opportunities.

Many believe the ongoing protests have tapped into wider public frustrations

"There is deep economic dissatisfaction and distress, which has translated into a lack of hope about the future," Aiyar says. "This could reflect growing exhaustion with a government that has been in power for a long time. Its failure to deliver on the promises it built its reputation on may have reached a tipping point."

For her, the protests are about far more than a single policy failure.

"The message from protesters is clear: we're exhausted. We need a new kind of politics, a new political grammar - one rooted in ideas and focused on governance and delivery."

Whether that frustration can be sustained as a political movement, however, is another question.

Political scientist Suhas Palshikar argues the Cockroach movement may simply provide an outlet for public anger before gradually losing steam, much like the farmers' protests.

"Where will the Cockroach movement go? Perhaps nowhere - it will allow venting of frustration and taper off," he wrote on X.

He also cautions against romanticising it as a Gen Z uprising.

"It would be a mistake to imagine a youth movement or a Gen Z movement - these are only convenient myths for commentators." The movement's "ideological and political character... is currently fluid", he argues, with different political forces likely to seek influence over it.

What the protesters have undeniably shown, he says, is a willingness to confront the state.

"The young have shown their angst and their disgust. They have borne the brunt of the democratic government's brutal police. They have also shown that only street action makes this regime... listen and talk."

Whether that energy can endure, Palshikar argues, will depend on many factors, including state repression, media blackouts, leadership challenges and the difficulty of building a broad social coalition.

Economist Surajit Mazumdar of Delhi's Jawaharlal Nehru University also believes the movement already points to something much larger than examination paper leaks.

Students participating in India's anti-corruption movement in 2011

"The current protest and the spontaneous response it has evoked is indicative of the widespread discontent of young people and students about the state of affairs in the country and in the education system which goes beyond the specific paper leak issue," he told the BBC.

Compared with earlier student mobilisations, he argues, the current movement appears "broader and larger in scale".

Mazumdar also rejects the idea that the protests are post-ideological. Like the farmers' movement before them, he says they show that "the democratic spirit is still alive in Indian society".

For Aiyar, that democratic impulse is already reshaping politics.

"People are seeking accountability for governance in sites that are non-electoral," Aiyar says. "The split between electoral legitimacy and democratic accountability outside electoral space is the new phenomenon of politics in India."

Whether that proves to be the defining legacy of the Cockroach Janta Party's protests - or merely a passing moment of public anger - will become clear only with time.

title: USA Today columnist who attacked Caitlin Clark goes after Heritage Foundation 'coming for' Title IX
excerpt:
Fox News contributor Jonathan Turley joins Fox & Friends to weigh in on the Supreme Court's oral arguments regarding transgender athletes in women's sports.

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour accused t...
body_text:
Fox News contributor Jonathan Turley joins Fox & Friends to weigh in on the Supreme Court's oral arguments regarding transgender athletes in women's sports.

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour accused the Heritage Foundation of "coming for" Title IX on Thursday, shortly after facing backlash for her controversial Caitlin Clark column.

Armour cited the conservative think tank report titled "Title IX’s Failed Experiment: Why Accommodating Sex Differences Beats Engineered Parity," released earlier this month, which criticized the modern application of the 1972 federal civil rights law barring sex-based discrimination in education activities or programs that receive federal funding.

The report's author, Scott Yenor, senior research fellow at the Heritage Foundation's American Citizenship Initiative, wrote that stricter interpretations of the law have enforced rigid equality in school sports to the detriment of both male and female student athletes.

MEGAN RAPINOE SAYS EFFORTS TO BAR TRANS ATHLETES FROM WOMEN'S SPORTS ARE 'ATTACK ON WOMEN IN SOCIETY'

The Heritage Foundation put out a report criticizing the narrow enforcement of Title IX regarding female sports. (Wavebreakmedia / Getty Images)

"Title IX evolved from a seemingly modest anti-discrimination statute into a powerful engine of feminist social engineering, complete with proportionality mandates," the Heritage Foundation report read. "The result has been two systemic distortions: a prejudice in favor of a male-normed competitive model for women’s sports and a prejudice against men’s non-revenue programs."

Armour claimed that this signaled the organization is seeking to remove protections for female sports and roll back women's rights.

"Our bodies, our self-confidence, our independence — they’re not going to stop until women have the same rights and opportunities they did before the 1970s. Which were almost none," Armour wrote.

ACTIVISTS UNLEASH A 'NEXT WAVE' BALLOT STRATEGY FOR WOMEN'S SPORTS INSIDE DEEP BLUE STATES

The USA Today columnist dismissed the idea that the Heritage Foundation’s efforts "will go nowhere" despite the law's widespread support.

"Project 2025 has done too much damage already not to take the threat to Title IX seriously. The Heritage Foundation wants a United States that reflects their White, Judeo-Christian and heteronormative 'values,' and is steadfastly working to eliminate anything that deviates from them," Armour wrote.

She added, "It’s also about enforcing a specific definition of femininity. It started with banning transgender women from sports. Now that that’s been accomplished, the Heritage Foundation and their ilk are going to try and make the rest of us women conform."

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour previously faced backlash accusing Caitlin Clark of putting minority WNBA players in danger. (Andy Lyons/Getty Images)

Fox News Digital reached out to the Heritage Foundation for comment.

Though Armour suggested that the Heritage Foundation is seeking to eliminate Title IX, Yenor instead argued in favor of reforms to the law that focused less on proportionality in male-dominated sports and more on opportunities for female-centric athletics such as cheerleading, gymnastics and volleyball.

NBC ANCHOR FORCED TO EXPLAIN USE OF TERM 'BIOLOGICAL MALE' DURING COVERAGE OF SUPREME COURT RULING

"Equality under Title IX does not require identical outcomes or the erasure of sex differences. It requires opportunity rooted in human nature that will reveal genuine differences between the sexes. Recovering an approach that accommodates the partly natural, partly cultural differences between the sexes can sustain vibrant women’s sports, protect and perhaps expand male opportunities, and contribute to a healthier, more humane social order," Yenor wrote.

Armour's latest column came after she received backlash for accusing Indiana Fever star Caitlin Clark of putting Black and queer WNBA players "in danger" by flopping and berating court officials.

Armour later apologized for her "inartful comparison" of Caitlin Clark to Emmett Till's murder. (AP Photo/Michael Conroy)

"It shouldn’t need reminding this country has an awful history of Black people being harmed, even killed, in the name of 'defending' white women. The WNBA’s All-Star Game this weekend is in Chicago, Emmett Till’s hometown, and a local art gallery is currently hosting an exhibit imagining his life if he hadn’t been lynched. While it’s incumbent on the WNBA to do more to protect its players, there also should be an expectation on Clark to recognize the wider consequences of her actions," Armour wrote.

Armour later apologized for her "inartful comparison" of Clark's actions to the murder of Emmett Till and has updated the column.

Lindsay Kornick is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to lindsay.kornick@fox.com and on Twitter: @lmkornick.

title: Accused killer's alleged disguises unravel as sheriff releases new clues in month-long manhunt
excerpt: Criminal defense attorney Donna Rotunno analyzes the manhunt for a woman accused of murdering a 43-year-old man in Florida. Rotunno details how the suspect has allegedly used aliases, altered appearan...
body_text:
Criminal defense attorney Donna Rotunno analyzes the manhunt for a woman accused of murdering a 43-year-old man in Florida. Rotunno details how the suspect has allegedly used aliases, altered appearances and manipulation of accomplices to evade the Walton County Sheriff's Department.

A Florida sheriff's office has revealed new identifying marks on a fugitive murder suspect accused in the death of a man found buried in a shallow grave, warning that she may have changed her hair and appearance as a monthlong manhunt continues.

Isabelle Johnson, 38, is wanted in connection with the murder of Jason Coulthart, 43, whose remains were discovered June 25 on a property in Freeport, according to the Walton County Sheriff's Office.

"One month of searching for Isabelle Johnson, and our search has not slowed," the agency wrote in a new appeal for tips.

Investigators now believe Johnson has several distinctive tattoos that could help members of the public recognize her despite any attempts to disguise herself.

FLORIDA WOMAN CHARGED WITH MURDER EVADING POLICE AFTER BODY FOUND IN SHALLOW GRAVE: SHERIFF

Isabelle Johnson, 38, is wanted for the slaying of 43-year-old Jason Christopher Coulthart, the Walton County Sheriff's Office announced. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

She is believed to have a rose or flower tattoo on her left hand, beginning below the thumb and extending toward the index finger, according to the sheriff's office. She also appears to have an unidentified tattoo on her right ankle and a large scorpion tattoo extending from her left buttock down her left thigh.

Authorities believe Johnson may have dyed and cut her hair in an effort to alter her appearance.

FLORIDA WOMAN ON THE RUN AND WANTED FOR MURDER RELIED ON NETWORK OF ALLEGED ACCOMPLICES TO EVADE CAPTURE: COPS

Isabelle Johnson, 38, was captured on security video days after the remains of Jason Christopher Coulthart, 43, were discovered last month. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

The sheriff's office once again invoked the aliases Johnson is believed to use.

"Today, we're releasing additional identifying features belonging to Isabelle, or Jessica, or Harley, or whatever name she's going by today," the agency wrote.

Johnson has previously been linked to several aliases, including Harley, Jessica Elaine Bowman, Jessica Elaine Thrush, Jessica Elaine Thrash, Jessica Dowdy and possibly "Denise."

'AMERICA'S MOST WANTED' FUGITIVE CAPTURED ON SAILBOAT OFF NJ COAST AFTER 20 YEARS ON THE RUN

The Walton County Sheriff’s Office says fugitive Isabelle Johnson has a hand tattoo, an ankle tattoo and a large scorpion tattoo on her left thigh. (Walton County Sheriffs Office)

Authorities say she has ties to Walton and Okaloosa counties in Florida, parts of Georgia and lower Alabama.

Investigators have also warned that she may manipulate people into providing transportation, money or shelter by using stories involving domestic violence or other hardships.

The Walton County Sheriff's Office has described Johnson as "cunning" and a "master manipulator," while urging anyone who may be helping her to stop.

"Every lead is pursued. Every tip is investigated. Every day brings us one step closer," the agency wrote. "Someone knows where she is."

Coulthart was reported missing May 24 after he was last seen leaving the College Condominiums on East College Boulevard in Niceville.

During the missing-person investigation, detectives developed information that led authorities to a property off Sunset Lane in Freeport. Investigators obtained a search warrant and found Coulthart's body buried in a shallow grave that evening, according to the sheriff's office.

Isabelle Rose Johnson, 38, wanted for murder has reportedly been using different disguises to avoid capture by law enforcement. Authorities shared different snapshots of her to ask for the public's help in tracking her down. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

The District 1 Medical Examiner's Office later identified the remains through DNA testing.

Five people have since been arrested in connection with the case. Michael Ray White, Kathleen Morris, Bobbi Wagstaff, Martin Leaverton and Sandra Leaverton have been charged with accessory to a capital felony after the fact.

Five people have been arrested in connection to the murder of Jason Coulthart. Isabelle Johnson, 38, is still wanted. (Walton County Sheriff's Office)

Authorities previously alleged that White knowingly helped Johnson evade law enforcement, while Morris admitted providing Johnson with transportation and supplies and misleading investigators about whether she had been at her home, according to local reporting.

Walton County Sheriff Michael Adkinson has warned that anyone still helping Johnson hide could also face charges.

"If you give her a place to stay, help her hide, or lie for her, you will face consequences," Adkinson said previously. "This is a homicide case. We will not allow anyone to get in the way of this investigation."

A combined reward of up to $16,000 remains available for information leading to Johnson's arrest.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Walton County Sheriff's Office seeking any additional updates on the investigation.

The sheriff's office ended its latest appeal with a warning aimed at Johnson.

"She can keep running. We won't stop looking," the agency wrote. "We know who will get tired first."

Fox News Digital's Louis Casiano contributed to this report.

Stepheny Price is a Writer at Fox News with a focus on West Coast and Midwest news, missing persons, national and international crime stories, homicide cases, and border security.

title: AP post on USAID cuts backfires as critics question taxpayer-funded Nepal transgender jobs
excerpt:
Gov. Kathy Hochul, D-N.Y., extends a $2.5M healthcare program for sex workers. Panelists Joe Borelli and Jayme Franklin discuss the impact of potentially decriminalizing sex work.

An Associated Press...
body_text:
Gov. Kathy Hochul, D-N.Y., extends a $2.5M healthcare program for sex workers. Panelists Joe Borelli and Jayme Franklin discuss the impact of potentially decriminalizing sex work.

An Associated Press social media post about transgender aid workers in Nepal turning to sex work after losing their jobs due to U.S. foreign aid cuts sparked a wave of conservative backlash, with critics arguing the video made the case for defunding USAID rather than saving it.

The AP post said that in Nepal, where "a conservative culture leaves openly transgender people with few legal job options," around 100 LGBTQ aid workers left jobless by U.S. funding cuts had turned to sex work to survive. An accompanying video focused on Rubi Lama, a former HIV outreach worker who said USAID-funded programs had provided free condoms, lubricant and HIV-prevention medication before the cuts.

"When USAID funding was there, condoms were free, lubricant was also distributed … for free," Lama said in the video, adding that HIV treatments, including PrEP, were also available without cost. "But, now it is gone."

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION TOUTS MORE THAN $1B IN UN BUDGET CUTS AS REFORM PUSH GAINS MOMENTUM

The framing quickly drew criticism from conservatives who said the story raised the opposite question: why American taxpayers were funding those programs in the first place.

The flag of the United States Agency for International Development, or USAID, flies in front of the USAID office in Washington, Monday, Feb. 3, 2025. (AP Photo/Manuel Balce Ceneta)

Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, was among the conservatives who argued the AP report made the opposite point from the one intended.

"Our tax dollars no longer fund employment for transgender Nepalese sex workers," Gill wrote on X. "Thank you for reminding us why defunding USAID was long overdue."

"Wow very grateful that my tax dollars are no longer funding a bunch of random trannies in Asia," mocked Daily Wire host Matt Walsh "Is that the point you were trying to make or did you expect us to be somehow sad about this?"

SOCIALIST-BACKED MICHIGAN SENATE HOPEFUL CALLS AMERICAN POLITICS A 'DISEASE' AFTER PROFESSING LOVE FOR US

Artist perform during the first drag queen show in Kathmandu, Nepal, on June 11, 2019. - First time in Nepal's LGBT community showing drag queen night with colorful costumes and makeup. (PRAKASH MATHEMA/AFP)

The backlash comes after President Donald Trump, immediately upon returning to office, ordered a 90-day pause on new foreign development assistance obligations and disbursements while his administration reviewed whether programs aligned with his foreign policy.

USAID later told partners to pause new funding obligations and issue stop-work orders or suspensions for existing awards, while critics have said the review led to major disruptions, staff reductions and the cancellation of critical foreign-assistance awards.

But Federalist editor-in-chief Mollie Hemingway argued the AP post exposed misplaced priorities, particularly among the media, saying the outlet presented U.S. tax dollars flowing to a "trans Nepalese job protection program" as though it was a good use of public money.

Holes are seen on the building after signage was removed (top) while black plastic covers the logo of the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) at its headquarters in Washington, DC, on February 7, 2025. ( MANDEL NGAN/AFP via Getty Images)

"The AP announces that your hard-earned tax dollars were flowing to a trans Nepalese job protection program as if that was an even remotely good use of your money," Hemingway wrote in a post on X. "AP is sick Democrat propaganda."

"How dare Elon cut USAID funds to the Nepalese trans sex worker community," quipped Republican activist Jack Posobiec.

Alec Schemmel is a Politics Reporter for Fox News Digital who is originally from Charlotte, North Carolina but now resides in the Washington D.C.-Baltimore Metro Area.

title: Oprah Winfrey sparks fresh backlash after she revisits Prince Harry and Meghan Markle bombshell interview
excerpt:
Hugo Vickers, author of "Queen Elizabeth II: A Personal History," details how the Duke and Duchess of Sussex's exit and Oprah interview added strain during the late monarch's final years.

Oprah Winfr...
body_text:
Hugo Vickers, author of "Queen Elizabeth II: A Personal History," details how the Duke and Duchess of Sussex's exit and Oprah interview added strain during the late monarch's final years.

Oprah Winfrey is facing a new wave of backlash over an old royal bombshell.

After revisiting her 2021 interview with Prince Harry and Meghan Markle, Winfrey's explanation of her "silent or silenced" question has sparked renewed debate online.

The media mogul's reflection on one of the most talked-about moments from the explosive sit-down has reignited debate over the interview that shook the monarchy, divided viewers and put Harry and Meghan's claims about royal life under a global microscope.

MEGHAN MARKLE, PRINCE HARRY'S INTERVIEW WITH OPRAH WINFREY: 6 BIGGEST BOMBSHELLS

Oprah Winfrey interviewed Prince Harry and Meghan Markle for a special in 2021 where they revealed their struggles with royal life. (Harpo Productions/Joe Pugliese via Getty Images)

During a recent Vanity Fair interview, Winfrey looked back on the viral exchange with Meghan, explaining why she challenged the Duchess of Sussex after she said she had been "silent" during her time as a working royal.

"I wanted to be clear because was she silenced? Were people telling her not to talk?... I said it for clarification because I didn't want later to hear, 'Well, no, it wasn't that. It really was that I was silenced. They wouldn't let me talk.' So it was all for clarification."

Meghan told Winfrey at the time, "I've always valued independence" and "I've always been outspoken, especially about women's rights."

"I mean, that's the sad irony of the last four years is I've advocated for so long for women to use their voice. And then, I was silent."

Winfrey responded with the question that instantly went viral.

Winfrey also revealed that Harry and Meghan did not know what questions she planned to ask before the interview.

"They didn't know obviously anything that I was going to ask them, but I had said to them ahead of time, 'I'm going to try to cover everything.'"

"This interview took three and a half hours... I was just trying to carefully go through everything that had come up in the past."

Oprah Winfrey sparks royal backlash after revisiting her viral 'silenced' question to Meghan Markle. (Chris Jackson - Pool/Getty Images)

The 2021 CBS special became one of the biggest celebrity interviews of the decade after Harry and Meghan opened up about their decision to step away from royal duties, allegations of a lack of support from the royal family, Meghan's mental health struggles, and claims about conversations surrounding their son Archie's royal status and security.

The couple's comments sparked worldwide debate, including Meghan's claim that there were "concerns and conversations" about "how dark his skin might be when he's born."

Harry also described feeling trapped within the royal institution.

"I need to do this for my family. This is not a surprise to anybody," he said.

QUEEN ELIZABETH CALLS FOR UNITY AHEAD OF MEGHAN MARKLE, PRINCE HARRY INTERVIEW IN COMMONWEALTH DAY SPEECH

"It's really sad that it's got to this point, but I've got to do something for my own mental health, for my wife's, and for Archie's as well, because I could see where this was headed."

Winfrey's latest comments quickly triggered a wave of reactions online.

Another critic commented, "Oh, Oprah… You failed to challenge obvious inconsistencies… and handed Harry and Meghan a worldwide stage to tell 'their story' without proper scrutiny or questioning. Now you want 'clarification?' That ship sailed! Spare me…"

Oprah Winfrey reignites backlash over the bombshell Prince Harry royal interview. (Karwai Tang/WireImage)

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTERA third added, "Five years later, Oprah is still trying to distance herself from the dastardly duo. Looks like scrubbing the interview from the internet was not enough. How can an industry giant like Oprah allow herself to be used by confirmed pathological liars?"

But supporters pushed back against the criticism.

"In that royal climate she was silenced she didn't have a voice. The royals can't express their own opinions, everyone knows that," one Instagram user wrote.

Prince Harry and Meghan Markle sit with their son Archie during their 2019 tour in Cape Town, South Africa.  (Samir Hussein)

"I remember every one of these moments. She is a TRUE icon. A pioneer. I love Oprah," a YouTube commenter added.

"Great look back moments... I remember them all," another fan wrote.

Years later, the question continues to fuel debate surrounding Harry and Meghan's departure from royal life.

Stephanie Giang-Paunon is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Minnesota Twins somehow have four players scheduled to get married on the exact same offseason day
excerpt:
Dan Dakich reacts to Sophie Cunningham doubling down on her stance to protect girls in sports.

One of the realities of being a professional baseball player is that many major life events have to wait...
body_text:
Dan Dakich reacts to Sophie Cunningham doubling down on her stance to protect girls in sports.

One of the realities of being a professional baseball player is that many major life events have to wait until the offseason, including weddings.

But the Minnesota Twins found themselves in a strange situation: Four players are all getting married this offseason.

COURTHOUSE WEDDINGS ARE BOOMING AS MANY COUPLES SKIP PRICEY TRADITIONAL CEREMONIES

I mean, let's say the offseason runs from October/November to mid-February. That's a fairly large window to choose a day to tie the knot, and yet four couples on the same team just happened to pick the same one.

The Twins clubhouse needs to go in on some Powerball tickets together.

The Minnesota Twins hit for the cycle when it comes to offseason weddings. (Photo by Stephen Maturen/Getty Images)

There might be some tough choices to make for wedding guests, and I imagine some brides-to-be were less than jazzed when they learned that their wedding wasn't going to be the only one that day.

But, if you're a would-be wedding guest, this is great.

Now, who knows if every person on the team is invited to all four weddings, but it's reasonable to assume that some will be.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

But every time someone came into the clubhouse after getting engaged, there had to be some guys sitting there going, "Dammit, there goes another weekend."

Those are valuable. Especially during college football season.

The great thing about getting invited to four weddings that are all on the same day is that you only have to go to one. (iStock)

So, when they found out about this bizarre scheduling quirk, they probably realized they had an automatic out for three of those weddings because — at least at the time of writing; technology moves fast these days — it isn't possible to be in multiple places at once.

That means only one weekend in the fall is occupied by a wedding. That's awesome.

Now all you need to do is get them a stand mixer or some corn-holders or whatever, and your duties as a wedding guest are complete.

title: Mamdani slow to respond to suspected antisemitic stabbing as Hochul, other NY officials condemn attack
excerpt:
Rep. Mike Lawler, R-N.Y., discusses the attempted New York City terror attack and criticizes Mayor Zohran Mamdani's response, calling it 'pathetic' for not explicitly condemning radical Islam.

New Yo...
body_text:
Rep. Mike Lawler, R-N.Y., discusses the attempted New York City terror attack and criticizes Mayor Zohran Mamdani's response, calling it 'pathetic' for not explicitly condemning radical Islam.

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani remained silent for serveal hours on what officials described as an antisemitic stabbing in New York City on Thursday afternoon, choosing to instead post about his millionaire housing tax initiative — even as the attack drew attention from other state officials.

"I have been briefed on today’s horrifying stabbings on the Upper West Side, where an Asian man and a Jewish man were attacked. According to witness and victim statements, the perpetrator yelled "Allahu Akbar" during both attacks. I am relieved that both victims are in stable condition," Mamdani wrote in a statement.

"The NYPD responded immediately and arrested Raul Morales in connection with both assaults—and I am grateful to the officers for their swift response."

The delayed response from Mamdani played into the fears of some observers that his critical stances towards Israel, views shared by many progressives, are feeding into hesitations about supporting Jewish communities in the U.S.

LIBERAL MS NOW WRITER CALLS MAMDANI PRIMARY SWEEP A 'GENUINELY SCARY NIGHT FOR NEW YORK CITY JEWS'

Julie Menin, speaker of the New York City Council, during an announcement in the Brooklyn borough of New York, US, on Monday, Jan. 12, 2026. (John Lamparski/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Councilwoman Inna Vernikov, another member of the New York City Council, more pointedly laid blame with Mamdani.

"According to credible reports I received, the attacker was yelling ‘Allahu Akbar’ during his attempted murder. I pray for the speedy recovery of the victims. [Mamdani,] you got blood on your hands," she wrote.

Authorities reported that a 57-year-old man was reportedly attacked near West 84th Street and Central Park West just after 1:30 p.m. local time. He was found conscious and alert with a stab wound to his back. He was taken to a local hospital in stable condition.

Investigators later found another 50-year-old man nearby with a stab wound to his torso. He was also conscious and alert and was taken to a nearby hospital.

City officials described the violence as an "antisemitic stabbing," though police have not yet identified a motive or said whether the attacks are being investigated as a hate crime.

Raul Morales, the suspect, is in custody, the New York City Police Department (NYPD) told Fox News Digital.

NYC REPUBLICAN LAWMAKER SLAMS MAMDANI ANTISEMITISM OFFICE AS A ‘BLACK HOLE’ LACKING PUBLIC-FACING RESOURCES

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani's video admitting that he lacks the legal authority to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu did little to ease Jewish leaders' concerns. (NYC Mayor's Office via YouTube/Handout via Reuters)

Almost immediately, the incident drew alarm and condemnation from observers, expressing dismay at another instance of violence against the Jewish community in New York City.

"Disgusted by what appears to be another senseless attack on New Yorkers simply because they’re Jewish," New York Gov. Kathy Hochul said in a post to X.

"While we await additional details to be confirmed, we know that what is happening in our city is abhorrent. The fear in the Jewish community is real. Every New Yorker deserves to feel safe in their neighborhood, regardless of their faith or identity."

"While we await additional details to be confirmed, we know that what is happening in our city is abhorrent. The fear in the Jewish community is real. Every New Yorker deserves to feel safe in their neighborhood, regardless of their faith or identity," Julie Menin, speaker of the New York City Council, wrote in a post to X

"A man yelling 'allahu akbar' stabbed a man in a kippah at 86th and Amsterdam Avenue on the Upper West Side at 1:30 pm today, then likely stabbed someone else at 84th and Central Park West. Has apparently been caught. On the Jewish day of mourning. In Zohran Mamdani's city," another observer wrote.

Even as the incident captured attention online, Mamdani remained notably silent on the attack. Instead, he made a post on his recent housing tax plan, a strategy to dissuade wealthy New Yorkers from purchasing and keeping an outsized number of residences.

"If you have a second home in New York City worth more than $5M, check your mailbox when you’re back in the five boroughs — because you've got mail. Today, we sent notification letters to property owners, letting them know that our new pied-à-terre tax is coming soon," Mamdani wrote at 3:55, almost two and a half hours after the attack.

JON STEWART PRESSES MAMDANI ON WHERE HE BREAKS WITH FELLOW DEMOCRATIC SOCIALISTS ON PRISONS AND BORDERS

In the first quarter of 2026, the New York Police Department has received 146 hate crime complaints and made 71 arrests, according to police data — an uptick from 137 complaints and 51 arrests in the first quarter of 2025.

Mamdani’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment on whether factors behind the scenes had caused a delay in response.

Leo Briceno is a politics reporter for the congressional team at Fox News Digital. He was previously a reporter with World Magazine.

title: Arab and Muslim countries condemn Israeli storming of Al-Aqsa Mosque
excerpt:
Eight Arab and Muslim-majority countries have strongly condemned the storming of the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in occupied East Jerusalem by far-right groups and thousands of Israeli settlers.

In a joi...
body_text:
Eight Arab and Muslim-majority countries have strongly condemned the storming of the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in occupied East Jerusalem by far-right groups and thousands of Israeli settlers.

In a joint statement issued on Thursday, the foreign ministers of Egypt, Indonesia, Jordan, Pakistan, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Turkiye, and the United Arab Emirates denounced the “mass incursions by settlers led by extremist Israeli ministers into the Noble Sanctuary” of Al-Aqsa.

The diplomats underscored that the storming of Islam’s third-holiest site took place under the protection of Israeli police forces.

“These provocative actions fuel hatred and extremism, and obstruct efforts to advance a just and lasting peace on the basis of the two-State solution,” the statement said, calling the escalation “an unlawful attempt to alter the historical and legal status quo” of the holy sites in Jerusalem.

“Israel has no sovereignty over occupied Jerusalem or its Islamic and Christian holy sites,” it said.

More than 4,200 Israeli settlers and members of far-right groups stormed the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound on Thursday, one of the largest incursions this year, to mark the Jewish holiday of Tisha B’Av.

Israel’s far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir also entered the site under heavy police protection.

Palestinian officials said Israeli forces imposed tight restrictions at the mosque’s gates, barred large numbers of Palestinian worshippers and students from entering, and assaulted several people.

Israel captured East Jerusalem in 1967 and formally annexed it in 1980. In 2024, the International Court of Justice ruled that Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian territory, including East Jerusalem, is illegal.

Under the status quo, the Jerusalem Waqf, a Jordanian-appointed body, manages the Al-Aqsa Mosque.

Thursday marked the Jewish day of mourning, Tisha B’Av, that commemorates the destruction, twice, of an ancient Jewish temple, according to religious tradition, believed to have stood at the site known to Muslims as Al-Aqsa and to Jews as the Temple Mount.

The joint statement on Thursday denounced “the illegal and extremist acts represented by incitement and calls to mobilise incursions, as well as acts of violence committed by Israeli ministers and extremist groups”.

The statement called on Israel, “as the occupying power, to immediately lift the restrictions” imposed on access to the Al-Aqsa Mosque.

It also called on the international community “to take a firm stance obligating Israel to cease its ongoing violations and illegal practices against the Islamic and Christian holy sites in Jerusalem, and its violations of the sanctity of these holy sites”.

title: Michelin-starred chef facing jail after allegedly serving nearly 50,000 ants to diners
excerpt:
"Next Level Chef" judge Richard Blais speaks to Fox News Digital from his home in California about cooking with insects as part of his upcoming new digital short, "Bug Bites."

The owner of a Michelin...
body_text:
"Next Level Chef" judge Richard Blais speaks to Fox News Digital from his home in California about cooking with insects as part of his upcoming new digital short, "Bug Bites."

The owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant in South Korea is facing up to a year in jail and a fine of roughly $13,510 after the eatery allegedly served ants atop its sorbets.

The proprietor of the Seoul restaurant, not identified in court documents, acknowledged serving ants, which are not among the insect species approved for human consumption under South Korea's Food Sanitation Act. The law approves 10 other insect species for human consumption — but not ants, the BBC reported.

The country's Ministry of Food and Drug Safety discovered the violation through customers' social media posts and online reviews mentioning ants, according to reports.

CYCLOSPORA OUTBREAK: IS IT STILL SAFE TO EAT AT RESTAURANTS? HERE'S WHAT TO KNOW

Grasshoppers, mealworms, silkworm pupae, locusts and two-spotted crickets are among the insects the government has approved as food, based on health and nutrition criteria.

A South Korean restaurant owner is facing jail time for serving ants as a garnish on dessert dishes. (iStock)

Prosecutors allege the ants served at the restaurant contained heavy metal levels up to 55 times the maximum limit allowed for other edible insects. They accuse the restaurant of importing dried ants and using approximately 49,000 of them in dishes since 2021.

Defense attorneys disputed that figure, according to The Chosun Daily.

Ants were offered as an optional garnish for the sorbet, the restaurant owner's defense argued, and only 60% of diners chose them over other options, including fermented vinegar and edible flowers, the South Korean newspaper reported.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

The court is expected to render a verdict in September.

Globally, the edible insect market is expanding, as Fox News Digital previously reported.

Insects are eaten widely around the world, and the global market for insect-based foods is predicted to expand. (iStock)

The market is estimated to grow from $1.73 billion in 2025 to $13.23 billion by 2034, driven by growing interest in sustainable nutrition, resource efficiency and diversified protein sources, according to Fortune Business Insights.

Beetles dominated the insect-based food market in 2025, accounting for 33% market share, Persistence Market Research reported.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

The yellow mealworm and lesser mealworm also topped the list, while crickets and grasshoppers were the fastest-growing insect segments.

A small study by researchers at the University of Beira Interior in Portugal recently found that people exposed to insect-based foods were more curious and receptive to them than expected.

Ants are one of the insects deemed unsuitable for human consumption in South Korea, though several other bugs are allowed to be eaten. (iStock)

It’s "exciting to see that consumers may be open to new abundant sources of nutrition," Ashley Gearhardt, Ph.D., a professor of psychology at the University of Michigan, told Fox News Digital.

Several insect-based food products are already available to consumers in the U.S. Chirps Chips are tortilla-style chips made with cricket flour, while other companies market cricket protein powders and snack bars.

Jiminy's grain-free cookies for dogs are made with crickets and grubs.

Teresa Mull is a freelance writer with the Lifestyle team at Fox News Digital.

title: Raiders tight end Brock Bowers makes sure his NFL Draft star girlfriend heads into the season with a ring
excerpt:
Dan Dakich calls Michigan athletics a rudderless ship after the announcement that Warde Manuel will step down at the end of the year

The wait is over for NFL Draft star Cameron Rose Newell. She is he...
body_text:
Dan Dakich calls Michigan athletics a rudderless ship after the announcement that Warde Manuel will step down at the end of the year

The wait is over for NFL Draft star Cameron Rose Newell. She is heading into her third year in the league with a ring on her finger.

That development took place on Wednesday when Las Vegas Raiders tight end Brock Bowers proposed to his longtime girlfriend. The move ensures that she'll start the regular season with the title of fiancée.

On Thursday, Newell shared the video of the surprise proposal on Instagram. She wrote in her social media announcement, "The best surprise of my life, right where it all started. Can’t wait to spend forever with you."

FORMER COLLEGE SOFTBALL STAR IS NOW AN AMERICA-LOVING GIANTS WAG, NORWAY FAN WON'T ROW & SPURS ANNOUNCER FIRED

The two have been together since high school, reports People. They made it work in college despite going to different schools. Bowers went to Georgia and Newell went to Tennessee.

They then entered the NFL together in the first round of the 2024 NFL Draft. Both were on our draft board prior to her stealing some of his spotlight on draft night. Credit to OutKick's own Zach Dean and his in-depth draft analysis for that.

Cameron Rose Newell and Brock Bowers attend Sports Illustrated and Tight End University Host Tight Ends & Friends at The Pinnacle in Nashville, Tenn., on June 23, 2026. (Getty Images)

TOM BRADY GIVES RAIDERS HARSH 'REALITY' CHECK AFTER THE TEAM WON ONLY THREE GAMES IN 2025

It hasn't been an easy start to life in the NFL. The Raiders have finished in last place in the AFC West in each of Bowers' first two seasons. He battled a knee injury last year that landed him on IR by the end of the season.

All of that is behind him now. He's participated in Las Vegas' offseason activities, he's now gotten down on his knee and proposed, it's time for the franchise that drafted him to turn the corner.

Cameron Rose Newell and Brock Bowers attend the 2026 ACM Awards at MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas, Nev., on May 17, 2026. (Getty Images)

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

Handing out a ring before the season in exchange for being on the receiving end of one at the end of it might be too lofty of a goal, but adding several wins to the win column after only collecting three last season is within reach.

Congratulations are in order for the star tight end and his influencer fiancée.

title: Trump DOJ expands religious liberty protections after major Supreme Court rulings
excerpt: Fox News' Kayleigh McEnany reports on the Department of Justice's investigation into Major League Baseball after warnings were issued to players for displaying Bible verses on their hats. McEnany emph...
body_text:
Fox News' Kayleigh McEnany reports on the Department of Justice's investigation into Major League Baseball after warnings were issued to players for displaying Bible verses on their hats. McEnany emphasizes that federal law, Title VII, requires employers to reasonably accommodate religious beliefs. She also questions MLB's apparent double standards, citing past allowances for campaigns like 'SOS Cuba' and 'BLM' slogans on player uniforms without similar warnings.

The Trump administration on Thursday directed federal agencies to give greater consideration to religious liberty when writing regulations, enforcing federal law and accommodating employees' religious beliefs, marking the first major update to Department of Justice (DOJ) guidance on the issue since 2017.

The updated guidance instructs executive agencies to ensure their policies comply with the First Amendment, the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, Title VII of the Civil Rights Act and recent Supreme Court decisions that have expanded protections for religious exercise.

The DOJ, led by Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, said the guidance reflects nearly a decade of new Supreme Court precedent and is intended to ensure federal agencies protect religious Americans in areas including employment, federal contracting, grants, rulemaking and enforcement actions.

DOJ ALLEGES 'SYSTEMATIC CULTURE' OF ANTI-CHRISTIAN BIAS ACROSS FEDERAL AGENCIES UNDER BIDEN

The updated guidance instructs executive agencies to ensure their policies comply with the First Amendment, the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, Title VII of the Civil Rights Act and recent Supreme Court decisions. (Li Rui/Xinhua via Getty Images, File)

Among the changes, the department said agencies should consider religious-liberty concerns earlier when developing regulations, accommodate religious expression in the federal workplace when required by law, and ensure religious organizations can compete for federal grants and contracts on equal footing with secular organizations.

The guidance also reaffirms constitutional protections for churches to govern their own internal affairs and highlights recent Supreme Court rulings recognizing parents' rights to direct the religious upbringing of their children.

"The updated guidance will ensure that the federal government will respect the religious liberty rights of all Americans when implementing the President's policies," Kelly Shackelford, president, CEO and chief counsel of First Liberty Institute, said in a statement.

GOP LAWMAKERS URGE SUPREME COURT TO STRENGTHEN FIRST AMENDMENT PROTECTIONS FOR STREET PREACHERS

President Donald Trump is being thanked "for his strong efforts to protect America's first freedom—religious freedom." (iStock)

Shackelford thanked President Donald Trump "for his strong efforts to protect America's first freedom—religious freedom."

First Liberty said the guidance references two Supreme Court victories involving the organization. One was Kennedy v. Bremerton School District, in which the Supreme Court ruled that a public high school football coach's postgame prayer was protected by the First Amendment.

The other was Groff v. DeJoy, which held that employers generally must provide religious accommodations unless doing so would impose substantial hardship.

According to the DOJ, the Office of Legal Policy will continue working with federal agencies to review proposed regulations and other government actions for compliance with federal religious-liberty protections.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche listens during a news conference at the Justice Department in Washington, D.C. (Manuel Balce Ceneta/AP Photo, File)

The updated memorandum replaces guidance issued during Trump's first administration in 2017 and incorporates Supreme Court decisions issued since then that the department says have clarified and strengthened constitutional and statutory protections for religious exercise.

A DOJ spokesperson did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for additional comment on the practical impact of the updated guidance.

Jasmine Baehr is a breaking news writer for Fox News Digital, where she covers politics, the military, legal debates surrounding life and family policy, as well as faith and culture.

title: Foster child allegedly drowned in lake by two teens on local politician’s property: authorities
excerpt:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

A temporary foster placement at the home of a South Carolina county council chairman ended in tragedy after investigat...
body_text:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

A temporary foster placement at the home of a South Carolina county council chairman ended in tragedy after investigators say two teens attacked a 13-year-old boy before holding him underwater until he died.

According to The Augusta Press, 13-year-old Daryl Martinez Lowry had only recently been placed in the home of McCormick County Council Chairman Bernie Hamby while awaiting another foster placement.

The outlet reported the two suspects were also foster children living at the home.

One of the teens, Landen Austin Moore, 17, was charged with murder after investigators said he and a 16-year-old got into a physical altercation with Lowry during the early morning hours of July 20, leaving the 13-year-old with knife wounds before the two teens allegedly held him underwater until he died.

TENNESSEE WOMAN ARRESTED AFTER ALLEGEDLY TRYING TO DROWN ONE OF HER CHILDREN IN BACKYARD POOL

Daryl Martinez Lowry, 13, was killed July 20 after investigators say two teens attacked him at a boat dock in McCormick County, South Carolina. (Chris King Memorial Chapel)

According to an arrest warrant posted online by the McCormick County Sheriff's Office, the confrontation happened at a private boat dock on Beauregard Drive in Modoc, South Carolina.

The warrant alleges Lowry suffered knife lacerations during the fight before Moore and the unidentified 16-year-old "did unlawfully, intentionally, and with malice aforethought" murder him by "holding him underwater until he became deceased."

MARSHALS NAB TEEN WANTED FOR MURDER OF PENN STATE STUDENT 1,700 MILES FROM CRIME SCENE, 2ND SUSPECT IN CUSTODY

Clarks Hill Lake in South Carolina, where investigators say a 13-year-old boy was held underwater during a deadly attack. (Katie Goodale / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images)

The warrant states the allegations are based on witness statements and evidence gathered during the investigation.

The South Carolina Law Enforcement Division (SLED) announced Tuesday that Moore was charged with murder after the McCormick County Sheriff's Office requested the state agency investigate the case.

Moore was booked into the McCormick County Detention Center.

MISSING TEEN FOUND HIDING IN CONVICTED SEX OFFENDER'S CLOSET IN FLORIDA, SHERIFF SAYS

Landen Austin Moore, 17, was charged with murder in connection with the death of 13-year-old Daryl Martinez Lowry, according to the South Carolina Law Enforcement Division. (McCormick County Sheriff's Office / Detention Center)

Authorities said the 16-year-old allegedly involved in the incident was transported to the South Carolina Department of Juvenile Justice. SLED has not publicly announced whether that juvenile has been charged.

According to the Augusta Press, Hamby was asleep inside the residence during the alleged attack. Lowry's body was later recovered from the lake.

Authorities have not indicated Hamby witnessed the incident or was involved in the alleged crime. Hamby, a longtime McCormick County councilman who serves as council chairman, has publicly advocated for foster care for years.

Just over a month before his death, Lowry was the subject of a missing child investigation in his hometown of Chester, South Carolina.

Family members described Daryl Martinez Lowry as "full of life, love, and charisma" following his death. (GoFundMe)

On June 12, the Chester Police Department asked for the public's help locating the 13-year-old after he was last seen the previous evening.

"At this time, Daryl's whereabouts are unknown, and we are asking the entire community to remain alert and assist in bringing him home safely," the department wrote in a social media post.

Police urged parents, neighbors and businesses to check surveillance cameras and report "anything suspicious or unusual," adding that officers and investigators were actively pursuing leads.

Later that same day, the department announced Lowry had been found safe.

Authorities have not publicly linked Lowry's earlier disappearance to the circumstances surrounding his death.

A GoFundMe created by Lowry's relatives says the teen had recently been living in a temporary foster home.

"My name is Zyon Simpson, I am the aunt of Daryl Martinez Lowry (13) who was recently murdered while in a temporary foster home," the fundraiser states.

The family described Daryl as "an amazing child full of life, love, and charisma."

"He loved his parents, family, and friends, and he always wore a smile," the fundraiser says.

A woman identifying herself as Lowry's aunt also mourned the 13-year-old in a Facebook post, vowing to seek justice.

"I love you my sweet nephew. We will get justice," she wrote. "You didn't deserve this. No child deserves this... Until we meet again, I will definitely keep your name alive."

Authorities say the fatal confrontation involving three juveniles occurred at a boat dock on Clarks Hill Lake in South Carolina. (Katie Goodale / USA TODAY NETWORK)

McCormick County Sheriff Chad Cox described the killing as an extraordinary tragedy for the rural community while thanking the agencies that helped investigate the case.

"We want to express our deepest condolences to the friends/family of the young man who had his life taken in such a horrendous manner," Cox wrote in a statement posted to the sheriff's office's Facebook page.

Cox thanked SLED, the South Carolina Department of Natural Resources, McCormick County Emergency Services, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the McCormick County Coroner's Office and others who assisted in the investigation.

"We are extremely grateful that we live/work in a community where violent crime is not the regular occurrence," Cox wrote. "But with that being said, no place is exempt from these types of incidents and we must remain diligent in our efforts to keep McCormick County a safe place to live, work and raise families."

SLED said its investigation remains active and ongoing. The case will be prosecuted by the South Carolina Attorney General's Office.

Fox News Digital has reached out to Hamby, the South Carolina Department of Social Services, the South Carolina Law Enforcement Division (SLED) and the McCormick County Sheriff's Office to independently confirm those details.

Authorities have not accused Hamby of any wrongdoing.

Stepheny Price is a Writer at Fox News with a focus on West Coast and Midwest news, missing persons, national and international crime stories, homicide cases, and border security.

title: US health panel recommends easing restrictions on controversial peptides
excerpt:
Vote from advisory group – whose members include people linked to peptides industry – likely to embolden RFK Jr

A panel of federal health advisers on Thursday narrowly recommended easing access to se...
body_text:
Vote from advisory group – whose members include people linked to peptides industry – likely to embolden RFK Jr

A panel of federal health advisers on Thursday narrowly recommended easing access to several peptides popular with wellness influencers and celebrities, despite warnings from government scientists that the chemicals have not been shown to be safe or effective.

In a series of votes, outside experts to the Food and Drug Administration advised the agency to drop current restrictions barring pharmacies from producing injectable peptides with names like BPC-157, TB-500 and KPV.

The panel’s vote is not binding but is likely to embolden the health secretary, Robert F Kennedy Jr, who has described himself as a “big fan” of peptides and vowed to overturn FDA restrictions put in place under Joe Biden.

Across several votes, the panelists voted 8-6, with one abstention, in favor of putting the peptides on FDA’s list of substances considered safe for pharmacy compounding. If FDA follows that advice, telehealth companies are expected to ramp up marketing of the chemicals for wellness and cosmetic uses.

Thursday’s vote tally reflected the unusual makeup of the panel. Before the meeting, more than a half-dozen people with connections to the peptide industry were added to the panel, including doctors, pharmacists or consultants who work in the field.

“I voted yes because it’s time to put this decision back in the hands of the patient, the physician and the pharmacist,” said David Pope, chief pharmacy officer of Xifin Pharmacy Solutions, after the vote on BPC-157.

The panel will on Friday vote on three more peptides. The FDA is not required to follow the group’s advice, though it usually does.

The positive votes came despite highly critical reviews from FDA staff scientists, who said there was little data that BPC-157, TB-500 and the other chemicals can be safely used for medical purposes.

Panel members with backgrounds in academia, rather than peptides, mostly voted against the substances, which are widely advertised on social media.

“I’m concerned we’re responding to a market-induced demand rather than a decision based in solid science,” said Dr Elizabeth Rebello of the University of Texas MD Anderson Center.

Peptide supporters include the podcaster Joe Rogan and longevity influencers such as Gary Brecka, a supporter of Kennedy’s “Make America Healthy Again” agenda who sells peptides through his website.

But medical experts describe the marketplace as a “wild west” of unproven, unregulated drugs that could cause serious harm, including infection, allergic reactions or other problems.

Peptides are the building blocks of more complex proteins, triggering hormones inside the body needed for growth, metabolism and healing. Drug companies have brought several to market as medications, including mainstay treatments for diabetes and weight loss.

But most peptides sold online are promoted for unproven uses, often combined in “stacks”, to purportedly heal injuries, build muscle, rejuvenate skin or increase energy.

title: UK ‘ready to defend itself’ after Iranian threat over US bases
excerpt:
IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’

The government has said the UK is “ready to defend itself” after Iran’s military warned...
body_text:
IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’

The government has said the UK is “ready to defend itself” after Iran’s military warned that any bases being used by the US were “legitimate targets”.

The UK has allowed the US to launch “defensive” operations from British bases hosting American planes since the start of its war on Iran but has refused to help in offensive operations.

That policy has not been altered by the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, who was notified last week that a decision had been made to extend the agreement with the US.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said on Thursday that “any base used for aggression against Iranian territory constitutes a legitimate target for our forces”.

In a statement published on the IRGC-linked Tasnim news agency, the group said American bombing missions had been launched from RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire two days earlier.

The statement came after the US carried out a 12th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran. The Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether Fairford was used by the US for operations against Iran this week.

The IRGC also accused the “British monarchy regime” of causing being the “primary cause of hardships in our region with a black record of partitioning Islamic nations, widespread massacres, imposing despotic regimes, and organising the occupation of Palestine”.

It warned the government “not to weigh down its record any further”.

A spokesperson for the British government said: “Our armed forces are ready to keep ⁠the United Kingdom safe from any kind of attacks, whether it’s on our soil ⁠or from abroad. The UK stands ready 24/7 to defend itself.

“This includes through ‌operating a layered approach to air and missile defence, ‌provided by Royal Navy, British Army and Royal Air Force assets equipped with a range of advanced capabilities, working closely with our Nato allies.

“We are committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict.”

On 1 March a drone struck the RAF base at Akrotiri in Cyprus, hitting a hangar, and prompting a partial evacuation of the facility. On 19 March, Iran fired two ballistic missiles at the UK’s Diego Garcia base in the Chagos Islands. One reportedly failed mid-flight, while the other was shot down by a US airship.

Iran has launched strikes against countries in the Gulf that house US military bases, including Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait, killing American service personnel in some of the attacks. Donald Trump said Iran would “pay a big price” for killing US troops.

On Thursday afternoon, the US president said he was considering ordering a “massive attack” on Iran. “I am close to making a decision,” he told Axios. “We’re all set for it.” He said he believed Iran’s leaders “want to negotiate”, but aren’t yet ready to make a deal.

In another sign of the conflict potentially escalating, Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels said they attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea on Thursday, as oil topped $100 a barrel.

title: Trump likens Shohei Ohtani to Babe Ruth as the World Series champion Dodgers visit White House
excerpt: President Donald Trump compared Los Angeles Dodgers two-way superstar Shohei Ohtani to Babe Ruth during the team's visit to Washington, D.C., where the team celebrated their World Series championship....
body_text:
President Donald Trump compared Los Angeles Dodgers two-way superstar Shohei Ohtani to Babe Ruth during the team's visit to Washington, D.C., where the team celebrated their World Series championship. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

President Donald Trump likened Los Angeles Dodgers star Shohei Ohtani to one of the best to ever do it, Babe Ruth.

The Dodgers visited the White House on Thursday afternoon during an off day on their East Coast road trip where Trump recognized Ohtani for his remarkable accomplishments.

"The 2025 Dodger season was one for the record books in 150 years of Major League Baseball. No player has ever earned more than one unanimous league MVP, except for an international legend named Shohei Ohtani," Trump said.

President Donald Trump holds a Los Angeles Dodgers jersey with his name and the number 47 during a ceremony honoring the 2025 World Series champions in the Rose Garden at the White House in Washington, D.C., on July 23, 2026. (Oliver Contreras / AFP via Getty Images)

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

"He's amazing. He's amazing. Everybody loves him. Last year, Shohei did what was previously unthinkable. Nobody thought it was possible. He collected his a third straight unanimous league MVP with a franchise record 55 home runs. And if you would have told me you can be the best pitcher and the best hitter at the same time, you know, Babe Ruth was pretty close to that."

Across the 2024 and 2025 seasons, Ohtani’s incredible career reached new heights, as he hit 99 home runs across 2024 and 2025, leading the National League with a 1.025 OPS in that span.

Ohtani did not pitch in 2024 after elbow surgery but returned to the bump last year and owned a 2.87 ERA and 11.9 strikeouts per nine, a figure he also put up in 2022, which led the American League.

Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Shohei Ohtani listens as President Donald Trump speaks during an event honoring the 2025 World Series champion Dodgers in the Rose Garden of the White House on Thursday, July 23, 2026, in Washington. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Ohtani, nicknamed by many the "Japanese Babe Ruth," is the only player in MLB history to have more than 300-plus plate appearances and more than 40-plus innings in six separate seasons. Ruth only did it twice and never stole 50 bases.

Trump called the Boston Red Sox trade of Babe Ruth to the New York Yankees the worst trade in the history of sports.

"Babe was 19 years old. They considered it the worst trade in the history of sports. I would say Babe will always say, ‘Take that.’ He was 19 and they got a 36-year-old third baseman and $100,000, so I would rate that pretty bad. They drafted him as a pitcher, but he did something very unusual. He led the league in home runs as a pitcher. That one you have to do," Trump said while gesturing toward Ohtani.

Yoshinobu Yamamoto, Miguel Rojas and Shohei Ohtani react as President Donald Trump hosts the 2025 MLB World Series champions in the Rose Garden of the White House on July 23, 2026, in Washington, D.C. The Dodgers defeated the Blue Jays in seven games to win their second consecutive World Series title and ninth overall. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images)

In 1920, the Red Sox sold Ruth to the Yankees for $100,000 and a $300,000 loan for Fenway Park.

Ohtani is in the midst of another brilliant season. As a hitter, he has a .283 batting average with 22 home runs, 60 RBI and six stolen bases in 94 games.

As a pitcher, he is 8-2 with a 1.79 ERA in 14 starts, but recently missed a start due to a knee issue. Ohtani will look to lead the Dodgers to third straight World Series title with a strong second half.

Fox News' Ryan Morik contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Ryan Canfield is a digital production assistant for Fox News Digital.

title: Rahm Emanuel spars with New Yorker interviewer who grilled him for not emphasizing trans issues
excerpt:
Former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel defended his calls for Democrats to shift away from far-left culture-war politics, arguing it distracts from kitchen table issues that affect most Americans.

Former ...
body_text:
Former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel defended his calls for Democrats to shift away from far-left culture-war politics, arguing it distracts from kitchen table issues that affect most Americans.

Former Democratic Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel locked horns with The New Yorker’s David Remnick on Tuesday over whether the Democratic Party should still prioritize transgender issues.

The former Chicago mayor is expected to be one of the Democratic Party’s potential candidates in 2028, and is one of many prominent Democrats who have spoken out numerous times about how they need to move away from far-left culture wars and focus on kitchen table issues.

Remnick grilled Emanuel on this during his interview on "The New Yorker Radio Hour," noting, "You say you're, quote, ‘Done with the discussion of locker rooms. I'm done with the discussion of bathrooms, and we better start having a conversation about the classroom.’ Now, bathrooms in this context, this means transgender rights in general."

He added further, "You'd like Democrats to be done with that, but Republicans clearly aren't. Should Democrats really be silent in response to attacks on trans people? It just seems that this line is clever, but it doesn't do service to some human beings who want their rights."

RAHM EMANUEL WARNS NEW GENERATION OF DEM SOCIALISTS DON'T LIKE AMERICA

Former Mayor of Chicago Rahm Emanuel defended his political record as he argued with the interviewer about the direction of the Democratic Party. ((Photo by Patrick T. Fallon / AFP via Getty Images))

Emanuel responded by both touting his success on education reform during his term as mayor as well as his early support for transgender access to bathrooms.

"Well, two things. Since it's about education, and I spent a good part of my tenure, the entire tenure as mayor, taking the worst public school system to what Sean Reardon and Stanford called the single best. In 2016, I passed a law, a decade ago, on bathroom access," he replied. "Did I take my eye off in the graduation rates that we took from 56% to 84%? Not a chance. That's why parents send kids to school."

Emanuel then appeared to argue that the Democrats, as a party, shifted toward far-left culture war initiatives rather than everyday issues that were critically important amid a pandemic.

"Because you have an issue, in which coming out of COVID also is a lightning rod that distracted," he argued. "I didn't take Abraham Lincoln's name off a school in San Francisco. I didn't ask for defunding the police. I didn't get yourselves wrapped around the axle on the term ‘Latinx,’ which nobody identifies with."

"It was an example, David, not just of this particular issue, of a party — when people's backs are against the wall, they're holding on to that windowsill, and they can't make a college payment, they can't make a home mortgage payment, their healthcare is now being denied to them, they expect us Democrats to show up," he warned. "And we're showing up talking about bathroom access, sporting events, who gets to participate?"

FAR-LEFT ACTIVIST CONFERENCE AT TEACHERS UNION HQ RAISES CLASSROOM CONCERNS

Rahm Emanuel distanced himself from the far-left excesses of the early 2020s, saying, "I didn't take Abraham Lincoln's name off a school in San Francisco. I didn't ask for defunding the police. I didn't get yourselves wrapped around the axle on the term ‘Latinx,’ which nobody identifies with." (Getty Images)

He recalled further how dire the circumstances were on a variety of issues that, unlike transgender politics, impact most of the population.

"We’d just come out of COVID. Our reading scores and math scores are in the tank. Fifty percent of our kids cannot read or do math at grade level. And we're going to talk about bathroom access?" he asked. "So my view is, if you want to win elections, and you also want to help kids get prepared for the future, focus on the bottom line of what schools —"

Remnick went on to compare transgender issues to the civil rights era battles in the time of Martin Luther King Jr., referring to one of his famous writings.

"I understand that, Rahm, but all I would say is that there were earlier times with other groups that didn't have their rights, and they were told to be patient too, and that focusing on their rights was distracting," Remnick said. "That's what ‘Letter from a Birmingham Jail’ is all about, patience versus urgency."

"As a country, we proved we couldn't do it because you haven't told me and asked me a single question how to get 50% of our kids — " Emanuel began.

When Remnick claimed that such questions were on their way later in the interview, Emanuel was not impressed, replying, "No, wait a second. You started where you thought was a priority."

Remnick pushed his argument further, saying that he was merely asking about the rhetoric by Emanuel that has become "a kind of a tagline to differentiate yourself. I think that’s the motivation."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

Then-Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel, right, and Chicago Police Superintendent Eddie Johnson, center, appear at a news conference Tuesday, March 26, 2019, (AP Photo/Teresa Crawford)

"It's not just a tagline!" Emanuel said. "I passed a bill in 2016, ten years beforehand. I made sure the kids had access to a bathroom. I just didn't let it distract from reading, math, or graduation rates. This is why I went down to Mississippi before anybody knew about the reading scores, because I pay attention to this. Not that others don't, but they went from 49th to ninth."

He went on to blast politicians both high and low and among both parties, warning that "people have accepted failure" to the point where "Fifty percent of our kids can't read at a third-grade level, which doesn't make fourth and fifth and sixth grade and high school any easier."

"You have to prioritize what's an A and a B. I'm going to make sure children feel accepted. And the best way to make sure they feel accepted, is they know reading, math, and high school graduation," Emanuel added further. "One of the things we did in Chicago — the only city to do it, the only education system — nobody gets a high school degree without showing us a letter of acceptance from a college, a community college, a branch of the armed forces, or a vocational school. That makes every child ready for tomorrow."

Alexander Hall is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Alexander.hall@fox.com.

title: Baltimore Ravens cut Heisman Trophy runner-up Diego Pavia before training camp even begins
excerpt:
Mike Renner tells Dan that Diego Pavia is likely done with football for good after college due to weak arm strength & that we will forget about him in a few years

Being the Heisman Trophy runner-up d...
body_text:
Mike Renner tells Dan that Diego Pavia is likely done with football for good after college due to weak arm strength & that we will forget about him in a few years

Being the Heisman Trophy runner-up does not guarantee anyone an NFL career, and Diego Pavia is learning that now.

The Baltimore Ravens cut Pavia, the former Vanderbilt University star quarterback, on Thursday. The Ravens open full-squad training camp practices next Wednesday, so Pavia didn't even make it to the start of camp.

DIEGO PAVIA’S STRIP CLUB VISIT ADDS TO DRAMA SURROUNDING QB AHEAD OF NFL TRYOUT WITH RAVENS

Vanderbilt quarterback Diego Pavia (2) celebrates after the team’s win Kentucky at FirstBank Stadium in Nashville, Tennessee, Saturday, Nov. 22, 2025. (Mark Zaleski/The Tennessean / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images)

It's probably the best timing for Pavia because he was a long shot to make the Baltimore roster anyway.

After going unselected in the 2026 NFL Draft, he is now free to sign with another NFL team before training camp begins, giving coaches a longer look as he competes for a roster spot.

If that's not an option, one supposes Pavia can set his sights on maybe catching on with a CFL team that might have a QB need as a result of an injury. The CFL season runs through October.

But being cut at this stage is also a blow to Pavia because it says the team that showed the most interest in adding him after the draft, bringing him in for a workout and keeping him around the entire offseason, has decided he's not good enough.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Miami Dolphins quarterback Skylar Thompson (19) aims a pass during the first half of an NFL football game against the New York Jets, Sunday, Jan. 8, 2023, in Miami Gardens, Fla. (AP Photo/Lynne Sladky)

The Ravens waived Pavia to make room for veteran center Ethan Pocic on the 90-man roster. That's the macro view of the roster.

Looking at the team's quarterback room, Baltimore decided Pavia was the third-best quarterback competing for the No. 3 quarterback job.

And the Ravens didn't need three guys competing for that back-of-the-roster spot. Baltimore narrowed that competition to veteran Skylar Thompson and rookie Joe Fagnano, who, like Pavia, was signed as an undrafted free agent.

The Ravens, in other words, decided Pavia was the fifth-best quarterback on their roster and they're only going with four now, with starter Lamar Jackson and presumed backup Tyler Huntley expected to make the roster.

Heisman Trophy candidate quarterback Diego Pavia of the Vanderbilt Commodores poses with the Heisman Memorial Trophy before the 2025 Heisman Trophy Presentation at Marriott Marquis Hotel on Dec. 13, 2025 in New York City. (Adam Hunger/Getty Images)

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

The Ravens had some familiarity with Pavia because new coach Jesse Minter took the advice of friend and Vanderbilt coach Clark Lea, who coached Pavia the past two seasons.

None of that ultimately mattered once the Ravens saw Pavia compete in the offseason.

Neither did his dynamic play at Vanderbilt and status as a Heisman Trophy runner-up.

Follow Armando Salguero on X: @ArmandoSalguero

Armando Salguero is OutKick's Senior NFL Writer.

title: Fox News Poll: Voters oppose the Iran war, see prolonged commitment
excerpt: Sen. Steve Daines, R-Mont., reacts to the House passing a war powers resolution asking whether Congress should limit presidential military authority and President Donald Trump's claim that Iran is rea...
body_text:
Sen. Steve Daines, R-Mont., reacts to the House passing a war powers resolution asking whether Congress should limit presidential military authority and President Donald Trump's claim that Iran is ready to negotiate.

Disapproval of President Donald Trump’s handling of Iran hit a record high in July, as a majority of voters oppose U.S. military action, and nearly two thirds think the conflict will last at least a year.

The latest Fox News survey finds 56% oppose U.S. military action against Iran, including 40% who "strongly" oppose. More Democrats oppose the war (84%) than Republicans support it (76%).

FOX NEWS POLL: A CLOSE SENATE CONTEST IS BREWING IN IOWA

Voters increasingly expect a long-term commitment. Today, 64% think the war will last a year or more, up 13 percentage points since May. Expectations vary sharply depending on attitudes toward the Iran war. Among supporters, 56% think it will be over in weeks or months, while 80% of opponents expect it to last at least a year.

FOX NEWS POLL: VOTERS WANT MAJOR CHANGE AMID ECONOMIC AND POLITICAL DISCONTENT

There is also doubt the conflict will produce an agreement. About half think the U.S. will not reach a deal with Iran, while the rest split between expecting a good deal (27%) and a bad one (25%).

Roughly half of Democrats, Republicans, and independents predict there will be no agreement with the Islamic Republic. Among those anticipating a deal, Democrats are 20 points more likely to expect a bad one, while Republicans are 27 points more likely to expect a good one.

Just 34% of voters approve of Trump’s handling of Iran, while 66% disapprove, his worst marks to date.

Trump’s overall standing remains steady, but underwater. Thirty-nine percent approve of his job performance, unchanged for the past three months. Sixty-one percent disapprove, tying a previous high. That includes 49% who "strongly" disapprove, matching a record high.

For historical context, Trump's best rating, 49% approve and 49% disapprove, came in April 2020. His current 39% approval is close to former President Joe Biden’s 40% (July 2022) and former President Barack Obama’s 42% (July 2014), during the summer of a midterm election year.

Trump continues to receive some of his strongest support from MAGA Republicans (97% approve) and conservatives (72%). But compared to June, his approval has declined to a near record low among rural voters (down 7 points to 44%) and an all-time low among White voters (down 3 points to 41%).

Yet the largest change comes among non-MAGA Republicans. Their support has fallen 13 points since last month to 51% approval, after hitting 70% at the beginning of Trump’s second term.

Trump’s ratings are also underwater on the economy (33% approve, 67% disapprove) and inflation (27-73%).

Voters are now divided over which party can better handle immigration: 50% say the Democrats and 49% the Republicans, a meaningful shift since April, when the GOP held an 8-point advantage. Women and voters under age 45 prefer the Democrats on immigration, while men and those ages 45 and older favor the Republicans.

"During the Biden administration years, immigration attitudes were often defined by border security considerations," says Republican pollster Daron Shaw, who works on the Fox News Poll with Democratic counterpart Chris Anderson. "Today, immigration enforcement and ICE often dominate, eroding the GOP’s edge on the issue."

Immigration remains Trump’s best performing issue in the survey, but his rating is still negative: 42% approve, while 57% disapprove.

Views of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) follow a similar pattern. ICE approval stands at 41%, unchanged since early this year but down from 49% last September.
