Majorities of Democrats (90%) and independents (68%) disapprove of ICE, while most Republicans approve (79%). Fully 78% of Democrats, and 50% of independents "strongly" disapprove, compared with 53% of Republicans who "strongly" approve.

Few voters support allowing all illegal immigrants to remain in the U.S. Just 7% take that position, including 11% of Democrats, 5% of independents, and 2% of Republicans.

Instead, 62% favor deporting those who have been charged with crimes while allowing others to stay and eventually qualify for citizenship. That position draws majority support from every demographic group except Republicans (39%), conservatives (41%), and White evangelical Christians (48%).

Thirty-one percent favor deporting all illegal immigrants, with support highest among MAGA Republicans at 68%.

When asked about their concerns about illegal immigration, voters are most worried about the strain on government services (39%). Smaller shares cite crime (19%), job losses for U.S. citizens (14%), changes to the country’s culture (13%), and terrorism (10%).

Overburdened government programs rank as the top concern across demographic groups. College graduates, those living in high income households, suburban voters and independents are among those most likely to cite it.

The immigration findings also reveal a consistent divide within the Republican Party. ICE approval is 91% among MAGA Republicans compared with 59% among other Republicans, one of the widest gaps in the survey.

A similar split appears in ratings of Trump’s handling of immigration, with 91% of MAGA Republicans approving compared with 63% of other Republicans. MAGA Republicans are also nearly 30 points more likely to favor deporting all illegal immigrants (68% vs. 41%).

Conducted July 17–20, 2026, under the direction of Beacon Research (D) and Shaw & Company Research (R), this Fox News survey includes interviews with a sample of 1,003 registered voters randomly selected from a national voter file. Respondents spoke with live interviewers on landlines (70) and cellphones (681) or completed the survey online after receiving a text (252). Results based on the full sample have a margin of sampling error of ±3 percentage points. The survey includes an oversample of 200 voters aged 18–29, bringing the sample size for that subgroup to 350 (±5). Sampling error for results among subgroups is higher. In addition to sampling errors, question wording and order can influence results. Weights are generally applied to age, race, education, and area variables to ensure the demographics are representative of the registered voter population. Sources for developing weight targets include the most recent American Community Survey, Fox News Voter Analysis, and voter file data.

Fox News’ Victoria Balara contributed to this report.

As head of the polling unit, Dana Blanton runs the Fox News Poll and oversees the Fox News Voter Analysis election survey.

title: Fox News Poll: Significant support for both voter ID laws and mail-in ballots
excerpt:
Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich discusses the popularity of the 'SAVE America Act' and more on 'The Story.'

As Congress debates the SAVE America Act — which would require photo identification to v...
body_text:
Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich discusses the popularity of the 'SAVE America Act' and more on 'The Story.'

As Congress debates the SAVE America Act — which would require photo identification to vote in federal elections — the latest Fox News national survey shows broad support for that measure.

In a July 16 primetime speech, President Donald Trump urged Americans to contact lawmakers to support the legislation, saying it would address election system vulnerabilities. The act passed the U.S. House of Representatives in February, and is currently stalled in the Senate. The survey was conducted after his remarks (July 17–20, 2026).

The new poll, released Thursday, finds roughly 8 in 10 registered voters favor requiring photo identification to register to vote (83%) and to cast a ballot (77%). Support for requiring photo ID to vote has remained consistently high, between 77% to 85%, in Fox News polling since 2011.

FOX NEWS POLL: VOTERS WANT MAJOR CHANGE AMID ECONOMIC AND POLITICAL DISCONTENT

"It’s a testament to gridlock and partisan polarization that no one seems capable of crafting a legislative compromise that can win passage of a bill featuring policies where large majorities support them," says Republican pollster Daron Shaw, who conducts the Fox News Poll with Democrat Chris Anderson.

Backing for voter ID spans the political spectrum, though intensity varies. Nearly all Republicans think it should be mandatory (96% to register, 95% to vote), as do roughly 8 in 10 independents (83%, 77%). Democrats are a bit less supportive, with 72% backing photo ID to register and 62% to vote. Outside party lines, most groups favor photo ID, including Hispanic voters (85% to register, 76% to vote) and voters under age 30 (82%, 75%).

Looking toward the fall midterms, most are broadly confident eligible voters will be able to vote (76%), though fewer feel sure ineligible voters will be prevented from doing so (61%). Confidence in eligible ballot access is high across party lines, while Republicans are less certain than Democrats and independents about ineligible voting.

FOX NEWS POLL: AN EARLY LOOK AT THE GEORGIA SENATE RACE

Nearly two-thirds (64%) favor universal mail-in voting, holding steady since September 2025. Partisan divisions persist, as nearly 9 in 10 Democrats and 7 in 10 independents support mail-in voting, while about 6 in 10 Republicans oppose it.

Despite majority backing, views are divided on the accuracy of mail-in ballots. A narrow 51% majority is confident they will be counted accurately, while 48% disagree. Two-thirds of Democrats trust the accuracy of the count, while roughly 6 in 10 Republicans and independents think it’s unlikely.

Most also favor counting ballots postmarked by Election Day, even if they arrive later. Seven in 10 support the practice, including large majorities of Democrats (88%) and independents (74%), while closer to half of Republicans oppose (53%).

In June, the Supreme Court upheld a Mississippi law permitting late mail-in ballots to be counted if postmarked by Election Day.

Regardless of voting method, more express confidence that votes will be counted accurately in their state (64%) than at the national level (53%). Democrats trust both, while more Republicans and independents are assured by state tallies. In addition, by a 2-to-1 margin, more voters think state governments (68%) rather than the federal government (31%) should have election authority, a view shared across all major political groups.

Overall, 6 in 10 are confident the final vote count will be fair and accurate, even if completing the tally takes several days after Election Day. Roughly 7 in 10 Democrats are confident, while more than half of Republicans are not. Independents are divided (52% confident, 47% not).

Conducted July 17-20, 2026, under the direction of Beacon Research (D) and Shaw & Company Research (R), this Fox News survey includes interviews with a sample of 1,003 registered voters randomly selected from a national voter file. Respondents spoke with live interviewers on landlines (70) and cellphones (681) or completed the survey online after receiving a text (252). Results based on the full sample have a margin of sampling error of ±3 percentage points. The survey includes an oversample of 200 voters aged 18-29, bringing the sample size for that subgroup to 350 (±5). Sampling error for results among subgroups is higher. In addition to sampling error, question wording and order can influence results. Weights are generally applied to age, race, education, and area variables to ensure the demographics are representative of the registered voter population. Sources for developing weight targets include the most recent American Community Survey, Fox News Voter Analysis, and voter file data.

title: Lynx star Kayla McBride becomes latest to speak out on WNBA 3-point contest drama: ‘Half a-- invite’
excerpt:
Minnesota Lynx star Kayla McBride says the WNBA texted her agent just before the event, and considered it an unofficial invite. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

The WNBA may have a communica...
body_text:
Minnesota Lynx star Kayla McBride says the WNBA texted her agent just before the event, and considered it an unofficial invite. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

The WNBA may have a communication problem.

Minnesota Lynx star Kayla McBride, like New York Liberty star Sabrina Ionescu, was forced to set the record straight regarding her absence from the WNBA’s 3-point contest and aired out her thoughts on the WNBA’s "half-a--" invitation.

"Also, for those that are SO concerned. I received a half a-- invite to the 3 pt contest, thru my agent, in a text randomly 48 hours ago..not officially," McBride posted on her Instagram Story on Wednesday.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Kayla McBride of the Minnesota Lynx reacts during the second quarter against the Seattle Storm at Climate Pledge Arena on July 22, 2026, in Seattle, Wash. (Jack Compton/Getty Images)

"Sooo posting things like i’m afraid of shooting smoke is weird and if you didn’t hear it from me prob shouldn’t believe it ," she wrote with a shrug emoji.

She said the attention should instead be on the competition’s participants: Portland Fire’s Bridget Carleton, Dallas Wings’ Azzi Fudd, Seattle Storm’s Natisha Hiedeman, Atlanta Dream’s Rhyne Howard, Toronto Tempo’s Marina Mabrey and Golden State Valkyries’ Janelle Salaün.

LIBERTY'S SABRINA IONESCU ADDRESSES ABSENCE FROM WNBA 3-POINT CONTEST: 'NEVER OFFICIALLY GOT INVITED'

Minnesota Lynx guard Kayla McBride shoots the ball while guarded by Seattle Storm forward Katie Lou Samuelson during the first half at Climate Pledge Arena in Seattle, Wash., on July 20, 2026. (Steven Bisig/Imagn Images)

"We should focus on the great shooters in it already!!! because there’s some killas in there. good luck to all those participating and when y'all start doing shooting contests coming off pin downs hit me," McBride posted.

The 34-year-old has participated in three previous 3-point contests but has never won the event. She most recently participated in 2024.

Minnesota Lynx guard Kayla McBride reacts during the second half against the Seattle Storm at Climate Pledge Arena in Seattle, Wash., on July 22, 2026. (Stephen Brashear/Imagn Images)

Ionescu is the reigning champion and won two of the three 3-point contests. She said she never got officially invited and took issue with the idea she declined to participate.

McBride is in the midst of a career year, averaging 18.2 points per game through 28 games, which matches her career-best output from 2018.

The event is set to take place on Friday at 8 p.m. ET and will be without McBride, Ionescu and Caitlin Clark.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Ryan Canfield is a digital production assistant for Fox News Digital.

title: Heidi Klum ditches bikini top, covers up with potato chip bag on beach vacation
excerpt:
‘America’s Got Talent’ judge Heidi Klum tells Fox News Digital it’s ‘no big deal’ that she went topless during her vacation.

Heidi Klum is serving up sunshine and a cheeky snack.

The supermodel shar...
body_text:
‘America’s Got Talent’ judge Heidi Klum tells Fox News Digital it’s ‘no big deal’ that she went topless during her vacation.

Heidi Klum is serving up sunshine and a cheeky snack.

The supermodel shared a series of beach videos on Instagram showing herself soaking up the tropical scenery alongside one of her beloved dogs.

In one video, Klum lounged on the sand wearing an oversized woven sun hat, dark sunglasses and zebra-print bikini bottoms while using a bag of Chipsfrisch potato chips to cover her bare chest.

GISELE BÜNDCHEN CELEBRATES BIRTHDAY IN LEOPARD-PRINT BIKINI AS SHE REFLECTS ON GRATITUDE

Heidi Klum sits on a beach topless while holding a bag of potato chips. (Heidi Klum / Instagram )

"Glazed ham on the beach," Klum captioned the video. The chips appeared to be glazed ham-flavored.

The model's comments section lit up with fire and heart emojis.

"Baaaaabe sooo 🔥🔥🔥 🌶️🌶️🌶️," Nicole Scherzinger wrote, while others called Klum an "icon" and praised her beauty.

One fan wrote, "The chips got the best product placement of 2026."

In another video, Klum was seen wading through crystal-clear turquoise water with her fluffy white dog.

She lifted the pup high above her head toward the sky with her back to the camera. The former Victoria's Secret model eventually turned and appeared topless while sharing a cuddle with her pet as her wide-brimmed hat shaded much of her face.

The Instagram post simply read, "Finally," alongside an island and paw-print emoji.

SYDNEY SWEENEY SHARES BIKINI AND UNDERWEAR PHOTOS IN INSTAGRAM DUMP FROM AUSTRALIA TRIP

Heidi Klum took a dip in the ocean with her dog. The model went topless, wearing just zebra print bikini bottoms and a hat. (Heidi Klum / Instagram)

The carefree beach outing comes after Klum partnered with German snack brand funny-frisch to launch a special line of Chipsfrisch by Heidi Klum potato chips, making the bag she used as a playful cover-up a fitting nod to the collaboration.

Klum's getaway comes just weeks after the supermodel opened up about embracing her body and tuning out Hollywood's growing weight-loss craze.

SUPERMODEL SLAMS GLP-1 TREND, SAYS WEIGHT LOSS SHOTS ARE A 'SMACK IN THE FACE' TO BODY POSITIVITY MOVEMENT

Earlier this month, the supermodel revealed she has no interest in jumping on the Ozempic bandwagon, saying husband Tom Kaulitz helped her ditch the fashion industry's long-standing pressure to be thinner.

"I'm not interested at all," Klum told Us Weekly when asked about weight-loss drugs.

Heidi Klum and Tom Kaulitz tied the knot in 2019. (Getty Images)

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Instead, Klum said she's embraced the changes that have come with age.

"I've gained weight over the years. I'm no longer a size 24 jean. But I think with age, we look better when we're not as skinny."

Heidi Klum walks the runway at the Victoria's Secret Fashion Show 2008 in Miami Beach, Florida. (Reuters)

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

The 53-year-old said it was Kaulitz who first challenged the modeling world's beauty standards.

"My husband was the first one who pointed that out. He said, 'You should eat more; you would look better if you had more meat on your bones.' I was like, 'What?' This is not something you're told in the industry, ever, because you're always supposed to be skinnier than you are."

Klum admitted she eventually agreed with her husband.

"When I look back at photos, I'm like, 'He's right!' Proportion-wise, I look better bigger."

Klum and the German guitarist secretly tied the knot in 2019 before celebrating with a lavish wedding aboard the superyacht Christina O. off the coast of Capri, Italy.

Stephanie Giang-Paunon is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Suspect in custody after 2 men wounded in possible 'antisemitic stabbing' attacks: officials
excerpt:
In the backdrop of conflict in the Middle East, synagogues and Jewish spaces are on high alert for any potential threats. Magen Am is a Jewish-run nonprofit focused on community security.

A suspect i...
body_text:
In the backdrop of conflict in the Middle East, synagogues and Jewish spaces are on high alert for any potential threats. Magen Am is a Jewish-run nonprofit focused on community security.

A suspect is in custody after two men were reportedly stabbed in Manhattan's Upper West Side Thursday afternoon, the New York City Police Department (NYPD) told Fox News Digital.

City officials described the violence as an "antisemitic stabbing," though police have not yet identified a motive or said whether the attacks are being investigated as a hate crime.

"I am absolutely sickened and furious by what appears to be an antisemitic stabbing on the Upper West Side," New York City Council Speaker Julie Menin wrote in a post on X.

The stabbings happened near West 84th Street and Central Park West, an area with a large Jewish community.

NYC ANTISEMITIC INCIDENTS NEARLY TRIPLE DESPITE OTHER CRIMES REACHING RECORD LOWS

FILE - Julie Menin, speaker of the New York City Council, during an announcement in the Brooklyn borough of New York, US, on Monday, Jan. 12, 2026. (John Lamparski/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

According to the NYPD, the two men were stabbed in what appeared to be separate attacks nearby.

A 57-year-old man was reportedly attacked near West 84th Street and Central Park West just after 1:30 p.m. local time.

Officials said officers found him conscious and alert with a stab wound to his back. He was taken to a local hospital in stable condition.

Investigators later found a 50-year-old man nearby with a stab wound to his torso. He was also conscious and alert, and was taken to a nearby hospital in stable condition.

CALLS FOR US TO DO MORE AS ANTISEMITIC ACTS SKYROCKET IN EUROPE: 'ENORMOUSLY PAINFUL'

FILE - An Israeli flag is fixed to a street sign as police stand by on June 1, 2025. (ELI IMADALI/AFP via Getty Images)

WABC reported that police are investigating whether the suspect used a knife or a screwdriver during the alleged attacks.

"While we await additional details to be confirmed, we know that what is happening in our city is abhorrent," Menin said. "The fear in the Jewish community is real. Every New Yorker deserves to feel safe in their neighborhood, regardless of their faith or identity."

"We must remain united in condemning antisemitism and ensuring those responsible are held fully accountable," she added. "I thank the NYPD for its swift response in apprehending the assailant."

FILE - Assembly member Micah Lasher speaks during a primary election night watch party in New York, US, on Tuesday, June 23, 2026. (John Lamparski/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

New York Assembly member Micah Lasher, a Jewish representative of the area, said he is also in contact with investigators.

"I am in touch with the NYPD and community leaders about the stabbing of two individuals on the Upper West Side today," Lasher said. "The NYPD responded immediately and has apprehended a suspect. I am praying for the survival and recovery of the victims and will provide more information as I have it."

This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

Bonny Chu is a Breaking and Trending News Writer for Fox News Digital

title: USA Today columnist who slammed Caitlin Clark declared US greed would ruin World Cup
excerpt:
‘Outnumbered’ panelists discuss the White House’s reaction to World Cup attendees embracing American culture and praising their hospitable experiences around the country.

USA Today columnist Nancy Ar...
body_text:
‘Outnumbered’ panelists discuss the White House’s reaction to World Cup attendees embracing American culture and praising their hospitable experiences around the country.

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour, who faced criticism Thursday over her commentary about WNBA star Caitlin Clark, had declared last month before the World Cup began that American greed and hostility had already cost the United States its opportunity to showcase the country, citing ticket prices and entry restrictions.

"The U.S. men’s national team could make a historically deep run and the tournament could be filled with wondrous goals and compelling games," Armour said. "It will not change the fact that we have shown ourselves to be a hateful and greedy nation, one where leaders are only willing to welcome a world as they want to see it rather than as it exists, and the love of the game is exploited for top dollar."

Armour predicted that the tournament would be remembered for the country's conduct instead of the competition and its ability to bring people together.

TRUMP ADMIN TAUNTS LEFT FOR ‘PRAYING’ FOR WORLD CUP DISASTER AS DOT TOUTS RECORD NUMBERS

USA Today columnist Nancy Armour predicted American greed and hostility would ruin the 2026 World Cup. (Etienne Laurent/AFP)

"That will be the legacy, and the shame, of this World Cup," Armour said. "This is a once-in-a-generation opportunity to showcase the best of the United States and embrace the joy that soccer brings the rest of the world and, instead, we chose to be ugly Americans."

The columnist criticized visa restrictions affecting Iran's team and the denial of entry to Somali referee Omar Abdulkadir Artan. She argued that U.S. entry restrictions conflicted with the country’s responsibility to welcome international participants and fans.

"The U.S. is supposed to be a World Cup host, not a World Cup bouncer," Armour said.

Armour’s columns received renewed attention after Barstool Sports founder Dave Portnoy and other critics condemned her on Thursday for invoking Emmett Till while arguing that Clark’s conduct toward WNBA officials endangered Black and queer players.

"It seems there is a White nationalist element to Clark’s fandom that is deeply, deeply problematic," Armour said.

WORLD CUP CHAOS AVOIDED BY TRUMP MOVES HE DIDN’T MAKE, ‘EQUALLY AS IMPORTANT’ AS ACTIONS TAKEN: TRAVEL EXEC

The World Cup drew nearly 7 million spectators despite Armour’s warning that the tournament would showcase an "ugly" America. (Olivianna Calmes)

The FIFA World Cup concluded Sunday after 104 matches, including 78 in the United States. Nearly 7 million people attended matches, breaking the tournament attendance record, while FIFA reported 99.7% capacity through the semifinals. More than 8.5 million people visited official fan festivals across the three host countries, according to FIFA.

The final between Spain and Argentina drew nearly 63 million U.S. viewers across Fox, Telemundo and Peacock, Reuters reported. Fox averaged 7.74 million viewers throughout the tournament, up 116% from 2022, while Telemundo averaged 6.3 million, a 143% increase.

Bank of America estimated that the World Cup generated approximately $40 billion in global economic activity, with about $20 billion occurring in the United States. The bank found faster spending growth in host cities, particularly at hotels, restaurants, bars and stores, The Independent reported.

CDC REVEALS MASSIVE BEHIND-THE-SCENES PUBLIC HEALTH OPERATION THAT KEPT FIFA WORLD CUP FANS SAFE

Armour’s World Cup column received renewed attention after criticism of her commentary about WNBA star Caitlin Clark. (Getty Images)

"It’s having this on-the-ground economic impact, and that spending is going into what we call bricks-and-mortar — going to bars and restaurants and things like that — not necessarily only the people in the stadium," Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan said in a CBS interview with Margaret Brennan.

Houston recorded more than 439,000 international airport arrivals during the tournament, up more than 2% from the previous year, while hotel revenue rose nearly 15%. Visitors interviewed by Axios praised the city's hospitality, food and diversity.

"People arrived in the United States, they arrived in Texas, they arrived in Houston, and it was a much different experience than what maybe they had originally thought, and in a very, very favorable way," Houston First CEO Michael Heckman said.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

A U.S. Travel Association survey of 2,205 adults found that 81% felt proud seeing international visitors enjoy the country, 86% felt joy watching them experience America, and 70% supported hosting additional global events. Half said positive visitor experiences build international goodwill and cooperation, according to the survey.

"The World Cup reintroduced America to the world. Our best ambassadors aren’t politicians or headlines — they’re the Americans visitors met, the communities they explored and the people who made them feel welcome," U.S. Travel Association President Geoff Freeman said.

CJ Womack is an associate editor at Fox News.

CJ joined Fox News Digital's team in 2026, which highlights the vital role journalism plays in shaping politics and culture. He has years of experience analyzing and reporting on the news media.

CJ graduated from Long Beach State University in 2025 with a Bachelor of Arts in Political Science and a minor in Journalism.

You can send news tips to cj.womack@fox.com and follow CJ on Twitter.

title: D4vd lawyers challenge police testing of evidence found in singer’s home
excerpt:
Defense attorneys question LAPD criminalist on third day of hearing to decide whether singer will stand trial

Attorneys for the singer D4vd scrutinized how law enforcement officials chose to test evi...
body_text:
Defense attorneys question LAPD criminalist on third day of hearing to decide whether singer will stand trial

Attorneys for the singer D4vd scrutinized how law enforcement officials chose to test evidence found in his home during the third day of a hearing to determine whether he will stand trial for murder and child sexual abuse.

Prosecutors say D4vd, born David Anthony Burke, fatally stabbed 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez last year to prevent her from exposing the sexual nature of their relationship amid his rising profile in the music world.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges of first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under 14, and mutilation of human remains.

Earlier in the week, prosecutors presented evidence that possible traces of blood were detected in areas of Burke’s former Hollywood Hills residence and his Tesla.

On Thursday, defense attorney Blair Berk questioned Lauren Wallace, a criminalist for the Los Angeles police department, about why certain items, including chainsaws located in the musician’s garage, were not tested.

Berk appeared to suggest that another individual’s DNA could have been detected.

“You were aware that multiple people lived at this residence, right?” she asked Wallace, in reference to the Hollywood Hills address, where the singer resided.

The exchange previewed how Burke’s lawyers might aim to poke holes in the prosecution’s case should it proceed to trial.

On Wednesday, Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, testified about a Caltrans worker finding a passport bearing Rivas Hernandez’s name and photograph in January 2026 on a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara. Buenrostro arrived on scene after the worker called law enforcement about the identification card.

During cross-examination, defense attorney Regina Peter appeared to cast doubt on possible theories of how the passport landed there: “Did you do anything to rule out the possibility that the card arrived there by means of some sort of storm or weather issue?” she asked.

Prosecutors brought a detective to the stand on Thursday who testified that cellular data and a Tesla system showed Burke traveled to the area where Rivas Hernandez’s passport was discovered.

The hearing is expected to conclude by early next week.

The Associated Press contributed reporting

title: Black bear caught on video wandering into California water district office after pushing open front door
excerpt: A curious black bear pushed open the front door of a Southern California water district office, wandered inside to sniff around as stunned employees watched, then calmly let itself out and returned to...
body_text:
A curious black bear pushed open the front door of a Southern California water district office, wandered inside to sniff around as stunned employees watched, then calmly let itself out and returned to the woods. (Crestline Village Water District)

A black bear wandered into a Southern California water district office Wednesday after pushing open the front door, briefly exploring the building before walking back into the surrounding forest.

Surveillance video from the Crestline Village Water District in the San Bernardino Mountains captured the small black bear entering the office Wednesday afternoon as employees watched the unexpected encounter unfold.

The bear spent several moments sniffing around the office and wandering through the building before returning to the entrance, pushing the door open again and leaving on its own.

BEAR CAUGHT ON CAMERA PERCHED ATOP NEW MEXICO UTILITY POLE BEFORE REPORTEDLY SUFFERING FATAL ELECTROCUTION

A black bear pushes open the front door as it leaves the Crestline Village Water District office in California on Wednesday. (Crestline Village Water District)

"It was exciting. It was really cool to see a bear that close. It’s rare that we’re that close to them," Crestline Village Water District employee Josselyn Brian told KTLA. "Thankfully, no one was in danger. There were no customers in there at the time, it was just us. So it couldn’t have been better. Scary, but wonderful."

Employees also told the outlet they watched as the bear wandered back into the woods after leaving the office. No injuries were reported, and the animal did not appear to damage the building.

The encounter is the latest unusual bear incident to capture attention this week.

HIDDEN DANGER AT POPULAR US NATIONAL PARK FORCES TRAIL CLOSURES AS BEAR SIGHTINGS CLIMB

Watch: Black bear pushes open office door before wandering inside. (Crestline Village Water District)

Earlier this week, a bear in rural New Mexico climbed to the top of a utility pole near Gladstone, about 170 miles east of Santa Fe, in an encounter that was captured on video and later shared by the U.S. Department of the Interior.

"We appreciate the enthusiasm for American Energy Dominance, but this isn't what we meant," the department joked in a social media post accompanying the footage.

The video, verified by Storyful, also included audio from a 911 caller reporting the unusual sight.

WOMAN STALKED BY CHARGING BEAR ON MORNING DOG WALK CAPTURES TERRIFYING ENCOUNTER ON CAMERA

A black bear wanders through the lobby of the Crestline Village Water District office in California after entering the building. (Crestline Village Water District)

"I'm calling to report a bear on top of a light pole," the caller told a dispatcher.

The dispatcher said wildlife officials had already been notified but could not safely tranquilize the animal while it remained atop the pole because it could fall.

The bear later died after being electrocuted before wildlife officials could safely rescue it, the New York Post reported.

Wildlife experts say black bears are strong climbers and may scale trees, utility poles or other tall structures when startled by people, traffic or dogs.

In mountain communities like Crestline, where bears regularly roam neighborhoods in search of food, encounters with people are not uncommon, though one wandering into an office building is considerably more unusual.

Brittany Miller is a Fox News Digital breaking news writer and former Independent reporter who graduated from Syracuse University in 2023.

title: TNA Wrestling's Elayna Black vows to be on upcoming Lockdown card, eyes Xia Brookside's title
excerpt:
TNA Wrestling star Elayna Black talks to Fox News Digital about the upcoming Lockdown PPV and her goal to be champion.

Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) will host its Lockdown pay-per-view at the ...
body_text:
TNA Wrestling star Elayna Black talks to Fox News Digital about the upcoming Lockdown PPV and her goal to be champion.

Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) will host its Lockdown pay-per-view at the Credit Union 1 Arena in Chicago on Aug. 23.

Elayna Black, a Chicago native, vowed to be on the card for the show in which every match will be held inside a steel cage. Black told Fox News Digital she enjoyed being in those types of matches.

COMPLETE PRO WRESTLING COVERAGE ON FOX NEWS DIGITAL

Elayna Black enters the ring at Slammiversary 2026. (TNA Wrestling)

"I definitely am going to be on that card," Black said. "One way or another we will make that happen. I am definitely not going to be having TNA be in Chicago, my hometown, my city, and not be on the show. So, we definitely will make that happen. But I'm very excited. Every match on that show is going to be a steel cage match.

"If you've been a fan of me for a while, you know that's my cup of tea. I love that. I grew up a fan of like hardcore wrestling. Jon Moxley was one of my favorite wrestlers. I love doing hardcore matches. I did a deathmatch right before I got signed. I did the WarGames match. That's my cup of tea. I love that type of stuff."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Elayna Black attempts to pin Indi Hartwell at Slammiversary 2026. (TNA Wrestling)

Black has also targeted Knockouts champion Xia Brookside.

She made clear that Brookside needs to watch her back as the days keep getting closer to Lockdown. Brookside won the championship over Lei Ying Lee at Slammiversary.

"So, No. 1, steel cage match. No. 2 in Chicago. And No. 3, Xia Brookside has a target on her back," Black told Fox News Digital. "That's all I'm going to say. So, we'll see. I have a little bit of extra motivation being on that show. If I got my friends and family there, I'm going to have a little more oomph in me that day.

Elayna Black plays to the crowd at Slammiversary 2026. (TNA Wrestling)

"So, all I'm going to say is, Xia Brookside, whatever you got going on that day, watch your back because you never know."

Ryan Gaydos is a senior editor for Fox News Digital.

title: Trump warns of largest strikes on Iran yet as Tehran lashes out across Gulf
excerpt: United States President Donald Trump says he may launch the largest strikes on Iran yet after Tehran carried out another wave of retaliatory attacks on US allies in the Gulf, including hitting a telec...
body_text:
United States President Donald Trump says he may launch the largest strikes on Iran yet after Tehran carried out another wave of retaliatory attacks on US allies in the Gulf, including hitting a telecommunications tower in Kuwait.

In remarks to US news website Axios on Thursday, Trump said he was “considering a massive attack” that would be “bigger than ever before”.

“I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it,” Trump said.

The threat raises the stakes yet again in a weeks-long military confrontation in which the US has attacked Iran for 12 straight nights, including hitting bridges and tunnels, according to Iranian media reports.

Iran’s retaliatory attacks have rippled throughout much of the Gulf, sparking fears of a worsening energy crisis.

The spiralling conflict has all but destroyed an interim peace deal signed by the US and Iran last month.

Iranian Minister of Foreign Affairs Abbas Araghchi warned on Wednesday that Tehran would retaliate “an eye for an eye” against any future attacks, including those on Iran’s infrastructure.

After another night of US attacks on Wednesday, Iran responded with missile and drone attacks on Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait on Thursday.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the attacks in Jordan and Kuwait targeted and hit US military assets.

The IRGC also acknowledged attacking a telecommunications tower in Kuwait, saying it was in retaliation for US attacks on telecommunications towers in Iran.

In a statement addressing the Kuwaiti people directly, the IRGC insisted that the US, not Iran, had breached the Gulf state’s “territorial integrity and sovereignty”.

Later on Thursday, Kuwait said that its Abdali border crossing with Iraq had also come under attack by “hostile drones”.

“The armed forces’ retaliatory attacks will continue as long as the US attacks on the country’s infrastructure and coastal areas continue,” said the Iranian army’s spokesperson, Mohammad Akraminia.

Meanwhile, Yemen’s Houthi rebels further expanded the confrontation on Thursday by firing at two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea, leading to broad international condemnation and a spike in global oil prices.

“The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable,” United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres told a meeting of the Security Council. “One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next.”

The Houthis have declared a blockade of Saudi Arabian ports, destabilising a second key global shipping route: the Bab al-Mandeb Strait.

The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively shut since the US and Israel launched the war on Iran in late February.

Reporting from Sanaa, Yemen, Al Jazeera’s Yousef Mawry cited Houthi sources as saying that the facilities of Saudi Arabia’s national oil company Aramco “could become a future target” of the Houthis if Riyadh does not lift its own blockade on Houthi-controlled ports.

The international oil benchmark Brent crude surged past $100 a barrel on Thursday for the first time in May, in what analysts said could have an even greater impact on the global economy.

“Much of the world’s spare production capacity has already been used, while strategic and commercial oil inventories are lower than when the war began, leaving the market with fewer buffers against a prolonged supply disruption,” said Janiv Shah, vice president of the Rystad Energy consultancy.

title: US-Saudi nuclear deal hinges on normalisation with Israel
excerpt:
US-Saudi nuclear deal hinges on normalisation with Israel

President Trump said a proposed US-Saudi civilian nuclear deal is “totally subject” to Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, which establ...
body_text:
US-Saudi nuclear deal hinges on normalisation with Israel

President Trump said a proposed US-Saudi civilian nuclear deal is “totally subject” to Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, which establishes diplomatic ties with Israel. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said negotiations are ongoing.

title: ICE arrests Southwest Airlines flight attendant in Nashville for overstaying visa since 2021
excerpt: President Donald Trump deploys ICE agents to U.S. airports to address extensive security delays and worker shortages, which are impacting spring break travel nationwide. TSA employees are facing zero-...
body_text:
President Donald Trump deploys ICE agents to U.S. airports to address extensive security delays and worker shortages, which are impacting spring break travel nationwide. TSA employees are facing zero-dollar paychecks for over 37 days due to the partial government shutdown. Administration officials like Tom Homan and Sean Duffy clarify that ICE agents will assist with non-specialized tasks to reduce passenger wait times and maintain security.

A Southwest Airlines flight attendant who allegedly overstayed his visa was arrested by federal immigration authorities earlier this month while at work, officials said.

Lorenzo Thompson, a Jamaican citizen, was arrested on July 14 in Nashville, Tennessee, according to local reports.

Thompson first entered the United States on April 17, 2021, on a visa that expired on Oct. 16, 2021, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) told Fox News.

MAN FLEEING ICE OFFICERS IN FLORIDA IS STRUCK AND KILLED BY TRACTOR-TRAILER, POLICE SAY

Interior of passenger airplane, Boeing 737-MAX 8 flying for Southwest Airlines. (iStock)

"Against our nation’s laws, he overstayed his visa and failed to depart. He will remain in ICE custody pending his immigration proceedings," an ICE spokesperson said.

The agency noted that illegal immigrants are offered $2,600 and a free flight to self-deport from the U.S.

"We encourage every person here illegally to take advantage of this offer and reserve the chance to come back to the U.S. the right legal way to live the American dream," the agency said. "If not, you will be arrested and deported without a chance to return."

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION ORDERS GREEN CARD APPLICANTS TO LEAVE THE US, APPLY FROM THEIR HOME COUNTRIES

A Southwest Airlines 737 comes in for landing at Portland International Airport. A Southwest flight attendant was arrested last week for allegedly overstaying his visa. (iStock)

TWU Local 556, the union that represents Southwest Airlines flight attendants, also confirmed the arrest.

"Every Member deserves to be treated with dignity and respect, and we are committed to ensuring this Member and their family know they are not alone during this difficult time," the union wrote on Facebook.

The Labor Council for Latin American Advancement (LCLAA) said Thompson was working toward obtaining U.S. citizenship.

TRUMP PUTS ASYLUM LAWYERS ‘ON NOTICE’ WITH NEW FRONT IN WAR ON FRAUD

"He had a valid work visa and was a dedicated flight attendant, touching countless lives with his kindness," the group wrote on social media.

An online crowdfunding page launched to cover Thompson's legal bills said he left Jamaica "seeking safety from life-threatening abuse."

United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents are present as passengers wait in line at a Transportation Security Administration (TSA) checkpoint after hundreds of Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents were ordered to deploy to airports to help fill TSA staffing gaps, at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport in Atlanta, March 23, 2026. (REUTERS/Alyssa Pointer)

"Since arriving, he has worked tirelessly to build a stable life, following every legal step toward citizenship," the page states. "He has no criminal record. No parking tickets. He holds a valid work visa, has a pending asylum case, and is a dedicated flight attendant for a beloved airline."

The page described him as "honest, caring, and loyal, always ready to lend a hand without expecting anything back."

Fox News Digital has reached out to the airline for comment.

Louis Casiano is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to louis.casiano@fox.com.

title: Arch Manning isn’t backing down from expectations, and neither is Steve Sarkisian, as CFP semis aren't enough
excerpt:
Trey Wallace explains why Lane Kiffin has such lofty expectations put on him for his first year of coaching at LSU.

TAMPA, FL - Taking the podium to an emptying out ballroom on Thursday afternoon at ...
body_text:
Trey Wallace explains why Lane Kiffin has such lofty expectations put on him for his first year of coaching at LSU.

TAMPA, FL - Taking the podium to an emptying out ballroom on Thursday afternoon at SEC Media Days, Steve Sarkisian did not shy away from the CFP expectations surrounding his Texas Longhorns this season.

After an early portion of the summer was seemingly dedicated to ruffling feathers within his own state of Texas, along with touching on the intricate courses of 'basketweaving' at Ole Miss, the attention finally turned to the lofty expectations that await the Longhorns this season.

Lane Kiffin goes off script: Hulk Hogan, Ole Miss and social media dominate SEC Media Days

This time last year, the conversation centered around the unrealistic preseason hype around his quarterback

"Arch Manning a year ago wasn’t on social media or television saying he was going to win the Heisman Trophy, or be the first pick in the draft," Steve Sarkisian said about the hype last year around Arch. "What he got put through would’ve melted 99% of the people in his shoes..

"What he got put through would’ve ruined 95% of the people in his shoes."

Texas Longhorns head coach Steve Sarkisian walks into Camping World Stadium ahead of the Citrus Bowl against the Michigan Wolverines in Orlando, Fla., on Dec. 31, 2025. (aAustin American-Statesman via Getty Images)

Some decided to put him in the Heisman Trophy conversation before he had actually taken meaningful snaps for the Longhorns, while others (hey there) kept the expectations at a reasonable position.

Even Arch was the one who had to remind reporters last year in Atlanta that he had not accomplished anything at Texas worthy enough to have him on the constant preseason awards watch list.

But now, after a second half progression last season for the Longhorns, Manning is worthy enough to be labeled a Heisman Trophy contender. It also helps that Steve Sarkisian loaded up in the transfer portal with talent to surround his quarterback on the offensive side of the ball, led by wide receiver Cam Coleman.

"Manning is a lot more confident," Sarkisian said about Arch. "I think last season, he found out a lot about himself. Any time we're face with adversity, and we got on the other side of it, you gain confidence. I think that you see a different leader in him, in the way he interacts with his teammates.

"He's very comfortable with who he is. He's not trying to be something that he's not."

Florida coach brings laughs, no filter and Lane Kiffin comparison rebuttal to first SEC media days

If there was ever a time to put Sarkisian on a list of coaches who needed to live up to the expectations, this would be the perfect opportunity.

"The expectations are a lot higher in our building than they are on the outiside," Steve Sarkisian told reporters.

Sure about that, Sark? Because most folks are expecting at the very least a CFP semifinal appearance this season.

Compared to last year's meeting with the media in Atlanta, this was obviously a different quarterback, with what seems like an urge to prove the doubters from last season wrong.

"No, there's nothing you can do. Everybody has an opinion and gets paid to talk in the media," Manning mentioned when asked about the 'bust' narrative last season. "I'm trying to control the controllables. But, it probably lights a little fire in me, and continue to try and prove myself one week at a time."

Pete Golding drops unfiltered truth on 'Ex' Lane Kiffin, and NCAA tampering: 'What have we created?'

Knowing his background, Arch will do everything in his power to prove some of those who doubted him wrong. But, he's not doing it for anyone outside of that Texas locker room.

Arch Manning visits with reporters at SEC Media Days in Tampa, Florida (Trey Wallace/FOX NEWS)

What we saw on Thursday was a young man who has grown over the past two years. Even if that did not meet the timeline for some, Arch has the character and purpose to make sure he is at least giving the Longhorns everything possible to compete for a national championship this season.

Last year, Manning looked like a quarterback who might have the best media-prep in college football, but understood he was going to need time to develop into the leader, and player, Steve Sarkisian needed him to be in Austin.

Joking about his golf game, finally breaking 80 last week, mentioning that he has taken the time to just enjoy life during the off-season, this is a player who is not getting bent out of shape over the things he cannot control.

"The best Manning golfer? It's probably Peyton, yea," Arch responded with a smile about family golf outings.

He's most certainly grown up over the past year, that's obvious. But, acknowledging his mistakes and outcomes from the previous season is another sign of his maturity.

"Yea, I had a few games like that last year," Arch said about the Florida game last season. "

"We do a good job about not talking about NIL," Arch mentioned. "I don't know how it is at other schools, that's big for us. It's about the players, and not worrying about how much someone makes."

Right now, Steve Sarkisian can talk about the amount of tough games that await in the 2026 season, but the expectations inside and outside the program are for this Texas team to compete for a championship.

Now, sometimes the conference champion is usually a good indicator of national championship hopes. For Steve Sarkisian, getting past Georgia is a stepping-stone to taking the next step as a program.

Texas quarterback Arch Manning (16) warms up before an NCAA college football game Mississippi State in Starkville, Mississippi, Saturday, Oct. 25, 2025. (AP Photo/James Pugh)

I'm not implying one has to win the SEC, or Big Ten and any other conference, to win the CFP.

But, Sarkisian understands that reaching the mountain top means that you probably need to take care of the reigning champion beforehand.

"If you want to be the champion, you've got to beat the champion. We've lost to Georgia 3 times. Credit to Kirby, they've done a great job. If we want to be the champion, I'm guessing we're probably going to have to beat the champion."

Trey Wallace is the Sr. College Sports Reporter for OutKick.

title: Michael J. Fox says going public with Parkinson's was 'hell' on wife Tracy Pollan
excerpt:
Deborah W. Brooks told Fox News Digital the foundation has "committed over three billion dollars worth of research worldwide."

Michael J. Fox is opening up about how going public with his battle with...
body_text:
Deborah W. Brooks told Fox News Digital the foundation has "committed over three billion dollars worth of research worldwide."

Michael J. Fox is opening up about how going public with his battle with Parkinson's has affected not only him, but his loved ones.

As part of the third annual Social Impact Summit, hosted by The Hollywood Reporter and Social Impact Fund ,the 65-year-old actor went into detail about why he chose to go public with his Parkinson's diagnosis, and the toll that decision had, and continues to have on his family, particularly his wife, Tracy Pollan.

"It was hell on Tracy, it was really hard on her — and it still is, because it’s always changing," he told The Hollywood Reporter. "But I said to her, ‘I have to go all the way here. No half measures.’ I started to understand what it was like for these people who didn’t have the options or the choices I had, didn’t have the connections and the reason to be optimistic...I needed to normalize it and just be that all the time. I said, ‘I have to not hide this. F— vanity.’"

The actor was first diagnosed with the degenerative disease in 1991 at the age of 29, keeping his diagnosis a secret for seven years, before going public in 1998 during the third season of his hit show, "Spin City."

Fox said his decision to go public with his Parkinson's diagnosis was "hell" for his wife. (Jamie McCarthy/Getty Images for The Michael J. Fox Foundation)

VINCE GILL GAVE AMY GRANT A REALITY CHECK DURING RECOVERY FROM BRAIN INJURY

After one more season on the show, Fox chose to retire from acting in order to devote his time to advocacy and fundraising for Parkinson's disease. It was then that he founded the Michael J. Fox Foundation in 2000, which is dedicated towards finding a cure.

Since its inception, the foundation has generated more than $3 billion in research, which has left Fox and Deborah W. Brooks, Co-Founder and CEO of the foundation, optimistic about where the research is headed.

WATCH: Co-founder of The Michael J. Fox Foundation is proud of what they have achieved

"Well, it's been the best state for Parkinson's research ever," Brooks said. "And in large part, so much of that progress has taken place in the course of our organization's existence. Not that we're responsible for everything, but we've scaled so significantly and have been able to activate scientists globally. And that is showing in the results and the pace of progress across our field."

She added: "We have now committed over three billion dollars worth of research worldwide, and so we're working with scientists everywhere, and we work with academics, biotechs and pharma. Our goal is to take aha moments and get them to the drugstore shelf. And so it's been that momentum and particularly a recent breakthrough in how we can detect Parkinson's even before symptoms are the kind of things that tell us. We are onto it, and we are not giving up."

Brooks credits Fox for his courage to live his life with Parkinson's in front of the world. (Axelle/Bauer-Griffin/FilmMagic)

Brooks credits Fox for his "courage and willingness to live his Parkinson's experience in front of the world," calling it "empowering for patients who face similar experiences in reducing stigma."

She also recalled her first board meeting at the foundation, noting it was clear Fox was not one to "just to put his name on something and hope for the best," and that his goal has always been "to do everything it took and get this done and close our doors."

WATCH: Deborah W. Brooks commends Michael J. Fox for his 'courage' to live openly with Parkinson's

"His purity of motive has been core to helping us think how what we're going to prioritize. It shapes our values in terms of speed. Something like trying to cure diseases, that's not a time-neutral agenda item for people who have degenerative and progressive disease. And so I think, you know, this notion that someone puts their voice out or lends their name, it's always been deeper than that for him. And I see that a lot more in philanthropy in general today, but it has been a game changer. For raising awareness, of course, but it's also been a game changer to put those values to work in our mission to cure Parkinson's disease."

Brooks has seen firsthand how Fox's decision to go public with his diagnosis has helped others, saying she gets emotional thinking about his interactions with other patients, adding, "It is so powerful – an experience to be seen and felt when you share the experience of Parkinson's disease."

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Fox initially retired from acting in 1998, but has since returned and is now nominated for an Emmy. (Savion Washington/FilmMagic)

While the "Back to the Future" star initially retired from acting in 1998, he returned in his first major on-screen role in 2004 for a guest appearance in "Scrubs," later starring in "The Good Wife," "Boston Legal" and "Curb Your Enthusiasm."

"It’s really prolonged my life and made my life interesting in a way that I didn’t think it would be to this point," Fox told The Hollywood Reporter. "I’m not retired from acting, but if I didn’t act again, it would be for a very good reason. I let Parkinson’s make decisions for me."

WATCH: Deborah W. Brooks gets choked up when thinking of Michael J. Fox interacting with patients

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Most recently, he received an Emmy nomination for his guest role on the hit Apple TV + show, "Shrinking," in which he played a man dealing with Parkinson's who meets Harrison Ford's character, who has also been diagnosed with the disease, in a doctor's office.

"He was happy that I was sharing my experience with him as a character and helping him do what he needed to do," he explained. "He was so loving. I didn’t know what to expect with Harrison because he’s famously a stoic, curmudgeonly guy — and he’s really a sweetheart. He was so affectionate to me and so welcoming to me."

Fox guest starred on "Shrinking," portraying a patient with Parkinson's who bonded with Harrison Ford's character. (Tommaso Boddi/WireImage)

Michael J. Fox and John Mayer attend the Third Annual Social Impact Summit at DGA Theatre on July 22 in Los Angeles. (Monica Schipper/Getty Images)

Fox and Brooks received the Philanthropic Organization of the Year award at this year's Social Impact Summit. Other honorees included John Mayer, who received the Philanthropist of the Year Award, and Stephen Curry and Ayesha Curry, who received the Philanthropists of the Year Award.

Lori A. Bashian is an entertainment news writer at Fox News Digital.

title: Louisiana House candidate accused of rape says he hopes to again ‘earn’ Trump endorsement
excerpt:
Republican Blake Miguez is weighing what district to run in after state redrew map following supreme court decision

A congressional candidate from Louisiana who was the subject of a police report acc...
body_text:
Republican Blake Miguez is weighing what district to run in after state redrew map following supreme court decision

A congressional candidate from Louisiana who was the subject of a police report accusing him of rape – which he denies – says he is hoping to again “earn” Donald Trump’s endorsement as he considers precisely where to run amid redistricting.

The candidate, Blake Miguez, is weighing running in one of two districts, neither of which he lives in but which he is allowed to run. He has been mulling that choice after the supreme court’s landmark decision in Callais v Louisiana prompted the state to swiftly redraw its US House districts – and after he had secured the president’s backing under the previous congressional map.

Approached on Wednesday after Miguez’s campaign website had become password protected and otherwise blank, a spokesperson said: “Blake was honored to have President Trump’s endorsement for [an] old … congressional district that no longer exists” after Callais, “and [he] hopes to earn it again after he announces a campaign under the new district lines.”

A White House spokesperson clarified that Trump did not formally rescind his endorsement – but added that the president “will look at the candidate field after candidate [qualifying] if necessary”.

“At this point, [Trump] has not made any changes nor does he have an intent to,” the spokesperson said. “If new information changes things, will cross that bridge when we get to it.”

The Republican Louisiana state senator secured the coveted backing of Trump in the race for a US House seat as he qualified to run in a 16 May primary for the post, which is safely held by their party. But that primary was postponed until the November midtermsafter the Callais decision.

Miguez’s campaign has also had to grapple with an Atlantic report that he had not disclosed to Trump’s team the existence of a 2007 rape accusation reported to local law enforcement on the same day of the alleged assault.

That police report – which the Guardian also independently obtained and reported on – never resulted in charges, and Miguez categorically denied the claim as false. But it reportedly raised concerns within the White House that Miguez “either wasn’t fully vetted or wasn’t forthcoming about discoverable documents from his past” while he successfully pursued Trump’s endorsement, as the Atlantic reported.

The statements from Miguez’s campaign and the White House sparked verbal sparring from one of the candidate’s Republican rivals.

Lionel Rainey, a spokesperson for a political action committee supporting another candidate in the postponed US House race, Rick Edmonds, said he found it “silly” to blame redistricting for evident uncertainty over Trump’s endorsement.

Separate 12 March articles from the Guardian and the Atlantic reported on a woman who alleged being raped by Miguez in late August 2007 when he purportedly was her “live-in ex boyfriend”.

A police report quoted the woman as saying the assault took place at the end of a night of drinking in Miguez’s home town of Erath, Louisiana. But the legal document said she did not want to press charges against Miguez – then in law school – because she did not want to get him into trouble.

One of the ways the Miguez campaign responded to those claims was to link to an article from a Louisiana news outlet that followed up on the Atlantic and the Guardian’s reporting with quotes from family members of the accuser. Those family members questioned the accuser’s credibility while citing mental health and substance abuse problems.

The campaign, meanwhile, also circulated an email attributed to her father that was sent to the state senator’s office which read in part: “Everything my daughter has reported about you were lies and she is a liar and has a drug problem.”

The Guardian subsequently wrote to the woman offering her the opportunity to address her family member’s statements. She did not reply.

In the months since the Miguez accuser’s claims, US House representatives Eric Swalwell and Tony Gonzales – a California Democrat and a Texas Republican, respectively – resigned under bipartisan pressure over allegations of sexual misconduct against both.

Furthermore, on 10 July, Democratic US Senate candidate Graham Platner of Maine withdrew his scandal-laden candidacy days after detailed sexual assault allegations were made against him.

Miguez won election to Louisiana’s state senate in 2023 after more than eight years in its house of representatives. He signed up to contest for a seat that congresswoman Julia Letlow vacated when, with Trump’s backing, she victoriously ran in a Republican US Senate primary at the expense of incumbent Bill Cassidy.

The boundaries of that seat – in Louisiana’s fifth congressional district – were outside Miguez’s Erath home when Trump endorsed him in February as a “warrior” of the president’s Maga movement. Miguez’s opponents seized on that to try to cast him as a political opportunist, and his campaign countered that he has also long kept a home as well as operated a business in the fifth district, which then included Baton Rouge, Louisiana’s capital, as the state’s Shreveport Times reported.

Under the state’s post-Callais congressional map, Erath is still outside Louisiana’s fifth congressional district. But the new sixth congressional district contains the Baton Rouge capital region.

And on Thursday, the sixth district incumbent – Democratic US House member Cleo Fields – said that he would not seek re-election under the altered boundaries of the post-Callais map.

The Times reported in June that Miguez opponents had sources telling them that he had asked Trump to let him carry the president’s fifth district endorsement over to the sixth.

US House candidates are not required to run in their home districts. They only need to live in the same state as the congressional district in which they are running.

Miguez’s campaign on Wednesday declined to say which Louisiana congressional district he may target when a three-day qualifying period opens on 5 August.

title: Nevada GOP nominee Carrie Buck says she’s ‘coming to retire Dina Titus’
excerpt:
Carrie Buck discusses housing, immigration, taxes and why she believes voters are ready to move on from Rep. Dina Titus

After flipping a Democrat-leaning state senate district in 2020, Carrie Buck is...
body_text:
Carrie Buck discusses housing, immigration, taxes and why she believes voters are ready to move on from Rep. Dina Titus

After flipping a Democrat-leaning state senate district in 2020, Carrie Buck is setting her sights on a bigger target this election cycle: Nevada's first congressional district, an urban Las-Vegas based district stretching east to the Arizona border.

But it will not be easy to oust Congresswoman Dina Titus, an 8-term veteran, with strong ties to organized labor, the tourism industry, and the business community.

MINIVAN MOM PUTS DEM INCUMBENT ON NOTICE IN TOP GOP TARGET DISTRICT: 'SHE HAS DONE NOTHING FOR US'

Nevada state Sen. Carrie Ann Buck, a Henderson Republican, is challenging Democratic Rep. Dina Titus in the state's 1st District. (K.M. Cannon / Las Vegas Review-Journal/Tribune News Service via Getty Images)

Recently, Buck sat down with Fox News Digital at Freedom Fest in Las Vegas, to discuss her swing district appeal, her background as a teacher and principal, and why she believes her take on kitchen table economics will enable her to pull off an upset come election day.

First and foremost, Buck is no stranger to challenging Democrats on their own turf:

"Well, in 2020, I flipped a blue district to Republican, and it represents 20% of this [NV-1] district. So, I know I can flip the entire district. People are just tired of my Democrat opponent, and they're ready for change."

U.S. Rep. Dina Titus (D-Nev.) listens as she heads a roundtable on heat, convened by the Nevada Conservation League, Chispa Nevada and Climate Power at the Whitney Library Conference Room on Thursday, May 28, 2026, in Las Vegas. (L.E. Baskow/Las Vegas Review-Journal/Tribune News Service via Getty Images)

Buck cites her three decades of experience in the Nevada education system as critical to understanding the electorate:

"Well, the past 30 years, I've served in the school system here in Nevada. I was a teacher…I taught adult ESL, I taught children for years. Then I became a principal of one of the lowest performing schools in the state and moved it to a national Title I school. I won the Milken Award in that time.

"And so I've been breathing life into children and families for years. When I knock on doors, when I'm out in the community, people are like, ‘Dr. Buck, you were my principal.’ And so, I think a lot of people know that I'm going to really deliver for Nevadans in DC."

Housing affordability is a signature issue for Buck.

She pledges to "ensure that Nevadans have access to lower-cost housing" while also "driving down interest rates so that people can buy whatever house that they want to."

TOP GOP TARGET DINA TITUS FENDS OFF HOUSE PRIMARY CHALLENGERS

Buck also argues that the immigration crisis during the Biden administration was a key driver on the housing affordability issue:

Former President Joe Biden speaks about foreign policy during a speech at the State Department in Washington on Jan. 13, 2024. (Susan Walsh/AP Photo)

"I think also what impacted housing is the immigration policies of the Biden years, where they allowed all these people into our country and that drove up [the cost of] all of our low-income housing.

"It depleted it. And so now that we're seeing people end up getting deported and going back to their original country, I know that it's definitely driving down prices here."

Buck promises that taking a hard line on the national debt will be a top priority:

"Spending is definitely out of control when we look at the waste of taxpayer dollars, waste, fraud and abuse. When we've seen some of these cases from across the nation, it really is a red alert. We need to make sure that we root that out.

"I just actually saw the vice president is heading up this task force, and they're finding lots of fraud…We can't just release taxpayer dollars. So reining in the waste, fraud and abuse and making sure that [government spending] goes to the seniors and the children and the veterans who need it."

Buck is a strong supporter of pathways to citizenship for immigrants, but also maintains a hard line on issues of border security and illegal immigration:

"There is a pathway to citizenship, and I think we need to get people signed up. I've been part of a movement to ensure that people have access to get citizenship. And that's the way, the legal way.

"But coming here illegally, you are breaking laws. Those people need to be deported. We've seen so many victims of crimes by illegal immigrants, and that has to stop. We need to make sure that we have safe communities. I'm a wife of a law enforcement officer and know how important our safe communities and our men and women are working out there in law enforcement."

Water issues remain an enormous concern west of the Mississippi, and particularly in Nevada. Buck argues that California could and should play a large role in ensuring Nevadans access to water:

The Hoover Dam is one of the National Park Service's most popular tourist attractions. The federal government is expected to declare a water shortage.  ((Ashley Soriano/Fox News))

"For too long, Nevada has been the conservationist…Our citizens conserve so much water…All it would take is California to build a basin and hold their rainwater and not release it into the Pacific.

"But they're very selfish neighbors…We need to make sure that our federal government can maybe step in and build these reservoirs to hold water."

David Unsworth reports on Latin America. You can follow David Unsworth on Twitter @LatinAmerUpdate

title: Australia news live: Jim Chalmers says he has ‘other priorities’ than a gas tax; Anthony Albanese calls to congratulate Andy Burnham
excerpt:
Treasurer points to other reforms after Labor members call for ‘fairer return’ from natural resources. Follow today’s news live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Thanks Mar...
body_text:
Treasurer points to other reforms after Labor members call for ‘fairer return’ from natural resources. Follow today’s news live

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Thanks Martin Farrer for kicking off the day, it’s Kat Wong here to take you through today’s news.

Australia’s resilient labour market continues to be a bright spot in the nation’s economy, Australian Associated Press reports.

But despite the addition of more than 76,000 jobs in June, dark clouds are gathering on the horizon.

After the Australian Bureau of Statistics released another strong labour force report on Thursday, bonds traders ratcheted up expectations that the Reserve Bank will raise interest rates for a fourth time in 2026.

The market is now full priced in for a rate hike by Christmas. A few weeks ago it was a 50-50 bet.

The resurgence in rate fears is not all down to domestic factors.

Renewed hostilities in the Middle East have sent oil prices rising again and threaten to add fresh inflationary pressures to the economy.

While June quarter inflation data next week will be crucial to the RBA’s next rates decision in August, the figures could already be dated by the time they are released.

Brent crude oil prices have risen from $US72 a barrel to more than $US100 a barrel in recent weeks.

As a rule of thumb, each dollar higher the benchmark oil price results in about a cent per litre increase in petrol prices at the bowser.

Jim Chalmers has said the government has “other priorities” over a gas tax despite Labor members’ growing calls for reform.

The government was given the green light from party members and MPs at Labor’s conference on Thursday to extract a “fairer return” from Australia’s natural resources. Anthony Albanese shut down any prospect that would translate into a new gas export tax in the foreseeable future, as you can read here:

Chalmers told the ABC’s 730 on Thursday night:

double quotation markI think that language in the platform reflected the balance that we have been seeking to strike ... I acknowledge that there are people around the country and including people in our movement who would like us to go further. But we did take some important steps in that regard to get a fairer return.

He pointed to his past reforms to the petroleum resource rent tax and the government’s plan to bring in a gas reservation for Australia’s east coast, while noting the government wanted to maintain relationships with countries relying on Australian gas. He said:

double quotation markThe reason why a gas tax has not been on our agenda is because we’ve got other priorities, whether it’s reservation, the PRRT changes I’ve already made, or making sure that we are a reliable international supplier. That’s the government’s focus.

Jim Chalmers has claimed rightwing political parties will “prey on” voter insecurities about a worsening economy while championing “anti-worker” policies.

The treasurer previewed Labor’s economic attack lines for the next election campaign in an interview from the party’s national conference in Adelaide with 7.30 last night. Chalmers claimed One Nation, the Liberal party and the Nationals did not have plans that would ease the pressure on workers. He said:

double quotation markWe’re actually acting to do something about it … our political opponents, the seek to prey on those pressures and those anxieties that people feel, at the same time as their anti-worker agenda would make things worse rather than better.

Chalmers said real wages had grown for most of the last two and half years, the government had cut taxes “five times in three different ways” and unemployment was low.

But real incomes are set to decline, with high inflation meaning workers are set to have less money left over after they pay for essentials. Asked how he would address declining real wages, Chalmers said:

double quotation markWe’ve done a whole range of things to boost wages in our economy because, deliberately, we believe that one of the best ways to help with these cost-of-living pressures is to make sure that people are paid decent wages. That’s been our [Labor’s] reason for being for more than a hundred years.

You can read about how economic pessimism is helping One Nation here:

Good morning and welcome to our live news blog. I’m Martin Farrer with the top overnight stories before your regular blogger takes the reins.

Jim Chalmers has claimed rightwing political parties will “prey on” voter insecurities about a worsening economy while championing “anti-worker” policies. It comes as markets are now expecting another rate hike by the end of the year. He also continues to push back at rank and file Labor members’ calls for a new tax on gas exports, saying the government had “other priorities” for reform. More coming up.

The International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons Australia has said it is alarmed that Saudi Arabia could use its uranium enrichment deal with the US to build nuclear weapons. More details soon.

title: Read the list: 44 lawmakers swept into Jack Smith text sweep as his legal reckoning grows
excerpt:
Senators spoke with Fox News Digital about newly released documents that Senate Republicans allege show serious misconduct on part of former special counsel Jack Smith.

Former special counsel Jack Sm...
body_text:
Senators spoke with Fox News Digital about newly released documents that Senate Republicans allege show serious misconduct on part of former special counsel Jack Smith.

Former special counsel Jack Smith’s team obtained and reviewed the contents of text messages exchanged between Trump White House officials and 44 current and former members of Congress, according to Justice Department records released by two Republican-led Senate committees.

The messages were contained in White House records subpoenaed from the National Archives covering Oct. 1, 2020, through Jan. 20, 2021. The Justice Department said Smith’s investigative team "apparently bypassed" a filter team established to screen potentially privileged material before investigators accessed it.

House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan, R-Ohio, on Wednesday issued a criminal referral for Smith, arguing that he misled Congress when he stated that he did not obtain or review lawmakers' text messages while carrying out his probes into President Donald Trump. The referral functions as an invitation for the Justice Department to launch an investigation into Smith's conduct.

JACK SMITH THRUST BACK INTO SPOTLIGHT AT BLANCHE HEARING OVER EXPOSED SENATORS’ MESSAGES

Jack Smith, US special counsel, speaks during a news conference in Washington, DC, US, on Tuesday, Aug. 1, 2023. Donald Trump has been indicted in Washington on federal charges over his efforts to overturn the 2020 presidential election, the third politically explosive criminal prosecution of the former president as he makes his latest run for the White House. Photographer: Al Drago/Bloomberg via Getty Images (Al Drago/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Among the senators targeted in the probe were: Sens. Chuck Grassley, R-Iowa; Ron Johnson, R-Wis.; John Cornyn, R-Texas; Lindsey Graham, R-S.C.; Josh Hawley, R-Mo.; Mike Lee, R-Utah; Cory Booker, D-N.J.; Cindy Hyde-Smith, R-Miss.; Dan Sullivan, R-Alaska; David Perdue, R-Ga.; Lamar Alexander, R-Tenn.; Martha McSally, R-Ariz.; Rand Paul, R-Ky.; Rick Scott, R-Fla.; Rob Portman, R-Ohio; Roy Blunt, R-Mo.; Susan Collins, R-Maine; Tim Scott, R-S.C.; Tom Cotton, R-Ark.; and Cory Gardner, R-Colo.

On the House side, the investigation targeted: Reps. Kevin McCarthy, R-Calif.; Steve Scalise, R-La.; Jim Jordan, R-Ohio; Devin Nunes, R-Calif.; Adam Smith, D-Wash.; Andy Biggs, R-Ariz.; Mark Walker, R-N.C.; Bryan Steil, R-Wis.; Dan Newhouse, R-Wash.; Doug Collins, R-Ga.; Dusty Johnson, R-S.D.; Elise Stefanik, R-N.Y.; John Katko, R-N.Y.; Josh Gottheimer, D-N.J.; Karen Bass, D-Calif.; Ken Buck, R-Colo.; Lee Zeldin, R-N.Y.; Mario Diaz-Balart, R-Fla.; Mo Brooks, R-Ala.; Morgan Griffith, R-Va.; Rodney Davis, R-Ill.; Russ Fulcher, R-Idaho; Scott Perry, R-Pa.; and Thomas Massie, R-Ky.

Fox News Digital reached out to every current lawmaker named on the list.

Grassley and Johnson, through their senior committee positions, were responsible for the release of the new documents.

Several lawmakers on the list had publicly documented roles in efforts surrounding the 2020 election, including those who have since condemned Smith’s review of their communications.

As president pro tempore of the Senate, Grassley played a procedural role on Jan. 6, 2021, as Congress moved to certify the results of the 2020 presidential election. The senator, for instance, called the Senate into session before senators moved to the House's chamber to participate in a joint session where the results of each state would be certified.

Prior to Jan. 6, Grassley suggested that he may preside over the Senate’s debate surrounding disputes over the 2020 election if Vice President Mike Pence didn’t show up.

"Jack Smith’s criminal investigation of President Trump was a runaway train that had no brakes," Grassley said of his messages being collected. "Based on the information that’s been produced to me and Senator Johnson, Biden DOJ and FBI investigators apparently ignored their own routine investigative protocols to obtain and review work-related messages from me and dozens of my Republican and Democrat colleagues who were outside the scope of the government’s investigation."

In the lead-up to Jan. 6, Johnson joined a group of Republican senators calling for an emergency 10-day audit of disputed election results and saying they would vote against certifying electors from contested states unless such a review occurred.

Documents released by the House Jan. 6 committee in early 2023 reported that Johnson was communicating with former chairman of the Wisconsin Republican Party about a plan to have the state’s GOP-controlled legislature choose its presidential electors. The senator responded that he had no recollection of the conversation and accused the committee of trying to smear him.

Republicans were intensely critical of the House Jan. 6 committee, arguing that it lacked a meaningful voice for the opposition seeing as then-House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., selected every member while rejecting the nominations put forward by McCarthy. They also argued that the committee focused too little on security and intelligence failures ahead of the breach of the Capitol.

"This is yet another grotesque example of the Biden administration’s weaponization of the Justice Department," Johnson said of the seized text messages. "Jack Smith’s team acted with impunity as they disregarded their own protocols to obtain and access White House text messages, including messages to and from 44 Members of Congress. This is precisely why Chairman Grassley and I have worked to uncover the truth, bring transparency to the American people, and hold accountable those who weaponized federal law enforcement."

Sen. Ron Johnson, R-Wis., gestures while addressing Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison during a hearing on Capitol Hill on Thursday, Feb. 16, 2026. (CSPAN)

BIDEN SPECIAL COUNSEL’S 'RUNAWAY TRAIN’ SCOOPED UP SENSITIVE LAWMAKER INFO: 'ABUSE OF POWER'

In the days after the 2020 election, Graham urged Trump not to concede. He later donated to Trump’s legal defense fund and, as chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, opened inquiries into election procedures in several battleground states and contacted state election officials.

The late South Carolina senator grew frustrated with Trump and his followers as their objections continued to fail in court and fully broke with the movement following the riot on Jan. 6.

"Count me out," he said at the time. "Enough is enough."
