Hawley is notable in that he, on Dec. 30, 2020, became the first senator to announce his intention to formally object to Electoral College votes during the certification process. His decision was consequential as federal law required objections from both a representative and a senator to force a debate on the election.

WATCH: HAWLEY FUMES AFTER 4 GOP SENATORS HELP SINK TRUMP-BACKED VOTER ID LAW

Trump attempted to call Hawley six times ahead of the Jan. 6 breach, according to White House call logs obtained by the House Jan. 6 select committee, showing that there was a line of communication between the two.

Though some senators abandoned their objections to the 2020 election after the Capitol was breached, Hawley maintained his objections and voted against certifying the results from Arizona and Pennsylvania.

Hawley called the documents released by Grassley and Johnson "more evidence of Biden corruption" adding that, "Jack Smith illegally scooped up the text messages of Senators — including mine."

Text messages turned over by former White House chief of staff Mark Meadows to the House Jan. 6 committee show that Lee discussed possible legal and constitutional avenues for challenging the 2020 election results.

Lee initially told Meadows that he supported exhausting every available legal and constitutional remedy to address concerns about the election. By Jan. 3, 2021, however, he warned that the effort "could all backfire badly," according to text messages Meadows turned over to the House Jan. 6 committee.

The senator ultimately voted against sustaining objections to Arizona’s and Pennsylvania's election results.

Mike Lee slammed Smith's collection of text messages this month as "un-American" and argued that it came "uncomfortably close to spying on a coequal, coordinate branch."

Scalise, as House Republican whip, was responsible for convincing representatives to vote in line with the party’s interests, providing a straightforward reason as to why White House staff may have been in contact with him.

Further, a publicly released deposition shows that a Scalise aide communicated with Pence’s legislative-affairs director regarding a Jan. 3 call organized by Jordan involving Trump, Meadows and members pressing certification objections.

House Majority Leader Steve Scalise looks on from the floor of the New York Stock Exchange before morning trading on April 15. (Adam Gray/Getty Images)

SCALISE URGES PRITZKER TO 'LOOK AT THE MAN IN THE MIRROR' AFTER HE BLAMES TRUMP FOR POLITICAL VIOLENCE

The Louisiana congressman called congressional action the "only remaining recourse" for addressing what he described as improper changes to election laws in several states.

While he condemned rioting, Scalise, like Hawley, ultimately voted to reject Biden’s electoral votes from both Arizona and Pennsylvania.

Jordan participated in efforts to challenge the 2020 election results, including discussions about delaying congressional certification and objecting to electoral votes.

On Jan. 2, the Ohio congressman led a conference call with Trump and other members of Congress to discuss strategies for delaying the Jan. 6 joint session, according to the House Jan. 6 committee’s final report.

Jordan also reportedly had a 10-minute-long call with Trump on the morning of Jan. 6, according to publicly released phone records. After the breach of the Capitol, Jordan condemned the violence but voted to sustain objections to the electoral results in Arizona and Pennsylvania.

"You can’t do that. As Senator Hawley just said, this is a separation of powers issue," Jordan told Fox News host Sean Hannity earlier this month. "There’s a speech or debate clause. It was supposed to be filtered and screened. It wasn’t. It was just given from the National Archives to Jack Smith’s team."

Smith’s attorneys called the criminal referral "meritless" Thursday in a letter previously obtained by Fox News Digital.

"The House Judiciary Committee’s referral to the Department of Justice of our client Jack Smith is based on a spurious claim that Mr. Smith provided false testimony during his eight-hour deposition on December 17, 2025. The referral is made not because Mr. Smith made a false statement—the letter concedes he did not—but because he did not proactively disclose information that was not responsive to a question asked," the lawyer wrote.

"During his testimony, Mr. Smith provided truthful, responsive answers and candidly discussed his work as Special Counsel. This referral reflects a chilling eagerness to weaponize the justice system by attacking a career, nonpartisan public servant who faithfully and fearlessly performed his duties by following the facts and the law."

Fox News Digital's Elizabeth Elkind contributed to this report.

title: India activist ends hunger strike after 26 days
excerpt:
The activist had set a number of conditions for ending the strike including a pledge by the authorities not to use force against protesters

India's activist Sonam Wangchuk has ended a hunger strike a...
body_text:
The activist had set a number of conditions for ending the strike including a pledge by the authorities not to use force against protesters

India's activist Sonam Wangchuk has ended a hunger strike after 26 days of fasting in support of youth-led protesters demanding education reforms.

In a post on X, Wangchuk - better known as "Sonam sir" - said he did so "after a long negotiation on various conditions" and also to avert "possible violence in the country".

Wangchuk, 59, went on strike to support the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP), whose members also demand the resignation of India's education minister.

The activist earlier said he had lost 11kg (24lb) but was "still alive". He was forcibly removed from a protest venue in Delhi last Saturday and taken to hospital.

Wangchuk ended his hunger strike after the government assured him no legal action would be taken against protesters rallying at Janta Mantar site, India's NDTV website reported.

India's state and health ministers had visited Wangchuk in hospital.

Reacting to the news, CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke wrote in a post on X: "We are relieved and grateful that Sonam sir has ended his hunger strike after 26 days."

But he added: "The Cockroach Janta Party's peaceful protest at Jantar Mantar will continue until [Education Minister] Dharmendra Pradhan resigns."

Mass protests have not stopped in the Indian capital, after the brutal police crackdown on Monday's march to parliament, which left scores of protesters and officers injured.

The CJP is now one of India's most visible youth movements.

It emerged in May as an online satirical movement to protest against paper leaks and other irregularities in India's top exams and has gained a massive following on social media.

The protesters, who call themselves cockroaches, have been demonstrating for a month now, with some members also going on hunger strike.

They are demanding the resignation of the Education Minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, after a key entrance exam for aspiring doctors was cancelled in early May following a paper leak.

They say the minister must take moral responsibility and quit.

This breaking news story is being updated and more details will be published shortly. Please refresh the page for the fullest version.

You can receive Breaking News on a smartphone or tablet via the BBC News App. You can also follow @BBCBreaking on X, external to get the latest alerts.

title: Deadly monsoon floods kill at least 40 in India
excerpt:
Deadly monsoon floods kill at least 40 in India

At least 40 people have died and up to 80 remain missing after heavy monsoon rains triggered severe flooding in northeast India. More than 650,000 peop...
body_text:
Deadly monsoon floods kill at least 40 in India

At least 40 people have died and up to 80 remain missing after heavy monsoon rains triggered severe flooding in northeast India. More than 650,000 people have been affected, with rescue teams evacuating people across 11 flood-hit districts in Assam state.

title: Trump can't help but take jab at Mets in speech while praising Braves CEO: 'They lose all the time'
excerpt:
President Donald Trump could not help but poke fun at the New York Mets for losing "all the time" despite their sky-high payroll this season.

Two years after making the National League Championship S...
body_text:
President Donald Trump could not help but poke fun at the New York Mets for losing "all the time" despite their sky-high payroll this season.

Two years after making the National League Championship Series, the Mets are on pace to lose 95 games, which would be their most since also losing 95 in 2003. But this season is much more embarrassing than that.

The Mets came into this season overhauling a roster that choked away the best record in the league last summer, which turned in a league—high $329 million payroll.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

New York Mets' Bo Bichette leaves the field after the top of the seventh inning against the Chicago Cubs in Chicago on April 17, 2026. (Erin Hooley/AP)

But it's been all for naught, and the president of the United States has taken notice.

"The Mets have the highest salary in baseball, and they lose all the time," President Donald Trump said to a crowd in Georgia - probably filled with happy Atlanta Braves fans.

The Los Angeles Dodgers, in fact, have the most money committed to their roster, but in terms of the 2026 payrolls, the Mets are tops in the league thanks to the Dodgers' deferrals.

Trump made the comment in praising Braves Development Company CEO Mike Plant, saying the Mets should "maybe steal this guy."

"You don't realize how good he is. There are teams where they never go down. Then you have other teams, they've been rebuilding for years. I don't want to mention teams, because I know all the owners are old friends of mine, I get myself in trouble, but they're always rebuilding. This guy never rebuilds. He just stays at the top. He's amazing," Trump said. "And then he loses the best player. He loses a great first baseman (Freddie Freeman), I would say great first baseman, and they keep going along and they get things for the first baseman. And he was really good, Dodgers got him. But I watched the Braves for years and they've done such a great job."

President Donald Trump shakes hands with Braves Development Company President and CEO Mike Plant as he invites Plant and former Atlanta Braves manager Brian Snitker on stage to speak during an event at Wheeler High School on July 22, 2026, in Marietta, Georgia. Trump traveled to Georgia to deliver remarks on education and promote his administration's recently launched Trump Accounts program. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images)

YANKEES PLAYER'S PITCH CLOCK ANTICS PROMPT WILD HOT-MIC MOMENT, BACKLASH: 'YOU'RE PICKING ON ME'

The Mets payroll is headlined by Juan Soto's record-breaking $765 million contract over 15 years that he signed in December 2024. Francisco Lindor is also making nearly $34 million.

The Mets let Pete Alonso and Edwin Diaz walk in free agency while trading Jeff McNeil and Brandon Nimmo, and brought in Jorge Polanco, Luis Robert, Bo Bichette, Devin Williams and Luke Weaver. However, despite the Mets ranking 27th in batting average, 26th in runs scored, and 28th in OPS, while their rotation has the ninth-worst ERA in the big leagues.

Polanco is hitting .165 in an injury-riddled season, and Robert is hitting .211 after injuries. Bichette is on pace for the worst season of his career, Semien is hitting .209 with a .606 OPS, and Williams owns a 4.54 ERA (Weaver's 1.98 ERA is a lone bright spot).

(L-R) Juan Soto and Francisco Lindor of the New York Mets look on during the national anthem prior to the game between the Pittsburgh Pirates and the New York Mets at Citi Field on Thursday, March 26, 2026, in New York, New York. (Dustin Satloff/MLB Photos via Getty Images)

New York is said to have just about everyone available in the trade market, except Soto and some young talent on the big league roster.

The Mets are 43-60 and are 17.0 games back of the Braves in the NL East.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

title: Co-pilot becomes sick midair as crew illness reports emerge after overnight layover
excerpt: Pilots requested security and returned to the gate after a frantic passenger attempted to open an emergency door during a hours-long delay on the tarmac in Atlanta, according to ATC audio that can be ...
body_text:
Pilots requested security and returned to the gate after a frantic passenger attempted to open an emergency door during a hours-long delay on the tarmac in Atlanta, according to ATC audio that can be heard here. (Source: LiveATC.net)

At least one British Airways pilot became ill during a flight from India to London after multiple crew members reportedly fell sick following an overnight layover in Hyderabad, India.

The incident took place earlier this week, on British Airways Flight BA276 from Hyderabad Rajiv Gandhi International Airport (HYD) to London Heathrow (LHR).

British Airways confirmed to Fox News Digital that one of its pilots became unwell during the flight, but said the aircraft continued safely to its destination and that the safety of the flight was never compromised.

WHY A BLOWN-OUT PLANE WINDOW NEARLY SUCKED A PASSENGER OUTSIDE AT 16,000 FEET

"The welfare of our colleagues is a top priority, and we are reviewing crew accommodation in this location following some isolated reports of sickness," the airline said.

The crew had traveled to Hyderabad on an earlier flight; they had stayed overnight there as part of their normal long-haul layover.

British Airways said it is reviewing crew accommodations in Hyderabad after isolated reports of sickness emerged involving crew members during a recent long-haul trip. (iStock)

The pilots ate breakfast together in the hotel lounge, according to The Sun — and all of them drank bottled water provided by an outside supplier.

The outlet reported that on the way back to London, the flight's co-pilot was "incapacitated" and had to be put on oxygen.

DELTA FLIGHT FORCED TO DIVERT AFTER PASSENGER GOES INTO LABOR AND DELIVERS BABY MIDAIR

The outlet also reported that two remaining pilots continued operating the aircraft but reportedly became more ill after landing in the U.K.

British Airways did not confirm those reported details when contacted by Fox News Digital.

British Airways confirmed that one of its pilots became unwell during a long-haul flight. (iStock)

The flight lasted about 10 hours and 30 minutes, according to FlightAware.

The incident sparked discussion online, with some users on a Reddi thread questioning how multiple pilots could become ill at the same time.

"I thought the rule was that pilots/captains cannot eat the same meal during a flight, in case one was dodgy," one person speculated. "But it seems this was the hotel’s breakfast food [that] got them."

"It could also be the water that’s contaminated," another observed.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

Others couldn't resist drawing comparisons to the 1980 comedy "Airplane!" — which centers on a flight whose crew is incapacitated by food poisoning.

British Airways emphasized that the aircraft continued safely to its destination despite one pilot becoming ill during the long-haul flight. (iStock)

"Wasn't there a movie made about this?" one person asked.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"Yes, the great documentary ‘Airplane!'" a Redditor joked.

Crew members spent the night in Hyderabad, seen above, before operating the scheduled British Airways flight back to London's Heathrow Airport. (Noah Seelam/AFP via Getty Images)

A third person wrote, "Surely you can't be serious."

Another replied with the film's famous line: "I am [serious], and don't call me Shirley."

Andrea Margolis is a lifestyle reporter for Fox News Digital and Fox Business. Readers can follow her on X at @andreamargs or send story tips to andrea.margolis@fox.com.

title: US government cancels subpoena against three New York Times reporters
excerpt: Attorneys for the United States government have withdrawn subpoenas seeking to compel three New York Times reporters to testify about their sources for a story about an Air Force One jet gifted to US ...
body_text:
Attorneys for the United States government have withdrawn subpoenas seeking to compel three New York Times reporters to testify about their sources for a story about an Air Force One jet gifted to US President Donald Trump by Qatar.

Judge Arun Subramanian slammed the government’s lawyers for what he deemed sloppy legal work in an exchange in court on Thursday.

“Subpoenas are not the first thing you do; they are the last thing you do,” an irritated Subramanian said.

“When you see something like this, if this were a civil proceeding, what I would normally do is ask the parties to show causes why sanctions should not be issued,” he added.

The Trump administration had sought to keep the proceedings secret as it pressured the reporters to reveal their sources for a story, a request that raised alarms for US press freedoms as the president and his allies continue to aggressively target critical media and political opponents.

Such efforts, however, have largely been rejected in court. The US government recently withdrew similar subpoenas that sought to compel testimony from reporters with the Washington Post and Wall Street Journal.

Lawyers for the New York Times challenged the legitimacy of the grand jury subpoenas along with subpoenas for the phone records of several journalists and their relatives. The paper’s managing editor and general counsel sat in the gallery during proceedings on Thursday.

The government has said that it is pursuing those who have leaked material to reporters for a story about Trump taking an older model Air Force One jet back from a summit in Turkiye, rather than the one gifted from Qatar.

The Secret Service reportedly advised that the new jet, which the government has spent $400m to retrofit and upgrade, lacks important security features such as antimissile capabilities.

“Reporters are not the targets; those leaking classified information are,” the Department of Justice said of the subpoena requests.

Obtaining information from government sources is a cornerstone of journalism, and national security reporting in particular and is considered essential to press freedom. Efforts by the US government under both Democratic and Republican administrations to compel reporters to disclose their sources have been viewed as an effort to weaken those protections.

title: How Sudan war is putting cancer patients at risk

How Sudan war is putting cancer patients at risk

Cancer patients in Sudan are struggling to afford life-saving medicines after free supplies were stopped following the outbreak of war in 2023. Doctor...

How Sudan war is putting cancer patients at risk

Cancer patients in Sudan are struggling to afford life-saving medicines after free supplies were stopped following the outbreak of war in 2023. Doctors say soaring costs and frequent power cuts are delaying treatment and putting lives at risk.

title: Trump endorsement fails to clear GOP field as former governor joins South Carolina Senate race
excerpt:
Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., discusses the sudden death of her brother, Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., and her future in public office after succeeding him on ‘Hannity.’

Former congressman and former S...
body_text:
Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., discusses the sudden death of her brother, Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., and her future in public office after succeeding him on ‘Hannity.’

Former congressman and former South Carolina Gov. Mark Sanford, a GOP critic of President Donald Trump, launched a Senate bid on Thursday as he became the latest high-profile Republican running for the seat of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham and joins a crowded field against Trump's endorsed candidate.

"We’re at a tipping point, and that’s why I’ve decided to run for the U.S. Senate," Sanford said in a launch video. "I’d ask that you look at my record in Congress and as governor in watching out for your family’s future."

Sen. Darline Graham, who was appointed last week to fill the remainder of her late brother's term, announced Monday in an interview on Fox News' "Hannity" that she's running for a full six-year term in the Senate. Graham's announcement came three days after she met with Trump at the White House, and the president gave her his "complete and total endorsement" as he encouraged her to run.

But Trump's backing of the late senator's sister isn't stopping other well-known South Carolina Republicans from jumping into the contest.

WHO'S UP AND WHO'S DOWN: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS ON THE MIDTERM ELECTIONS

Rep. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., arrives to the swearing in for Darline Graham Nordone as U.S. Senator at the U.S. Capitol on July 14, 2026 in Washington, D.C. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

Republican Rep. Ralph Norman, who unsuccessfully campaigned this year for the GOP gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster, announced his intention to run this past weekend.

Two-term GOP Rep. Russell Fry followed on Monday. And businessman Mark Lynch, who lost to Lindsey Graham by nearly 30 points in last month's GOP Senate primary, also announced he'd take another shot at the Republican nomination.

The special Republican Senate primary will be held on Aug. 11. If no candidate tops 50%, a runoff election will be held among the top two voter-getters on Aug. 25.

WHAT DARLINE GRAHAM TOLD FOX NEWS' SEAN HANNITY

Former congressman and former South Carolina Gov. Mark Sanford is running in next month's special Republican Senate primary in the race to fill the seat of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham. (Sean Rayford/Getty Images)

Sanford, who served three terms in the U.S. House in the 1990s, won election as South Carolina governor in 2002 and was re-elected four years later. A rising conservative star in the GOP, he grabbed national attention and derailed his political career following an extramarital affair in 2009. But Sanford made a political comeback, winning election to his old seat in Congress in 2012 and served three terms.

He briefly launched a longshot and unsuccessful presidential nomination challenge against Trump in the 2020 primaries.

Known as a fiscally conservative budget hawk, Sanford pointed to the nation's debt as a key motivator behind his decision to launch a Senate campaign.

"Our country is at a financial tipping point, and I can’t sit on the sidelines while Washington adds a trillion dollars to the debt every 100 days," Sanford explained. "I’m worried for our children because for the first time, America is borrowing from the future faster than we’re building it."

Unlike Sanford, Fry and Norman, who are both Trump allies in the House, are highlighting their support for the president.

"South Carolina doesn't need just another senator. It needs President Trump's strongest ally in the United States Senate. Make no mistake: I will be President Trump’s favorite U.S. Senator, and you will have a MAGA fighter you can count on," Fry wrote in a social media post as he announced his Senate run.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Rep. Russell Fry, R-S.C., arrives for the House Republican Conference caucus meeting at the Capitol Hill Club in Washington on Wednesday, May 13, 2026. (Bill Clark/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

Fry described himself as a "MAGA warrior" in an accompanying video, which included pictures of him smiling alongside Trump.

Norman emphasized that he's "running to represent the people of South Carolina in the U.S. Senate because we need a fighter who will stand with President Trump and carry on Lindsey Graham’s legacy!"

And pointing to Trump's top legislative priority, he noted, "I want to get the SAVE America Act passed in the Senate!"

Republican Sens. Mike Lee of Utah and Rick Scott of Florida, conservatives and staunch Trump allies in the chamber, both cited Norman's support for Trump's election reform bill as they went against the president and endorsed the congressman. Norman is also being backed by the political arm of the powerful conservative group Turning Point USA.

Norman also landed the backing of Rep. Nancy Mace of South Carolina. The endorsement by Mace, first reported by Fox News Digital, came after the retiring congresswoman who earlier this year unsuccessfully ran for governor, announced on Monday that she wouldn't run for the Senate.

Besides the president's powerful endorsement, Graham does have the backing of heavy hitters in Congress, including Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso, R-Wyo., who said, "Darline Graham is an excellent senator. She’ll continue to be an excellent senator, and she has my full support."

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., however, is staying out of the race for now.

Thune said that Graham is a "strong candidate," and though she lacked elected experience, he noted she was "very accomplished in her own life."

Sen. Darline Graham Nordone, R-S.C., participates in a ceremonial swearing-in in the Old Senate Chamber at the U.S. Capitol on July 14 in Washington, DC. (Finn Gomez/Getty Images)

"She has a right, like everybody else, to get in that race," Thune said.

Backed by Trump's endorsement and enjoying plenty of name recognition thanks to her well-known last name, pundits view Darline Graham as a frontrunner in the three-week sprint to the Republican primary. And the winner of the GOP nomination will be considered the clear favorite against Democratic nominee Annie Andrews in the general election in the solidly red southeastern state.

Trump's endorsements aren't infallible, but his track record so far this cycle, albeit a couple of setbacks, is impressive.

Veteran Republican strategist and communicator Ryan Williams noted that "the Trump endorsement is a major boost for any candidacy, but it doesn’t necessarily give you a lock on the seat."

"Senate seats in South Carolina do not come up very often, and politics is all about timing. You’ve got to seize the opportunity when it presents itself," Williams told Fox News Digital. "If you’re an aspiring House member in South Carolina, this is your time to run, regardless of whom the president is supporting."

And Williams, referring to Norman and Fry, noted, "There’s no downside for running. They’re going to run as Trump Republicans, and they’re going to flatter the president on the campaign trail."

That's likely not the case for Sanford, who didn't mention Trump in his social media post or his accompanying campaign launch video.

Fox News' Liz Elkind contributed to this story.

Paul Steinhauser covers the national campaign trail from coast to coast for Fox News

title: Jen Psaki worries Democrats are being drowned out by Trump press coverage, suggests 'maybe we're all at fault'
excerpt:
Jen Psaki, host of MS NOW's "The Briefing," said during an interview on Wednesday with Terry Moran that Democrats are having a hard time breaking through on policy in a Trump-dominated news cycle.

MS...
body_text:
Jen Psaki, host of MS NOW's "The Briefing," said during an interview on Wednesday with Terry Moran that Democrats are having a hard time breaking through on policy in a Trump-dominated news cycle.

MS NOW host Jen Psaki worried on Wednesday that the Democratic Party was having a hard time breaking through in the current media environment, wondering if she was partly to blame because President Donald Trump often dominates the news cycle, even on her own show.

Psaki spoke to former ABC News journalist Terry Moran, who said Democrats didn't have the lead one would expect ahead of November's midterm elections, as Trump's approval ratings remain low.

Psaki said enthusiasm was high for Democrats, but that the party had a hard time presenting themselves as something more than anti-Trump.

"It has taken some time for a lot of the leading Democratic voices to move from just anti-Trump to more than anti-Trump, right? To, ‘We’re not for Trump, but here’s what we’re offering,’" she said.

JOHN FETTERMAN RIPS DEMOCRATS FOR RUNNING CAMPAIGNS ON 'F--- TRUMP' INSTEAD OF CONSTRUCTIVE MESSAGING

MS NOW host Jen Psaki said Democrats struggle to break through Donald Trump's dominance of the news cycle, wondering if journalists like herself are partly to blame. (Shannon Finney/NBC)

"I would also say, to take a little bit of the blame off of leading Democrats, it’s very hard to break through with that stuff," Psaki added. "And I realize I have a platform, you have a platform — maybe we’re all at fault for this — but because the things Trump is doing every day are so crazy, it is hard for Democrats to break through with, ‘Here’s my plan for covering children under the age of six with additional health care benefits. Here’s my plan for giving more assistance to pre-K.'"

The MS NOW host said it was hard to decide whom she was going to book outside the Trump-focused news reaction, and said, "Maybe that's a mistake."

"So, I think there’s a lot of factors at play here, but a big one is I don’t think the public knows enough about the Democrats’ plans for making their lives better, as much as they don’t like Trump," she said.

TUNING OUT: WHY MANY AMERICANS ARE SICK OF THE NEWS – ESPECIALLY TRUMP NEWS

In April, Psaki said she didn't know why some people on the left were spending so much time talking about using the 25th Amendment against President Donald Trump. (Pablo Morano/BSR Agency/Getty Images)

Moran agreed with Psaki, and questioned how journalists should approach the issue.

"And, you know, he is chaos and dominates every second of every day as much as he can," he said. "And so that does leave any journalist, any program, any independent journalist, broadcast journalist, with the same problem: Do you cover the Trump show, or do you cover the Trump administration and what people should know about it?"

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

In April, Psaki told Stephen A. Smith she didn't know why some people on the left were spending so much time talking about invoking the 25th Amendment against President Donald Trump.

"I think there are times, I think a lot of people on — and I can't speak for everybody on the left, I don't agree with everything everybody says on the left either — you know, sometimes it's like, not constructive," Psaki said at the time.

"I mean, you've talked about the 25th Amendment," she continued. "I have no issue with people saying they're for invoking the 25th Amendment, but it's not going to happen. So, it's like why are we spending so much time, you know?"

Hanna Panreck is an associate editor at Fox News.

title: Trump's patience 'running out' as Thune fires back over stalled election security bill
excerpt: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt warned that President Donald Trump’s patience with Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., was running thin in the GOP’s struggles to pass the SAVE Amer...
body_text:
White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt warned that President Donald Trump’s patience with Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., was running thin in the GOP’s struggles to pass the SAVE America Act.

The fight over President Donald Trump’s SAVE America Act has moved from squabbles within the GOP to the top Senate Republican and White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt publicly sniping at one another.

Leavitt suggested on Thursday that Trump’s patience was wearing thin with Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., over his flagship election security legislation.

"His patience is running out. He wants to see as much of the Save America [Act] passed as possible by the August recess," Leavitt said of the heavy-handed president. "He knows that's what the American people want to see passed."

REPUBLICAN SAYS TRUMP'S TOP ELECTION PRIORITY 'DEAD' IN SENATE AS GOP FRACTURES AHEAD OF MIDTERMS

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., wasn’t happy with White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt and her assertion that the president’s patience was wearing thin with him over the SAVE America Act. ( )

"And I would just add, you know, the American people … elected overwhelmingly Republicans to lead not just this White House, but also the House and the Senate," she continued. "The House did its job. The Senate needs to do its job, too."

But in the Senate, the political reality looks much different than Leavitt’s assertion that Republicans in the upper chamber weren’t doing enough. Still, Thune has become the dartboard for conservatives, online and at 1600 Pennsylvania Ave., to throw their frustrations over the SAVE America Act at.

The SAVE America Act is universally despised by Senate Democrats, who argue that it would disenfranchise millions of Americans from voting. Thune would need Democrats to pass the bill, compared to the House, where Republicans can ram through legislation without Democratic help.

TRUMP'S VOTER ID BILL CATCHES UNLIKELY BREAK AS MCCONNELL REMAINS SIDELINED

Further compounding the issue is the fact that not every Republican is on board with the legislation.

And even though House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., and Trump are hoping that Thune and Republicans can pass a version of the SAVE America Act through the party-line budget reconciliation process, which requires 50 votes to work, there’s no guarantee that the support is there.

"Well, maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes, right? It's 50 around here," Thune said. "So instead of, you know, pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats. And if there are Republicans that they think are gettable, get on the phone. Let's get them to yes."

GOP CIVIL WAR ERUPTS OVER TRUMP'S LATEST AGENDA PUSH AS KEY REPUBLICANS THREATEN TO SINK MEGABILL

And in the Senate, Republicans are growing increasingly frustrated with the expectations that are being made surrounding the bill that aren’t feasible given the math problem bogging the legislation down in the upper chamber.

Sen. Thom Tillis, R-N.C., makes his way to a vote in the U.S. Capitol on Thursday, April 30, 2026. (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc)

Thune is considering using the reconciliation process to keep the government open, too, in the event that Democrats renege in ongoing negotiations to craft a short-term funding deal as the typical government funding process struggles to move forward.

Sen. Thom Tillis, R-N.C., one of the most vocal opponents of the SAVE America Act, said that Republicans should turn to reconciliation to fund the government, and that some were "being dishonest to the American people to say that we need SAVE Act passed because there's no way on God's green Earth it can be implemented by the election."

When asked about Leavitt’s comments against Thune, Tillis dug in.

"I have 1,000% confidence in Thune, and anybody in the White House who thinks he's not a good leader isn't qualified for their job," he said.

Alex Miller is a writer for Fox News Digital covering the U.S. Senate.

title: Iran war could pave the way for a former fan-favorite track to rejoin the Formula 1 calendar
excerpt: President Donald Trump hosts an event at the White House to promote the upcoming inaugural Freedom 250 Grand Prix in Washington, D.C., next month. IndyCar champions Alex Palou and Felix Rosenqvist are...
body_text:
President Donald Trump hosts an event at the White House to promote the upcoming inaugural Freedom 250 Grand Prix in Washington, D.C., next month. IndyCar champions Alex Palou and Felix Rosenqvist are among the notable attendees.

The situation in the Middle East has already cost Formula 1 a pair of races in the early part of the season, but it could also lead to a former fan-favorite track making a shock return.

A new report from ESPN says that Malaysia's Sepang Circuit is in the running to make a return if the series can't schedule a make-up date for the Bahrain Grand Prix.

The Bahrain Grand Prix and Saudi Arabian Grand Prix were both scheduled for April, but the ongoing conflict in the Middle East forced F1 to take both off the calendar, causing a month-long gap in the schedule.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

Formula 1 is reportedly looking at backup plans if they are unable to reschedule the Bahrain Grand Prix that was originally scheduled for April. (Photo by Andrej ISAKOVIC / AFP)

While the race in Saudi Arabia on the Jeddah Corniche Street Circuit has been sidelined entirely this season, the hope was that Bahrain could potentially slot into the weekend of Oct. 4.

According to the report, as tensions in the region are flaring up again, F1 would like to race that weekend. That's because there is now uncertainty around the final two races of the season, which are slated to take place in Qatar and Abu Dhabi.

Malaysia is one of several circuits reportedly under consideration, and it makes logistical sense. The next race on the schedule after the weekend of Oct. 4 is the following weekend in Singapore.

MAX VERSTAPPEN KEEPS IT BLUNT, YET CRYPTIC WHEN ASKED ABOUT FUTURE WITH RED BULL

Red Bull's Max Verstappen speeds past a Malaysian flag during the final Malaysian Grand Prix in 2017. (Photo by MANAN VATSYAYANA / AFP via Getty Images)

The notoriously hot, humid and physically demanding circuit was popular with fans and drivers and a fixture on the schedule from 1999 to 2017. Fans would certainly be fired up about seeing it make a surprise appearance.

Hopefully, there will be a decision soon as the FIA's World Motor Sport Council is meeting this weekend to talk about racing in the Middle East.

The field races into Turn 1 of the Sepang International Circuit at the start of the 2009 Malaysian Grand Prix. (Photo by Paul Gilham/Getty Images)

F1 isn't the only series impacted by the war. The World Endurance Championship canceled a race in Qatar earlier this year, and there are still questions about whether or not their season finale in Bahrain, slated for early November, can take place.

title: Top House Democrat says anti-Israel activists’ threats kept him from town halls for a year
excerpt: Rep. Adam Smith, D-Wash., told "Breaking Points" that he has not been able to hold a town hall in more than a year due to threats and harassment from anti-Israel activists. (Credit: Breaking Points vi...
body_text:
Rep. Adam Smith, D-Wash., told "Breaking Points" that he has not been able to hold a town hall in more than a year due to threats and harassment from anti-Israel activists. (Credit: Breaking Points via Youtube - July 21)

A senior House Democrat divulged this week that he has not been able to hold a town hall for over a year due to threats from anti-Israel activists.

Rep. Adam Smith, D-Wash., the lead Democrat on the House Armed Services Committee, made the explosive claim in an interview with Breaking Points on Tuesday.

"This is my biggest problem with the left," Smith said. "I have been unable to hold a town hall meeting for a year because a bunch of leftists show up and scream at me and don't let anyone else speak.

"You talk about illiberal," he continued. "Having people stand out in front of my house, screaming death threats at me and threatening my wife."

Rep. Adam Smith, D-Wash., ranking member of the House Armed Services Committee, speaks during a hearing in Washington, D.C., on Wednesday, April 29, 2026. The hearing is set to examine the Defense Department's 2027 budget request. (Bloomberg via Getty Images)

JEWISH CALIFORNIA CONGRESSIONAL CANDIDATE SAYS HARASSMENT FROM GAZA AGITATORS WENT ‘BEYOND FREE SPEECH’

Smith, a member of the Democratic Party’s establishment wing, has previously condemned far-left activists who vandalized his home in 2023 over his support for Israel, including spray-painting "baby killer" in red on his garage doors.

"And the left [says], ‘Oh, well of course we don’t support [that]. We're peace activists. We would never do something like that.’ Okay. How about you just say, ‘No, they did it. It's wrong. We're against it. We are going to be liberal.’"

He also recounted attending a hearing at the University of Washington during which pro-Israel speakers were shouted down by far-left activists during a debate on divesting from the Jewish state.

"So the first three people got up and testified in favor of divesting. [The] fourth person got up and testified against divesting," Smith said in the "Breaking Points" interview. "Two sentences in, [they were] shouted down, screamed at, wouldn't stop."

"I think that whole thing has created a major problem as well," he added. "And we have to be able to have an intellectual discussion."

Pro-Palestinian supporters gather outside the U.S. Capitol to protest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's speech to Congress in Washington, D.C., on July 24, 2024. (Anadolu via Getty Images)

FBI NABS 7 FOR ALLEGED 'CAMPAIGN OF VIOLENCE' TO PRESSURE UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN, BUSINESSES OVER ISRAEL TIES

Smith also said the left has dubbed him a "Trump supporter" for pushing respectful debate.

He held up socialist New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani as an example of civility on the left.

"I don't agree with everything that he's doing, but he's having legitimate, thoughtful conversations instead of screaming matches over who can threaten the loudest," Smith reflected.

The Washington Democrat is not the only member of the House Democratic Caucus who has faced harassment from anti-Israel activists.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani speaks during a primary-night watch party for congressional candidate Claire Valdez at 99 Scott Studio on June 23, 2026 in Brooklyn. (Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images)

A New York City coffee chain told Rep. Dan Goldman, D-N.Y., in June that he was not welcome at their storefronts over his support for Israel.

Days later, Goldman, who is Jewish, lost reelection to Brad Lander, a Mamdani-backed challenger, who put criticism of the Jewish state at the center of his campaign.

Fox News Digital reached out to Smith for comment.

title: Justice Department sues Colorado over in-state tuition and financial aid for illegal immigrants
excerpt:
The 'Outnumbered' panel discusses a lawsuit filed by the Department of Justice against Gov. Gavin Newsom D-Calif., over college tuition breaks being offered to illegal immigrants.

The U.S. Department...
body_text:
The 'Outnumbered' panel discusses a lawsuit filed by the Department of Justice against Gov. Gavin Newsom D-Calif., over college tuition breaks being offered to illegal immigrants.

The U.S. Department of Justice is suing Colorado over the offering of in-state tuition and financial aid for illegal immigrants attending public colleges and universities, the latest legal challenge over what critics say amounts to discrimination against U.S. citizens.

The lawsuit, filed Thursday in Colorado federal court, names the Colorado Commission on Higher Education, the state Department of Higher Education, and its director, JB Holston, as defendants.

"For three decades, Congress has made clear that Colorado cannot give education benefits to illegal aliens that it does not give to all American citizens," said Associate Attorney General Stanley Woodward. "By granting illegal aliens in-state tuition, Colorado is not only violating federal law but subsidizing education for illegal aliens at taxpayers’ expense."

NEBRASKA ENDS IN-STATE TUITION BENEFITS FOR ILLEGAL IMMIGRANTS UNDER DOJ DEAL

University of Colorado Denver on the Auraria Campus in Denver, Colo., on Wednesday, Oct. 22, 2025. (Denver Post via Getty Images)

"Our litigation efforts will not cease until we have fulfilled President [Donald] Trump’s promise that illegal aliens will not obtain preferential treatment over our Nation’s own citizens," Woodward added.

Fox News Digital has reached out to both agencies named in the lawsuit for comment, along with the office of Colorado Gov. Jared Polis.

The complaint argues that the Advancing Students for a Stronger Economy Tomorrow (ASSET) Act and related state policies violate a federal statute that prohibits states from offering higher education benefits to illegal immigrants based on their residency, unless the same benefits are available to all U.S. citizens.

FIRST ON FOX: DOJ sues Spanberger’s Virginia over laws kneecapping federal agents as mask war escalates

In an effort to expand access to those benefits, Colorado reduced the high school attendance requirement from three years to one year, and removed a requirement that students must be admitted to college within 12 months of graduating from high school, according to the federal complaint.

The state also repealed a requirement that mandated illegal immigrant students submit an affidavit stating they had applied for lawful presence or would apply as soon as they were eligible, the complaint states.

By providing lower tuition rates and state-funded scholarships to local illegal immigrants while denying them to out-of-state citizens, Colorado is accused of creating an unconstitutional preference that is preempted by federal authorities.

The Justice Department also argues that lower tuition incentivizes illegal immigration and rewards them for breaking the nation's laws.

University of Colorado students pass under the University Hill bridge between classes on Friday. The Justice Department on Thursday filed a lawsuit against Colorado over the offering of illegal immigrants in-state tuition and financial aid. (MediaNews Group via Getty Images)

Thursday's lawsuit marks the fourteenth launched by the Trump administration challenging in-state tuition for illegal immigrants.

"This Department of Justice is committed to fulfilling President Trump’s promise that illegal aliens will not obtain taxpayer benefits or preferential treatment over our own citizens," the Justice Department said.

The lawsuit asks the court to declare several Colorado state laws associated with the matter unconstitutional, as well as award court fees.

Louis Casiano is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to louis.casiano@fox.com.

title: DSA member leads Dem primary in Midwest battleground — but trails likely GOP nominee: poll
excerpt:
Wisconsin Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong was asked about appearing on far-left Twitch streamer Hasan Piker's livestream on MS NOW Wednesday.

A socialist-backed candidate is leading...
body_text:
Wisconsin Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong was asked about appearing on far-left Twitch streamer Hasan Piker's livestream on MS NOW Wednesday.

A socialist-backed candidate is leading the field in a high-stakes Democratic gubernatorial showdown in battleground Wisconsin.

Wisconsin state Rep. Francesca Hong, a Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) member, stands at 26% support among voters likely to cast a ballot in the state's Aug. 11 Democratic primary for governor, according to a Marquette Law School poll released this week.

Former Democratic Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes, who narrowly lost the state's 2022 U.S. Senate election to GOP Sen. Ron Johnson, stood at second place in the survey at 15%, with nearly half of those questioned still undecided. Hong and Barnes are part of a crowded field of Democrats in the race to succeed retiring Democratic Gov. Tony Evers.

The primary is also shaping up, along with the Aug. 4 Democratic Senate primary in Michigan, as a key statewide showdown in a crucial swing state between the far-left and the center-left establishment for the ideological future of the party.

CHECK OUT FOX NEWS' NEW 2026 POWER RANKINGS IN THE BATTLES FOR GOVERNOR

Francesca Hong, a Democratic state lawmaker and socialist candidate for Wisconsin governor, speaks to voters at a retirement home, Tuesday, July 7, 2026, in Madison, Wis. (Scott Bauer/AP Photo)

Hong, who is backed by Wisconsin-based DSA chapters, has also landed the support of top nationally known progressives, including Reps. Ilhan Omar of neighboring Minnesota and Ro Khanna of California, a potential 2028 Democratic presidential contender.

Her platform includes guaranteed paid leave as well as a one-year moratorium in Wisconsin on building AI data centers. Hong has faced scrutiny over years-old social media posts calling for defunding and abolishing the police. Her campaign says she no longer supports cutting police budgets or abolishing police departments.

In the poll, Wisconsin's current lieutenant governor, Sara Rodriguez, received 11% support. Rodriguez dropped out of the race last week, after the poll was conducted, following controversy over her campaign’s mishandling of financial reports.

When Rodriguez's supporters’ votes were transferred to their second choice, the poll indicated Hong leading Barnes by a slightly smaller 27% to 18% margin.

THE LEFT-WING FACES OFF AGAINST THE CENTER-LEFT ESTABLISHMENT IN A DEMOCRATIC PARTY CIVIL WAR

Then-Senate candidate Mandela Barnes is seen leaving a canvas launch event on Nov. 7, 2022, in Glendale, Wisconsin. (Scott Olson/Getty Images)

Two other Democratic candidates, former Wisconsin Department of Administration Secretary Joel Brennan and state Sen. Kelda Roys, registered in the low single digits.

After Rodriguez suspended her campaign, former Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley jumped back into the race. Crowley had recently dropped out to back Rodriguez.

Evers, who decided against seeking a third term as Wisconsin governor, quickly backed Crowley, calling him the "best candidate" to defeat Republican Rep. Tom Tiffany in November.

Tiffany is on a glide path to capture the Republican nomination as he faces off with long-shot medical service technician Andy Manske in the GOP primary.

MEET THE FAR LEFT INSURGENTS WAGING WAR ON THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN THE 2026 MIDTERMS

Rep. Tom Tiffany, R-Wis., cheers as Donald Trump is announced as the winner of Wisconsin's electoral college votes during a joint session of Congress in the House chamber on Monday, January 6, 2025 (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

The poll indicates Barnes edging Tiffany 44%-40% in a hypothetical general election matchup, with Hong trailing Tiffany 43%-40%.

The survey was conducted July 8-16, with 838 Wisconsin registered voters, including 695 likely voters, questioned.

The poll's overall sampling error is plus or minus 4.5 percentage points, with a margin of error of plus or minus 6.1 percentage points for likely Democratic primary voters.

Paul Steinhauser covers the national campaign trail from coast to coast for Fox News

title: Megan Rapinoe says efforts to bar trans athletes from women's sports are 'attack on women in society'
excerpt:
Former U.S. Women's National Team star Megan Rapinoe said efforts to bar transgender athletes from competing in women's sports amount is "an attack on women in society."

Former U.S. Women's National ...
body_text:
Former U.S. Women's National Team star Megan Rapinoe said efforts to bar transgender athletes from competing in women's sports amount is "an attack on women in society."

Former U.S. Women's National Team star Megan Rapinoe said Tuesday that efforts to bar transgender athletes from competing in women's sports amount to "an attack on women in society," arguing the debate extends far beyond questions of fairness in athletic competition.

Appearing on ABC's "The View," Rapinoe dismissed arguments that restrictions on transgender athletes are primarily about protecting girls' and women's sports. Instead, she argued, opponents are using the issue as part of a broader campaign against women's empowerment.

"They're going after trans people in sports, and they're using this as a wedge issue because we love sports. And, you know, we don't want our little girls to be unsafe in sports. And it's like, ‘Unsafe from who? But okay,’" Rapinoe said.

CALIFORNIA BILLIONAIRE TOM STEYER DEFENDS TRANS ATHLETES IN HIGH SCHOOL SPORTS AS GOVERNOR'S RACE HEATS UP

Former US women's national team player Megan Rapinoe gave her take on the US men's team's World Cup loss to Belgium. (Photo by Soobum Im/USSF/Getty Images))

"But now we're using this as like, an overarching thing, when really what we're attacking women, we're saying, ‘If you look like this as a woman, you can play, if you look like this as a woman.' There's a bill in Washington happening right now that's going to require all girls, just like full stop, to go through a sex test, an invasive, like examining their genitals to see if they're a woman," Rapinoe said.

Initiative IL26-638, according to the House Republican Caucus, would "prohibit students it defines as ‘biologically male’ from competing in certain school athletic activities intended for female students only. It would require verification of biological sex by students’ healthcare providers."

TRANS ATHLETES DROP LAWSUIT TO GAIN ACCESS TO GIRLS' SPORTS IN NEW HAMPSHIRE AFTER SCOTUS RULING

Photo of Megan Rapinoe with pink hair. (Photo by Kimberly White/Getty Images for Vox Media))

"They don't like women in sports because it helps us with confidence and power," Rapinoe said. "And we see so many women in the C-suite played elite level sports. It's just great for you physically and all of it. But it's an attack on women in society."

WNBA PLAYER FACES BACKLASH OVER OP-ED CRITICIZING IOC FOR POLICY TO PROTECT WOMEN'S SPORTS

In a recent article in Marie Claire entitled, "Anti-Trans Legislation Isn't About 'Protecting Girls'—It's About Distracting Us From What Matters," Rapinoe wrote, "The truth is, trans people have been competing in sports for decades, and will continue to participate as this smoke screen from bad faith legislators and anti-LGBT groups cloak the real damage being done. As with so many other manufactured issues we fall for, women and girls will suffer the most."

Rapinoe did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for comment.

Rachel del Guidice is a culture reporter for Fox News covering the intersection of politics, faith, family, and American culture.

title: Nancy Guthrie mystery: 'Today' show intruder sparks security shakeup at NBC studio
excerpt: The hosts of NBC's "Today" addressed an incident in which an intruder entered an unauthorized area of Studio 1A, saying the person was arrested quickly and that the show is cooperating with the NYPD i...
body_text:
The hosts of NBC's "Today" addressed an incident in which an intruder entered an unauthorized area of Studio 1A, saying the person was arrested quickly and that the show is cooperating with the NYPD investigation. (Credit: NBC)

NEW YORK — The "Today" show is upping its on-set security after an intruder accosted co-host Craig Melvin in a vestibule, hurling a racial slur and landing himself in jail facing hate crime charges, according to a new report.

"We wanted to reach out with an important update regarding studio access for ‘Today’ interviews," reads an excerpt from an email show staff sent to Hollywood PR firms, first published by Variety. "In light of the recent events last week, we are implementing stricter security protocols effective immediately."

The primary change is that guests can now be accompanied by only two people — whether they be stylists, spokespersons, social media aides or other "support personnel," according to the report.

'TODAY' SUSPECT CAUGHT ON CAMERA BEFORE HATE CRIME ARREST, RAP SHEE REVEALS ATTEMPTED KIDNAPPING CONVICTION

A man resembling the suspect in the racial attack on "Today" co-host Craig Melvin appears in the background of the show on the morning of the July 16, 2026, attack at Rockefeller Center in New York. ('Today'/NBC)

"We’ll defer to you on who those two individuals should be, but there will be no exceptions or additional personnel permitted in the studio during the interview," the message continued. Additional staffers would be asked to remain in the green room, outside of the studio.

The report comes a week after Andrew Truelove, a 41-year-old Virginia native, allegedly snuck in behind someone going through a security gate leading into a restricted area of the NBC studio at Rockefeller Center. He was allegedly hunting for Al Roker and found Melvin instead — then allegedly screamed a racial slur at him just before 9 a.m., according to city police.

No one was physically hurt, the show said in a statement, but the security lapse reportedly cost one guard his job.

SAVANNAH GUTHRIE WAS 'SURPRISED AND DISMAYED' BY DOUBTS SHE WOULD RETURN TO 'TODAY' AFTER MOM'S DISAPPEARANCE

Andrew Truelove was arrested after allegedly entering NBC’s Rockefeller Center studios during a live broadcast of the Today show and shouting racist slurs at co-host Craig Melvin. Authorities initially said he was looking for Al Roker. Security personnel quickly intervened and police took him into custody. (Kevin C. Downs/Redux)

"NBC and 'Today' take the safety and security of our employees, talent, staff and guests extremely seriously," the show's statement continued. "We are reviewing the incident and our security protocols and remain committed to providing a safe and secure environment for everyone who works at and visits our studios."

"Today" show clips from that morning show Truelove loitered outside the ground-floor studio's window for at least a half-hour, appearing in multiple shots between 8:03 .m. and 8:34, when actress Anne Hathaway was on set to discuss her role in the latest adaption of "The Odyssey."

The incident comes just six months after another "Today" co-host's mother was the victim in a home invasion abduction from her house in Tucson. Savannah Guthrie's mother Nancy remains unaccounted for, and no suspects have been publicly identified.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Craig Melvin at the live performance of Megan Moroney on the Today Plaza as part of the Citi Concert Series on NBC's Today Show on July 10, 2026 in New York City. (Dinara Khairova/Sipa USA)

Truelove, the man accused of hate crime burglary and menacing in the Melvin attack, had felony convictions in his home state and more than a dozen pending charges in New York before the incident. He is currently being held at a New York City jail on $10,000 bail.

‘TODAY’ SHOW SECURITY GUARD FIRED AFTER INTRUDER ENTERS UNAUTHORIZED AREA, TARGETS CRAIG MELVIN: REPORT

Truelove pleaded not guilty at his arraignment last week and his lawyers waived an appearance at a follow-up hearing Wednesday. He's due back in court on Aug. 5.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

In October 2007, Truelove was accused of trying to abduct an 8-year-old from a school in Norfolk, Virginia. He was convicted at trial and sentenced to 10 years in prison, of which six were suspended, followed by 10 years on probation, court records show.

A psychiatrist testified he had symptoms of bipolar disorder and had stopped taking medications before the incident because they made him gain weight, according to The Virginian-Pilot.

Other charges in Virginia included trespassing, making bomb threats, destruction of property and repeated probation violations.

In 2014, he was arrested on charges of stealing signs from memorials dedicated to the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting victims.

And in 2023, after getting off of probation, he left Virginia for Southern California, where he was profiled in the Los Angeles Times in a lengthy story on his battle with homelessness.

title: USA Today Reporter apologizes for invoking Emmett Till in column about Caitlin Clark after upsetting the woke
excerpt: Riley Gaines addresses the controversy surrounding WNBA star Caitlin Clark, asserting that concerns for Clark stem from her exceptional talent, not her race or sexuality. Gaines suggests jealousy from...
body_text:
Riley Gaines addresses the controversy surrounding WNBA star Caitlin Clark, asserting that concerns for Clark stem from her exceptional talent, not her race or sexuality. Gaines suggests jealousy from other players is fueling the on-court abuse and the league's 'self-imploding' nature. She questions the double standards applied to different athletes.

USA Today "reporter" Nancy Armour has apologized for invoking Emmett Till in a column targeting — who else? — Caitlin Clark.

"In my recent column, I made an inartful comparison with the murder of Emmett Till. I intended to connect the issues the WNBA is currently facing with its All-Star Game being hosted in Till’s hometown of Chicago," Armour wrote in an apology posted Thursday on — where else? — Bluesky. "I obviously did not provide enough context for that."

USA TODAY WRITER SAYS CAITLIN CLARK PUTS BLACK AND QUEER WNBA PLAYERS 'IN DANGER' BY FLOPPING

For background, Armour published one of the most desperate and irresponsible sports columns of the year earlier this week, arguing that Clark's reactions to missed calls put "Black" and "queer" WNBA players "in danger."

Paraphrasing Armour's argument wouldn't do it justice. So here it is:

"It shouldn’t need reminding this country has an awful history of Black people being harmed, even killed, in the name of 'defending' white women. The WNBA’s All-Star Game this weekend is in Chicago, Emmett Till’s hometown, and a local art gallery is currently hosting an exhibit imagining his life if he hadn’t been lynched. While it’s incumbent on the WNBA to do more to protect its players, there also should be an expectation on Clark to recognize the wider consequences of her actions."

In other words, according to Armour, defending Clark's flopping somehow puts Black women at risk of lynching.

Indiana Fever guard Caitlin Clark (22) plays against the Connecticut Sun at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Ind., on July 22, 2026. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

Honest question: How did USA Today not fire her on the spot?

Forget her opinions or her right to voice them. A national news outlet allowed a columnist to turn a routine basketball play — flopping — into a discussion about the lynching of Black Americans.

The analogy is so detached from reality that it raises questions about the editorial process that allowed it to be published. At the very least, Armour should be fired for exercising such stupidity.

We can only imagine what was going through her head when she wrote those words. She likely believed she was scoring points with the Bluesky crowd by showing how willing she was to vilify the straight White superstar and her fans. We haven't seen someone in sports media try this hard to impress the online activist class since ESPN's Mark Jones falsely accused police officers of trying to murder him.

Connecticut Sun forward Diamond Miller was called for a technical foul for after-whistle contact on Indiana Fever guard Caitlin Clark in the second half at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Ind., on July 22, 2026. (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

ESPN WRITER WHO BLAMED RACISM FOR HIS ARREST SAYS RACISM IS NOT TO BLAME FOR CAITLIN CLARK HATE

OutKick asked USA Today whether it instructed Armour to issue the apology. We did not receive a response by the time of publication.

But we're not convinced the outlet made the call.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

In fact, Armour may have outdone even herself. In trying so hard to prove she's woke, she ended up offending many of the same people on Bluesky, who accused her of minimizing and trivializing the murder of Emmett Till.

Caitlin Clark No. 22 of the Indiana Fever reacts after a flagrant foul by Diamond DeShields No. 0 of the Chicago Sky at Wintrust Arena in Chicago, Ill., on Aug. 30, 2024. (Getty Images)

These people truly are incredible. They cannot cover Caitlin Clark normally.

Still, the fact that a column about Clark ended up comparing her on-court behavior to lynching black people is a level of excess that would impress even Jemele Hill.

title: WATCH: Dem demands 'one case' where sanctuary cities didn't turn over illegal immigrants. DHS provides list
excerpt:
Rep. Daniel Goldman, D-N.Y., asks Republicans to  "cite one case where there was a criminal conviction... and he was not turned over by the city or the state to the Department of Homeland Security."

...
body_text:
Rep. Daniel Goldman, D-N.Y., asks Republicans to  "cite one case where there was a criminal conviction... and he was not turned over by the city or the state to the Department of Homeland Security."

A New York City congressman drew swift pushback from Republicans and the Department of Homeland Security after claiming sanctuary jurisdictions turn over removable criminal illegal immigrants to federal authorities upon completion of their sentences.

Rep. Daniel Goldman, a Democrat representing Lower Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn, was responding to remarks about the immigration system from Reps. Darrell Issa and Tom McClintock, R-Calif., during a markup of a bill that would enshrine President Donald Trump’s border security orders into law.

"Sanctuary cities… I ask you please cite one case where there was a criminal conviction, the defendant served his time and was convicted of a removable offense under the [Immigration & Nationality Act of 1952] and he was not turned over by the city or the state to the Department of Homeland Security," Goldman addressed his Republican colleagues during the markup.

DHS UNLOADS ON 'SANCTUARY CALAMITY' VIRGINIA AFTER ILLEGAL ALIEN ACCUSED OF HEINOUS CRIME RELEASED: 'SICKO'

"You have not and are unable to provide one, is my guess. Maybe you'll have one or two, but the policy of sanctuary cities and sanctuary states is that if you are removable after you're convicted after you complete your sentence, you are turned over to the Department of Homeland Security."

The claim was met with immediate skepticism after Goldman finished speaking, as Rep. Brad Knott, R-N.C., noted he prosecuted such cases in the Tar Heel State.

"Sanctuary jurisdictions do have a very wide paradigm in how they're operated. There's some sanctuary leaning jurisdictions in my home state of North Carolina: We had to pass a law at the state legislature to override the discretion," Knott said, adding that recent cases in Fairfax County, Virginia, support his assertion that sanctuary jurisdictions do not cooperate after an illegal immigrant is convicted.

NEW YORK SHERIFFS 'MAD AS HELL' AS HOCHUL PUSHES TO BAN KEY LAW ENFORCEMENT PARTNERSHIP

Rep. Dan Goldman, D-N.Y., stands outside the Delaney Hall Detention Center in Newark, New Jersey. (Rashid Umar Abbasi for Fox News Digital.)

DHS also rebuked Goldman’s claim of common cooperation, citing thousands of cases in the lawmaker’s home state to make their point.

Since January, New York’s failure to honor ICE detainers resulted in the release of 6,947 criminally convicted illegal immigrants, according to statistics DHS shared with Fox News Digital.

"The crimes of these aliens include 29 homicides, 2,509 assaults, 199 burglaries, 305 robberies, 392 dangerous drugs offenses, 300 weapons offenses, and 207 sexual predatory offenses," said Assistant DHS Secretary Lauren Bis, who added there were around 7,000 illegal immigrants in the state with active detainer requests as of last winter.

"Sanctuary politicians must stop putting politics above public safety. This type of insanity leads to more crimes and more innocent victims," Bis said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Goldman for comment.

HOUSE REPUBLICANS MOVE TO MAKE TRUMP'S BORDER POLICIES PERMANENT

During his remarks, Goldman also criticized Issa’s invocation of Ellis Island’s policies toward European immigrants at the turn of the century.

"First of all, my grandmother also came through Ellis Island. There was no immigration law at that time. And, certainly there was no NCIS where you could look at a database and figure out whether someone has a criminal record from their own country," said Goldman, whose district includes the New York State portion of the now-defunct facility.

Washington, D.C.: Rep. Ritchie Torres (D-Bronx) and Rep. Daniel Goldman (D-Brooklyn) speak to reporters outside of Rep. George Santos' (R-NY) office on Jan 10, 2023, after delivering to Santos a copy of their complaint to the House Ethics Committee. (Photo by Laura Figueroa Hernandez/Newsday RM via Getty Images)

He said tuberculosis patients were housed and rehabilitated to complete their immigration proceedings and that 98% of those who came through Ellis were allowed into the U.S.

"So I don't think you wanna go back to the quote ‘orderly process’ unquote that you said Ellis Island is," he said.

DHS cited at least 12 cases it said contradicted Goldman's claim, including several involving illegal immigrants arrested for violent crimes who New York authorities released despite ICE detainer requests.

Among them was a Dominican national arrested for attempted murder who was released in the Bronx before ICE later apprehended him last September.

DHS also cited a Crips gang member with a prior assault conviction who was arrested last August on a criminal weapons possession charge and released despite a detainer request.

The agency also pointed to Turkish national Selman Cevik, who was on the Known or Suspected Terrorist (KST) list and became the subject of an ICE detainer after his arrest on a first-degree menacing charge. New York City authorities released him before ICE later tracked him down, DHS said.

Born and raised in Allentown, Pennsylvania, worked in both the New York and Washington bureaus for Fox News since 2013.

title: Smokin' Hot Charley Hull suffers a brutal quadruple bogey, USA Today gasbags & Angel Reese is back at it!
excerpt: Sophie Cunningham drops unfiltered thoughts defending women’s athletics, setting off a massive media firestorm. From ESPN’s hot takes to the WNBA media’s fierce reaction, Dan breaks down why Cunningha...
body_text:
Sophie Cunningham drops unfiltered thoughts defending women’s athletics, setting off a massive media firestorm. From ESPN’s hot takes to the WNBA media’s fierce reaction, Dan breaks down why Cunningham is quickly becoming the most talked-about face in the league—and why she is completely embracing the backlash. Plus, Caitlin Clark makes waves by declining the 3-Point Contest at WNBA All-Star Weekend.

'Nightcaps' is a daily column that catches you up on the biggest sports, news and culture stories you may have missed while pretending to do work at your desk. It runs Monday–Friday on OutKick.com.

Over the hump and safely through to the other side of the week. We're eight days away from August. Down here in Florida, that means we're about a week away from the real start of hurricane season.

Technically, it started on June 1. But us Sunshine State vets know the real season goes from about Aug. 1 through October. The Tropics wake up when the calendar turns to August. They really wake up around Labor Day.

Once in a while, they'll stay asleep until a few weeks before Halloween. Those are the absolute worst.

Anyway, it's been a quiet summer thus far. Always is. But there are storms coming. This is why we GRIND.

Charley Hull of England smokes as she waits on the 18th hole during Day 3 of the HSBC Women's World Championship 2025 at Sentosa Golf Club on March 1, 2025, in Singapore. (Getty Images)

Welcome to a Thursday Nightcaps — the one where Smokin' Hot LPGA golfer Charley Hull suffered one of the most brutal quadruple bogeys I've ever seen during this morning's Scottish Open.

What else? I've got a Red Sox prospect hitting a tank and then slapping the catcher, USA Today's Nancy Armour being an absolute lunatic, the Big Js harassing Sophie Cunningham, and can I interest anyone in some Angel Reese bikini pics before the All-Star break?

Grab you some vanilla ice cream for National Vanilla Ice Cream Day, and settle in for a Thursday 'Cap!

First of all — how great is my state? Goodness, I will never leave. The most innovative state in America, by a mile. Can't believe I hadn't thought of that!

Secondly ... I did my Mount Rushmore of Ice Cream Flavors last week, so no need to rehash for Vanilla Ice Cream Day. I will say, in the pantheon of the "Big Three" ice cream flavors (strawberry, vanilla and chocolate), vanilla is easily — easily — No. 1.

Nobody's ever said, "I don't like vanilla ice cream." It might be boring, but it gets the job done in any situation. It's not going to win any awards, but it's always going to be in the mix.

And that's your ice cream Ted Talk for the day. You're welcome!

Now, let's get this class started with golfer Charley Hull — famous for ripping cigarettes on the course — grinding through the worst quadruple bogey I've ever seen in the UK today:

Lordy. The movement on the initial putt is just absurd. That green should be illegal. And then the three failed chips. Just a brutal hole, and some brutal conditions.

Hell of a quadruple bogey save from Charley, though. I would've already been in the next tee box getting ready to rip one to the moon. Good for her.

Charley Hull of England reacts as she prepares to play a shot on the third hole during Round 1 of the Amundi Evian Championship at Evian Resort Golf Club on July 10, 2025, in Evian-les-Bains, France. (Getty Images)

Speaking of hitting things to the moon and being frustrated, let's check in on Boston's No. 3 prospect, Justin Gonzales, who belted a home run to Virginia last night, and then promptly ignited a brawl after touching them all.

There is SO much going on there, I don't even know where to begin.

For starters ... what a call by those minor league announcers! I was ready to run through a brick wall. Electric.

But how about the slap from Gonzales? Not a punch. A slap. So degrading. So humiliating. He's such a large human (6-5, 260), it's almost impossible not to get in his way.

Finally, Look at the umpire. He attempts to stand in the middle of the two, but quickly retreats. He couldn't abandon ship fast enough. He was outta there.

That's what we call a "business decision," folks. I don't blame him. Fight or flight, and he wasn't gonna win that fight.

Justin Gonzales #35 of the Boston Red Sox bats during a spring training game against the Tampa Bay Rays at JetBlue Park at Fenway South on March 7, 2026, in Fort Myers, Fla. (Getty Images)

Let's get Justin Gonzales to Fenway this weekend and start a new winning streak! Trade deadline be damned. We already have our ace in the hole down in South Carolina.

OK, let's rapid-fire this Thursday class into a big Thursday night. First up? The WNBA is an absolute embarrassment, Part 1:

That's an actual paragraph from insufferable USA Today columnist Nancy Armour's latest drivel, in which she asserts that Clark’s reactions to missed calls put "Black" and "queer" WNBA players "in danger," essentially blaming her for alleged racist messages sent by people she doesn’t know and hasn't publicly condemned.

What a lunatic. What a nutbag. Here's my one Nancy Armour story. If you've been in this class for a while now, you've probably already heard it, so feel free to skip to the next chapter.

At my previous job at the Daytona Beach News-Journal, I somehow became the sports editor for about a year. Incredible, right?

Anyway, I was in charge of putting together what would appear in our newspaper section the next day (newspapers!), and this was during the awful COVID era when vaccines were a huge topic.

Nancy spent weeks obsessing over all the "anti-vax" athletes, and would occasionally write something dumb about them. One day, she took aim at Aaron Rodgers (of course), and it appeared in our next day's paper.

I was so annoyed and exhausted by her (as was our 80-year-old readership), that I told the page designer that I didn't want one more Nancy Armour in my section. Not one single byline.

Our readers, naturally, were thrilled. Believe me, I heard from them. It lasted about a week until the higher-ups at USA Today caught wind of it and asked me about it. I told them how insufferable she was, and then they hemmed and hawed and eventually said I couldn't ban her.

That's how insane they are over there. That, by the way, is why I ended up at OutKick about five months later.

OK, story time is OVER! Next? The WNBA is an absolute embarrassment, Part II:

Just listen to those weirdos. They are FURIOUS with Sophie Cunningham. They can't believe someone dare speak out against the machine.

Lordy, they are fuming right now. It's the best. Sophie is the best. God bless her.

OK, that's it for today. Good work, everyone. Let's end our day by checking in with Angel Reese posting thirst traps to social media even though she once famously complained about people "sexualizing" her.

Is Nancy Armour insane? Email me at Zach.Dean@OutKick.com.

title: Southern California suburb alarmed as ‘exceptionally rare’ cancer sickens multiple children
excerpt:
Residents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma

Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

Concerns are growing in southern Californi...
body_text:
Residents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma

Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

Concerns are growing in southern California over a cluster of “exceptionally rare” cancer cases that has sickened multiple children in a suburban community.

Families living in the Orange county neighborhood of Ladera Ranch, home to about 26,000 people, have reported that six children in the community were diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma, Katrina Foley, the vice-chair of the county board of supervisors, said in a statement.

Ewing sarcoma is a rare cancer that primarily affects bone and soft tissue and is largely seen in young adults and children.

At a community meeting this week, hundreds of residents turned up to voice their concerns, including several who spoke about additional cancer cases in adults and pets. While the American Cancer Society reports there are “no known lifestyle-related or environmental causes” of Ewing sarcoma, many residents believe the cancer cases are linked to the use of herbicides and pesticides.

“We’re very concerned about pesticides that are being sprayed all around the community,” Liesl Heizt told ABC7. “There’s so many times that I walk around and they’re spraying, and I ask what they’re spraying and they’re not telling us.”

During the meeting, Brian Dalton spoke about his daughter Lily who died earlier this year after being diagnosed with a rare cancer linked to a genetic abnormality seen in the other cases, KTLA 5 reported.

“I don’t want another mother holding her child’s hand through endless treatments, praying for one more birthday, planning a funeral instead of a future,” Dalton said.

In a letter sent last week, Foley urged the state to expedite a review of the cases and give residents “timely, transparent updates”.

“Ladera Ranch families deserve answers, transparency and urgency from the state,” Foley said. “We do not yet know what caused these cancers, but that uncertainty demands a comprehensive review of every credible factor, including possible environmental exposures, along with direct communication and the public release of the findings.”

Last week, Bill Essayli, an assistant US attorney, asked the Environmental Protection Agency to investigate the cluster to determine if there is an environmental cause behind the cases. At least six children have been diagnosed with the rare bone and soft-tissue cancer, he wrote.
