Other photos in the post showed the mom of three looking up at the sun with her hand on her chest and her eyes closed, as well as a shot of her birthday cake, which was in the shape of a heart.

Her husband, Joaquim Valente, showed her some love in the comments section, writing, "👏🎉✨❤️🥳."

Others joined in to wish Bundchen a happy birthday, with one writing, "Hope you had the happiest birthday 🎂 🎉," and another adding, "Happy birthday my beautiful angel🎉🥳🪽." Fellow supermodel, Heidi Klum also sent her well wishes, writing, "Happy Birthday G🐐❤️."

The celebratory post comes just a few days after Bundchen shared photos from her recent family getaway to Tahiti, which included snaps of her older children Benjamin Rein, 16, and Vivian Lake, 13, whom she shares with her ex-husband Tom Brady, 48, and rare photos of her with her youngest child who she shares with Valente.

Bundchen welcomed her youngest son with Valente in February 2025. (Michael Simon/Getty Images for Garnier)

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

She and Valente announced they were expecting their first child together in October 2024, with outlets reporting their son was born in February 2025.

Following the birth of their son, Bundchen and Valente tied the knot during a private ceremony in Surfside, Florida in December 2025.

Just last month, the model shared a series of photos of Valente spending time with all three of her children, in honor of Father's Day, calling him "an incredible role model" in the caption.

"Happy Father’s Day @joaquimvalente !Thank you for leading by example, and for the values you embody, love, humility, integrity, discipline, kindness and consistency," she wrote. "You are an incredible role model. We are all so grateful for you, and we love you so much."

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Bundchen wished her husband a happy Father's Day on Instagram. (Taylor Hill/Getty Images)

Lori A. Bashian is an entertainment news writer at Fox News Digital.

title: Israeli UN envoy blasts Mamdani as 'clown' over call to arrest Netanyahu: 'It's a stunt'
excerpt:
Thiessen criticizes Mamdani for calling on federal authorities to arrest Netanyahu on an International Criminal Court warrant despite a lack of legal authority.

Israel’s ambassador to the United Nati...
body_text:
Thiessen criticizes Mamdani for calling on federal authorities to arrest Netanyahu on an International Criminal Court warrant despite a lack of legal authority.

Israel’s ambassador to the United Nations on Wednesday blasted New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani as a "clown" for demanding federal authorities arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu when he arrives in the city.

Danon’s remarks came after Mamdani accused Netanyahu of being a "war criminal" and the "architect of a horrific genocide" against Palestinians while calling on the federal government to execute an International Criminal Court arrest warrant against the Israeli leader.

Netanyahu is scheduled to address the United Nations General Assembly in Manhattan in September.

NETANYAHU FIRES BACK AT NYC MAYOR MAMDANI'S VOW TO ENFORCE 'BOGUS ARREST WARRANT'

Israeli Ambassador to the UN Danny Danon speaks during a United Nations emergency security council meeting on Lebanon at the UN Headquarters in New York, on Mar. 31. (CHARLY TRIBALLEAU / AFP via Getty Images)

"Those baseless remarks about harassing the Prime Minister—it's a joke, and he's a clown," Danon told Fox News Digital on Wednesday.

"Everyone knows it's a show. It's a stunt," Danon said. "A mayor should be dealing with the homeless people, the garbage in New York, and leave foreign affairs to the people in Washington who are elected to do it. He should take care of the security of the people in New York—especially the Jewish community, which is seeing the rise of antisemitism—that is his first responsibility."

The YouTube channel for the New York City Mayor's Office released a video on July 21 titled, "Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal."

"Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people," Mamdani begins, restating comments he made in an interview with The New York Times.

Mamdani leveled a series of allegations against the Israeli leader, claiming he was "responsible for the killing of more than 73,000 people, for the maiming of tens of thousands of children, as those who survive undergo amputation without anesthesia."

He also accused Netanyahu of targeting neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers and alleged that he blocked food and humanitarian aid.

CNN LEGAL ANALYST CALLS OUT 'UTTERLY RIDICULOUS' CLAIM BY MAMDANI ABOUT NETANYAHU ARREST

"Just last month, the U.N. confirmed that Palestinian children continue to be deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli armed forces, more than eight months after the so-called ceasefire," Mamdani said. "All as we, as Americans, pay for the bombs that do the killing. There is a reason the International Criminal Court issued a warrant for his arrest."

Danon pushed back sharply against the mayor's accusations.

"There is a lot of humanitarian aid coming into Gaza every day," Danon argued.

"Nobody speaks about those issues anymore, but he wants to resume spreading blood libels—promoting the claims of genocide, starvation and all those lies that were spread by him, by his wife and by other people during the war."

"He wants to go back to this situation, but today there are no humanitarian issues in Gaza," Danon added.

"Mamdani continues to promote those blood libels against Israel and against the Jews."

Mamdani also said that City Hall cannot act on the warrant unilaterally.

"It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant. The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant," Mamdani stated, adding: "Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large."

MAMDANI’S ‘DERANGED’ ISRAEL TAKE RESURFACES AS HE TARGETS NETANYAHU

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said his administration is exploring whether the city has legal authority to arrest Netanyahu if he visits for the U.N. General Assembly. (Ronen Zvulun/Pool/AFP via Getty Images)

Israeli officials strongly deny all charges. Netanyahu’s office has rejected the ICC’s jurisdiction, labeling it "a kangaroo court that has no jurisdiction over Americans or Israelis."

"People are smart; they know there's no legal basis for his remarks," Danon said. "The U.S. is not part of the ICC. There is no warrant that is actually effective against the Prime Minister in the U.S. So it's a political stunt."

"We have serious issues to deal with—the war with Iran and other critical matters," Danon continued.

"The Prime Minister is not actually dealing with Mamdani's remarks at all. He said it very clearly: he intends to come and speak at the U.N."

"Prime Minister Netanyahu will come to New York and will speak in the General Assembly," Danon said. "The only thing the mayor can do is come to the U.N., grab a seat, listen to the Prime Minister and maybe learn something from his speech."

"Last year, Mayor Adams came to the Prime Minister's speech," Danon noted. "A local leader can come and hear. That's the only thing he can do."

Emma Bussey is a breaking news writer for Fox News Digital. Before joining Fox, she worked at The Telegraph with the U.S. overnight team, across desks including foreign, politics, news, sport and culture.

title: Burger King makes major changes after thousands of customers sounded off
excerpt:
Recent graduate Mykale Baker has been given scholarship money after a photo of him working at Burger King in his graduation regalia went viral.

Visitors to Burger King may soon notice several changes...
body_text:
Recent graduate Mykale Baker has been given scholarship money after a photo of him working at Burger King in his graduation regalia went viral.

Visitors to Burger King may soon notice several changes inspired by updates that customers of the fast-food chain said they wanted.

"Earlier this year, we opened our phone lines and invited people to share their honest feedback, and they did," a Burger King representative told Fox News Digital.

"One of the biggest takeaways was that our guests want to feel welcomed when they walk in, know their order will be made the way they asked and have confidence that if something isn't right, we'll make it right."

BURGER KING FANS SWEAR A SIMPLE TRICK GETS YOU A FRESHER WHOPPER, BUT NOT EVERYONE IS CONVINCED

Burger King said the initiative generated thousands of customer phone calls and text messages.

Four months later, the company is introducing two strategies aimed at improving customer service and order accuracy.

Burger King launched two new initiatives after listening to customer feedback. (Eva Marie Uzcategui/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

The Your Way Champion is a "reimagined restaurant manager role designed to empower restaurant leaders and put the guest experience first," Burger King announced in a news release. The champion will wear a dedicated uniform and serve as a point person for customers, overseeing order customization and satisfaction.

The Whopper Guarantee promises that if a Whopper, Burger King's signature hamburger, doesn't meet a customer's expectations, "the brand will not only continue to remake it on the spot, but they'll offer the guest's next Whopper free of charge," redeemable with a QR code.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"This is about bringing 'Have It Your Way' to life in every restaurant, every day," Burger King told Fox News Digital.

"The Your Way Champion puts hospitality front and center, while the Whopper Guarantee gives guests confidence we'll stand behind our flagship product being served just the way they want it. Together, they're about earning trust one visit at a time."

Burger King's new "Whopper Guarantee" promises to meet customers' expectations or remake the burger and offer them a free one. (Julian Stratenschulte/picture alliance via Getty Images)

Burger King's announcement of the changes drew varied reactions from social media users.

"Listening to your guests and acting on their feedback is what builds long-term loyalty," one user wrote on LinkedIn. "Excited to see Burger King continuing to invest in both the guest experience and the restaurant teams that make it happen every day!"

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"Love to see a brand that actually listens to its guests," another wrote on the platform. "The 'Your Way Champion' role + Whopper Guarantee is a brilliant move. This is how you raise the bar in QSR [quick service restaurants]. Excited to experience it!"

Burger King is making good on its "Have It Your Way" slogan as the "Home of the Whopper." (John Keeble/Getty Images)

"Shout out to a couple of champions," a Facebook user wrote of a recent experience at a Burger King in Canada.

"I don't think I have ever seen two people enjoy doing their job and looking after the customers as I did. Aalisha and Erwin were both happy, personable and an absolute delight to observe while I sat in this super clean restaurant. I have been in the hospitality business for over 20 years and am hard to impress ... but they did it. Well done."

A couple of other social media users expressed disappointment, claiming Burger King is prioritizing its customers over its employees.

"Burger King is the worst place to work for," one person wrote on LinkedIn. "I've been there seven and a half years, and I'm a breakfast coordinator. I make $16.35 an hour, and they refuse to give me a raise, which is bull crap."

"We don't make that much and work way too hard to be doing all that," someone else wrote on Instagram. "I've noticed this company really likes their customers more than their own employees. It's sad."

Burger King's new "Your Way Champion" will wear a recognizable uniform and respond to customers' requests. (Julian Stratenschulte/picture alliance via Getty Images)

Several other commenters chimed in on specific menu items they have missed or would like to be brought back or changed. Many users expressed love for the Whopper, which Burger King describes as "America's favorite burger" and "the brand's most beloved item."

"Actually, I don't care about the service, I just really like the Whopper!" someone wrote on Facebook.

Burger King will continue gathering feedback and using it to "turn those insights into meaningful improvements across our restaurants," the chain told Fox News Digital.

Teresa Mull is a freelance writer with the Lifestyle team at Fox News Digital.

title: CDC reveals massive behind-the-scenes public health operation that kept FIFA World Cup fans safe
excerpt: Andrew Giuliani outlines the significant economic and security preparations for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. He reveals 78 matches will occur across 11 cities, projecting a $30.5 billion boost to the Amer...
body_text:
Andrew Giuliani outlines the significant economic and security preparations for the 2026 FIFA World Cup. He reveals 78 matches will occur across 11 cities, projecting a $30.5 billion boost to the American economy.

FIRST ON FOX: The World Cup tested not only the players on the pitch, but the Trump administration's large public health operation to safeguard the public as fans from all over the world moved across the United States.

Amid the fandom, as 11 American cities hosted 39 teams and 78 matches, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) operated largely behind the scenes to detect emerging threats and to respond to public health emergencies.

"Readiness is not only measured by how we respond to emergencies, but by the work we do every day to prevent them," acting CDC Director Dr. Jay Bhattacharya said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"The FIFA World Cup demonstrated the strength of CDC’s partnerships, preparedness and commitment to protecting the health of every community we serve."

CONGO TEAM MUST ISOLATE TO ENTER UNITED STATES FOR WORLD CUP AMID EBOLA OUTBREAK

Fans gather before the FIFA World Cup 2026 final match between Spain and Argentina at New Jersey Stadium, also known as MetLife Stadium, in East Rutherford, N.J., July 19, 2026. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention tracked public health threats behind the scenes during the tournament. (Anadolu via Getty Images)

In total, the FIFA World Cup hosted 48 teams across 16 cities in the U.S., Canada and Mexico, with more than 5 million tickets sold.

An estimated 6.5 million fans from around the world converged in the three host countries, bringing their passion, enthusiasm and, possibly, germs. Overlapping with Independence Day celebrations, large gatherings were common, as well as the backdrop of concurrent international disease activity, including the Ebola and Hantavirus outbreaks.

Infectious disease can easily spread in large crowds, and experts were already looking at wastewater, a possible uptick in hospital visits and social media for any signs of a potential outbreak.

Diseases like measles are always a top concern. As of July 16, there were 2,260 confirmed measles cases in the U.S., according to the CDC.

"The matches drew the world's attention. The work that kept them healthy did not — and that was the point," the CDC said in a news release Sunday.

FBI BOSS KASH PATEL DETAILS HOW AGENCY AND PARTNERS SECURED RECORD-SHATTERING FIFA WORLD CUP

MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, N.J., hosted games during the 2026 World Cup. (Getty Images)

The CDC's effort included a World Cup data dashboard that provided health departments with real-time data on disease trends across multiple cities as fans and teams traveled throughout the tournament, as well as around-the-clock health operations at U.S. ports of entry in coordination with U.S. Customs and Border Protection to monitor international passengers.

The agency also deployed its experts to strengthen disease surveillance, laboratory capacity, wastewater surveillance and preparedness planning in host cities.

In all, the agency coordinated across a broad network, including multiple CDC divisions, the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response (ASPR) and the White House FIFA World Cup Task Force.

WHITE HOUSE ADDRESSES GROWING PARASITE OUTBREAK AFTER MORE THAN 400 AMERICANS SICKENED

In March, the CDC released guidance providing health officials with a framework for preparing for a broad range of threats, including biological, chemical, radiologic/nuclear, environmental and mass casualty incidents.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) headquarters in Atlanta. (AP Photo/Jeff Amy, File)

The guidance encouraged every jurisdiction to approach the tournament through a risk-based lens, recognizing that travel between venues connects communities far beyond the host cities.

The tournament is over, but the work is not. The same preparedness, surveillance and partnerships that supported the World Cup are part of CDC's readiness and response mission every day of the year, continuing through the close of the activation on July 31 and well beyond.

Louis Casiano is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to louis.casiano@fox.com.

title: Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally
excerpt:
President visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidency

Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing...
body_text:
President visits swing state to promote ‘Trump accounts’ and says high prices lingering from Joe Biden’s presidency

Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies.

“So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration.

Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections.

The president was in Georgia, a politically divided state where high-profile contests for governor and Senate are playing out, to promote his newly funded “Trump accounts”, which establish tax-free investment funds for every child born through 2028, seeded with $1,000 from the federal government.

Speaking before the president, treasury secretary Scott Bessent described the accounts as the “triumph of capitalism over socialism”, taking a swipe at New York mayor Zohran Mamdani.

“Look around our campuses and classrooms today – apart from those here at Wheeler, of course – you will find that our students are being sold the seductive lie that government can solve every problem, redistribute every dollar and supplant its judgment for our own,” Bessent said.

“In New York, the current mayor rose to power by repackaging failed ideals of the past as a moral cause of the future. But in Georgia, you know that beneath that youthful veneer lies the oldest impulse in politics which is to concentrate power and then to call it compassion.”

Several Republican candidates joined Trump in Marietta, where the president likened US senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat seeking re-election in one of the most closely watched races of the cycle, to the character Pee-wee Herman, portrayed by the late actor Paul Reubens. Polling shows Ossoff with a lead over the Republican nominee in the Senate race, US congressman Mike Collins, who was in Washington where his vote was needed to help pass a budget bill to fund the Iran war and other White House priorities.

Rick Jackson, Georgia’s Republican candidate for governor, pledged to ask the state legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts if elected. If they failed to do so, Jackson said he would match the figure himself.

Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3bn.

In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126m in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15bn in the bank, with $9bn in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures.

That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support.

Parents, friends and employers will be able to deposit as much as $5,000 a year into the Trump accounts, which will be controlled by parents and guardians until their children turn 18, after which the account holder gains control and can use saved-up funds to pay for college, buy a home or start a business. The $1,000 contributions will cost the government $17bn through 2028, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget.

“For decades, Washington politicians gave our children absolutely nothing but debt,” Trump said. “But the Trump accounts, we’re finally seeing and ensuring that every young American gets a great start in life and a really fair shot at the American dream, which is what we all want. They all aspire to that.”

Trump increased the national debt by about $8tn in his first term, more than any other president on an inflation-adjusted basis and nearly as much as the government accrued during Barack Obama’s two terms in office. Spending during Trump’s second term is on pace with his first-term mark, with an additional $3bn accrued so far.

Inflation currently stands at about 3%, with a decrease of 0.4% last month, driven primarily by a nearly 10% drop in gasoline and oil prices following the short-lived truce in the Iran war. Core inflation was flat for the first time in five years.

But gasoline prices have again risen above $4 a gallon in the US, according to AAA tracking, a direct result of oil prices climbing after reawakened hostilities with Iran and the contested strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil and gas shipments.

Trump said he had just come from Dover air force base in Delaware to attend the dignified transfer ceremony for US service members killed in the conflict with Iran. The family of 1st Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii accompanied the president on Air Force One to Georgia and attended the event in Marietta, Trump said.

Trump continued to claim that his policies have drawn pledges of $19.2tn into the US economy, a figure that economists from the libertarian Cato Institute and elsewhere consider inflated by illusory investment promises.

title: Big things brewing off the field in Tampa thanks to Jake Browning's wife & her plans to upgrade her fit checks
excerpt:
Armando Salguero details why John Harbaugh is under more pressure this year in the NFL

What an offseason it has been for Stephanie Niles. After leaving it all on the field for her last fit check as a...
body_text:
Armando Salguero details why John Harbaugh is under more pressure this year in the NFL

What an offseason it has been for Stephanie Niles. After leaving it all on the field for her last fit check as a member of the Cincinnati Bengals, she has landed with a new team and added a new ring.

That's right, with Jake Browning signing with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers and the two of them tying the knot, it's been a busy few months for the couple as they prepare for the quarterback's eighth season in the NFL.

Tampa Bay Buccaneers quarterback Jake Browning goes through drills during the team's mini camp June 16, 2026, at the AdventHealth Training Center in Tampa, Fla. (Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)

But the practice squad girlfriend turned backup quarterback fiancée has big plans for the upcoming season after officially becoming an NFL wife. Now that she's introduced herself at the local country club, she's getting to work.

OLIVIA CULPO PULLS A VETERAN MOVE BY WISHING CHRISTIAN MCCAFFREY A HAPPY BIRTHDAY WITH STRING BIKINI PICS

Mrs. Browning is giving her closet an upgrade this season and has already started working on her outfit for the Buccaneers home opener. She's going big or settling for a T-shirt from the team shop.

Her obsession began, she admits, with the viral bodysuit she wore a couple of years ago in Cincinnati. Ever since that moment, she's been preparing as if she's a starter and made the most of every opportunity.

That's taken Stephanie Browning to a new level when it comes to her approach to her game day fits. Her ideas have surpassed simply putting her trust in an "alarming little amount of thread."

She announced on Wednesday that she's learning to sew and the rest of the league has officially been put on notice.

RAMS' SEAN MCVAY AND HIS MODEL WIFE PUT THE REST OF THE NFL ON NOTICE WITH NOTHING BUT SWIMSUITS ON

What a veteran move. When you feel like you've done all you can with your current skill set, you add some more skills.

That's how you make it off the practice squad and onto a roster. That's how you land a backup role and start some games when the starter goes down.

Jake Browning of the Cincinnati Bengals shakes hands with fans after an NFL game against the Jacksonville Jaguars at Paycor Stadium on Sept. 14, 2025, in Cincinnati, Ohio. (Getty Images)

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

I don't want to toot my own horn here, but I hit the nail on the head with the "big things brewing off the field in Tampa" proclamation. If you're new to the league or haven’t found your footing yet, take notes.

A well-timed viral moment can only take you so far. You have to be prepared for what comes next, and Stephanie Browning has taken it all in stride.

title: Even camels can't cope: Africa's ships of the desert hit by rising temperatures
excerpt:
Livestock herders in the Horn of Africa and North Africa have switched to camels over the years because of their famed resilience

Camels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these ...
body_text:
Livestock herders in the Horn of Africa and North Africa have switched to camels over the years because of their famed resilience

Camels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa.

The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert.

"I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC.

"They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels.

"Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult.

"Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years.

He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat.

The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas.

Camels carry bricks of salt cut from the mineral-laden lakes in the Danakil Depression, where temperatures can top 50C

North Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump.

Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions.

But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health.

"Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said.

In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households.

The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%".

Two of Ali Umer's shepherds in Ethiopia's Afar region bathe a one-month-old camel to cool it down

Extreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say.

An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour.

Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought.

"We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC.

"In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat."

Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external.

Hassan Nuya Guyo, a vet in north-eastern Kenya, says in the last two years there has been a 15% increase in camel deaths in the area he oversees

A similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves.

"Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said.

The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981.

It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024.

WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025.

Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature.

By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation.

Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day

"But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa.

"In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC.

Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion.

"Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells,"  he told the BBC.

"Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves."

For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue.

That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders.

Pastoralists who rely on camels for milk are suffering as studies show that heat stress has reduced yields

Professor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels.

Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said.

While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say.

This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say.

"And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia.

Ali Umer says that within the last month he has lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat

"They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC.

The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam.

It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought.

Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now.

His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma.

"While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said.

Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot Kassa

One boy's fight to save his family from starvation

Camel milk: Kenya's new 'white gold' Video, 00:01:43Camel milk: Kenya's new 'white gold'

The women saving Somalia's animals. Video, 00:01:24The women saving Somalia's animals

Go to BBCAfrica.com, external for more news from the African continent.

Follow us on Twitter @BBCAfrica, external, on Facebook at BBC Africa, external or on Instagram at bbcafrica, external

title: Newsom turns to Hunter Biden’s high-powered ex-lawyer as federal scrutiny closes in on his family, allies
excerpt:
Gov. Gavin Newsom's wife, Jennifer Siebel Newsom, dodged on her husband's presidential aspirations Friday and said they were focused on California and saving democracy.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom a...
body_text:
Gov. Gavin Newsom's wife, Jennifer Siebel Newsom, dodged on her husband's presidential aspirations Friday and said they were focused on California and saving democracy.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom and his wife, Jennifer Siebel Newsom, have turned to Hunter Biden’s former attorney as federal investigators scrutinize financial and nonprofit matters involving the first partner and a separate federal fraud case has ensnared the governor’s former chief of staff.

The hiring of prominent Washington defense lawyer Abbe Lowell, whose recent clients have included high-profile figures who have clashed with Trump, including New York Attorney General Letitia James and former President Joe Biden’s son, comes as Newsom dismisses the scrutiny as a politically motivated "fishing expedition" aimed at one of President Donald Trump’s most visible Democratic opponents.

In a news conference Tuesday, the California governor confirmed he had obtained Lowell's counsel.

GAVIN NEWSOM CLAIMS TRUMP ORDERED DOJ PROBE TARGETING HIM AND HIS WIFE

Lowell, a veteran white-collar defense attorney who served as House Democrats’ chief minority counsel during former President Bill Clinton’s impeachment, has represented a bipartisan roster of political heavyweights over his decades-long career.

His past clients include Hunter Biden, Trump’s daughter Ivanka Trump and son-in-law Jared Kushner, former Democratic Sens. Bob Menendez and John Edwards and an employee of longtime Trump ally Tom Barrack.

More recently, Lowell has represented several prominent figures who say they have been targeted by the Trump administration, including Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, former Department of Homeland Security official Miles Taylor and whistleblower attorney Mark Zaid.

Lowell did not respond to Fox News Digital's questions in time for publication.

Hunter Biden, left, and Attorney Abbe David Lowell  (Tom Williams/Getty Images)

At least two criminal investigations connected to Newsom’s family and political orbit have been handled by the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the Eastern District of California, according to CalMatters. Citing a Justice Department source familiar with the matter, the outlet reported that both originated with whistleblowers and people in Sacramento.

WHAT TO KNOW ABOUT THE NEWSOM-LINKED CHARITIES REPORTEDLY CAUGHT IN DOJ’S SIGHTS

One inquiry reportedly involves tax-related scrutiny involving Jennifer Siebel Newsom and financial arrangements connected to her nonprofit and filmmaking work. The other centered on former Newsom chief of staff Dana Williamson, who later pleaded guilty in a separate federal fraud case that prosecutors say grew out of an investigation launched during the Biden administration.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom and first partner Jennifer Siebel Newsom embrace during a campaign event in 2025. (Gabrielle Lurie/San Francisco Chronicle via Getty Images)

The Los Angeles Times reported that nonprofits and a for-profit production company founded by Siebel Newsom are at the center of the federal scrutiny into her charity and business finances. The Justice Department and FBI have not commented publicly on the investigation, according to the Times. The Justice Department did not respond to Fox News Digital’s questions in time for publication.

Among the organizations identified by the Times is the Representation Project, a nonprofit that federal tax records show has paid Siebel Newsom more than $1.9 million in compensation. The nonprofit also awarded roughly $2.2 million in contracts to Girls Club Entertainment, a for-profit production company separately owned by Siebel Newsom.

CRUZ PUSHES BILL TO HOLD TAX-EXEMPT SPONSORS ACCOUNTABLE AS DOJ PROBES SINGHAM NETWORK

The Times also identified the California Partners Project, another nonprofit founded by Siebel Newsom. State disclosures reviewed by the outlet show it has received nearly $5.1 million in "behested payments" reported by the governor since 2020. Behested payments are donations made at an elected official’s request for charitable, legislative or governmental purposes rather than contributions to the official’s political campaign.

Newsom and his wife have maintained that the current Justice Department is weaponizing investigations in retaliation for the pair's criticism of Trump and his national political profile. However, in an interview last week with MS NOW, Siebel Newsom was asked about the origin of the probes and said she did not know the details.

U.S. President Donald Trump (Brandon Bell/Getty Images)

Newsom's office did not respond to Fox News Digital's questions about whether he has been contacted by any federal investigators in relation to these cases or any others. However, Newsom did tell KCRA-3 in California during a news conference Tuesday he believes it is just a matter of time until he does.

"It's inevitable, come on," Newsom said when asked about any contact from federal investigators. "Look at who we're dealing with."

Fox News Digital reached out to the White House, which referred all questions to the Justice Department.

Fox News Digital's Amanda Macias contributed to this report.

Alec Schemmel is a Politics Reporter for Fox News Digital who is originally from Charlotte, North Carolina but now resides in the Washington D.C.-Baltimore Metro Area.

title: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on Wildberries
excerpt:
Four Wildberries warehouses have been targeted, including this one in Nevinnomyssk early on Wednesday

Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer...
body_text:
Four Wildberries warehouses have been targeted, including this one in Nevinnomyssk early on Wednesday

Ukrainian drones have hit several retail warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in a matter of days, dealing a blow to a large number of small and medium-sized businesses.

After months of drone attacks on oil depots and refineries, Kyiv says it is now targeting logistics hubs used by Russians to buy military equipment, and disrupting supplies to the Russian army.

But it has also frustrated hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods, making the war feel closer to home after almost four and a half years.

"I am a mother of a disabled child, I've built the business myself," one woman complained on an expletive-laden social media post that went viral.

"I've lost 1m roubles (£9,500; $12,700)... We're already getting screwed over from every direction as it is."

In Ukraine's first attack on Wildberries at the weekend, two warehouses were hit, at Elektrostal east of Moscow and in Kotovsk in the Tambov region, some 400km (250 miles) from the Ukrainian border.

Seven workers on a nightshift were killed in Kotovsk and one was fatally wounded at Elektrostal. Dozens more were injured and goods worth an estimated at £750m ($1bn) were destroyed.

Kyiv said it had struck "major logistics facilities" used to "supply sanctioned components for drone production and navigation equipment".

Further attacks overnight into Wednesday hit Wildberries logistic hubs at Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk in the Stavropol region.

The company's owner Tatiana Kim said further casualties had been reported.

"We are, quite justly, bringing the war back home - to Russia," said Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelensky.

Russia has targeted Ukrainian cities with drones and missiles since the start of the full-scale invasion in February 2022 and recent Russian attacks have hit Ukrainian warehouses and cargo terminals.

Sorting terminals for Ukraine's largest private postal operator, Nova Poshta, have  come under attack, so too have warehouses owned by retail chain ATB and confectionery group Roshen.

Ukrainian drones hit Russian online giant retailer Wildberries for second time

Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?

With the situation on the front line now close to a stalemate, Ukraine's long-distance drone campaign has hit supply routes and energy facilities, in effect cutting off annexed Crimea and leading to fuel shortages across Russia.

Businesses had already been disrupted before the warehouse attacks, but now some have lost much of their stock overnight.

Wildberries and its rival Ozon are used by 85-90% of Russia's economically active population, it is estimated. In many remote places lacking accessible stores, Russians depend on them for much of their shopping, from clothes to household appliances.

A screengrab from the Wildberries site shows a search for "components for drones"

The Kremlin has denied that online platforms are used for army supplies, but a variety of military-related goods such as body armour are on sale, alongside items with both civilian and drone-production applications.

Pro-war bloggers say the platforms are routinely relied on by volunteers to buy kit that is then sent to the front.

There is, however, no independent confirmation that the warehouses targeted in the attacks stored these supplies.

A satellite image shows the extent of the fire that engulfed the Wildberries hub in Elektrostal, east of Moscow

Unlike Western retail giant Amazon, which sells goods directly alongside its marketplace business, Wildberries and Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods and handling payments.

This means many of the losses incurred in the attacks have been borne by businesses.

For one woman, who sells stationery items, Wildberries is the only distribution channel. The day after she delivered her goods to the Elektrostal warehouse near Moscow last Friday, she learned that it had all gone up in flames.

She still has other inventory left, but this time of the year is important for her business, as parents and students stock up for the new autumn term.

Another Wildberries trader who sells teenage clothing, told the BBC she had lost about 1.4m roubles (£13,300) worth of stock. She is halting new supplies for a few weeks.

"The outlook for working with Wildberries is very murky," she said. "Right now I have enough of a safety cushion to survive the current losses, but if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage."

Neither woman expects compensation. Earlier this month, less than two weeks before the attacks, a clause was added to their contracts, including drone attacks in the list of "force majeure events" for which Wildberries is not liable.

Company owner Tatiana Kim has promised "not to abandon" the sellers, and she has offered temporary discounts and waived shipment fees, but this has done little to calm fears of further attacks.

Watch: Warehouses on fire after Ukraine hit Russian online retailer Wildberries

Supporting small and medium-sized businesses has been among Vladimir Putin's declared priorities even in wartime, and framed as a backbone of resilience in the face of Western sanctions.

But the Russian state has been unable to shield the sector from the war's impact, and there have been several shocks to businesses since the start of the year.

VAT went up from 20% to 22% in January, with extra funding earmarked for defence, and tax breaks for some businesses were scrapped.

Some 209,000 small and medium-sized companies closed in the first quarter of 2026, according to business intelligence platform Kontur.Fokus. That is 9% more than in the first three months of 2025.

Widespread internet shutdowns and a crackdown on popular messaging apps led to further troubles, and Moscow businesses lost tens of millions of dollars in one week in March, according to some estimates.

And then came the fuel crisis, triggered by Ukrainian strikes on oil depots, refineries and supply routes.

"There are already a lot of nails, and they keep hammering them in. Is [the attack on Wildberries warehouses] another nail in the coffin? Well, yes, of course," Prof Ruben Eniklopov of Pompeu Fabra University in Barcelona told the BBC.

Small businesses in Russia have proved resilient in the past, but inflation is high, the budget deficit is widening, and oil and gas revenues are 23% lower than in the first six months of last year.

Russia has vast financial reserves, accumulated in peacetime, but its war in Ukraine is increasingly swallowing resources, starving the civilian economy of growth.

title: One UK mosque attacked ‘every five days’, says British Muslim Trust
excerpt:
More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism

One mosque is attacked in Britain every five days, according to new data released by ...
body_text:
More than 70 attacks have been recorded over past year, including acts of violence, property damage and vandalism

One mosque is attacked in Britain every five days, according to new data released by the government’s partner for monitoring anti-Muslim hatred.

More than 70 attacks on mosques in the UK have been recorded over the past year, which include acts of intimidation, violence, property damage, harassment, anti-Muslim hate speech, online abuse and vandalism.

The British Muslim Trust warned that momentum on attacks was building and called for an urgent response from the government.

The top three hotspots for attacks are London, Greater Manchester and the West Midlands. There have been nine separate mosque attacks in London, and one mosque in Birmingham has suffered three attacks in the last 12 months.

Data from the Home Office shows anti-Muslim hate crime in England and Wales rose by 19% in the 12 months to March 2025.

Akeela Ahmed, the chief executive of the British Muslim Trust, said: “We are seeing a steady rise in the number of attacks against mosques, and we believe the government should be doing everything it can to help mosques protect themselves.”

Earlier this year, the government announced it would provide up to £40m to mosques as part of the protective security for mosques scheme. The initiative would allow mosques to apply for funding for physical security measures such as CCTV, alarms, fencing and other security personnel services.

Home Office guidance says mosques should apply for “security measures” if they have experienced or feel vulnerable to hate crime, or if there has been hate crime in the area towards other places of worship or their congregants. The guidance warns applications that “do not include strong evidence or do not explain the risk clearly are unlikely to be successful”.

Ahmed has previously urged ministers to drop these requirements for applying for protective security, saying it created a significant barrier to support.

The British Muslim Trust said mosques applying for funding have found the scheme to be difficult to access, with some waiting up to 18 months to receive funding. The monitoring group added that not only was there a backlog, but that many were being denied funding without explanation.

“A mosque is vulnerable purely because it’s a mosque. Currently, they’re waiting for months while living in fear of another attack because of the backlog. In many cases, police aren’t even responding to low-level incidents, leaving people frightened and vulnerable,” Ahmed said.

“It is great that this funding and initiative exists, but we need to recognise that the momentum on attacks is building, and we need to react and prepare for this.”

Ahmed added: “We recommend exploring better ways to get the funding to mosques as smoothly as possible including that the fund is devolved in order to speed up its delivery, especially given recent events.”

Last month, a study by the British Muslim Trust and British Future found that one in six Britons believe the growth of the Muslim population “poses a foundational threat to UK culture”, raising alarm that hostile attitudes towards Muslims are being normalised.

A Home Office spokesperson said: “Muslims in the UK face appalling levels of religious hate crime. It’s crucial that they not only are safe but feel safe.

“This government has increased funding for protective security at mosques, Muslim faith schools and community centres to record levels – up to £40m in 2026-27.

“We are working to process all applications as quickly as possible.”

title: Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
excerpt:
From Taiwan to the US, activists are lobbying for China to pass laws against animal cruelty

When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese inte...
body_text:
From Taiwan to the US, activists are lobbying for China to pass laws against animal cruelty

When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action.

But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14.

China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident.

"We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement.

But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support.

In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have.

Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned.

Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate.

Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay.

Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified.

Animal cruelty is not punishable by law in China

Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them.

For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity.

In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion – keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" still come up as trending search terms. Many are still posting about it, and even raising awareness offline.

Still, users have also reported that their posts have been removed, or their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter.

Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves.

Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight.

It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback.

Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way".

"We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says.

The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities.

China's animal lovers have taken their fight global, with some putting up billboards in places as far away as New York

In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes.

One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads.

CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity".

"It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight.

"The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing.

Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online.

Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains.

Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty.

A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game.

Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse.

Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao.

And people have continued to rally online.

"Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo.

"Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another.

title: Iranian missiles show deadly precision amid US-Iran war escalation
excerpt: An Iranian attack on a United States military site in Jordan that killed three US soldiers has shown the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’s (IRGC) ability to inflict a deadly toll five months into th...
body_text:
An Iranian attack on a United States military site in Jordan that killed three US soldiers has shown the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’s (IRGC) ability to inflict a deadly toll five months into the US-Israel war on Iran.

The strikes on Friday were the first time US soldiers have been killed by Iranian attacks on US military installations since March 1, just hours after the US and Israel launched the war.

While the Pentagon has not released full details of the attack, analysts say the deadly incident underscores an uncomfortable reality for Washington: Despite barrage after barrage of US air strikes, Iran maintains the military capability it needs to continue a prolonged asymmetric war.

“Iran’s military capabilities remain formidable. It has reconstituted faster than expected,” Jennifer Kavanagh, the director of military analysis at the Defense Priorities think tank, told Al Jazeera.

“Its attacks are becoming more precise over time, likely with the help of Russian and Chinese intelligence,” she added.

“Iran’s military is certainly not ‘combat ineffective’ as the Pentagon has suggested. It has sustained significant damage but is learning and adapting over time.”

Iran’s ability to continue to kill US personnel in the region is due to a combination of many factors, undergirded by the location and design of the US military sites spread across the Middle East and the Gulf, according to Andreas Krieg, a senior lecturer at the School of Security Studies at King’s College London.

Some 50,000 US forces are operating from “exposed, fixed bases within range of Iran’s increasingly sophisticated missile and drone arsenal”, he said.

Many personnel are “often housed in semi-permanent facilities, designed primarily to protect against rockets, mortars and improvised explosive devices, rather than precision ballistic missiles carrying large warheads”.

Such strikes, therefore, can “cause substantial damage”, he said. Near misses can lead to traumatic brain injuries from “blast overpressure”, while interceptions can cause injury from falling debris.

All told, the official US military death toll in the war stands at 18, including the seven soldiers killed in the March 1 Iranian strikes on a tactical operations centre in Kuwait and the Prince Sultan airbase in Saudi Arabia.

Aside from the three killed in Jordan, the military has said other deaths were not the direct result of Iranian strikes. They include six airmen killed in an accidental refuelling crash in March and, most recently, an Army sergeant killed during the controlled detonation of a downed armed drone in Iraq.

About 500 US soldiers have been wounded since the war began, with about 220 structures and pieces of major US military equipment or buildings reported damaged or destroyed following the breakdown of the ceasefire on July 8.

Iran has relied on its “increasingly sophisticated” missile and drone arsenal to overwhelm US air defence systems, including the surface-to-air Patriot systems and the Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) ballistic missile intercepting system, according to Krieg.

It has developed ballistic and cruise missiles, as well as armed drones, that can approach targets “along different trajectories and different speeds”. When coupled with decoys and dispersed sites, they can render the US defence systems less effective.

“Even when most incoming weapons are destroyed, a small number penetrating the defensive shield can inflict considerable casualties,” Krieg said.

Mona Yacoubian, the director of the Middle East programme at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS), said that Iran appeared to show “greater intent” in attacking US military assets in the recent round of attacks, compared with the early weeks of the war.

“I think what’s important to underscore is that this does appear to be a new pattern in terms of, I would argue, the intensity of Iranian strikes that are seeking to hit US military targets,” she said.

“That seems to be much more the case now than even in other periods of the war.”

Yacoubian added that the toll of the latest strikes may be a result of increased accuracy, the degradation of US defence and radar systems, or more “lucky hits” from the sheer scale of attacks. It may be a combination of the factors, she said.

As of Wednesday, the US and Iran have traded strikes for 11 consecutive days.

Iran’s army said it most recently targeted F-15 warplanes, drone preparation sites and a helicopter storage facility at the Prince Hassan and King Faisal bases in Jordan. Bahrain’s military also said it had intercepted several aerial attacks from Iran.

The US, meanwhile, repeatedly struck sites along the Strait of Hormuz in the most recent escalation, with Central Command (CENTCOM) saying on Tuesday that US forces had targeted “Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities, and military logistics infrastructure”.

On Wednesday, US Senators grilled Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on his claims that Iran’s military had been “functionally destroyed” in the war.

“Mr Secretary, you stated that on day 14 of this conflict, on the second week of the conflict, you stated that Iran’s military had been ‘destroyed,’ and ‘made combat ineffective.’ Was that an accurate statement, yes or no?” Senator Jon Ossoff asked.

Hegseth maintained the war had been a “historic military victory”, with Iran’s navy, air force and “defence industrial base” rendered ineffective.

However, he conceded, “We never expected they would stop having capabilities to shoot. The question is at scale against opponents like the United States of America.”

But Jason Campbell, senior fellow at the Middle East Institute and former Pentagon official, said the US attacks appear to indicate that not only does Iran maintain its missile and drone capabilities, but that it may be tapping advanced weapons not previously used.

“We’re aware that Iran had a number of tools that it had refrained from using early on in the war that it could bring to bear later on if it chose to,” Campbell said.

“And one of those is using some of its larger, more capable ballistic missiles that can be, as they’ve just demonstrated, fired with great precision and have greater capability to evade air and missile defences”.

Campbell said that “US senior leadership, at many stages along this way, has vastly either overstated the degree to which Iran and Iran’s capabilities have been degraded, or focused on aspects of Iran’s capabilities that really are not very prominent in their ability to carry out their own strategic objectives”.

Patrick Bury, a senior associate professor at the University of Bath and former NATO research analyst, agreed that Iran will likely be able to continue to exact a deadly toll on US assets and personnel for the foreseeable future, despite Washington’s vast military superiority.

“The real issue American military power faces is that there is an asymmetry of cost imposition,” he told Al Jazeera.

“It’s well documented that the Iranian stockpiles, particularly of drones and missiles, are much cheaper to produce and fire than the kit needed to intercept them at the moment.”

“That’s why Iran is able to inflict damage in the strategic sense”, he added, “and why this problem isn’t going away at all”.

Or, as security lecturer Krieg explained: “Iran does not need to defeat the United States militarily; it needs only to penetrate its defences often enough to impose human, operational and political costs on America’s regional presence.”

title: Fox News Poll: Voters want major change amid economic and political discontent
excerpt: Republican strategist Collin Reed and Fox News contributor Richard Fowler weigh in on the escalating midterm election battles as 'America's Newsroom' anchor Bill Hemmer breaks down the power rankings ...
body_text:
Republican strategist Collin Reed and Fox News contributor Richard Fowler weigh in on the escalating midterm election battles as 'America's Newsroom' anchor Bill Hemmer breaks down the power rankings for the House and Senate.

Several findings in the latest Fox News Poll show voters are unhappy and want major change, which bodes well for Democrats in November: President Donald Trump’s job approval remains near a record low, more Democrats are motivated to vote, Republicans join Democrats and independents in rating the economy negatively, and 4 in 10 voters want "dramatic" or "revolutionary" change. Plus, more view the GOP as tied to elites than the Democratic Party.

Voters say Democrats can better handle income inequality (+22 points), healthcare (+21), inflation (+10), the economy (+9), and foreign policy (+7).  Republicans hold the advantage on crime (+7 points), while government spending (Democrats +3) and national security (R +2) are more evenly divided. On immigration, the GOP had an 8-point edge in April. Now, Democrats are preferred by one point.

FOX NEWS POLL: AN EARLY LOOK AT THE GEORGIA SENATE RACE

More Democrats (68%) say they are extremely motivated to vote in November than Republicans (57%) by an 11-point margin. By a 25-point spread, more MAGA Republicans (66%) are extremely motivated than non-MAGA Republicans (41%).

FOX NEWS POLL: A CLOSE SENATE CONTEST IS BREWING IN IOWA

If the election were today, 53% of voters would back the Democratic candidate in their House district and 46% the Republican. That 7-point edge is just outside the poll’s margin of error. Democrats were up by 5 points in April. Notably, 53% is the highest percentage either party has received on the generic House ballot in Fox News polling since 1996.

When looking only at counties where the 2024 presidential vote was close — Trump and former Vice President Kamala Harris within 10 points — the Democratic candidate leads by 59-41%.

"Although these data look promising for the Democrats, some of their support is in less-contested districts, padding their national totals without adding House seats," says Republican pollster Daron Shaw, who conducts Fox News polls with Democrat Chris Anderson. "The best estimates I’ve seen suggest the GOP could retain the House even if the Democrats win the national vote by 2-3 points, but if Democrats win by 6-7 points, they probably take the House."

The Democratic advantage comes amid weak ratings for Trump and continued economic pessimism. Trump’s job approval stands at 39%, unchanged for the third month in a row and one point above his low of 38% in October 2017. Approval of Trump is a touch lower than former President Barack Obama’s at the same point in his second term (42% in July 2014).

Trump received net-negative ratings for handling immigration (42% approve, 57% disapprove), Iran (34-66%), the economy (33-67%), and inflation (27-73%).

Three-quarters rate the economy negatively, about the same for the past six months.

Another three quarters say inflation has caused them financial hardship, including 34% who say "serious" hardship, up from 26% last year.

Fifty-four percent expect the economy to worsen in the coming year; only Republicans express optimism (71%), while Democrats (75%) and independents (64%) expect it to worsen.

Dissatisfaction extends beyond individual figures to both political parties and the broader economic system.

"Democrats and Republicans have a surprisingly broad array of shared beliefs about politics today," says Shaw. "Both think elected officials answer to the elites, both think the system is rigged, both want to see new blood in office, both feel betrayed by their party, both disapprove of their members in Congress, and both want a mandatory retirement age for Congress."

Some 70% say the Republican Party is closely tied to elite interests, while 55% say the same about the Democratic Party. The 15-point gap reflects more Democrats and independents saying the GOP is tied to elites, yet even Republicans are equally likely to describe both parties as close to elites.

There’s disillusionment in both parties. Many Democrats (48%) and Republicans (41%) say they feel betrayed by their party, and for both it is primarily for selling out to special interests.

Perceptions that the political system favors elites are reshaping views of the economy as well. The number who accept income inequality because "that’s how our economy works" has fallen 16 points compared to 2014 (from 62% to 46%), while the share who want government action has more than doubled (from 13% to 30%).

At the same time, an increasing number, 65%, think the country’s system is rigged in favor of the wealthy (up 9 points compared to 2019).

By 2-to-1, voters say it is more important to elect new people than keep those with experience.

Overall, 19% say they want revolutionary change in the nation’s government, while many others want substantial (38%) or dramatic (23%) change.

Almost twice as many Democrats (52%) as Republicans (28%) want dramatic or revolutionary change.

Nearly 7 in 10 voters disapprove of both Democrats and Republicans in Congress.

Some 32% of Republicans disapprove of their party’s lawmakers, while 39% of Democrats disapprove of theirs. More than 8 in 10 independents disapprove of both.

Fully 85% support a mandatory retirement age for members of Congress, up 20 points since 2002.

Of those in favor of setting a cap, 80% think it should be before age 75, with 61% selecting a cutoff between 65 and 74 — a boundary supported by more than half of every generation.

Just 50% of voters have a positive view of capitalism, down from a high of 57% in 2019. For socialism, it’s 30% favorable, up from 27% last year but below the 32% who said the same in 2021. The Democratic Socialists of America are viewed positively by 31%.

By an 11-point margin, more Democrats have a favorable opinion of socialism (52%) than capitalism (41%), while by a 50-point margin, more Republicans have a positive view of capitalism (61%) than socialism (11%).

Forty-five percent of voters have a favorable view of Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., That makes him more popular than Vice President JD Vance (41% favorable), Trump (38%), and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., (35%).  Each is viewed more unfavorably than favorably. Voters under 30 and those ages 65+ are equally likely to view Sanders positively (48% and 46% respectively). For AOC, more voters 65+ (41%) have a favorable view than young voters (33%), and three times as many of those under 30 say they’ve never heard of her (30% vs. 9%).

Overall, 44% have a favorable view of Israel, down from 58% a decade ago. Positive ratings are lower today among Democrats (by 14 points), Republicans (-12), and independents (-21). Voters ages 65 and over (56%) are twice as likely to view Israel favorably as those under 30 (27%).

Conducted July 17–20, 2026, under the direction of Beacon Research (D) and Shaw & Company Research (R), this Fox News survey includes interviews with a sample of 1,003 registered voters randomly selected from a national voter file. Respondents spoke with live interviewers on landlines (70) and cellphones (681) or completed the survey online after receiving a text (252). Results based on the full sample have a margin of sampling error of ±3 percentage points. The survey includes an oversample of 200 voters aged 18–29, bringing the sample size for that subgroup to 350 (±5). Sampling error for results among subgroups is higher. In addition to sampling errors, question wording and order can influence results. Weights are generally applied to age, race, education, and area variables to ensure the demographics are representative of the registered voter population. Sources for developing weight targets include the most recent American Community Survey, Fox News Voter Analysis, and voter file data.

Fox News’ Victoria Balara contributed to this report.

As head of the polling unit, Dana Blanton runs the Fox News Poll and oversees the Fox News Voter Analysis election survey.

title: Travelers unite in powerful moment of silence as fallen US service member returns home
excerpt: A large group of travelers at Raleigh-Durham International Airport stood in silence, many with their hands over their hearts during the dignified transfer of a fallen American service member. (Credit:...
body_text:
A large group of travelers at Raleigh-Durham International Airport stood in silence, many with their hands over their hearts during the dignified transfer of a fallen American service member. (Credit: The White House)

A powerful display of respect unfolded inside a busy North Carolina airport as strangers united to honor a fallen American service member's final journey home.

Video shared by the White House and many others on social media shows a large group of travelers gathered along the terminal windows at Raleigh-Durham International Airport, many with their hands over their hearts, during the dignified transfer of a fallen service member on the tarmac below.

"BEAUTIFUL MOMENT: Hundreds of Americans at Raleigh-Durham International Airport stood in silence to honor a fallen service member as they returned home," the White House wrote Wednesday in a post on Instagram. "A grateful nation remembers. God bless our troops."

The moving tribute quickly drew an outpouring of emotional reactions online, with many commenters praising the travelers who paused their own journeys to pay their respects.

GOLD STAR WIDOW SAYS 'BLESSINGS ARE INFINITE' AFTER STRANGERS FLOOD HUSBAND'S ARLINGTON GRAVE

"God bless and comfort these families. I am so thankful for their service. The dark will not outshine the light!" one person wrote.

"There is hope for the USA. God bless our fallen heroes," another commenter added.

AS AN ARMY WIDOW, I WILL NEVER FORGET HOW ORDINARY AMERICANS HONORED MY HUSBAND

Others said the tribute left them emotional.

"This brought me to tears," one person wrote.

The United Service Organizations (USO) confirmed to Fox News Digital that its Raleigh-Durham Airport Center Honors Support Team assisted with a dignified transfer at the airport on July 21, but declined to identify the fallen service member.

PENTAGON IDENTIFIES ARMY SOLDIER KILLED IN ACTION DURING IRAQ OPERATION

Travelers at Raleigh-Durham International Airport stand in silence, many with their hands over their hearts, as a dignified transfer for a fallen U.S. service member takes place on the tarmac outside. (The White House via Instagram)

"Out of respect for the service member, the family, and military notification procedures, we are unable to provide additional information regarding the individual or the transfer," the USO said.

The organization said its staff and volunteers are prepared to support military families during some of their most difficult moments.

"When a service member makes their final journey home, our staff and volunteers are prepared to provide compassionate support to families and work alongside our military partners to help ensure a dignified transfer and the respectful honoring of our nation’s fallen," the USO said.

The organization added that it works to ease logistical burdens and provide families with a welcoming space and essential services so they can focus on honoring their loved one.

The airport tribute came as the nation mourned four U.S. service members recently killed during the war with Iran.

Earlier Wednesday, the Department of War's Rapid Response account announced that four American service members who "made the ultimate sacrifice for our nation" had been welcomed home during dignified transfer ceremonies.

"Today, we welcomed home four brave Americans who made the ultimate sacrifice for our nation," the account wrote on X. "Their courage will never be forgotten."

President Donald Trump salutes as a U.S. Army carry team carries the flag-draped transfer case containing the remains of 1st Lt.
