Australia is not ruling out stepping up joint patrols of the South China Sea following “destabilising” missile testing in the region, Australian Associated Press reports.

The foreign affairs minister, Penny Wong, was asked last night about the potential of more regular regional sea patrols in tandem with the United States and Japan, following China’s launching of nuclear-capable missiles.

“The maritime zone is critical to stability in the region so, when we have a conversation about the South China Sea, that is something I’m sure we should all keep in mind,” she told reporters in Manila where she is attending the Asean foreign ministers’ meeting.

“We are concerned about some of the aggression we are seeing, we are concerned about some of the destabilising conduct we are seeing, and the countries of the region have a choice to make about how we all respond to that.”

Wong met her Chinese counterpart Wang Yi on the sidelines of the talks on Tuesday evening to highlight her concerns.

“You speak about the importance of mutual understanding,’’ she said.

“I agree, and I expect we will discuss our concerns ... including around peace and stability in our region, safe and professional military conduct, and the importance of transparency and reassurance of intent.”

China’s foreign ministry hit back at Wong’s comments, saying Australia was not party to the South China Sea issue and had no right to intervene.

“China urges relevant countries to stop stirring up tension and inciting confrontation,” spokesman ​Lin Jian told a regular briefing in Beijing.

It came as Wong prepared to warn China in a speech today that Australia would not be bullied by Beijing as it builds up its military strength.

Good morning and welcome to our live news blog. I’m Martin Farrer with the top overnight stories and then it will be Caitlin Cassidy with the main action.

More than 35,000 teachers, principals and education support staff are expected to gather in Melbourne’s CBD today as they go on strike after rejecting a pay deal proposed by the state government. More coming up.

Australia is not ruling out stepping up joint patrols of the South China Sea after “destabilising” missile testing in the region, Penny Wong said last night as she attends the Asean summit in Manila.

But China has hit back, with its foreign ministry spokesman urging “relevant countries to stop stirring up tension and inciting confrontation”. We have more on this soon.

title: Ryan Clark made it too difficult for ESPN to justify keeping him after years of controversy
excerpt:
Dan Dakich reacts to Stephen A Smith's message about Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN

ESPN parting ways with Ryan Clark should not come as much of a surprise. The decision was fairly obvious.

We do...
body_text:
Dan Dakich reacts to Stephen A Smith's message about Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN

ESPN parting ways with Ryan Clark should not come as much of a surprise. The decision was fairly obvious.

We don't say that to dance on his proverbial grave. There's a difference between celebrating someone's downfall and acknowledging that the network made the right decision.

ESPN had plenty of reasons to cut Clark. It had hardly any reason to keep him. Let's go through them.

EXCLUSIVE: COLIN COWHERD EXPLAINS WHY HE STUCK TO SPORTS AS ESPN BECAME 'RUDDERLESS' DURING TRUMP'S PRESIDENCY

Reasons to cut him: Clark made around $2 million per year, roughly four times what the average NFL analyst earns. He was unpopular with much of the audience. He had a strained reputation with staffers and a mixed one among on-air colleagues. Over the past year alone, he generated a string of negative headlines for ESPN, including shaming Robert Griffin III for marrying a White woman and making dubious claims about police officers involved in what was ultimately ruled a suicide. He also created both on- and off-air tension by belittling Peter Schrager, around whom ESPN is now building a new television show, because Schrager never played football. Clark also had a habit of injecting race into discussions where it simply didn't belong.

ESPN is parting ways with longtime NFL analyst Ryan Clark following an 11-year run at the network. (Michael Allio/Icon Sportswire via Getty Images)

ESPN did not make more money because Ryan Clark was on television. He wasn't boosting viewership. In fact, sources inside ESPN say there was even a slight, albeit not dramatic, dip in quarter-hour ratings during his appearances on NFL-specific programming.

Even if one believed Clark was a better NFL analyst than some of his peers, he would have had to be significantly better to offset that lengthy list of negatives.

This isn't a Ryan Clark-specific phenomenon. Very few people in sports media provide a quantifiable net positive.

Pat McAfee does. Michael Wilbon and Tony Kornheiser do. Colin Cowherd does. Stephen A. Smith did for many years, though his star has undeniably faded over the past nine months.

Others create value by elevating the people around them. Rece Davis, Ernie Johnson, Laura Rutledge and Mike Greenberg each fit that description.

A select group also matters because they consistently break news. Adam Schefter, Ian Rapoport and Brian Windhorst belong in that category. (Tom Pelissero, whom ESPN mistakenly let go this week, belonged there as well.)

Then there are the elite game analysts and play-by-play voices. Joe Buck and Troy Aikman are ESPN's two most valuable on-air talents by a wide margin. Mike Breen and Kirk Herbstreit are top five.

ESPN analyst Cam Newton appears on the set of "First Take" in San Francisco, Calif. (Aaron M. Sprecher/Getty Images)

EXCLUSIVE: ESPN TO PART WAYS WITH CAM NEWTON AS PART OF TALENT LAYOFFS THAT ALSO INCLUDED RYAN CLARK

As large as their egos may be, the overwhelming majority of people at ESPN — or any major television network — are simply filling airtime. They are interchangeable. There is no meaningful financial difference between having one analyst versus another.

That's not to say these analysts and reporters aren't good at their jobs. Many are. It's simply that their value above replacement is essentially nonexistent. The same is true across corporate America. The gap between good and indispensable is significant.

The difference is that most working Americans understand they're replaceable. Apparently, many former ESPN personalities did not.

Put simply, Ryan Clark wasn't good enough to shame people for marrying White women, create unnecessary drama on and off the air, and repeatedly make factually dubious statements about law enforcement and politics. He didn't provide enough value to justify the baggage.

And contrary to what Jemele Hill and Emmanuel Acho have argued, Clark was never a central reason for NFL Live's recent success. He was a rotational contributor.

Elle Duncan wasn't valuable enough to turn ESPN programming into a platform for protesting political legislation or discussing abortion. Bomani Jones failed far too many times to justify the inflammatory remarks he routinely made. Mark Jones wasn't nearly talented enough to falsely accuse police officers of trying to kill him. David Dennis Jr. isn't important enough to repeatedly embarrass the network with racial idolatry and his commentary about Caitlin Clark. Cam Newton wasn't valuable enough to offset the buffoonery and his disparagement of women with children.

Ryan Clark is being let go by ESPN after more than 11 years with the network, OutKick has learned. (Ric Tapia/Getty Images)

There's now a narrative that ESPN is abandoning "woke" programming because people politically aligned with OutKick are gaining influence inside the company following the latest layoffs. That framing lacks important context.

Sure, there are employees at ESPN who agree with OutKick. There are also plenty who don't. That's true of virtually every major media company.

Despite what some media reporters have claimed, ESPN's recent layoffs do not signal a shift toward conservative politics and White commentators. ESPN is trying to eliminate headaches, as in people who create unnecessary controversy, internal friction and negative publicity. It has nothing to do with political preference.

There was a time when that was not true. Former ESPN head John Skipper proudly platformed people with no experience covering sports, but who were willing to talk about race, politics, and talk down to the average sports fan. Current ESPN CEO Jimmy Pitaro took a similar approach in the aftermath of George Floyd's death in 2020 amid pressure from parent company Disney.

Today, ESPN truly just wants to cover sports. Of course, there are still many more people it must weed out to accomplish that mission. David Dennis Jr., Ogwumike, Monica McNutt, Holly Rowe, and Nicole Briscoe are among them.

According to multiple sources inside the company, ESPN's preferred direction is straightforward: elevate people who enjoy sports, stick to sports, and entertainingly talk about sports. It sounds so obvious. Yet fewer personalities consistently fit that description than one might think.

Sources say ESPN is high on the following personalities moving forward: Pat McAfee, Peter Schrager, Laura Rutledge, Josh Pate, Will Compton, Taylor Lewan, Mina Kimes, Jason McCourty, Ian Rapoport, Stacey Dales, Malika Andrews, Kyle Brandt and Kendrick Perkins.

Personally, I wouldn't put every name on that list in the category of moderate voices who avoid unnecessary political or racial detours. Several have histories that suggest otherwise. ESPN apparently sees it differently, at least for now.

Regardless, the company reached the right conclusion on Ryan Clark. He had become far more trouble than he was worth. The network is better off without him. He had made it too difficult to justify employing him.

Some quick-hitting thoughts on ESPN's layoffs:

1) I've seen speculation from Rob Parker and others that Cam Newton's interview with Jason Whitlock — during which he discussed Stephen A. Smith's fraudulent claims in his memoir — played a role in his ousting. The theory is that Smith was so upset Newton appeared with Whitlock that he pushed ESPN to remove him.

For one, Smith doesn't have that kind of power. He wanted ESPN to keep Ryan Clark, Shannon Sharpe and Michael Irvin. The network parted ways with all three. My understanding is that ESPN simply concluded Newton was overpaid for someone who contributed to "First Take" just twice a week.

Further, many of ESPN's top decision-makers agree with Whitlock about Stephen A.

2) ESPN essentially runs unchallenged when it comes to NBA breaking news. Since Adrian Wojnarowski's retirement, Shams Charania has had no real competition.

The network could have built that same kind of advantage in the NFL. Instead, it laid off Tom Pelissero this week. That was a mistake.

On X, Underdog NFL cites nearly every major NFL scoop within seconds of it being reported. Three insiders consistently break the biggest stories: Adam Schefter, Ian Rapoport and Pelissero. Pelissero may not have the following of Schefter or Rapoport, but he breaks just as much news. He's also an excellent guest host on The Rich Eisen Show.

Wherever he lands, Pelissero is going to take scoops away from ESPN. CBS, Fox, NBC, Amazon, Netflix and The Athletic should all look to hire him.

3) Look for ESPN to announce a new 2 p.m. show in the coming week with Peter Schrager as the host.

title: ‘Revenge of the normies’: Halo veteran-turned-candidate pitches outsider revolt in Nevada swing district
excerpt:
Marty O'Donnell says independent voters are embracing 'common sense' after years of what he calls left-wing 'nonsense'

Marty O'Donnell spent decades creating some of the most iconic music in video ga...
body_text:
Marty O'Donnell says independent voters are embracing 'common sense' after years of what he calls left-wing 'nonsense'

Marty O'Donnell spent decades creating some of the most iconic music in video game history. Now, the Halo composer is devoting his creative energies to an entirely different type of project: a conservative upset in one of America's most competitive swing districts.

Speaking with Fox News Digital at Freedom Fest in Las Vegas, the Nevada Republican candidate explained how he plans to bring his real-world business experience and innovation to a Washington perennially clogged with career politicians.

GAMING-WORLD VETERAN WHO RIPPED ‘WOKE’ CULTURE SCORES TRUMP-BACKED BATTLEGROUND PRIMARY WIN

Composer Marty O'Donnell, known for his work on the original Halo games, is shown alongside artwork for Halo: Campaign Evolved. (Playstation )

"How did being a composer prepare me to run for Congress? Well, frankly, I don't think it does. But I don't think anything prepares anybody to run for Congress. What I'm tired of…people who have their entire career in politics. They have never made anything, they don't really know anything, and we don't need more people like that.

"I've had an entire career creating stuff, shipping stuff, working with other teams, leading teams, making businesses, making deals. That's what I think prepares me for Congress."

O'Donnell puts basic kitchen-table economics at the foundation of his outsider bid for Nevada's third district:

"I really believe that it's time to get back to a place where one good job is enough…enough to raise a family, buy a home, get your kids a good education, take a vacation. That is the way I grew up. That's the America I grew up in. And there's no reason why that shouldn't be able to come back."

He has a fighting shot, but in a district long represented by 4-term Democrat Susie Lee, he needs to hone his appeal to political independents:

Rep. Susie Lee, D-Nev., arrives to the U.S. Capitol before the House passed the budget resolution on Thursday, April 10, 2025.  (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

"The independent part of Nevada, and especially the district I'm in, CD3, is huge.

"The left has gone so far left that the only thing that we need to be able to present to the people who are in the middle is that we believe in common sense solutions. We believe in common sense. Not nonsense.

‘HALO’ VIDEO GAME MUSIC COMPOSER AIMS TO REARRANGE WASHINGTON

"You know, transing your kids, boys in girls' sports, having open borders, the ever-growing federal government and fraud, that all has to stop and I think people are ready for that, and I think the independents are ready for that."

With a business background and a common sense approach, O'Donnell argues that his mainstream appeal will carry him through in the Las Vegas suburbs.

"Well Las Vegas is a town that has grown up being all about hospitality and casinos and hotels and entertainment, but it's also becoming a high-tech place.

Las Vegas also ranked high on the list of the most fun cities in America.  (iStock)

"I think the business community here, especially in the district I'm from, which is on the northwest side of Las Vegas, is ready to just explode. Summerlin area, there are all sorts of business opportunities there. And it's unique because it's a diverse community. We have a huge Asian population, Hispanic, people who have retired, people who work in hospitality and are part of unions.

"But these people are, are family people and family people are what I consider to be normal people and I think this midterm could be the revenge of the normies. I think normal people are going to come out and say they've had enough with the crazies."

O'Donnell strongly supports Donald Trump's foreign policy and is not adverse to military action to topple the longstanding communist regime in Havana:

"I am absolutely ready for Cuba to have the same fate that Venezuela had. The people of Cuba have been suppressed almost my entire lifetime. It's time for Cuba to be free again. The people of Cuba deserve to be free. Fidel Castro died a long time ago.

U.S. President-elect Donald Trump’s nominee for Secretary of State, Sen. Marco Rubio (R-FL) testifies during his Senate Foreign Relations confirmation hearing at Dirksen Senate Office Building on Jan. 15, 2025 in Washington, D.C. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images)

"There are so many smart, successful people from Cuba, especially in Florida, Marco being one of them, of course. And it's time. We don't necessarily need to do a military intervention, but if that's what needs to happen, I would be all for it because, I think the administration knows what needs to happen in Cuba, and I'm looking forward to a free Cuba over the next year."

Finally, O'Donnell pledges to hold the line on spending and not fall victim to the establishment GOP drift to big government spending:

"My plan is to take the ring of power and throw it into the fires of Mount Doom. That's a little shout-out to my nerd friends. Listen, the government is too big. There was a point where President Clinton said 'the era of big government is over.' Obviously, he didn't mean it, but it appeals to people still to this day.

"There's so much fraud, there's so much waste in big government. How is it not time for the government to balance its budget, to cut... 10 percent, at least, of every program it has and go after all the fraud and waste that is just rampant? We cannot continue to spend like drunken sailors. We can't continue to increase the budgets and tax the people. It's just unacceptable."

David Unsworth reports on Latin America. You can follow David Unsworth on Twitter @LatinAmerUpdate

title: House Republicans to write $95bn bill to fund Iran war and aid farmers
excerpt:
GOP-controlled chamber paves way for reconciliation bill, while making long-shot attempt at new voting restrictions

House Republicans on Wednesday agreed to begin the process of writing a $95bn bill ...
body_text:
GOP-controlled chamber paves way for reconciliation bill, while making long-shot attempt at new voting restrictions

House Republicans on Wednesday agreed to begin the process of writing a $95bn bill to fund the war with Iran as well as provide new aid to farmers, while making a long-shot attempt to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.

In a virtually party-line 216-214, the Republican-controlled chamber passed a budget resolution that paves the way for the creation of a reconciliation bill, which can advance through the Senate under special rules that circumvent the Democratic filibuster and requires only a simple majority to pass. Two Republicans – Warren Davidson of Ohio and Thomas Massie of Kentucky, as well as Kevin Kiley, an independent of California – joined all Democrats in opposing the measure.

The resolution’s passage comes amid an intensification of the conflict with Iran, which Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, told the Senate on Tuesday had thus far cost $37.5bn. The Trump administration last month asked Congress to authorize approximately $88bn in supplemental funding, the majority of which – up to $67.1bn – will go to pay for the war.

The budget resolution passed by the House largely answers that call by authorizing the armed services committee to propose $60bn in spending for the fiscal years through 2036, and the intelligence committee an additional $13bn. The $73bn sum is expected to go towards military operations against Iran.

The agriculture committee has been allocated $12bn in spending changes, which it is expected to propose using on measures to help farmers rocked by the global effects of the conflict with Iran, as well as Trump’s tariff regimen against major trading partners.

The House administration committee authorized a further $10bn, which is set to be used to make an unlikely effort to pass the Save America Act into law. The measure, championed by Donald Trump, would ban mail-in ballots nationwide and impose enhanced strict identification requirements on voters when they cast ballots and register.

But legislation seeking to advance through the Senate under the rules of budget reconciliation can only deal with spending, revenue and the debt limit, making it likely that the Save America Act’s policy changes will be stripped out when the chamber takes up the measure.

Mike Johnson nodded to that fact on Tuesday, when he told reporters the goal of the bill was to pass the Save America Act into law “as much of that as possible”.

“As always, Republicans support our troops, our farmers and election integrity and we will demonstrate that again this week,” the Republican House speaker said.

The budget resolution is the third attempt Republicans have made in Trump’s second term to use the party-line reconciliation procedure to enact their agenda. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed last year, was a reconciliation bill, as was the measure the GOP approved last month that funded agencies involved in Trump’s mass deportation campaign through the remainder of his term.

Democrats blasted the budget proposal, with Hakeem Jeffries saying before the vote: “President Trump and Republicans in this House continue to act indifferent to the economic wellbeing of the American people and to actively do things that are hurting everyday Americans financially, like this reckless and costly war of choice.”

“Republican extremists apparently have money for wars, but can’t feed the poor,” the House minority leader added.

The House’s approval of the resolution came two days before the chamber recesses until the end of August. But John Thune, the Senate’s Republican majority leader, signaled no urgency in passing the blueprint.

At a press conference on Tuesday, he said he would “hold” the measure in case negotiations with Democrats on funding the government break down before the 30 September deadline. If that happens, Thune said, the Senate could repurpose it to pass a stopgap funding measure along party lines.

Even if that scenario were avoided, the budget resolution could face headwinds in the Senate Republican conference, where some lawmakers have blanched because it does not bring total defense spending to the $1.5tn the Trump administration has requested for the 2027 fiscal year. Others have objected to the lack of offsets to the new spending the bill will approve, amid the US’s rising debt load.

title: Homan blasts Dem hypocrisy on deportations, recalls past when 'Obama gave me an award' for enforcing border
excerpt:
On ‘The Katie Miller Podcast,’ border czar Tom Homan argued that his past award from former President Barack Obama for deportations proves that modern Democrats are hypocrites on border policy.

Borde...
body_text:
On ‘The Katie Miller Podcast,’ border czar Tom Homan argued that his past award from former President Barack Obama for deportations proves that modern Democrats are hypocrites on border policy.

Border czar Tom Homan argued on Tuesday’s episode of "The Katie Miller Podcast" that commonsense border security was hailed during past administrations but is demonized by current Democrats.

Podcaster Katie Miller, the wife of White House deputy chief of staff for policy Stephen Miller, asked Homan, "Why do you think American society has moved to saying that deportations are a bad thing when, 20 years ago, even Democrats were for them. And you were able to do your job so much more easily, deport tons of people without so much as, you know, anyone caring?"

"I really think it has to do with our president and the hatred for this president," Homan replied. "They didn't want to see this president succeed. And I truly believe that because I actually got an award from President Obama for removing the record number of illegal aliens. President Obama gave me an award because we removed 409,000 people that year.

"What's turned the tide is the false rhetoric, hateful rhetoric by the media, you know, about the inhumane policies of the Trump administration," he continued. "That's why I'm out there every day saying, ‘Wait a minute now, inhumanity?’

DEM REP SETH MOULTON SAYS PARTY HAS NO IMMIGRATION PLAN, IS ONLY ‘OPPOSITION TO TRUMP’

White House border czar Tom Homan blasted modern media and politicians for condemning ICE. (Andrew Harnik/Getty Images)

"Let's talk about Joe Biden for a minute," Homan began. "When he opened the border up, over 4,000 aliens died making that journey. Historic record. Quarter million Americans died from fentanyl that came across the open border. Historic record. Don't talk about humanity. He was killing Americans and aliens at record numbers. President Trump has illegal immigration down 96%. Which means 96% less people are coming."

Under Trump’s administration, he asked, "How many women aren't being raped by the cartels? How many pounds of fentanyl isn't getting in the country? How many known suspected terrorists [aren’t] coming to the country. Securing the border is the most humane thing you can do."

Miller noted how hard it is for the typical working mom to look past the rhetoric of deportations and realize how normal and acceptable this work was in the past.

"You were able to go and deport these families, remove them together. You were able to do these deportations, and largely society thought that was a good thing," she said. "You had Harry Reid on the Senate floor back in the '90s saying that birthright citizenship was an insane concept. Why is it that society has moved so far?"

Homan replied that, among the many reasons, "I think the media has moved people far to the left because of their false reporting. I think the colleges — I speak from my own experience with one of my sons — that they move people to the far left. Look, I read the press releases every day. Ninety-eight percent of it's negative, right? They misstate what we're actually doing."

HOUSE REPUBLICANS MOVE TO MAKE TRUMP'S BORDER POLICIES PERMANENT

The deportation policies of former President Barack Obama, seen as a progressive liberal in his time, have been used as a yardstick to show how far left the modern Democratic Party has gone. (Toni L. Sandys/The Washington Post via Getty Images)

He went on to argue that the media and various politicians have normalized noncompliance with ICE to the point it encourages violent clashes.

"But between the media and members of Congress who will spew lies about ICE, if anybody's terrorizing immigrant communities, it’s representatives of Congress who say that ‘ICE are fascists. They're the Nazis. They're the secret police,’ which is why I think some of the recent tragedies have happened," Homan said.

"People think it's OK not to comply with ICE now because they're hearing over and over again they're not a real law enforcement agency. So, you can impede, you can interfere, you don't have to comply with them," Homan warned.

"I said yesterday — I took a lot of hate from the left — the recent shootings, as tragic as they were, if they simply would have complied with federal law enforcement agencies on the road stops, they probably wouldn't be dead.

"I don't think they deserve to die, but if they would have simply complied with law enforcement, tragedy can be avoided."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

White House border czar Tom Homan suggested that Democrats and the media, by using hyperbolic rhetoric about ICE, have encouraged people to refuse to comply with agents, leading to violent encounters. (Reuters/Mike Blake)

"The Katie Miller Podcast" can be found on YouTube here.

Alexander Hall is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Alexander.hall@fox.com.

title: California toddler bitten by rattlesnake ‘hiding under’ Amazon package
excerpt:
Three-year-old girl landed in ICU after snake bit her while grabbing a package outside her family’s Santa Clarita home

A three-year-old girl landed in the intensive care unit earlier this month after...
body_text:
Three-year-old girl landed in ICU after snake bit her while grabbing a package outside her family’s Santa Clarita home

A three-year-old girl landed in the intensive care unit earlier this month after a rattlesnake bit her while she was trying to grab an Amazon package.

Addy, the young girl, ran out with her mother on 13 July to pick up a package dropped over the gate to their home in Santa Clarita, north of Los Angeles.

“Tragically, a southern pacific rattlesnake was hiding under the brown padded envelope and bit Addy’s left hand,” her father, Manny Barajas, wrote in a fundraising campaign note.

Addy’s mother, Lynn, took the girl to meet first responders at a nearby grocery store parking lot, who took her to hospital and administered a dose of anti-venom. The hospital then transferred her to another facility with a pediatric ICU, where she received two more doses of anti-venom, according to Barajas.

“She’s doing great now,” Barajas told KTLA, adding: “She’s still bruised on her left hand where she was bitten. Her spirits are high, her demeanor is great, but she keeps asking why she was bitten by a snake.”

Rattlesnake bites have become more common in California this year, with heatwaves since March increasing snake activity. Three people have died this year, which is about two more than usual. The California poison control system has received reports of 277 bites so far this year, according to the Los Angeles Times – almost as many as it normally received in an entire year.

Rattlesnakes typically avoid humans, but will bite when they feel threatened, cornered or surprised.

California poison control and the Los Angeles county fire department did not immediately return requests for comment.

title: Tesla’s profits slide despite growing revenue as it pivots to robotics and AI
excerpt:
Shares in Elon Musk company fall more 3% in after-hours trading, as earnings per share miss Wall Street expectations

Tesla reported its second-quarter earnings on Wednesday, disclosing far lower prof...
body_text:
Shares in Elon Musk company fall more 3% in after-hours trading, as earnings per share miss Wall Street expectations

Tesla reported its second-quarter earnings on Wednesday, disclosing far lower profits than expected. The company’s already beleaguered stock, which had fallen about 14% this year to date, dipped further following the earnings report.

Elon Musk’s automaker, once the pinnacle of his tech empire, has taken a back seat to SpaceX. Musk’s rocket and AI company held the largest stock market debut in history last month, turning the richest man on Earth into the world’s first trillionaire, though his net worth has since fallen from its peak.

Tesla revealed earnings of 31 cents per share, a measurement of profits divided by the number of outstanding shares, less than the 51 cents per share Wall Street predicted. Its revenue was $28.23bn against an expected $25.71bn. Shares in the company fell over 3% in after-hours trading immediately after the numbers were released. Last year’s second-quarter earnings were equally shaky.

SpaceX and Tesla stock has slumped this year, with the rocket company down about 26% since its debut. Tesla has, meanwhile, suffered from the end of electric vehicle tax subsidies in the US last year and increased competition from cheaper Chinese automakers.

Although Tesla missed revenue expectations in its last quarterly earnings report, the company revealed earlier this month that it exceeded Wall Street’s predictions for its second-quarter auto sales. The stark turnaround was driven largely by sales in Europe, where electric vehicle subsidies are still in place and gas prices have surged as a result of the US-Iran war, leading consumers to buy electric.

Tesla’s vehicle sales are no longer as crucial to its market performance as they once were, however, as the company has pivoted towards bets on robotics, autonomous driving and AI. Musk claimed last year that Tesla’s Optimus robot, which has not yet entered widespread production and already faces a slew of Chinese competitors, would be the biggest product of all time and end poverty.

The company’s driverless taxi service, Robotaxi, has become a major focus for Tesla as a potential new line of revenue. Tesla announced earlier in the week that it would add Tampa and Orlando to where Robotaxi can operate. The service is currently available in parts of Austin, Dallas, Houston and Miami.

Musk has for years declared that the autonomous driving service will have almost infinite demand and claimed that millions of the self-driving cars would soon be on US roads. As is frequently the case with Musk’s promises, he has failed to reach those lofty targets and rollout has been slow.

Only about 50 Robotaxis now operate in Austin, where Tesla launched the service.

title: Johnson scores major win as House GOP advances Trump’s top election priority
excerpt:
Eric Shawn reports on the stunning revelation that thousands of non-citizens were improperly registered to vote in New Jersey due to a software error, with nearly 400 casting ballots.

Speaker Mike Jo...
body_text:
Eric Shawn reports on the stunning revelation that thousands of non-citizens were improperly registered to vote in New Jersey due to a software error, with nearly 400 casting ballots.

Speaker Mike Johnson’s plan to bypass Democratic opposition on a key election integrity bill took a major step forward in the House of Representatives on Wednesday.

It’s a major victory for Johnson, R-La., who had to overcome a wall of left-wing opposition and threats of a rebellion within his own GOP ranks.House Republicans are working to move the Safeguarding American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act as part of a larger $95 billion budget bill that, if successful, would completely sideline Democrats to implement election reforms sought by President Donald Trump.

They’re using a mechanism called budget reconciliation, which allows the party in power to pass sweeping fiscal policy by lowering the Senate’s threshold for advancement from 60 votes to a simple majority. In exchange, however, the legislation is subject to intense scrutiny under a strict set of guidelines about what actually counts as true fiscal policy.

Speaker of the House Mike Johnson, R-La., talks with reporters in the U.S. Capitol on June 10, 2026. (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

The bill up for consideration on Wednesday was just the first House-wide step in passing a reconciliation package, which is adopting a framework with spending instructions for relevant committees who will then cobble individual portions together into a massive piece of legislation. Both the House and Senate must pass identical versions before it goes to the president’s desk.

All but three members of the House Republican Conference supported the measure: Reps. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., Warren Davidson, R-Ohio, and Kevin Kiley, I-Calif., voted against it.

The framework was adopted in a narrow 216 to 214 vote, with all Democrats opposed.

REPORTER'S NOTEBOOK: HOUSE'S FINAL SPRINT PUTS SAVE AMERICA ACT AND IRAN FUNDING IN PLAY

It comes after fiscal hawks warned for days that they are uneasy with the lack of budget cuts in the framework to offset the $95 billion in new spending.

Rep. Chip Roy, R-Texas, said he was leaning in support but still undecided.

Donald Trump speaks to members of the media outside the Oval Office of the White House in Washington, D.C., U.S., on Monday, April 13, 2026. (Salwan Georges/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

"People wonder why the so-called 3AM caucus exists and why we hold our votes to the last minute to put pressure on Republicans in Congress to deliver? It’s because they revert to the mean — which is spending, amnesty, unlimited immigration and political show votes. I want FULL SAVE. I want this unpaid-for spending to be paid for in the 4th reconciliation bill. I want clarity on our war mission while we are backfilling munitions," he told Fox News Digital.

TOP TRUMP OFFICIAL MAKES NEW SAVE ACT CASE TO REPUBLICANS BEHIND CLOSED DOORS

Rep. Eric Burlison, R-Mo., had told reporters, "I just wanted to let them know if you're doing a whip count right now, I wouldn't put me in the yes column…I'm not 100% convinced that the SAVE language is going to be held solid through the Senate. In fact, I would bet against it. I just, I don't have any confidence that the Senate will keep that language tight."

Both voted in favor of adopting the framework, however.

With just a razor-thin majority, Johnson could lose no more than than two Republicans to still pass anything on partisan lines.

The budget plans for for $73 billion aimed at the U.S. war with Iran, $12 billion for aid to American farmers, and $10 billion for states to implement election integrity measures in the SAVE America Act — namely federal voter ID rules and a proof of citizenship mandate to register to vote.

U.S. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, Democrat from New York, speaks before an upcoming House vote on funding for the US Department of Homeland Security, on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., on March 5, 2026. (Brendan Smialowski / AFP via Getty Images)

Democrats and some Republicans have been wary about funding the Iran war without additional oversight from Congress. And Democrats in both the House and Senate have been vehemently opposed to the SAVE America Act, calling it tantamount to voter suppression.

But Republicans have near-uniformly argued that it is aimed at enhancing confidence in American elections.

Trump posted on Truth Social on Tuesday, "Thank you to all of the House Republicans who are supporting the first step towards getting as much of THE SAVE AMERICA ACT, as possible, in the Budget Bill, and to our Great Speaker, Mike Johnson. I am asking all House Republicans to VOTE YES on this very important Budget Resolution. I am watching the vote very closely, and hope Republicans can UNIFY to secure Elections against all forms of Foreign Country Interference, and Illegal Alien voting."

Elizabeth Elkind is a politics reporter for Fox News Digital leading coverage of the House of Representatives. Previous digital bylines seen at Daily Mail and CBS News.

Follow on Twitter at @liz_elkind and send tips to elizabeth.elkind@fox.com

title: House passes $1.5tn defense bill despite standoff over Israel and Trump’s voter agenda
excerpt:
Passage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approval

The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-p...
body_text:
Passage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approval

The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.

A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with all but six Democrats opposing its passage. Seven House Republicans broke ranks to oppose it. The measure now goes to the Senate.

Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military.

“For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote.

But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed.

Democrats also soured on the bill after Mike Johnson, the Republican House speaker, acceded to the demands of rightwing lawmakers and inserted into the legislation the Save America Act, a Trump-backed proposal to ban mail-in ballots and impose new identification restrictions on voters at the polls and when they register.

The twin dynamics combined to make the Democrats’ opposition nearly unanimous two days before House lawmakers are scheduled to depart Washington DC for a recess that stretches until the end of August.

Another sticking point for Democrats was the measure’s overall cost of $1.15tn, and the plan to slash funding elsewhere in the government a year after Congress’s Republican majorities approved extending an array of lower tax rates indefinitely.

“Our country faces threats and DoD has modernization needs, but I cannot and do not support meeting these needs by making massive cuts to critically important domestic programs and slashing taxes,” said Adam Smith, the top Democrat on the House armed services committee.

“We need to be more fiscally responsible and DoD needs to become more innovative and efficient in terms of developing capabilities to meet our national security needs in a more cost-effective manner.”

Amid a wider backlash within the party over US support for Israel, some Democrats said they opposed the NDAA because of its inclusion of provisions that would deepen cooperation between the two countries’ defense industries.

“This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy,” said the progressive congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, who was among the no votes.

The House’s passage of the bill came after the chamber considered dozens of amendments. While many were agreed to unanimously, others offered barometers for how far the Republican-controlled chamber was willing to go on the most divisive issues before the parties.

A proposal by the Republican Eli Crane to halt all military aid to Ukraine was resoundingly defeated by a 76-350 vote, while an amendment from Republican Lauren Boebert to codify Trump’s executive order banning transgender service members failed by a 212-217 tally.

However, the House agreed to amendments from Nancy Mace barring the military health insurer from offering some gender-related care, and banning transgender athletes from girls’ sports at defense department-run schools.

Beyond the NDAA, House Republicans are also moving forward with a party-line attempt to authorize $60bn in new military spending, much of which is expected to cover costs related to the war with Iran. But that proposal falls far short of the $350bn the Pentagon originally proposed to bring overall defense spending to $1.5tn for the upcoming fiscal year.

title: India’s Rahul Gandhi speaks out after release
excerpt:
India’s Rahul Gandhi speaks out after release

Indian opposition leader Rahul Gandhi has spoken out publicly for the first time since he was arrested during protests in New Delhi, voicing support for ...
body_text:
India’s Rahul Gandhi speaks out after release

Indian opposition leader Rahul Gandhi has spoken out publicly for the first time since he was arrested during protests in New Delhi, voicing support for student demonstrators who are demanding the resignation of the education minister.

title: Fire kills 10 members of same family in Peru, police say
excerpt:
Police said they would maintain a significant presence in the area to protect the property from "criminal acts"

A fire has killed 10 members of the same family, including five children, at a home in ...
body_text:
Police said they would maintain a significant presence in the area to protect the property from "criminal acts"

A fire has killed 10 members of the same family, including five children, at a home in Peru's capital Lima, police say.

The blaze ripped through the property in the southern La Victoria district early on Wednesday, Gen Oscar Arriola from the Peruvian National Police told local media.

Five children, aged between three and 15 years old, were killed, as well as four adults aged between 29 and 72, and a fifth whose age was not given. Two people were also reportedly injured.

Police are investigating reports that the fire was started by arsonists. Local media say the family, who manufactured mattresses and owned electric motorcycles, had received threats and demands for extortion payments.

According to a report by the government news agency Andina, external, neighbours said unknown individuals had set fire to motorcycles parked outside, with the flames spreading rapidly through flammable mattress-making materials.

Seventeen members of the family were in the building when the fire started at 03:00 local time (08:00 GMT) but seven managed to escape, the Infobae news site, external reports.

Listing the exact ages of the victims, the police said the children were aged three, six, six, eight and 15. The adults were aged 29, 35, 42 and 72.

Arriola said their bodies were burned beyond recognition.

"Today, all of Peru falls silent in the face of a tragedy that plunges our nation into mourning," a statement from the National Police of Peru said.

title: Dozens of House Democrats reject bill fusing congressional stock ban with federal voter ID
excerpt: President Donald Trump is joined by children in the Oval Office to ring the New York Stock Exchange and NASDAQ opening bells, marking the launch of Trump Accounts, designed to give American children a...
body_text:
President Donald Trump is joined by children in the Oval Office to ring the New York Stock Exchange and NASDAQ opening bells, marking the launch of Trump Accounts, designed to give American children a stake in the economy.

A vote on legislation to curb lawmakers' ability to profit from the stock market as well as implementing federal voter ID measures divided House Democrats on Wednesday afternoon.

The two measures were combined in a single legislative package after months of debate in the House of Representatives on both issues. While unrelated, both voter ID and banning congressional stock trading have received overwhelming support in multiple public polls.

A whopping 198 House Democrats, including their top leaders, voted against the bills. Just 13 members of their caucus voted with Republicans to pass the measure 231 to 198.

Despite bipartisan negotiations, particularly on banning members of Congress from trading stocks, a significant number of Democrats still opposed the final package — though it passed.

EX-HOUSE DEM WHO RIPPED CONGRESSIONAL STOCK TRADING BAN AS 'BULLS---' CHANGES HER TUNE IN COMEBACK BID

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., spoke during a news conference at the U.S. Capitol on July 13, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

Rep. Zach Nunn, R-Iowa, one of the key supporters of banning lawmakers from the stock market, argued the two issues "go hand in hand."

"One, the overwhelming majority of Americans want this. Two, it's something that we can get passed this week that gets it into the president's hands before the August return-to-district [period]," Nunn told Fox News Digital.

He said of Democrats' criticism, "Only in Washington can you have two wins for 90% of Americans, and Democrats will say that's one win too many."

TRUMP INTERVENES AS HOUSE GOP REVOLT PUTS SAVE AMERICA ACT AT CENTER OF SPENDING SHOWDOWN

Meanwhile, Rep. Stephanie Bice, R-Okla., told Fox News Digital of the stock trading ban specifically, "The American people deserve to know their elected officials are serving the public, not their own financial interests. Members of Congress should be held to the highest standards of transparency and accountability, and this legislation is an important step toward restoring trust in Congress."

Not all Republicans are on board, however. Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., accused his fellow GOP lawmakers of trying to dissuade Democrats from supporting the bill by attaching the voter ID requirement.

"We are voting on a stock trading ban for members of Congress… BUT Republicans added voter-ID in the legislation. Not to get Dems to vote for voter-ID, but to get them to vote against the stock trading ban, so they can use it in the Nov. election," Massie wrote on X. "If we really wanted to ban stock trading for members of Congress, we would offer stand alone legislation and could get a large bipartisan majority, and the bill would have a chance of passing on the Senate."

Rep. Zach Nunn, R-Iowa, speaks during a news conference at the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., on Oct. 15, 2025. (Eric Lee/Bloomberg)

SEN MARK WARNER CALLED OUT IN ABC INTERVIEW FOR INACCURATE VOTER ID CLAIMS

An August 2025 poll from the Pew Research Center showed that 83% of U.S. respondents favored "requiring all voters to show government-issued photo identification to vote."

But Democrats have argued this week that the voter ID portion goes too far, and the stock trading ban portion does not go far enough.

"I've said repeatedly, House Democrats strongly support a stock trading ban for members of Congress. We also support a ban on the president, the vice president and federal judicial officers from trading individual stock as well. But none of that is before us today," Rep. Joe Morelle, D-N.Y., the top Democrat on the committee charged with elections oversight, said during debate on the bill Monday.

"Rather, this bill is a crude combination of two completely unrelated bills, the so-called Stop Insider Trading Act and the Voter ID Act, the latter of which is already a crude reformulation of at least three other failed Republican voter  suppression proposals."

Democrats are criticizing the voter ID portion for its requirement of identification extending to mail-in voting, which they have argued is a route to suppress Americans' ability to cast ballots that way.

Rep. Thomas Massie, a Republican from Kentucky, prior to a news conference outside the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., on Wednesday, Sept. 3, 2025.  (Graeme Sloan/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Left-wing lawmakers have attacked the stock trading bill because it does not require members of Congress to sell their existing stocks but rather bans them from buying more stock in the future.

Lawmakers must also go through a public disclosure process before selling that existing stock.

While it extends the ban on buying new stocks to lawmakers and their families, it effectively allows them to retain a blind trust. Democrats have also complained that it does not extend to the executive branch, namely, President Donald Trump and his family.

Elizabeth Elkind is a politics reporter for Fox News Digital leading coverage of the House of Representatives. Previous digital bylines seen at Daily Mail and CBS News.

Follow on Twitter at @liz_elkind and send tips to elizabeth.elkind@fox.com

title: Chaos erupts as House passes $1.15T defense bill with Israel provision: 'Stop stoking Jew hatred'
excerpt:
'Special Report' anchor Bret Baier discusses the intensifying political divide within the Democratic Party regarding a House bill to cut off aid to Israel.

The House passed its $1.15 trillion annual ...
body_text:
'Special Report' anchor Bret Baier discusses the intensifying political divide within the Democratic Party regarding a House bill to cut off aid to Israel.

The House passed its $1.15 trillion annual defense policy bill Wednesday after a bipartisan revolt over a provision expanding U.S.-Israel defense technology cooperation.

The fiscal year 2027 National Defense Authorization Act passed 216-212, with Republicans largely supporting and Democrats mostly opposing the measure.

The annual bill, which sets Pentagon policy and typically draws broad bipartisan support, received only six Democratic votes as much of the caucus objected to Section 219 and Republican-backed amendments restricting transgender care and easing access to personal guns on military bases.

HOUSE DEMOCRATS FRACTURE BADLY OVER MASSIE AMENDMENT TO CUT $3.3B IN U.S. AID TO ISRAEL

Opposition came from a coalition of progressive Democrats and Republican skeptics of U.S. support for Israel, who accused congressional leaders of shielding the provision from scrutiny after lawmakers were denied a separate vote to remove it.

The fight erupted as support for Israel has weakened among House Democrats and some "America First" Republicans during the war in Gaza and the widening conflict with Iran. Just one week earlier, 103 Democrats joined Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., in voting to block $3.3 billion in U.S. aid to Israel.

For opponents, Section 219 would move the relationship in the opposite direction by embedding U.S.-Israel cooperation more deeply inside the Pentagon’s weapons-development and acquisition system. The provision would direct the war secretary to designate a Pentagon "executive agent" to coordinate and accelerate bilateral defense research, testing and industrial cooperation.

Critics argued that structure would make the relationship harder for future administrations or Congresses to unwind and could shift parts of the partnership away from highly visible aid votes into less visible technology, procurement and production arrangements. Supporters said it would coordinate longstanding programs and give the United States faster access to Israeli defense technology.

The House of Representatives passed the National Defense Authorization Act. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty)

THE US-ISRAEL STRATEGIC ALLIANCE – PARTNERSHIP, NOT CHARITY

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., urged lawmakers to reject the entire defense bill, arguing that the provision would "merge parts of our military with the IDF," referring to the Israel Defense Forces.

"This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy," Ocasio-Cortez wrote on X prior to the vote, referring to Section 219. "Every member of Congress must vote NO."

Rep. Mike Lawler, R-N.Y., shot back on X, "Good grief, you are either obtuse or intentionally lying. We are not merging our military with the IDF. As we do with many of our allies, we enter into cooperative agreements, share intelligence, develop technology and ammunitions, and conduct military exercises together. Stop stoking Jew hatred and do better."

Massie similarly opposed both the rule governing debate and the final bill after Republican leaders denied a floor vote on his bipartisan amendment to strike the provision.

"We should not merge our military tech and supply chain with Israel’s," Massie said on X.

Beyond creating the executive agent, Section 219 would direct the Pentagon to identify jointly developed or Israeli-origin technologies that could be used in U.S. weapons programs and promote joint ventures, licensing agreements and U.S.-based production partnerships with Israeli companies.

Before final passage, the House adopted two amendments from Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla. One removed four uses of the word "integration," while the other extended annual reports to Congress for as long as the executive agent remains in place. The original reporting requirement would have ended in 2030.

The amendments left the initiative’s central structure and cooperative programs intact, falling short of opponents’ demand that Section 219 be removed entirely.

SEN JOHN FETTERMAN PLEDGES TO BE 'LAST MAN STANDING' IN THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN SUPPORT OF ISRAEL

A series of rockets were fired from southern Lebanon over Haifa, located in northern Israel, Sept. 30, 2024. While the rockets were viewed from Haifa, Israel, some of the rockets were destroyed by the Israeli Iron Dome air defense system. (Mostafa Alkharouf/Anadolu via Getty Images)

Massie’s amendment was backed by Democratic Reps. Ro Khanna of California, Jim McGovern of Massachusetts, Jesús "Chuy" García of Illinois and Rashida Tlaib of Michigan.

Section 219 would not combine the countries’ command structures, place U.S. troops under Israeli authority or give Israel control over U.S. military operations. The United States and Israel also already cooperate extensively on defense technology, including the Iron Dome, Arrow and David’s Sling missile-defense systems, counter-drone technology and anti-tunnel capabilities.

The provision’s principal change would be to place a designated Pentagon executive agent in charge of coordinating and expanding those efforts across the War Department.

House Armed Services Committee Chairman Mike Rogers, R-Ala., said the provision would improve oversight and efficiency by assigning responsibility for existing initiatives to a single official.

"In no way does it give away command and control of our military operations, personnel or equipment," Rogers said last month.

Critics argued that the executive agent structure would give U.S.-Israel projects a privileged path through the Pentagon and further entrench Israeli technology and companies in the American defense supply chain.

The fight threatened the broad coalition traditionally needed to pass the NDAA, one of the few major bills Congress has routinely approved with bipartisan support. Democratic opposition was also fueled by Republican amendments restricting gender-affirming medical care for troops and military dependents and transgender participation in sports at Defense Department schools.

The fight threatened the broad coalition traditionally needed to pass the NDAA, one of the few major bills Congress has routinely approved with bipartisan support. (Xavier Piron/Photonews via Getty Images)

The House voted 219-208 Tuesday to prohibit Tricare from covering certain gender-transition procedures and treatments, with five Democrats supporting the amendment and one Republican opposing it.

Lawmakers separately voted 221-203 to prohibit transgender girls from participating in girls sports at War Department schools.

The House rejected a separate attempt by Rep. Lauren Boebert, R-Colo., to codify President Donald Trump’s executive order barring transgender troops from serving in the military. That amendment failed 217-212 after four Republicans joined every Democrat in opposition.

Lawmakers also narrowly approved an amendment making it easier for qualified service members and civilian War Department employees to receive permission to carry personal guns on military installations. The measure passed 215-214.

An amendment that would have largely prohibited Ukraine security assistance failed, though 76 Republicans supported it. The proposal would have preserved funding only for security at the U.S. Embassy in Kyiv.

The underlying NDAA would authorize roughly $1.15 trillion for national defense, including pay raises ranging from 5% to 7% for service members, expanded weapons and munitions production and new investments in missile defense, autonomous systems, hypersonics and other emerging technologies.

The bill would also formally change the Department of Defense’s name to the Department of War, deeming references to the defense secretary and department in federal law to mean the secretary and Department of War.

The NDAA sets defense policy and authorizes spending levels but does not provide the money itself. Congress must separately pass defense appropriations legislation to fund the programs and priorities included in the measure.

Consideration of the legislation had been delayed for three weeks by a separate Republican fight over the SAVE America Act, Trump-backed election legislation requiring documentary proof of citizenship to register to vote and identification to cast a ballot in federal elections.

The House approved the election measure in February, but it stalled in the Senate, where Republicans lacked the 60 votes needed to overcome a filibuster.

Republican leaders revived the legislation through a procedure directing the House clerk to combine it with the NDAA after passage but before sending the package to the Senate. That approach allowed the House to vote on the defense and election measures separately before transmitting them as one bill.

The plan initially collapsed June 30 when 13 Republicans joined Democrats in voting against the procedural rule. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla., and other holdouts later relented after Speaker Mike Johnson promised to attach the election legislation to appropriations bills and other must-pass measures.

The combined defense and election package faces an uncertain future in the Senate, where Democrats blocked consideration of their chamber’s NDAA last week in protest of the Trump administration’s war with Iran.

The debate also comes as the Pentagon seeks additional money to cover the conflict and replenish U.S. weapons stockpiles. The White House has requested an $87.6 billion supplemental package, including $67.1 billion for defense needs, while House Republicans are considering additional military and intelligence funding through a party-line reconciliation package.

title: AOC surges past Buttigieg, leaves Harris in the dust in key 2028 battleground poll
excerpt: Colin Reed, a Republican strategist, and Fox News contributor Richard Fowler analyze Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez's increasing political influence. They discuss her national tour, visiting Pennsylvan...
body_text:
Colin Reed, a Republican strategist, and Fox News contributor Richard Fowler analyze Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez's increasing political influence. They discuss her national tour, visiting Pennsylvania, Georgia, Alabama and Montana, and her potential presidential run in 2028. Fowler highlights her ability to mobilize supporters around key issues, potentially making her a strong contender for leadership.

Progressive firebrand Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has surged to the front of the prospective 2028 Democratic presidential field in New Hampshire, edging Pete Buttigieg and leaving Kamala Harris far behind in a new poll.

Ocasio-Cortez drew 22% support among likely Democratic primary voters in the University of New Hampshire Granite State Poll released Wednesday, one point ahead of former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg and 17 points ahead of former Vice President Kamala Harris.

Ocasio-Cortez gained seven points since the February survey, while Harris and California Gov. Gavin Newsom lost ground — another sign of the New York lawmaker’s growing strength among Democratic voters in the early presidential battleground.

MIDTERM BATTLE FOR THE HOUSE: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez speaks during a news conference on April 29, 2026, outside the U.S. Capitol. (Tom Brenner/Getty Images)

Ocasio-Cortez has been coy about her 2028 plans and careful not to rule out either a White House run in the race to succeed term-limited President Donald Trump, or a bid for the Senate against longtime Democratic Leader Sen. Chuck Schumer, who is up for re-election in two years. When asked about a run for higher office, Ocasio-Cortez recently said her goal is to "change the country."

Buttigieg, a moderate who as the former mayor of South Bend, Indiana, came in second in the 2020 New Hampshire presidential primary, has crisscrossed the country in support of fellow Democrats the past year as speculation builds around a possible 2028 run.

"Nearly half of socialists and one-third of progressives support Ocasio-Cortez, while pluralities of liberals and moderates support Buttigieg," Andrew Smith, the director of the University of New Hampshire Survey Center, said.

KAMALA HARRIS' TRAVELS AND COMMENTS CLEARLY POINT TO A 2028 RUN

Former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg greets patrons during a stop at a restaurant in Manchester, New Hampshire, on Feb. 19, 2026. (Paul Steinhauser/Fox News)

Sen. Mark Kelly of Arizona, a retired astronaut, stood in third place in the poll, at 9%, with term-limited Newsom a point back. Newsom's support dropped seven points since the February survey.

Independent Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont, the 2016 and 2020 runner-up in the Democratic presidential primaries, stood at 6%. The 84-year-old Sanders, asked about another White House run, said last month, "I suspect that's not going to happen." He jokingly added, "I know I look like I'm 30. I am not."

Meanwhile, Harris, the party's 2024 nominee after she replaced then-President Joe Biden, who dropped out less than four months before the election following a disastrous debate against Trump, grabbed only 5% support in the new poll, down from 10% in February.

While the former vice president's numbers in the new New Hampshire survey are nothing to brag about, she tops an average of the latest national polls in the hypothetical race for the 2028 Democratic nomination. Surveys in the early stages of a presidential cycle are often heavily influenced by the name recognition of the potential candidates.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Former Vice President Kamala Harris speaks with patrons during a stop at Crave restaurant ahead of a South Carolina Democratic Party fundraiser on April 15, 2026, in Greenville, S.C (Meg Kinnard/AP Photo)

Everyone else in the large field of potential contenders tested in the new New Hampshire poll registered at 2% or less.

New Hampshire held the first-in-the-nation presidential primary for a century, before Democrats upended their nominating calendar in the 2024 cycle, moving South Carolina to the lead-off position. The Democratic National Committee's (DNC) Rules and Bylaws panel is likely on Friday to unveil a new calendar for the 2028 cycle, which would be voted on at the DNC's summer meeting in Austin, Texas, next month.

Republicans have not altered their calendar, keeping the Iowa caucuses as their lead-off state, followed by New Hampshire.

In the race for the GOP presidential nomination, 36% of likely Republican primary voters in New Hampshire said they would support Vice President JD Vance, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio at 26%.

Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio during a meeting at the White House on March 17, 2026. (Yuri Gripas/Abaca/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky, who ran for the nomination in 2016 and is mulling another bid, stood at 8% in the survey, along with former ambassador and former South Carolina Gov. Nikki Haley, who was the runner-up to Trump in 2024.

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis, who also bid for the nomination in 2024, grabbed 6% support, with everyone else tested at 4% or less.

Paul Steinhauser covers the national campaign trail from coast to coast for Fox News

title: First lady Melania Trump files motion for sanctions against Michael Wolff over tossed lawsuit
excerpt:
Melania Trump publicly denies ties to Jeffrey Epstein, calling for an end to disgraceful lies and demanding congressional action for survivors. The first ladys senior advisor Marc Beckman explains.

F...
body_text:
Melania Trump publicly denies ties to Jeffrey Epstein, calling for an end to disgraceful lies and demanding congressional action for survivors. The first ladys senior advisor Marc Beckman explains.

First lady Melania Trump filed a motion for sanctions on Tuesday against journalist Michael Wolff.

Wolff unsuccessfully sued Melania Trump to keep her from suing him for $1 billion for alleged defamatory statements he made about her involvement with Jeffrey Epstein. Earlier this year, a federal judge tossed Wolff’s lawsuit against the first lady, calling it a "contorted" effort and "not how the federal courts work."

Melania Trump is now seeking costs and reasonable attorneys’ fees incurred in these proceedings, along with other appropriate relief, saying Wolff’s lawsuit was "a fundraising drive and a political spectacle."

First lady Melania Trump filed a motion for sanctions on Tuesday against journalist Michael Wolff.  (AP Photo/Julia Demaree Nikhinson; Lorenzo Bevilaqua/Disney General Entertainment Content via Getty Images)

Trump’s lawyer, Alejandro Brito, feels sanctions are warranted for at least three independent reasons. Brito accused Wolff of manufacturing a jurisdictional dispute in an attempt to keep the case in New York state court, using litigation for improper purposes and making a "frivolous" claim.

"Plaintiff and his counsel asserted a legally frivolous claim, propped it up with factual contentions that lacked any evidentiary support, and used them to run a political and commercial campaign. They should pay for Mrs. Trump’s fees and expenses incurred for the process they abused," the first lady’s legal team claims.

The first lady’s legal team said Wolff wrongly claimed she was heavily involved in the Epstein scandal. Her team sent a pre-action notice under Florida’s anti-defamation law identifying defamatory statements and demanding a retraction and apology. According to the court filing, Wolff never answered the demand letter, instead filing the unsuccessful lawsuit against Melania Trump.

JUDGE TOSSES MICHAEL WOLFF'S LAWSUIT AGAINST MELANIA TRUMP, CALLS IT 'CONTORTED' AND NOT HOW 'COURTS WORK'

First lady Melania Trump has denied any association with Jeffrey Epstein. (Jacquelyn Martin/AP Photo)

"Plaintiff filed a patently meritless lawsuit and used it to harass Mrs. Trump in public. The requested sanctions do not depend on a ruling that Plaintiff’s statements were defamatory. They rest on the unsupported and mischaracterized federal submissions, the use of those papers for improper purposes, counsel’s multiplication of the federal proceedings, and the Court’s findings that the action itself was abusively presented as textbook bad-faith forum-shopping. Those independent grounds support sanctions against Plaintiff and his counsel," the first lady’s legal team argued.

Wolff, a controversial author known in media circles for his divisive reporting ethics, has written four bestsellers about the president: "Fire and Fury," "Siege," "Landslide," and "All or Nothing."

The first lady has denied any association with Epstein, who died behind bars in 2019 while awaiting trial on child sex trafficking charges. His death was reported as a suicide.

DAILY BEAST APOLOGIZES TO MELANIA TRUMP OVER RETRACTED EPSTEIN ALLEGATIONS ARTICLE

Michael Wolff is caught up in a legal battle with first lady Melania Trump. (Jared Siskin/Patrick McMullan)

The Daily Beast retracted an article last summer called "Melania Trump ‘Very Involved’ in Epstein Scandal: Author," that was based on an interview with Wolff, after the outlet received a letter from Brito.

Wolff did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Fox News Digital’s Brie Stimson contributed to this report.

Brian Flood is a media editor/reporter for FOX News Digital. Story tips can be sent to brian.flood@fox.com and on Twitter: @briansflood.

title: Vulnerable Democrats flip on women's sports vote after opposing similar bills as midterms loom
excerpt: Concerned Women for America spokesperson Macy Petty Charles and assistant United States attorney Julie Hamill discuss the Supreme Court's decision allowing states to ban transgender athletes from comp...
body_text:
Concerned Women for America spokesperson Macy Petty Charles and assistant United States attorney Julie Hamill discuss the Supreme Court's decision allowing states to ban transgender athletes from competing in girls' sports on ‘Fox News @ Night.’

Several vulnerable House Democrats are breaking with their party on men competing in women’s sports as Republicans continue to hammer the issue ahead of November's midterm elections.

Reps. Shomari Figures and Terri Sewell, D-Ala., joined Republicans in voting for an amendment to the annual defense policy bill Tuesday that would bar biological males from participating in women’s sports in Department of War-run schools.

The vote marked a notable shift for both Alabama Democrats, who opposed similar legislation as recently as last year, and comes as Republicans’ redistricting efforts have scrambled their re-election prospects.

Figures now represents one of the GOP's top pickup opportunities after Alabama's newly drawn congressional map added more GOP voters to his district, dramatically increasing the difficulty of his re-election bid. The National Republican Congressional Committee (NRCC), House Republicans' campaign arm, added the freshman lawmaker to its target list last week.

Congressman Shomari Figures, D-Ala., speaks at a press conference with other members of the House Democratic caucus in front of the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., on Nov. 12, 2025. (Nathan Posner/Anadolu via Getty Images)

BLOCKBUSTER SUPREME COURT VOTING RIGHTS RULING IGNITES REDISTRICTING WAR ACROSS SOUTHERN STATES

Sewell’s Black-majority seat anchored around Birmingham remained largely unchanged, though she also bucked Democratic leadership on the amendment.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., and 202 other Democrats voted against the measure. All Republicans present voted in support.

Both lawmakers voted against the Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act in 2025, which barred federally funded schools from allowing biological males to participate in girls' and women’s sports.

The reversal comes as transgender issues continue to divide Democrats, with public polling showing broad opposition to transgender athletes competing in women's sports.

A New York Times/Ipsos poll conducted in 2025 found that nearly 80% of Americans opposed biological males competing in women's sports. Roughly seven in ten Democrats or Democrat-leaning respondents agreed, according to the survey.

Protesters against transgender athletes competing in women's sports gathered outside the Supreme Court in Washington, D.C., on Jan. 13, 2026. (Heather Diehl/Getty Images)

DEMOCRATS STAY QUIET ON NEXT STEPS AFTER SUPREME COURT TRANSGENDER SPORTS RULING

Republicans sharply criticized the Alabama Democrats' votes, arguing the move reflected political necessity rather than a genuine change in position.

"Out of touch Democrats Shomari Figures and Terri Sewell are desperately trying to hide their far-left views now that it's politically expedient, but it's too little too late," NRCC spokesman Reilly Richardson told Fox News Digital.

"Alabamians know Figures and Sewell support Democrats' radical agenda of allowing men to compete in women's sports and will hold them accountable at the ballot box in November," he continued.

Figures, however, argued his views on transgender participation in women's sports have been consistent and pointed to a 2025 amendment vote restricting transgender athletes that he supported in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"As I have said before, I do not believe that men should be allowed to participate in women’s sports," Figures said. "I voted against the Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act because that bill would have created an open season for pedophiles to conduct sex checks of young girls, with no safeguards."

Proponents of the measure disputed during floor debate that the legislation would put girls at risk.

Fox News Digital reached out to Sewell for comment.

Reps. Don Davis, D-N.C., and Henry Cuellar and Vicente Gonzalez, D-Texas, also voted with Republicans in favor of the Mace amendment.

The three Democrats, who are all vulnerable to GOP challengers, frequently cross party lines to support Republican-authored bills, though Davis voted "present" on the Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act.

Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., who sponsored the amendment, argued the measure was intended to protect women’s sports.

Rep. Terri Sewell, D-Ala., speaks during a Congressional Black Caucus news conference in the U.S. Capitol on the Supreme Court's ruling in Louisiana v. Callais on Wednesday, April 29, 2026. (CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

"Men are not women. Men will never become women. Yet male athletes are stealing medals, invading locker rooms, and putting girls in the hospital, all in the name of gender ideology," Mace said Tuesday. "Our NDAA amendment would end this nonsense."

title: 'The View' co-host whines Smithsonian hearings are about Trump administration not wanting to pay reparations
excerpt: "The View" co-host Sunny Hostin claimed on Wednesday that the hearings on Capitol Hill scrutinizing the Smithsonian Institution were about the Trump administration avoiding paying reparations to Black...
body_text:
"The View" co-host Sunny Hostin claimed on Wednesday that the hearings on Capitol Hill scrutinizing the Smithsonian Institution were about the Trump administration avoiding paying reparations to Black Americans.

"The View" co-host Sunny Hostin claimed Wednesday that the Congressional hearings on the Smithsonian institution were about the Trump administration not wanting to award reparations to the descendants of slaves.

Smithsonian National Museum of American History Director Dr. Anthea Hartig faced questions on Capitol Hill on Tuesday after a White House report published earlier this month found that the Smithsonian Institution's National Museum of American History has become a taxpayer-backed institution of "ideological capture" and "extreme political activism."

"The real reason behind this is about financial repair to the descendents of the enslaved Africans," Hostin said, as co-host Whoopi Goldberg pushed her to clarify what she meant.

"Reparations," Hostin said. "I think this reflects a broad effort by the Trump Administration to reshape the way the nation’s history is told, so this country does not have to pay Black people for the forced labor and the unpaid labor that built this country. I really think that this is what this is about."

NEW YORK FAR-LEFT GROUP CLAIMS REPARATIONS 'OWED' AS BLACKS' MEDIAN WEALTH 'NEARLY 15 TIMES LESS' THAN WHITES

"Even after 250 years, there’s so much repair that needs to be done when it comes to aggrieved groups in this country, but especially African Americans," Hostin said.

"Let’s face it, when you have truth and reconciliation commissions, and you've have them when it comes to interned Japanese Americans, you've had them when it comes to the Holocaust, you’ve had them when it comes to Native Americans, it has not happened — truth and reconciliation commissions, when it comes to slavery. And that is because they’re trying to bury the truth part. You cannot have true reconciliation, and then you cannot have true financial repair unless you have the truth. And they’re trying to erase that truth," she said.

Hostin noted that the United Nations General Assembly voted to declare the transatlantic slave trade to be "the gravest crime against humanity," in March 2026. The U.S., Argentina and Israel voted against the resolution, and 123 countries voted in favor. Fifty-two countries abstained from the vote.

TRUMP ADMINISTRATION TOUTS MORE THAN $1B IN UN BUDGET CUTS AS REFORM PUSH GAINS MOMENTUM

The View co-host said the Trump administration was looking to avoid paying African Americans "our due." (ABC/TheView)

The co-host of "The View" quoted Ambassador Dan Negrea, U.S. representative to the UN Economic and Social Council, as saying the U.S. "does not recognize a legal right to reparations for historical wrongs that were not illegal under international law at the time they occurred," prior to the vote.

"And so what this is about is money," Hostin said. "This is not about anything else. It’s about this country trying to avoid paying African Americans our due."

Hostin appeared on the PBS documentary show "Finding Your Roots" in 2024, and was stunned at the time to find out her ancestor was a slaveholder. Henry Louis Gates Jr., the host of the show, revealed one of Hostin's ancestors on her maternal side was likely involved in the slave trade in colonial Spain.

Her third great-grandfather also "owned at least one human being," he said.

Hostin, whose mother is Puerto Rican and father is Black, confessed the revelation left her "in shock."

'THE VIEW' CO-HOST SUNNY HOSTIN DOUBLES DOWN ON AMERICAN FLAGS MAKING HER FEEL 'UNSAFE'
