title: Mamdani sets internet ablaze with video explaining cave on plan to arrest Netanyahu: 'What a joke'
excerpt: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani branded Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" and urged the federal government to arrest him under an International Criminal Court warrant. (Cre...
body_text:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani branded Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" and urged the federal government to arrest him under an International Criminal Court warrant. (Credit: @NYCMayor via X)

New York City socialist mayor Zohran Mamdani was widely mocked by conservatives on social media Tuesday after announcing that one of his campaign platforms, arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, isn’t a tool he has at his disposal.

In a video post on X that was viewed over 36 million times, Mamdani called Netanyahu a "war criminal" while conceding that New York City could not enforce the International Criminal Court's warrant for the Israeli leader’s arrest on its own, despite the mayor saying he had looked at exploring avenues to do so.

Mamdani has often said on the campaign trail and as mayor that he wanted to arrest Netanyahu over what the mayor calls a "genocide" in Gaza.

While Mamdani’s post was well received by many on the political left, conservatives on social media quickly mocked the mayor over the situation while taking issue with his determination to enforce the ICC warrant, which the United States government does not recognize.

MAMDANI’S ‘DERANGED’ ISRAEL TAKE RESURFACES AS HE TARGETS NETANYAHU

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced on Tuesday he determined he did not have the power to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. (Getty Images)

"Trying to find where Mamdani called for the arrest of the Hamas terrorists who plotted the October 7th attacks," Arkansas Republican Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders posted on X. "Disgusting and Disgraceful. "

"This may be the most horrific package of lies against the State of Israel ever promoted by a government official," former U.S. Ambassador to Israel David M. Friedman posted on X. "Even Hamas does not lie to this extent! It is despicable and entirely unbecoming a mayor of New York City. To those who voted for this Jew-hater, shame on you. "

"Zohran Mamdani calling Benjamin Netanyahu a war criminal is utterly ridiculous," Sen. Tim Scott, R-S.C., posted on X. "The same crowd that rushes to condemn Israel never has much to say about the terrorists who started the war, massacred civilians, raped women, kidnapped children, and still hide behind their own people. Mamdani wants the headline that comes from attacking Netanyahu, not the consistency that comes with confronting Hamas."

MAMDANI CALLS OUT DEMOCRATIC PARTY AS 'WELL-PAID CONSULTANTS AND BIG MONEY INTERESTS'

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said his administration is exploring whether the city has legal authority to arrest Netanyahu if he visits for the U.N. General Assembly. (Ronen Zvulun/Pool/AFP via Getty Images)

"The mayor of New York is just straight up promoting antisemitic blood libel courtesy of Hamas propaganda from an official government social media account tonight," New York City Republican Councilwoman Vickie Paladino posted on X.

"As I was saying… he doesn’t have the authority," US Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz posted on X. "More political theater."

"I had a dead homeless body in front of my apartment, but the mayor’s focused on playing Model UN," Sam Raus, David Boaz Resident Writing Fellow at Young Voices, posted on X. "What a joke."

Mayor Zohran Mamdani speaks at a rally in New York alongside 1199SEIU members and immigration advocates after the Supreme Court ruled the Trump administration can revoke protections for Haitians and Syrians. (Lev Radin/Pacific Press/LightRocket via Getty Images)

"Zohran Mamdani admitted tonight that he has zero legal right to arrest Netanyahu," political commentator Eyal Yakoby posted on X. "Like everything that he does, it was all just an act for clicks on social media."

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani’s office for comment.

Although Mamdani acknowledged he could not arrest Netanyahu as mayor, he called on the federal government to execute an International Criminal Court's arrest warrant if the Israeli leader visits New York.

"I want to be equally clear: Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large," he said.

Fox News Digital’s Michael Sinkewicz contributed to this report.

Andrew Mark Miller is a reporter at Fox News. Find him on Twitter @andymarkmiller and email tips to AndrewMark.Miller@Fox.com.

title: Phillies star JT Realmuto makes inexplicable base-running blunder to end the game, leaves manager dumbfounded
excerpt:
Philadelphia Phillies J.T. Realmuto makes a Little Little-esque blunder during a rundown to make the final out. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

Every once in a while, a big leaguer looks li...
body_text:
Philadelphia Phillies J.T. Realmuto makes a Little Little-esque blunder during a rundown to make the final out. (This video contains AI-assisted content)

Every once in a while, a big leaguer looks like a little leaguer on the diamond.

Unfortunately for the Philadelphia Phillies, star catcher J.T. Realmuto’s Little League-esque blunder ended the game.

In the bottom of the ninth inning of the Phillies’ 2-1 loss to the Los Angeles Dodgers on Tuesday at Citizens Bank Park, the Phillies had runners on second and third with one out.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Los Angeles Dodgers shortstop Mookie Betts tags Philadelphia Phillies' Justin Crawford in a rundown with J.T. Realmuto heading back to second base during the ninth inning of a baseball game Tuesday, July 21, 2026, in Philadelphia. (AP Photo/Chris Szagola)

Justin Crawford, who pinch-ran for Alec Bohm, was on third base, while Realmuto was on second base. Right fielder Brandon Marsh was up against closer Tanner Scott.

Marsh hit a weak ground ball to third base, and Crawford started to go home but stopped after a few steps.

Interim Phillies manager Don Mattingly said postgame the contact play was on, which means Crawford was supposed to run home no matter what. However, Crawford said the contact play was on unless the ball was hit to third base.

So Crawford, now caught between third base and home plate, got in a rundown. Realmuto ran to third base but inexplicably turned around to go back to second base when he saw Crawford run back to third base.

ANGELS THIRD BASEMAN BRINGS BASKETBALL ONTO THE DIAMOND, TURNS SLICK DOUBLE PLAY WITH UNCONVENTIONAL MOVE

Los Angeles Dodgers infielder Tommy Edman reacts after tagging out Philadelphia Phillies catcher JT Realmuto in the ninth inning at Citizens Bank Park in Philadelphia, Pa., on July 21, 2026. (Kyle Ross/Imagn Images)

Mookie Betts tagged Crawford out and then whirled and fired to second baseman Tommy Edman to nab Realmuto going back to second, completing a chaotic 5-2-6-4 double play to end the game.

Phillies announcer John Kruk was in disbelief after seeing Realmuto run back toward second base.

Realmuto said postgame it was a bad baserunning play.

"Just a bad baserunning play, really," Realmuto told reporters postgame. "We were going on seeing it through there, and once (Crawford) gets in the rundown and I'm on third base, there’s no reason for me to go back to second. Just a lack of judgment. You can't make two outs on that play. I’ve just got to stay in scoring position."

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Los Angeles Dodgers second baseman Tommy Edman reacts after getting Philadelphia Phillies' J.T. Realmuto out at second base for the final out of a game Tuesday, July 21, 2026, in Philadelphia. (AP Photo/Chris Szagola)

"Just a lapse in judgment. I thought Crawford could get out of the rundown for some reason. The chances of that are so small, you just can't take that chance there of making two outs on one play."

Realmuto said he should have played it through in his head before it happened and been more proactive in his preparation. Mattingly said he wasn’t sure what his star catcher was thinking.

"I don’t know what JT was thinking there, honestly, because he got to third," Mattingly told reporters postgame.

"With contact on," Mattingly said, "just hoping (Crawford) stays in (the rundown) long enough to get Marsh to second, and you end up in the same spot."

J.T. Realmuto #10 of the Philadelphia Phillies looks on against the Los Angeles Dodgers at Citizens Bank Park on July 20, 2026, in Philadelphia, Pa. The Phillies defeated the Dodgers 10-7. (Mitchell Leff/Getty Images)

"I’m not quite sure what he’s thinking. I don’t know if he thought that (Crawford) was going to get back or they were going to let him get back. It’s something we’ll talk about."

The baserunning blunder comes in the midst of a down season for Realmuto. The three-time All-Star catcher is hitting just .215 with seven home runs, which well below his career batting average of .268.

The Phillies (56-46) will look to bounce back from the tough loss and take the third game of the three-game set against the Dodgers (64-38) on Wednesday at 6:40 p.m. ET.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

Ryan Canfield is a digital production assistant for Fox News Digital.

title: Prosecutors argue D4vd had financial motivation to kill Celeste Rivas Hernandez
excerpt:
Details emerge about how much Interscope Records paid the singer at hearing to determine if there is probable cause for murder case to proceed to trial

Singer D4vd had a multimillion-dollar recording...
body_text:
Details emerge about how much Interscope Records paid the singer at hearing to determine if there is probable cause for murder case to proceed to trial

Singer D4vd had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label testified on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had a financial motivation to kill Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

Details emerged of how much Interscope Records was paying the singer, who was born David Anthony Burke, on the second day of a hearing to determine if there is probable cause for him to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Rivas Hernandez.

The teenager’s body was found last year in gruesome conditions in a car registered to the musician.

Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at his Los Angeles residence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship as his career was on the rise.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Hernandez’s death.

On the first day of hearings on Tuesday, prosecutors showed photos of the dismembered and decomposing body of Rivas Hernandez, and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her body. They also sought to show that the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel.

The preliminary hearing is expected to last between three and five days.

Burke, now 21, was arrested in April and has been held in custody since then without bail.

If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole.

Rivas Hernandez’s family was in court for the start of the hearing on Tuesday. After Burke’s arrest in April, the family said in a statement: “Celeste was a beautiful, strong girl who loved to sing and dance. Every Friday night was movie night and we spent wonderful times together. We love her very much and she always told us that she loved us. We miss her deeply. All we want is justice for Celeste.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report

title: US judge sets 2027 trial date for Venezuela’s Maduro and wife
excerpt: A United States judge has set a trial date for former Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores, both abducted from the South American country during a US military attack in Januar...
body_text:
A United States judge has set a trial date for former Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores, both abducted from the South American country during a US military attack in January 2026.

Judge Alvin Hellerstein set a trial date on Wednesday for June 1, 2027. Both Maduro and Flores have pleaded not guilty to drug trafficking charges while denying the basic legitimacy of the trial.

Neither spoke during the 15-minute court hearing. The administration of US President Donald Trump has justified its military raid on Caracas in January and its abduction of the couple, widely termed illegal by international legal scholars, by alleging that Maduro was part of a conspiracy to traffic cocaine to the US in his capacity as head of state.

“I am not guilty. I am a decent man, the constitutional president of my country,” Maduro said in his arraignment in January.

Maduro’s lawyer, Barry Pollack, said that he expects to challenge the legality of the military raid to capture Maduro and bring him to the US for trial. He stated on Wednesday that he would first challenge the case on grounds of sovereign immunity, which, if successful, would mean that Maduro no longer has to litigate the case.

Neither Maduro nor Flores has asked to be released on bail, and Judge Hellerstein set next year’s trial date at the request of attorneys from both sides.

Maduro faces four counts, including “narco-terrorism” conspiracy and cocaine importation conspiracy, and could face life in prison if convicted. The Trump administration has often used concerns about drug trafficking to assert its influence in countries across Latin America.

The Trump administration has called the attack on Venezuela, which Venezuelan authorities say killed 83 people, a “surgical law enforcement operation” in a criminal case first put forward six years ago.

The Venezuelan presidency has since been filled by interim President Delcy Rodriguez, who has aligned the country more closely with US interests.

title: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84
excerpt:
1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world

Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuild...
body_text:
1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world

Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduce Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world, has died. He was 84.

Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause.

“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”

Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.

In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.

In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.

Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.

Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.

“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”

Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.

Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.

Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.

“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.

“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”

In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.

title: Man killed in ICE ‘encounter’ was on vacation in US with tourist visa – reports
excerpt:
Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán ran into traffic and killed by semi after federal officials approached him at Florida gas station

The Mexican man who was hit and killed by a semi-truck in Florida when run...
body_text:
Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán ran into traffic and killed by semi after federal officials approached him at Florida gas station

The Mexican man who was hit and killed by a semi-truck in Florida when running away from federal immigration officials was vacationing in the US with a tourist visa, according to news reports this week.

The 28-year-old, Juan Jairo Coronilla Durán, was killed on 14 July after an “encounter” with federal officials, including those with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), a state highway patrol spokesperson said last week.

After federal officials approached him and a group of four men at a gas station that Tuesday morning, they all took off running. The Florida highway patrol spokesperson said Coronilla ran into traffic and was hit by a semi-truck.

Coronilla’s death is one of the four ICE-related deaths that took place in less than one week. Two people, one in Texas and one in Maine, were shot and killed by ICE officers. And a fourth person died in ICE custody, while being transferred to an immigration detention center in Georgia. The four deaths have led to renewed calls by the public and lawmakers to reign in ICE and its parent agency, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), as they carry out the Trump administration’s aggressive “mass deportation” campaign promise.

According to the Miami Herald, Coronilla, from the Mexican state of Guanajuato, had only been in Florida for two days when he died, having arrived in the US only three weeks prior. Coronilla had traveled to Atlanta at first to see family and friends and went to Florida to enjoy the beaches.

Coronilla’s wife, who flew to the US last week to make arrangements, had not heard from her husband and found out about his death while watching the news, a local immigrant rights group told the Miami Herald. She then contacted the group, Colectivo Arbol, to ask for help. The group said Coronilla left behind a seven-year-old son and seven-month-old daughter.

DHS has not publicly identified Coronilla, but the Associated Press confirmed his identity with a Mexican government official.

Although the Florida highway patrol’s statement said it was an ICE operation, a homeland security source familiar with the case told the Guardian last week that border patrol agents were involved in the incident.

title: IndyCar teams come to terms on the series' first-ever charter sale, which shakes up the grid for 2027
excerpt:
Check out the best moments from the NTT INDYCAR SERIES: Borchetta Bourbon Music City Grand Prix at Nashville Superspeedway with commentary from Will Buxton, Townsend Bell and James Hinchcliffe!

The N...
body_text:
Check out the best moments from the NTT INDYCAR SERIES: Borchetta Bourbon Music City Grand Prix at Nashville Superspeedway with commentary from Will Buxton, Townsend Bell and James Hinchcliffe!

The NTT IndyCar grid is going to look a little different in 2027 with news that the first sale of an IndyCar charter has been completed, and it will bring a new team to the grid.

Albeit one that fans are very familiar with.

On Wednesday, it was announced that Rahal Letterman Lanigan Racing had agreed to sell one of its three charters to Drewy & Reinbold Racing, with the transaction subject to IndyCar approval.

TRUMP TOUTS INDYCAR DRIVERS' 'SPECIAL' ABILITY AT FREEDOM 250 GRAND PRIX SHOWCASE, SEEMINGLY ENDING HOT DEBATE

It's the first sale since the charter system was introduced in 2024, and it will see RLL dropping from a three-car operation down to a two-car operation, and DRR rejoining the egrid full-time with a single car.

Dreyer & Reinbold last competed in IndyCar full-time back in 2012 and has become a regular fixture in the Indianapolis 500. They had two cars in this year's field, which were driven by Conor Daly and Jack Harvey.

Jack Harvey drove one of Dreyer & Reinbold Racing's two entries in this year's Indianapolis 500. (Photo by Jeffrey Brown/Icon Sportswire)

This news comes just weeks after the death of DRR founder and owner Dennis Reinbold on June 13.

TRUMP TOUTS INDYCAR DRIVERS' 'SPECIAL' ABILITY AT FREEDOM 250 GRAND PRIX SHOWCASE, SEEMINGLY ENDING HOT DEBATE

"I want to thank Bobby Rahal, Mike Lanigan, David Letterman and Jay Frye for the way they approached this from our very first conversation," DRR CEO Derek Reinbold said in a statement. "They treated this as a partnership between two organizations that want to see each other succeed, and this announcement today reflects that.

The teams did not name any drivers or which of RLL's three cars' charters will head to DRR.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

RLL currently fields the No. 15 Honda driven by Graham Rahal, the No. 35 Honda driven by Louis Foster, and the No. 47 driven by rookie Mick Schumacher.

According to FOX Sports' Bob Pockrass, both Rahal and Foster are under contract for next season, while Schumacher is not.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

That has led to speculation that Schumacher is the odd man out. He is, however, coming off a season-best finish at Nashville.

After the race, he told Pockrass that he feels there's a high chance he will be on the grid in 2027.

We'll see how it shakes out, because this has already been one of the crazier IndyCar silly seasons in recent memory, with even the likes of Christian Lundgaard — who has two wins on the year and his fifth in the standings — hunting for a new ride for 2027.

title: Trump’s war on Iran is unpopular and expensive. He’s perfectly OK with that
excerpt:
Trump’s response to the Iran war’s mounting death toll and astronomical costs? ‘It won’t have any impact on me’

This was originally published in This Week in Trumpland. Sign up to receive it in your ...
body_text:
Trump’s response to the Iran war’s mounting death toll and astronomical costs? ‘It won’t have any impact on me’

This was originally published in This Week in Trumpland. Sign up to receive it in your inbox every Wednesday

Could it be … that Donald Trump isn’t a team player?

The midterm elections are less than four months away, and Republicans in the House and the Senate – hampered by a deeply unpopular president and what might be an even less popular war – are increasingly fearful of losing their jobs. Even Trump’s base is beginning to turn against the conflict.

Yet asked on Tuesday if Iran is trying to affect the midterm elections by threatening the strait of Hormuz, the Republicans’ de facto leader was pretty blase.

“Probably. It won’t have any impact on me. I’m just going to do the right thing. I don’t view it as: ‘Gee, we’re having an election in a period of time.’ You know, everyone says: ‘Oh, I’m doing something before’ – no. The election – I can’t think about that having to do with this,” Trump said.

He added: “I think people are very impressed.”

Unfortunately for Trump – and for Republicans in swing districts – all available evidence suggests people are very unimpressed with the president’s nearly five-month war in Iran.

Just 28% of Americans think the Iran war is worth fighting, a Washington Post-Ipsos poll found last week; 68% believe it is not worth fighting. The same survey found that 29% of people believe “America’s leadership in the world has gotten stronger” under Trump; 52% think it has weakened.

Amid surging prices linked to the Iran war, just 17% of Americans said they were better off financially than they were when Trump became president.

Even Trump’s most hardcore supporters – the ones who heckle journalists at his rallies and wear T-shirts that say things like “Stomp my flag [and] I’ll stomp your ass” – are souring on the war.

About 37% of self-identified Maga Trump voters say the US should continue the war in Iran only if it doesn’t increase costs, according to a Politico poll released on Wednesday. Moreover, 57% of voters who self-identify as Maga blame the war in Iran for the increase in gas prices.

It is Not Good News, in other words, and this week some Fox News pundits issued urgent warnings to Trump to end the war.

“With the midterm elections a little more than 100 days away, the clock is ticking for President Trump to get out of this war before voters head to the polls. It’s making a lot of Republicans nervous. It’s already, as we know, unpopular,” Laura Ingraham, usually a staunch Trump supporter, said on her show on Monday.

“Republicans cannot let this become an endless distraction, especially if they want to win in November.”

With no clear exit strategy, news of mounting injuries and more deaths of service members, there are good reasons for voters to focus on the war. And then there is the financial cost.

During a contentious Senate hearing on Tuesday, the defense secretary, Pete Hegseth – he of alleged strip club stage-storming fame – said the Iran war had cost $37.5bn so far, as he asked Congress to approve an $88bn supplemental funding request. With gas prices running at more than $4 a gallon – up from about $3 when Trump took office – the spiraling costs are hardly likely to be a vote-winner.

It might be making Republicans in Congress nervous. But Trump, a president in his second term who is not eligible to run for re-election, arguably has less to lose. A Democratic-majority House could impeach him for the third time, but the Senate would be highly unlikely to convict. Whenever and however Trump leaves the White House, his landing will probably be softened by his soaring personal wealth – his income last year was $2.2bn.

With a president embroiled in a costly war, and seemingly ambivalent about how it affects his colleagues, could the midterms be a disaster? Maybe. Would Trump get over that? Probably.

title: 10-foot great white shark spotted swimming dangerously close to young people
excerpt: A drone photographer spotted a shark swimming dangerously close to a surfer off Seascape Beach in Aptos, California — then revved his drone motors to warn the surfer before the shark finally turned aw...
body_text:
A drone photographer spotted a shark swimming dangerously close to a surfer off Seascape Beach in Aptos, California — then revved his drone motors to warn the surfer before the shark finally turned away.

A 10-foot great white shark was spotted swimming alarmingly close to a group of young swimmers along the Southern California coastline — with the children appearing completely unaware of the predator nearby.

The encounter was captured on drone video by Carlos Gauna, a wildlife photographer known as the "Malibu Artist," who said it was one of the few times he felt genuinely uneasy while observing sharks.

"I don't often get nervous, but this was one of those rare instances where I did," Gauna wrote on Instagram. "The young swimmers had no idea."

SHARK SIGHTINGS TRIGGER BEACH ALARM AFTER SWIMMER IS BITTEN DURING SUMMER SPIKE

The shark had a large boat propeller strike wound across its back and dorsal fin, Gauna said — which he believes may have affected its behavior.

"My observations showed the shark to be rather active," he wrote, adding that he watched it breach twice in one day.

A 10-foot great white shark (not pictured) was spotted swimming alarmingly close to a group of young swimmers along the Southern California coastline. (Joseph Prezioso/AFP via Getty Images)

"Because of the injury, I don't know if it's searching for easier prey or if it's more alert," Gauna also wrote. "Either way, this shark does approach humans at a higher frequency. At least by my observations."

The wild video sparked hundreds of comments — with many viewers saying they would immediately leave the water if they noticed a drone hovering overhead.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"If I'm swimming and see a drone over me, I'm out of there," one user commented.

Others said the footage showed that great white sharks often investigate people without attacking.

The children shown in the video (not pictured) were completely unaware that the shark was nearby — and it eventually turned away. (iStock)

"It just proves they aren't interested in us," one viewer wrote. "It went to see what the splashing was and turned away. It still had my heart racing, though."

Several commenters also suggested that beachgoers should be alerted when sharks are spotted nearby, with some proposing warning lights or audible signals to help swimmers safely exit the water.

Fox News Digital previously reported that shark experts have urged beachgoers to stay alert this summer as sightings increased along several U.S. coastlines.

Several commenters suggested that beachgoers should be alerted when sharks are spotted nearby. (iStock)

Marine biologist Mike Heithaus of Florida International University told Fox News Digital that shark bites remain unusual, even during the busy summer beach season.

"People should always be aware of local conditions and listen to lifeguards, but shark bites are very rare," he emphasized.

The shark encounter was captured on drone video by a videographer. (iStock)

He recommended that people avoid murky water, that they stay away from areas where large schools of fish are visible and that they avoid places where other people are actively fishing.

Kelly McGreal is a production assistant with the lifestyle team at Fox News Digital.

title: Hamas’s new leader calls for permanent Gaza ceasefire

Hamas’s new leader calls for permanent Gaza ceasefire

Hamas’s new leader calls for permanent Gaza ceasefire

In his first speech since being elected Hamas political chief, Khalil al-Hayya called for a permanent ceasefire in Gaza, a full Israeli withdrawal, and faster reconstruction. He also urged Palestinian unity, rejected displacement, and called for stronger regional ties amid the US war on Iran.

title: Nolan Wells independent autopsy doesn't point to foul play, former medical examiner says
excerpt:
Nolan Wells was found dead on Horn Island two days after vanishing from a Fourth of July boat trip, but questions surround the Mississippi teen's death.

Attorney Ben Crump revealed that the results o...
body_text:
Nolan Wells was found dead on Horn Island two days after vanishing from a Fourth of July boat trip, but questions surround the Mississippi teen's death.

Attorney Ben Crump revealed that the results of an independent autopsy into Nolan Wells' death are "inconclusive" as a former medical examiner said the findings aren't consistent with a "cover up" or any wrongdoing.

Crump announced the independent autopsy's results on Wednesday at the NAACP National Convention in Chicago, stating that an area of "red discoloration" was found on Wells' head. He said Dr. Roger Mitchell, who performed the autopsy, couldn't come to a conclusion "pending further investigation." The official autopsy hasn't yet been completed.

"Why is this dark red here in the back of his head?" Crump said. "This pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells."

When asked by Fox News Digital about the case, forensic pathologist Dr. Michael Baden said the head injury doesn't point to foul play.

"So that could be from bumping into something in the water. There's a lot of debris in the water, for example. It depends how deep it is, but it's describing a really superficial impact that could have happened before death or after death," Baden said.

SEARCH CALLED OFF AFTER BODY MATCHING MISSING TEEN NOLAN WELLS FOUND OFF MISSISSIPPI COAST

Nolan Wells smiles on the field while playing wide receiver for Southwest Mississippi Community College. (Christine Wonsley/Facebook)

"What he's describing is a minor injury that could be... totally innocent," Baden said.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Nolan Xavier Wells disappeared on the Fourth of July while boating with friends, according to authorities. (Jackson County Sheriff's Department via Facebook)

Baden said Mitchell was "reaching for straws" in the independent autopsy report, adding that the findings are consistent with accounts from local law enforcement that his death was accidental and no foul play was involved.

Wells, 18, initially disappeared July 4 after he went with friends on a boat trip to Horn Island, which is a barrier island off Mississippi's Gulf Coast. Officials and search crews searched for two days before a National Park Service ranger located his body on July 6 while on the island's northwestern tip.

Attorney Ben Crump speaks at a press conference outside the federal courthouse on July 15, 2020, in Minneapolis, Minnesota. (Getty Images)

Officials haven't released a cause of death as officials continue to investigate.

Adam Sabes is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Adam.Sabes@fox.com and on Twitter @asabes10.

title: Mamdani dodges on ‘political theater’ of empty Netanyahu arrest threat, calls for US action and NYC protests
excerpt:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani says protests will be respected and calls on the U.S. to join the ICC and pursue accountability for accused 'war criminals' like Benjamin Netanyahu.

New York City M...
body_text:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani says protests will be respected and calls on the U.S. to join the ICC and pursue accountability for accused 'war criminals' like Benjamin Netanyahu.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani declined Wednesday to say when he learned the city had no authority to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, after repeatedly campaigning on a promise to detain the Israeli leader if he came to New York.

Pressed over whether the now-abandoned arrest threat amounted to "political theater," Mamdani said his administration had reviewed the applicable laws and concluded that only the federal government could execute the International Criminal Court warrant.

"It’s clear that the city does not have the authority to execute this warrant," Mamdani admitted at a New York City Hall news conference Wednesday, urging the federal government to act on the ICC warrant even though the U.S. is not a member of the ICC and does not recognize the court’s jurisdiction in the U.S. or over Israel, which is also not a member.

With the Trump administration already fully rejecting his call to arrest Netanyahu for being a "war criminal," Mamdani called for New Yorkers to take to protest "if anyone feels a level of frustration or opposition."

MAMDANI GOES BACK ON CAMPAIGN PROMISE, SAYS NYC CAN'T ARREST NETANYAHU

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani has been forced to admit his threat to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has no force or measure in the law. (Getty Images)

"I believe that this is an arrest warrant that should be executed, and also that I would follow the laws that we had, and I would be respectful of those laws," he added. "And that is why I asked my administration to review of all applicable laws. And I believe, however, in that same finding, that the United States government does have the ability to both joining the International Criminal Court and executing that work, and that is something that I want to see happen."

Short of that, Mamdani suggested that people protest when the Israeli leader visits for the United Nations General Assembly, potentially in September.

"This is a city where, if anyone feels a level of frustration or opposition, protest is something that we will always respect," Mamdani told reporters. "I leave it to New Yorkers themselves to make that decision."

The mayor was repeatedly asked why he did not resolve the legal question before taking office, given that experts had challenged the legality of his arrest pledge during the campaign.

HAMAS, HEZBOLLAH FLAGS FLY AT ANTI-ISRAEL PROTEST MINUTES AFTER MAMDANI CONDEMNS TERROR PLOT AGAINST JEWS

Mamdani would not provide a date for when his administration informed him that the city lacked the power to carry out the ICC warrant.

"That’s a review that I asked them to do," he said. "They made it clear that this is not possible through applicable law."

Mamdani said Palestinian New Yorkers disappointed that he cannot fulfill his arrest promise had reason to be frustrated, declaring that Netanyahu would no longer be welcomed by City Hall.

"I understand it because we are talking about someone who has been charged with crimes against humanity, someone who has visited the city on many an occasion and who has been welcomed by previous mayors," Mamdani said.

"I want to make it very clear that that welcome ends with me," he continued. "I will not be welcoming Benjamin Netanyahu or any other war criminals when they come to this city."

NYC REPUBLICAN LAWMAKER SLAMS MAMDANI ANTISEMITISM OFFICE AS A ‘BLACK HOLE’ LACKING PUBLIC-FACING RESOURCES

President Donald Trump has forcefully rejected New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani's threats to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu under an unenforceable International Criminal Court arrest warrant that the U.S. nor Israel are a party to. (Timothy A. Clary/AFP via Getty Images)

Mamdani also faced questions over criticism that his focus on Netanyahu created a one-sided account of the Israel-Hamas war.

Asked directly whether he considered Hamas’ Oct. 7, 2023, terrorist attack to be genocide, Mamdani did not use that term.

"I consider Oct. 7 to be a horrific war crime," he said. "I’ve considered it to be something that should be deeply opposed with every single fiber of our being."

Mamdani said his approach was rooted in what he called the "universality of humanity," arguing that officials should not "pick and choose which crimes against humanity we oppose."

The mayor also said he would support honoring ICC warrants against Hamas leaders as well as Netanyahu.

"My interest in executing that warrant is not specific to Benjamin Netanyahu, but to anyone who they put a warrant towards for war crimes and crimes against humanity," Mamdani said.

He accused Netanyahu of having "orchestrated this genocide" in Gaza and described the Israeli prime minister as a "war criminal." Israel has repeatedly rejected genocide allegations and condemned the ICC case as illegitimate.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said his administration is exploring whether the city has legal authority to arrest Netanyahu if he visits for the U.N. General Assembly. (Ronen Zvulun/Pool/AFP via Getty Images)

Mamdani said he remained committed to protecting Jewish New Yorkers, including those who disagree with his position.

"My responsibility to them is to ensure that they are cherished, they are celebrated and they are safe in their city," he said, "and that never are they made to feel as if their opinions on any one issue is the precondition for that safety."

Eric Mack is a breaking news reporter and writer Sunday through Thursday, 6 a.m. ET to 2 p.m. ET, with a particular interest in stories that lead the news cycle on politics, elections, foreign affairs and government.

title: WATCH: Tlaib tells Mamdani to 'call me' for help arresting Netanyahu as Democrats remain divided
excerpt: Democratic lawmakers offered split reactions toward the NYC mayor's proposal to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, with Rep. Rashida Tlaib urging for the arrest and Rep. Jerry Nadler citing legal hurdles. (Cr...
body_text:
Democratic lawmakers offered split reactions toward the NYC mayor's proposal to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, with Rep. Rashida Tlaib urging for the arrest and Rep. Jerry Nadler citing legal hurdles. (Credit: Nicholas Ballasy for Fox News Digital)

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s abandoned pledge to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu divided House Democrats, with some backing his proposal while others said the mayor lacked the legal authority to carry it out.

"Arrest him," Rep. Rashida Tlaib, D-Mich., told Fox News Digital. "He’s a war criminal."

Throughout Mamdani’s campaign, he repeatedly insisted that he would order the New York Police Department (NYPD) to enforce the warrant issued by the International Criminal Court (ICC) and arrest Netanyahu for his role in the war in Gaza.

MAMDANI GOES BACK ON CAMPAIGN PROMISE, SAYS NYC CAN'T ARREST NETANYAHU

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s abandoned pledge to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu divided House Democrats. (Kyle Mazza/Anadolu via Getty Images)

But Mamdani finally acknowledged his inability to do so as the city lacks the "independent legal authority" to do so. On Tuesday night, he took to social media with a video calling on the federal government to execute the warrant by the ICC to arrest Netanyahu, also stating to be "equally clear" that the prime minister is "not welcome in New York City."

"I think that whether or not he has the authority about it, I don’t really have an opinion on," Rep. Summer Lee, D-Penn., said. She continued, "But I do think that Netanyahu is a war criminal."

"I wish that we had a country that didn’t excuse war criminals," Lee said.

Tlaib also offered Mamdani help with the arrest as she wasn’t sure if he had the authority to complete the arrest.

"I don’t know if it applies to citizen’s arrest," she said. "If he needs help, tell him to call me."

CNN LEGAL ANALYST CALLS OUT 'UTTERLY RIDICULOUS' CLAIM BY MAMDANI ABOUT NETANYAHU ARREST

Rep. Rashida Tlaib, D-Mich., backed the attempt to pursue arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani to try executing the International Criminal Court's warrant against him while visiting New York. (Justin Sullivan/Getty Images; Ronen Zvulul / POOL / AFP via Getty Images; Anna Connors - Pool/Getty Images)

Rep. Jerry Nadler, D-N.Y., was one of the few House Democrats that Fox News Digital spoke with who immediately shut down the idea that Mamdani would be able to pursue this arrest under any avenue explored by the city, arguing it would violate a treaty with the United Nations.

He said, "There's a treaty under which the U.N. is lodged in the United States. Pursuit to that treaty, any diplomat can come to the U.N. and be immune from process."

The U.N. Headquarters Agreement is a 1947 treaty between the United States and the United Nations that governs the legal status of the U.N. headquarters in New York and provides protections for foreign officials attending U.N. meetings.

MAMDANI SAYS NYC IS EXPLORING WHETHER IT CAN ARREST NETANYAHU IF ISRAELI LEADER VISITS FOR UN GENERAL ASSEMBLY

The U.S. is not a member of the ICC, nor is it a party to the Rome Statute, which requires member countries to arrest individuals wanted under ICC warrants.

Mamdani’s call on the federal government to arrest Netanyahu comes after President Donald Trump posted to Truth Social on Monday that the prime minister "will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form."

Secretary of State Marco Rubio also recently threatened to "dismantle" the ICC as he claims the court’s force of international law is overreaching its powers and "threatens every aspect of our political and legal system."

Hannah is a Production Assistant for the Politics team at Fox News Digital.

title: FCC Chairman Carr says spectrum auction will make wireless faster, cheaper while raising billions for Treasury
excerpt:
FCC Chairman Brendan Carr says the spectrum auction could raise $30 to $60 billion for the U.S. Treasury while making wireless faster and cheaper.

The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) on Wedne...
body_text:
FCC Chairman Brendan Carr says the spectrum auction could raise $30 to $60 billion for the U.S. Treasury while making wireless faster and cheaper.

The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) on Wednesday took a major step toward setting up the United States to be the global leader in wireless innovation, which Chairman Brendan Carr believes will make wireless both faster and cheaper for consumers.

The FCC voted to adopt rules to auction 160 megahertz of spectrum in the Upper C-Band by July 2027, which combines terrestrial wireless operations across the entire C-Band to create a single "super-band" spanning 440 megahertz (3.70-4.14 GHz) of prime, mid-band spectrum. While the details are technical for most Americans, Carr said he is simply fulfilling the Trump administration’s agenda.

"One of the things that President Trump did right off the get-go was restore U.S. leadership in wireless. During the Biden years, we fell drastically behind other countries when it came to making spectrum available," Carr told Fox News Digital.

HOW THE US MESSED UP ITS NEW 5G ROLLOUT: ‘IT WASN’T OUR FINEST HOUR’

FCC Chairman Brendan Carr. (Al Drago/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Indeed, the U.S. fell behind countries like China during the 5G rollout during the Biden era. But Carr said the passage of Trump’s Working Families Tax Cut authorized and directed the FCC to bring more airwaves into the commercial marketplace, in order to both increase speeds and drive down prices.

"When most people pick up their smartphone, they usually don't think why it works or what it takes to make it faster or better. But the FCC is now poised to do exactly that by bringing more spectrum, usually invisible airwaves that your phone actually operates on, and push more of that into the commercial market, so we get more competition, faster speeds, more downward pressure on prices," Carr said.

FCC REVIEW OF SPORTS BROADCASTING RULES DRAWS THOUSANDS OF COMMENTS AMID STREAMING BACKLASH, CHAIRMAN SAYS

President Donald Trump shakes hands with House Speaker Mike Johnson after signing his signature bill of tax breaks and spending cuts at the White House, Friday, July 4, 2025, in Washington.  (AP Photo/Julia Demaree Nikhinson)

The order adopts competitive bidding procedures, allowing wireless providers to bid on wireless licenses.

"That's expected to raise many billions of dollars for the U.S. Treasury, anywhere between $30 to $60 billion or more," Carr said.

"So, it's going to be a good win for consumers in that they get faster speeds and better price services," he continued. "It's also a win for the government as well, because we're raising significant revenues by doing that."

Senate Commerce Committee Chairman Ted Cruz, R-Texas, also celebrated the move, saying it will "result in better coverage and faster speeds for consumers."

FCC CHAIR HAS NO REGRETS OVER THE DRAMATIC JIMMY KIMMEL SAGA LAST YEAR

FCC Chairman Brendan Carr said the passage of Trump’s Working Families Tax Cut directed the FCC to bring more airwaves into the commercial marketplace, in order to increase speeds and drive down prices for consumers. (iStock)

The order reconfigures 160 megahertz of the Upper C-band in the contiguous United States for terrestrial wireless flexible use in 3.98–4.14 GHz, with a 20-megahertz guard band at 4.14– 4.16 GHz, according to the FCC. It also folds the Lower C-band and Upper C-band into a single 3.7 GHz Service from 3.7–4.14 GHz in the contiguous United States for terrestrial wireless flexible use and largely harmonizes the licensing, operating and technical rules across the entire band.

The FCC said this promotes "a successful co-existence environment with adjacent band radio altimeters through the adoption of specific technical measures and a carefully coordinated timeline for the introduction of terrestrial wireless operations in the Upper C-band starting in December 2030 that aligns with forthcoming radio altimeter retrofit requirements to be issued by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA).

Brian Flood is a media editor/reporter for FOX News Digital. Story tips can be sent to brian.flood@fox.com and on Twitter: @briansflood.

title: Sophie Cunningham sparks social media meltdown by speaking out to protect women's sports
excerpt: WNBA star Sophie Cunningham shares her powerful message about standing up for girls' sports amidst criticism. She reiterates her commitment to truth and protecting young girls in locker rooms and spor...
body_text:
WNBA star Sophie Cunningham shares her powerful message about standing up for girls' sports amidst criticism. She reiterates her commitment to truth and protecting young girls in locker rooms and sports from competing against biological men, despite receiving negative feedback about her stance on transgender athletes. This comes after she retweeted a CBS Sports host years ago.

WNBA star Sophie Cunningham sent shockwaves in the ongoing culture war over the WNBA and trans athletes in women's sports on Tuesday after revealing her stance on the issue to ESPN.

Cunningham's recent statements sparked debate on social media after she revealed she is in support of protecting women's sports.

"I got a lot of negative feedback about me hating trans. And I'm like, 'I never once said that,'" Cunningham said.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Jun 16, 2026; Indianapolis, Indiana, USA; Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham (8) celebrates a made shot in the first half against the Toronto Tempo at Gainbridge Fieldhouse. Mandatory Credit: Trevor Ruszkowski-Imagn Images (IMAGN IMAGES via Reuters Connect)

"I think that I am here to extend love. But I also think with that love is truth, being honest. And I want to protect young girls in a locker room, or young girls in sport who shouldn't have to go against biological men."

Many prominent figures praised Cunningham for speaking out.

GOP politicians, including Ted Cruz and Eric Schmitt, even chimed in.

Cunningham's statement also garnered praise from athletes and activists involved in the "save women's sports" movement including Riley Gaines, Jennifer Sey and Martina Navratilova, as well as other conservative influencers.

However, Cunningham's statements also prompted some backlash from those who believe biological male trans athletes should be allowed to compete in women's sports.

Some users compared Cunningham's take to that of Minnesota Lynx head coach Cheryl Reeve, who recently told reporters that she opposes bans on trans athletes in women's sports.

Cunningham previously garnered backlash from the left in March 2022 after re-sharing a tweet from CBS Sports host Josh Pate about the controversy surrounding former Penn transgender swimmer Lia Thomas competing on the women's team.

Indiana Fever guard Sophie Cunningham plays in the second half against the Los Angeles Sparks at Gainbridge Fieldhouse in Indianapolis, Ind., on June 27, 2026. (Trevor Ruszkowski/Imagn Images)

"Female collegiate athletes deserve so much better than this," Pate’s tweet said.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Cunningham faced so much backlash over the re-share that she released a statement.

"To my teammates, friends, family, supporters and fans, you know that I stand with all people, especially my fellow athletes. I do not judge, but support all races, religions, sexual preferences and gender equity. We all have a place in this world to compete and I hope we are able to grant everyone that opportunity fairly." she wrote.

Cunningham has emerged as one of the biggest stars in the WNBA over the last year, especially among conservatives.

Riley Gaines, who tied with Thomas at the 2022 NCAA women's swimming championships, told Fox News Digital on the last day of June, after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled to uphold state laws that keep biological males out of women's sports, that she hoped the ruling would be a "clarion call" for athletes like Cunningham to speak out in support of protecting women's sports.

"Let this be a clarion call, not just to Simone, but to every, I think especially elite female athlete, professional female athlete, the likes of Caitlin Clark and Sophie Cunningham, Serena Williams, to link arms," Gaines said.

Jackson Thompson is a sports reporter for Fox News Digital covering critical political and cultural issues in sports, with an investigative lens. Jackson's reporting has been cited in federal government actions related to the enforcement of Title IX, and in legacy media outlets including The New York Times, The Los Angeles Times, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Associated Press and ESPN.com.

title: Former NFL player, funded by league notables including Roger Goodell, wins Arizona GOP Congressional Primary
excerpt:
President Trump's endorsement proves powerful as Rep. Andy Biggs wins the Arizona Republican gubernatorial primary with 72.6% of the vote. He will face Democrat Gov. Katie Hobbs in November.

Jay Feel...
body_text:
President Trump's endorsement proves powerful as Rep. Andy Biggs wins the Arizona Republican gubernatorial primary with 72.6% of the vote. He will face Democrat Gov. Katie Hobbs in November.

Jay Feely, who spent 14 seasons as an NFL kicker for six teams, took a big step toward becoming a United States congressman representing a district that includes parts of Phoenix and most of Scottsdale by winning an Arizona Republican primary Tuesday night.

Feely defeated former state Rep. Joseph Chaplik and businessman John Trobough in a race in which he had the endorsement of President Donald Trump but was accused by his opponents of not being conservative enough.

HALL OF FAMER TONY DUNGY ENDORSES MICHELE TAFOYA IN CROWDED MINNESOTA REPUBLICAN PRIMARY FOR US SENATE

CBS Sports reporter Jay Feely during the game between the Carolina Panthers and the Los Angeles Chargers at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California on Sept. 27, 2020. (Kirby Lee/USA TODAY Sports)

Feely will face the winner of a still-uncalled Democrat primary between former state Rep. Amish Shah and former broadcast journalist Marlene Galán-Woods in the general election.

"Why did I decide to run for office? It is a desire to leave this country better for our children and our grandchildren," Feely told his supporters at his victory celebration. "That's it in its core.

"You know, what we're doing is fighting for the future of this country. I truly believe that. When you look at the two parties, they've never been further apart ideologically. Never. And the Democrat Party has embraced Democratic socialism.

"They have chosen to move further to the left. They are talking about abolishing the border. They're talking about abolishing the police department. They are talking about abolishing the Senate now. They want to get D.C. and Puerto Rico statehood.

"The things that they are talking about are changing the very foundation of this country, and what has made it great for 250 years. And we're not going to let it happen."

NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell addresses the media during the Super Bowl Winning Team Head Coach and MVP Press Conference at the Mandalay Bay North Convention Center on Feb. 12, 2024 in Las Vegas, Nevada. (Don Juan Moore/Getty Images)

The Democrats spent approximately $7 million in their primary race, which illustrates how important they believe getting the right candidate to face off against Feely would be.

Feely, making his first bid for public office at age 50, was also well funded for his primary battle and, not surprisingly, at lot of that cash came from the sports world.

NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell contributed $7,000, which is the legal maximum for an individual.

Cardinals owner Michael Bidwill similarly gave $7,000 to lead a handful of owners joining the Feely cause. Atlanta Falcons owner Arthur Blank ($3,500), New England Patriots owner Bob Kraft ($7,000), Miami Dolphins owner Stephen Ross ($5,000) and Denver Broncos owner Rob Walton ($7,000) all contributed.

Cleveland Browns owner Jimmy Haslam also donated.

FILE - In this Feb. 25, 2020 file photo Denver Broncos general manager John Elway speaks during a news conference at the NFL football scouting combine in Indianapolis. NFL teams are having to rely more heavily on game film of college prospects as they prepare for the draft at a time when the COVID-19 pandemic has halted business as usual. On Tuesday, March 31, 2020 Elway said in a conference call, "We'll just have to conclude the best we can from what we can see on the tape."

Former players similarly helped fund Feely's campaign. Former quarterbacks John Elway, Rich Gannon and Trent Green contributed. So did Jeff Saturday, Todd Heap, Richie Incognito, and Pro Football Hall of Famer Brian Urlacher.

And this: Charles Barkley contributed the maximum $7,000.

Feely should expect a tough campaign and close race in the coming months regardless of his opponent.

District 1 was reliably red from 1953 to 2009, but massive population growth over the years and a 2022 redrawing of the district's lines turned it into a race Democrats won five times starting in 2013 and believe they can flip this year after losing in 2022.

Follow Senior NFL Writer Armando Salguero on X: @ArmandoSalguero

Armando Salguero is OutKick's Senior NFL Writer.

title: Kelsey Grammer warns of chilling danger facing Americans who forget nation's founding history
excerpt:
Kelsey Grammer reflects on starring in "Young Washington" and explains why he believes the Founding Fathers' vision still offers guidance for America's future.

Actor Kelsey Grammer issued a warning a...
body_text:
Kelsey Grammer reflects on starring in "Young Washington" and explains why he believes the Founding Fathers' vision still offers guidance for America's future.

Actor Kelsey Grammer issued a warning about the state of American education, arguing that citizens who fail to learn the nation's founding history risk being manipulated.

Speaking on "Planet Tyrus" Tuesday, Grammer called for an overhaul of the nation's education system while pushing back against critics who dismiss the Founding Fathers as "old White guys in wigs."

"If you don't know where you come from, you don't know who you are. If you don't know who you are, you can be controlled," Grammer warned.

SPEAKER JOHNSON WARNS STUDENTS ARE 'INDOCTRINATED' TO SEE US THROUGH 'WRONG LENSES' AS NATION CELEBRATES 250TH

Kelsey Grammer speaks onstage during a screening of "Frasier" Season 2 at Quad Cinema on Sept. 16, 2024, in New York City. (Roy Rochlin/Getty Images for Paramount+)

"There's a whole bunch of things that just have got to stop with our educational system, and we got to retool it. There's no question," he said.

Grammer recently starred in the film, "Young Washington," which follows the Founding Father before he became the nation's first president. Grammer also hosted the six-episode Fox Nation series "The Patriot War," narrating key points in the American Revolution ahead of the nation's 250th anniversary.

The former "Frasier" and "Cheers" actor said America's past serves as a blueprint for its future — one he believes many young people are dismissing.

KELSEY GRAMMER URGES AMERICANS TO LOOK TO FOUNDING FATHERS FOR GUIDANCE AS NATION FACES DEEP DIVISIONS

"Yeah, I'm pretty tired of the old White guy in [a] wig thing," Grammer told Tyrus. "I mean Thomas Jefferson was 33 when he wrote what is arguably the most important political document in the history of man," he said.

Kelsey Grammer portrays George Washington in the 2003 historical drama "Benedict Arnold: A Question of Honor." (Entertainment Pictures)

Grammer emphasized that the American Revolution was fought on radical ideas rather than identity and called for a stronger push to teach early American history.

"It never had anything to do with the way you looked," Grammer said. "It had everything to do with the way you thought and the way you dreamed, and that's all America."

WATCH THE FULL 'PLANET TYRUS' INTERVIEW ON YOUTUBE

He argued the Founding Fathers did not claim America would be perfect but envisioned an evolving quest for better.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

"The land of the possible. Yeah, absolutely. It's always [going to] fall a little short, but that's just it. It's the world of becoming. We're becoming a more perfect union, always, rather than we just are one," Grammer said.

Grammer said that most of the 56 people who signed the Declaration of Independence "ended in ruin" by sacrificing everything for the nation. He added, "Some of their children were murdered, some of their wives were raped," and said their spirit of resilience in hard times remains today.

"They paid the ultimate sacrifice, the ultimate one. And they would probably do it again. And I think that sort of character is what still is in most of us. I believe in America that way," Grammer said.

Madison Colombo is a writer for Fox News Digital’s Flash, Media, and Culture team, covering daily breaking news and trending topics with an award-winning background in broadcast and digital journalism.

title: Iran’s judiciary warns against ‘infiltrators’, announces new executions
excerpt: Tehran, Iran – Iran’s judiciary has announced several executions this week linked to unrest over recent months, as part of a campaign against those accused of trying to “create divisions” during the w...
body_text:
Tehran, Iran – Iran’s judiciary has announced several executions this week linked to unrest over recent months, as part of a campaign against those accused of trying to “create divisions” during the war with the United States.

The judiciary’s official news outlet on Wednesday announced the execution of Mehdi Khanaki, who was identified as an “active element of a terrorist group” arrested during nationwide protests in January.

State television showed security forces raiding a home in Karaj near Tehran, where several handguns, ammunition and explosives were allegedly found. A “revolutionary court” in the city tried him for “operational action in favour of the Zionist regime, the United States and hostile groups”, among other things.

On Sunday, two more men were executed in connection with a 16-defendant case arising from deadly protests in Isfahan, in central Iran, on January 8. The authorities said the men played a role in the killing of several police officers, but foreign-based activists have countered the state narrative and alleged a lack of due process and the use of torture.

The protests started last December 28 in central Tehran over economic issues but quickly morphed into nationwide expressions of frustration at the establishment, as in previous rounds of demonstrations.

The authorities refer to the unrest, when thousands were killed across the country, mostly on the nights of January 8 and 9 , as a “coup” orchestrated by the US and Israel.

They also reject findings by Amnesty International and other international human rights organisations that security forces carried out “mass unlawful killings on an unprecedented scale” during a total internet and communications shutdown.

Tens of thousands have been arrested in the aftermath of the protests, as well as the US-Israeli war that started on February 28 .

The Coordinating Council of Iranian Teachers’ Trade Associations, a non-governmental body inside Iran, last week condemned what it called “intensified prison sentences and continued pressure on teachers’ guild activists” by the authorities.

Security-related offences follow a law passed after the 12-day war with Israel in June 2025, which aims to more severely punish “espionage” and other actions perceived to be cooperating with “enemies” and undermining national security.

Hundreds of Iranians, including businessmen and celebrities based in Iran and abroad, have also had their entire assets confiscated by the state.

State TV also periodically airs “confessions” from people arrested or executed for those charges.

The latest was broadcast on Monday, when a young man with a blurred-out face could be seen saying he posted Instagram stories that opposed the Islamic Republic during the current war. Tehran’s police force said the unidentified man released “norm-breaking and anti-homeland content” online in an attempt to “damage the psychological security of the society”.

“I made a mistake and I will not repeat it. I am now sure that we must all now stand together to build Iran, nobody else has a right to commit an aggression against our country,” he said.

On July 13 , Iran’s Ministry of Intelligence released a list of “hostile media” that included dozens of media networks, 61 individual “human-media” figures and 310 accounts or online pages. It warned that sending photographs, video or information to a listed outlet, person or account can trigger a prison sentence and permanent exclusion from public employment, unless authorities invoke a more serious charge.

The message goes along with rhetoric of steadfastness and togetherness that has been repeatedly emphasised by Iranian authorities during the war.

Earlier this week, Mojtaba Khamenei, who was selected as Iran’s new supreme leader in March after his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed in the opening salvo of the war, called for a “sacred unity” among the people and political factions.

Speaking during a televised meeting on Monday with President Masoud Pezeshkian and senior clerical judges in the judiciary, Chief Justice Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei doubled down on the same message – complete with heavy religious undertones.

“We must not allow devils among humans and jinn to infiltrate the ranks of believers and our people to create division and schism,” he said.

The judiciary chief said these “alarmists, rumour-spreaders, hypocrites and infiltrators” would not all declare themselves as such, instead disguising themselves as insiders who would set people against each other.

This comes as the US increasingly points to alleged rifts among Iranian authorities, and categorises them into a faction that wants to keep fighting, especially over the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, and another group that wishes to reach a negotiated resolution soon in order to stop the country from being destroyed.

Iranian authorities have rejected such classifications.

title: Two Russians handed down jail terms for terrorism and spying in Angola
excerpt:
(From left to right) Russians Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were on trial with two Angolans, Amor Carlos Tomé and Francisco Oliveira

Two Russians have been found guilty of terrorism and spying by a...
body_text:
(From left to right) Russians Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were on trial with two Angolans, Amor Carlos Tomé and Francisco Oliveira

Two Russians have been found guilty of terrorism and spying by a court in Angola's capital, Luanda.

Political consultant Igor Ratchin was sentenced to 11 years in prison and translator Lev Lakshtanov was given eight years. They had denied the charges.

They were arrested last August over accusations of stirring up anti-government protests, conducting a campaign of disinformation and attempting to interfere in next year's presidential election.

The Russians' lawyers had previously requested their extradition to Russia under bilateral co-operation agreements. The BBC has not been able to establish whether the court approved that request.

In the indictment, seen by the BBC, the authorities alleged that Ratchin and Lakshtanov were involved in a political influence operation linked to Africa Politology, which the Angolan authorities described as a Russian political network that emerged from the now-defunct Wagner mercenary group.

Prosecutors further alleged Africa Politology sought to spread anti-Western narratives, cultivate political contacts and influence politics in Angola ahead of next year's presidential elections.

The defendants had denied any connection to Wagner or the Russian state, saying they were working on plans to establish a Russian cultural centre in Luanda.

Political operatives linked to Wagner have been active across Africa for more than a decade, in particular in the Central African Republic, Mali and Madagascar.

Inside the alleged Russian operation to trigger anti-government protests in Angola

Two Angolans, sports journalist Amor Carlos Tomé and political activist Francisco Oliveira, were also on trial on similar charges for their alleged part in the Russian-linked influence operation, which they both denied.

Tomé received a two-year suspended sentence and Oliveira was acquitted.

Prosecutors appealed immediately after the judgement, which was read out after 23:00 local time on Tuesday.

Tomé is expected to remain in custody pending the outcome of the appeal.

The BBC has contacted the Russian embassy in Angola for comment but had not received a response at the time of publication.

Elizeu Nguiniti, the lawyer representing Ratchin and Lakshtanov, told the BBC he would not comment on the decision.

After the judgement was read out, he told the court he disagreed with the verdict, which found his clients guilty of four of 11 charges - criminal association, espionage, terrorism and illegal retention of currency - but said he accepted the court's decision.

According to Angola's privately owned news site Novo Jornal, the Russians told the court during previous hearings they had held private meetings with political figures from both the ruling MPLA and opposition Unita parties, but denied that the meetings were connected to any illegal activity.

It reported that the court had rejected a defence request to call several of those politicians as witnesses, ruling that their testimony was "not necessary" for establishing the facts of the case.

Prosecutors alleged that the defendants played a role in organising deadly unrest in Angola in 2025.

They alleged the Russians paid local journalists and experts more than $24,000 (£18,000) to spread pro-Russian and anti-government content aimed at provoking political change in Angola.

They also cited publications linked to the operation, notes and photographs related to the 2025 protests found on the defendants' devices, as well as alleged contacts with senior Angolan political figures.

Many Angolan activists strongly disagree with the prosecution's argument that the protests did not happen organically.

Novo Jornal previously reported that Lakshtanov told the court during one of the hearings that he had lived and worked in Angola for nearly four decades and that his long academic career was incompatible with participation in terrorist organisations.

Earlier Ratchin had explained to the court that he was a businessman who had come to Angola to establish a Russian cultural centre and that payments he made to various individuals were compensation for services rendered, Novo Jornal reported.

He reportedly said he was surprised by his arrest in August 2025 and the subsequent charges.

Early proceedings of the trial, which began in March, were marked by a controversy.

During one of the initial hearings, the prosecutor declined to read the indictment aloud in court. She argued that the defendants were already aware of the accusations against them and therefore formal reading was not required.

The defence disagreed. They argued that since the trial was public, those attending the proceedings, including journalists, should be able to hear and understand the allegations.

Nevertheless, the prosecutor maintained that reading the indictment was optional and refused to do so.

The indictment itself had been criticised even before the trial began.

The defence has since consistently challenged its contents, arguing it often relied on assumptions rather than concrete evidence.

The BBC had previously identified a number of misspellings and apparent factual errors in the document, but received no clarification from the prosecution about these.

Additional reporting by Ilya Barabanov and BBC Russian's Investigations Team

Why Russia's Africa propaganda warrior was sent home

'My skin was peeling' - the African women tricked into making Russian drones

War ‘tour’, football and graffiti: How Russia is trying to influence Africa

Go to BBCAfrica.com, external for more news from the African continent.

Follow us on Twitter @BBCAfrica, external, on Facebook at BBC Africa, external or on Instagram at bbcafrica, external

title: Jason Alexander apologizes after Courtney Stodden says 2012 comedy sketch objectified her as a minor
excerpt:
Fox News Flash top entertainment and celebrity headlines are here.

Jason Alexander is taking accountability for his past mistakes.

The 66-year-old actor recently apologized to Courtney Stodden for p...
body_text:
Fox News Flash top entertainment and celebrity headlines are here.

Jason Alexander is taking accountability for his past mistakes.

The 66-year-old actor recently apologized to Courtney Stodden for participating in a 2012 Funny or Die sketch in which he held a cellphone up to her chest and made comments about her body when she was a minor.

"Looking back at the comedy sketch in which Ms. Stodden and I participated in 2012, I completely agree that it was inappropriate and I truly regret it," he told Fox News Digital in a statement. "But more importantly, I am deeply sorry for any harm or distress it has caused Ms. Stodden. I offer her my sincerest apologies."

His apology comes one day after Stodden called out Alexander on Instagram as one of the writers of the sketch, adding, "As an adult, that fact has become harder—not easier—for me to understand."

GOLDIE HAWN SOUNDS THE ALARM ON CHILDHOOD CELEBRITY CULTURE: 'ALL I CAN SAY IS IT'S A NIGHTMARE'

Alexander apologized to Stodden for a 2012 sketch in which he objectified her body. (Getty Images)

In the Instagram post, she shared a photo of her and Alexander in the sketch, in which she is wearing a bikini, and the actor is rubbing a cellphone across her chest.

"I was 17 years old in this photo. The older I get, the more impossible this is to understand," she wrote. "During this comedy sketch, Jason Alexander repeatedly rubbed his phone across my breasts while saying there was 'no signal' in my head but 'plenty of signal' in my chest. Later in the sketch, he joked that when I turned 18 he’d like to take me behind the couch and 'have his way' with me."

She added that as a minor she "didn’t have the legal power to decide whether I wanted to participate" and that "the contracts were signed by adults."

At the time, she was married to Doug Hutchison, who she tied the knot with when she was 16 and he was 51, noting the paycheck for this appearance went to him.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Stodden described feeling alone while on the set of Funny or Die. (Michael Buckner/Variety via Getty Images)

"I was the only child in the room. I felt so alone," she added. "Looking back now, I don’t see comedy. I see a 17-year-old girl whose body became part of an adult joke. People still say, 'You chose to be there.' Legally, I couldn’t make those decisions for myself. That’s the point."

She ended the post by acknowledging that while she isn't able to change what she went through, she finds herself asking, "How did so many adults look at a minor and decide this was acceptable?"

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

"That’s exactly why I speak out today," she concluded. "Children deserve laws that protect them. They shouldn’t have to become adults before realizing the law should have protected them all along."

The caption also included a call to support California Assembly Bill 1267, which aims to permanently end child marriage by setting the minimum legal age for marriage in California to 18 with no exceptions.

Stodden married Hutchison when she was 16 and he was 51, and is now advocating for legislation banning child marriage without exception. (JB Lacroix/WireImage)

Stodden and Hutchison got married in 2011 despite their 34-year age gap. They split up and got back together a few times before officially calling it quits in 2016, although divorce papers were not filed until March 2018, with the divorce being finalized in March 2020.

She announced their divorce in an Instagram post featuring her wedding photo, calling it "an emotional day," but assured her fans "it’s for the better."

Stodden announced her divorce on Instagram in March 2020, saying in the caption she now feels taken advantage of. (Tibrina Hobson/Getty Images)

"I look back at this picture and feel absolutely taken advantage of," she wrote. "I’ve been scared to even speak up about feeling groomed or being verbally abused during the almost 10 year marriage because I was a child and he was 50 when we married but I’m a woman now and it’s time for me to put my big girl pants on and speak on this matter."

She continued: "I’ve felt completely trapped, manipulated and at times abandoned by adults // growing up in such an environment — it became a lonely and dark place."

Lori A. Bashian is an entertainment news writer at Fox News Digital.

title: JONATHAN TURLEY: Judge Boasberg whines about Trump’s power and then gets caught exceeding his own
excerpt:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

Chief Judge James Boasberg is frustrated ... and he is not alone. Recently, the D.C. federal judge reportedly groused ...
body_text:
Fox News Flash top headlines are here. Check out what's clicking on FoxNews.com.

Chief Judge James Boasberg is frustrated ... and he is not alone. Recently, the D.C. federal judge reportedly groused that the D.C. appellate court had ordered him to reconsider a case about whether the D.C. government engaged in biased enforcement against political graffiti. Conversely, the litigants are equally irked over what they see as Boasberg's bias after he responded by, again, dismissing their claims without a trial.

For a second time, Boasberg has barred pro-life groups from access to a jury after members were arrested for writing in chalk, "Black Pre-Born Lives Matter" on a sidewalk while Black Lives Matter writings were left untouched.
