Angelica Stabile is a lifestyle reporter for Fox News Digital.

title: Ukrainian drones hit more sites of Russian online retailer Wildberries
excerpt:
Watch: Warehouses on fire after Ukraine hit Russian online retailer

Ukrainian drones have hit warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer again, injuring 15 people, according to the comp...
body_text:
Watch: Warehouses on fire after Ukraine hit Russian online retailer

Ukrainian drones have hit warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer again, injuring 15 people, according to the company's founder and regional governors.

Logistics hubs run by Wildberries in two south-west cities were impacted by overnight attacks and workers evacuated, the founder said.

It is the second time Ukraine has attacked the firm's facilities in four days, with warehouses in Tambov and Elektrostal hit over the weekend, killing eight people.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said on Wednesday that Ukraine had hit logistics hubs in the Krasnodar and Stavropol regions "involved in providing the Russian army with drone components, navigation equipment and other gear".

Wildberries founder Tatyana Kim said workers were evacuated after the attacks on logistics centres in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk in the Stavropol region.

"There are no words to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on us, who are simply doing our jobs," she wrote on Telegram. "But these situations demonstrate the most important thing: we must continue to do what we do for the benefit of people."

Five people were hurt in a warehouse complex on the outskirts of Nevinnomyssk, Governor Vladimir Vladimirov said, with everyone receiving outpatient treatment.

In Krasnodar, ten people were injured in an attack on a warehouse complex, with three in a serious condition, the region's governor Veniamin Kondratyev said.

BBC Verify has analysed footage and confirmed fires in the two locations.

Reports of the strikes first emerged just after 00:00 GMT, with the Russian state news agency TASS reporting that air defences had been activated in Nevinnomyssk. Dramatic footage filmed by locals began to emerge shortly after.

In both locations, the impact appears substantial, with large fires engulfing the buildings and huge columns of smoke rising into the air.

A still taken from a social media video verified by Reuters shows smoke rising above a warehouse

Krasnodar's governor said the region was hit by a "massive attack last night", with drone debris also hitting the grounds of a company in Armavir and killing one employee. The city administration reported that an oil depot was struck, causing a fire.

Drone fragments also hit two apartment buildings in Krasnodar and two homes in the village of Dinskaya, Kondratyev said. There were no injuries.

Zelensky said Ukraine had struck an oil depot, as well as a tanker and four cargo ships of the Russian shadow fleet in the waters of the Sea of Azov and Black Sea.

Russia has been operating a "shadow fleet" to evade international sanctions imposed on its oil exports after its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The overnight strikes mark the second targeting of Wildberries' facilities in less than a week.

Recently published satellite imagery gives an indication of the damage caused to other Wildberries sites that were hit by Ukrainian strikes over the weekend.

In Elektrostal, a city near the capital Moscow, three buildings were completely gutted.

Serhii Kuzan, chairman of the Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Centre, previously told the BBC that Wildberries' website had been used by Russian volunteers to buy military equipment.

"The primary rationale for striking Wildberries' warehouses is to disrupt Russian logistics and the supply of dual-use goods, critical electronics, sanctioned goods and the like to the Russian army and Russian arms manufacturers," he said.

"The collateral damage from such strikes could also have a serious impact on the Russian economy, as well as a psychological effect on Russian society and, likely, on continued support for the war."

Unlike retail giant Amazon, Wildberries and its rival Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods, and handling payments.

The recent strikes have impacted hundreds of sellers whose livelihoods depend on online marketplaces.

Trader Elena, not her real name, told BBC Russian she had lost clothing she sells in the attacks, which is her only income.

She said that "if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage. It's terrifying to keep loading my goods into what feels like a furnace".

Additional reporting by Paul Brown, BBC Verify, Vitaly Shevchenko, and BBC Russian

Russian online retail warehouses hit by deadly Ukrainian strikes

title: FOX reports massive, record-shattering ratings for 2026 FIFA World Cup Final between Spain and Argentina
excerpt: Kayce Smith joins Will Cain & The Crew to address Argentina's classless World Cup actions. Plus, AOC's fake southern accent, Hasan Piker's chilling "collaborator" rhetoric, and Olive Garden's new ID s...
body_text:
Kayce Smith joins Will Cain & The Crew to address Argentina's classless World Cup actions. Plus, AOC's fake southern accent, Hasan Piker's chilling "collaborator" rhetoric, and Olive Garden's new ID system, not for voting but endless pasta.

Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, many doubters around the world and within sports media, claimed that the United States would be a disastrous host.

That hosting the tournament under President Donald Trump would mean fewer international visitors, and that the US shouldn't host because soccer was still a niche domestic sport. And surprise, surprise, turns out those concerns were completely invalid.

Attendance broke records before the end of the group stage. Visitors flowed into the US from around the world, then gave glowing reviews to their hosts, stadiums, and local fans. Some were even overcome with emotion at the kindness they experienced in the country.

TRUMP ADMIN TAUNTS LEFT FOR ‘PRAYING’ FOR WORLD CUP DISASTER AS DOT TOUTS RECORD NUMBERS

Argentina's Lionel Messi (10) stands next to Spain's Pedri (20) during the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Frank Franklin II)

The other concern? That fans wouldn't watch? Turns out that could not have been wrong.

Already, in the round of 16, the match between the US Men's National Team and Belgium set records for the most watched English language broadcast of a soccer game in the history of the country. The World Cup Final however, featured two teams that have little connection to the United States: Spain and Argentina. Yes, Lionel Messi and Lamine Yamal added star power, but rosters on both sides did not rise to the level of familiarity most have with say, France or England.

And yet, Tuesday afternoon, FOX reported that ratings and viewership blew that USMNT-Belgium game out of the water. The network announced that Spain-Argentina drew a whopping 38.937 million viewers, peaking at nearly 52 million. That shattered the record for most-watched English-language soccer broadcast in the country's history.

UNITED STATES-BOSNIA AND HERZEGOVINA WORLD CUP MATCH DELIVERS MASSIVE TV RATINGS

FIFA President Gianni Infantino (right) with President Donald Trump as Spain lifts the FIFA World Cup trophy after the FIFA World Cup 2026 Final match at New York New Jersey Stadium on July 19, 2026 in East Rutherford, New Jersey. (Catherine Ivill - AMA/Getty Images)

Not only that, but it represented the highest viewership for a non-Super Bowl sporting event since the Winter Olympics in 1984. That's remarkable, and it shows how far soccer has come in the United States, and how interest in the World Cup has surged.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

It also raises the possibility that the US should be considered a bit of rotating host for the tournament. If the field expands to 64 teams, as expected, few other countries have the existing infrastructure to host that many games in large stadiums. And growing the sport in the US is a key goal for FIFA.

Spain's forward Ferran Torres celebrates scoring his team's first goal during the 2026 World Cup football tournament final match between Spain and Argentina at the New York/New Jersey Stadium in East Rutherford on July 19, 2026. (FRANCK FIFE / AFP via Getty Images)

OUTKICK IS NOW ON THE FOX APP: CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD

Just imagine how high those numbers would be in a 2038 tournament, if the USMNT can go deeper into the tournament. Even reaching a World Cup final. Hopefully we won't have to imagine it.

title: EU bids to finalise new Russia sanctions amid discontent from some member states – Europe live
excerpt:
EU ambassadors head to Brussels aiming to sign off on 21st package of sanctions, after previous attempts ended without success

Rubio gets asked about his plans to meet Russia’s foreign minister Lavro...
body_text:
EU ambassadors head to Brussels aiming to sign off on 21st package of sanctions, after previous attempts ended without success

Rubio gets asked about his plans to meet Russia’s foreign minister Lavrov tomorrow.

He says “the war in Ukraine, I think, has in many ways impeded the ability of Russia and the US to find areas of agreements on other topics,” but the US can still cooperate with Moscow in some areas.

He says the US “would like to see that war come to an end,” and “it would be good for Russia if that war came to an end,” so “we will raise it.”

“The US remains open and willing to play a constructive role in bringing that war to an end if that opportunity presents itself,” he says.

He says the talks between the US and Russia didn’t make much progress in recent months, but “perhaps there are now new conditions in place that will allow that conversation to happen and it is one of the things we will explore tomorrow.

Right on cue, US secretary of state Rubio is speaking to reporters in Manila now.

He’s, perhaps understandably, mostly getting questions about Iran, but I am listening in for any lines on Europe, Ukraine or Russia ahead of his Lavrov meeting later this week.

Russian foreign minister Sergei Lavrov just told reporters that he will be meeting US secretary of state Marco Rubio on the sidelines of a regional Asean (that’s the Association of Southeast Asian Nations) forum in Manila this Thursday.

The Venice Biennale said it would challenge a European Union decision to withdraw a €2 ⁠million grant in response to the return of Russia to its 2026 edition, despite continuing aggression on Ukraine, Reuters reported.

The Italian government sharply criticised the move, while the European Commission warned it ​would suspend or terminate funding if the Russian ‌pavilion returned.

The European Commission confirmed on Tuesday that it had decided to revoke the funding following legal advice and a recommendation from Brussels.

“Cultural events financed with European taxpayers’ money should safeguard democratic values ‌and promote dialogue, diversity and freedom of expression, values that are not respected in present-day Russia,” said Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier, according to Italian ​media.

And after this quick round up of news from across Europe, let’s get the latest on Ukraine and related news.

Greece is expected to experience its hottest day of the year on Wednesday, with temperatures reaching up to 43C according to meteorologists, and several regions placed on alert due to the risk of wildfires, AFP reported.

Outdoor work bans have also been announced during the warmest hours of the day for health reasons.

The civil protection authority has placed the greater Athens area, central Greece and the Peloponnese on maximum wildfire alert for the first time this season.

A court order directing the French government to ban companies from trading or investing in the Palestinian occupied territories is being sought today, in a novel action by five human rights organisations that tests the enforceability of international law.

The legal request to France’s highest administrative court argues the French government has failed to implement an advisory opinion issued two years ago by the International Court of Justice (ICJ), that declared the Israeli occupation of the Palestinian territories was unlawful and countries should not do anything to aid the occupation.

The five NGOs taking the action are the International Federation for Human Rights (FIDH), Jurists for the Respect of International Law (JURDI), the International Centre of Justice for Palestinians (ICJP), La Ligue des Droits Humains (LDH), and Law for Palestine.

The French foreign minister Jean-Noël Barrot on Monday called for “the end of imports of products from illegal settlements into the European Union”. The human rights groups argue however that France has a national legal obligation and cannot simply wait for the creation of a consensus inside the EU, where there is an impasse on the issue.

The ICJ, in a landmark ruling in July 2024, found that all states are under an obligation not to recognise the Israeli occupation of the Palestinian territories as lawful nor to aid or assist it.

The UN general assembly in September 2024 also resolved that countries had an obligation to distinguish between the territory of the state of Israel and the Occupied Palestinian Territory, in order to “take steps to prevent trade or investment relations that assist in the maintenance of the illegal situation created by Israel in the OPT“.

In other news, Hungary’s ⁠former nationalist prime minister Viktor ⁠Orbán confirmed ⁠that prosecutors had taken ⁠his Fidesz party’s servers during a ⁠raid on ​Tuesday, as we reported on Europe Live.

Orban, ‌who was ‌ousted in an ‌election in April, also told a briefing that officials to ‌be elected by the new ​Tisza government would be “illegitimate” and Fidesz would use ⁠all peaceful means to ​fight ​against what ​he called ​was ‌tyranny by prime minister ​Péter Magyar’s government.

A Ukrainian artist, writer and cultural manager from Mariupol, and the founder of Platform TU, an independent cultural initiative now operating in exile after Russia’s full-scale invasion.

Over the years of working with western colleagues, I had learned to accept that they don’t fully understand what war feels like from the inside. They would ask me to find someone in occupied Mariupol for an interview, or seem genuinely puzzled that I couldn’t just log on at a time that suited them. I made my peace with this long ago – they don’t have to understand. But this time, it was a Ukrainian who asked.

It was winter, the kind where every online meeting had to be carefully scheduled around power outage timetables – and sometimes around shelling timetables, as we had already begun to joke among ourselves in that dark way. The question about rescheduling the meeting came from a colleague who had left for Europe in 2022, and it hit me almost physically, precisely because she was the one to say it. That innocent query had no business feeling like an accusation – and yet it did. For a long time, I couldn’t understand why.

We knew that war would bring pain and rupture. But there are ruptures no one warned us about – ruptures that are quiet and slow, and for that reason the most devastating. One of them runs not between Ukraine and the world, but through Ukrainians ourselves – between those who left and those who stayed. Almost no one talks about it. But it’s there, and with every passing month it grows wider.

Ukraine ⁠has agreed to export ⁠drones ⁠to the ​United States to ⁠participate in the Pentagon’s ⁠Drone Dominance ​programme, ‌a source ‌told Reuters.

Six Ukrainian companies have received permission ‌to export about 100 drones ​each, the source said.

There was ⁠no official ​confirmation from ​Kyiv ​or Washington so far.

Some much-awaited changes in the way the Ukrainian army is organised will kick in today, after president Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests.

In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said.

Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after his removal as defence minister prompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion.

Ukraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war.

“We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline.

Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored.

Ukraine’s president Volodymyr Zelenskyy praised the overnight attack on “logistics hubs involved in supplying the Russian army with drone component, navigation equipment and other gear.”

“We are, quite justly, bringing the war back home – to Russia. Peace is needed, and Ukraine has presented the Russian side with every proposal. They must be forced to turn to diplomacy.”

EU ambassadors are meeting in Brussels today in a bid to sign off on the 21st package of sanctions against Russia after previous attempts ended with no success.

Lithuania’s ambassador to the EU, Nerijus Aleksiejūnas, told the Lithuanian media that he was sensing a growing fatigue with the sanctions amid concerns that some measures could actually pose difficult questions for some of the member states.

“The argument heard most often is that sanctions should not harm European countries and their economies compared with Russia,” he said, as reported by LRT.

As a result, the EU dropped proposed restrictions on Russia’s fishing sectors, a proposed ban on Russian soldiers entering Europe (as France, Italy, and Spain issued the highest number of visas to Russian citizens last year, LRT said), and on sanctions against Russian Orthodox patriarch Kirill. Greece continues to oppose proposed restrictions on Russian LNG supplies.

It’s far from given whether the ambassadors will be able to sign off on the 21st package today, before they break for summer holidays. I will keep an eye on this. The meeting starts 10am local time (9am UK).

In the meantime, Ukraine continues long-range strikes inside Russia as it hit more warehouses of Russia’s largest online retailer, Wildberries, overnight. Eight people were reportedly injured.

Elsewhere, I will keep an eye on developments across Europe for you, as we are expecting updates from France, Hungary, and Italy, among others.

It’s Wednesday, 22 July 2026, it’s Jakub Krupa here, and this is Europe Live.

title: Transport secretary ‘100% sure’ new cap on bus fares in England is fully funded – UK politics live
excerpt:
Earlier this week, Burnham’s new government faced questions about how the removal of VAT on electricity bills would be funded

Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of UK politics.

Andy Burnh...
body_text:
Earlier this week, Burnham’s new government faced questions about how the removal of VAT on electricity bills would be funded

Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of UK politics.

Andy Burnham’s new government is facing fresh questions on how it will fund its new policies aimed at easing the cost of living, after the prime minister announced a £2 cap on single bus fares this morning.

The cap on single bus fares in England will be in place from January, reducing the current cap of £3. The government said the measure is backed by £454m of funding, including money to allow devolved governments to take similar action. About £400m of that funding will come from switching investment in international climate finance to loans, with the rest from savings in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero budget.

It follows yesterday’s announcement of a cut to VAT on household electricity bills from 1 October, which the government said will be funded by cancelling the digital ID scheme. But former cabinet minister Darren Jones suggested the scheme was “unfunded” and said the government “will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget”.

When posed this question on the bus fare cap, transport secretary Heidi Alexander said she was “100% sure” the new policy was fully funded, saying the government was “reprioritising spending”.

“The prime minister has been absolutely clear that we’re going to observe the fiscal rules and that you won’t get any unfunded spending commitments from this government,” she told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme.

She also insisted the cut to VAT on domestic electricity bills was properly financed, telling BBC Breakfast that it was “not true to say the money isn’t there to implement the changes on electricity bills”.

She criticised Jones, who was sacked as the Duchy of Lancaster, for questioning how new government policies will be funded on social media, telling Sky News she was “not sure it was either the most timely or wise tweet that Darren has ever put out”.

Conservative shadow chancellor Mel Stride said the government could not answer the question: “Where is the money coming from?” Speaking to BBC Breakfast, he said there was not any money to be saved from scrapping the digital ID scheme because “it was never funded in the first place”.

Wes Streeting is speaking now at the Farnborough airshow, you can watch here:

The new defence secretary Wes Streeting is in Hampshire for the Farnborough airshow, where he is expected to speak in a couple of minutes. We will bring you the latest lines.

PA has reported some of his earlier remarks about the armed forces needing to boost their use of artificial intelligence (AI).

He described a new deal with BAE Systems as a “groundbreaking moment for UK defence”, adding: “We must match the speed at which our adversaries are developing AI technology.”

BAE Systems announced the Ministry of Defence has given it a contract extension worth £708m to continue developing a next generation combat aircraft as part of the global combat air programme.

Meanwhile, the new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh was met by campaigners from Fossil Free London outside the Department of Energy Security and Net Zero this morning, demanding that she scrap the Rosebank oilfield, Damian Carrington reports.

Protesters dressed as firefighters held placards reading, ‘We’re already putting out fires, don’t start new ones.’
 Robin Wells, director of Fossil Free London said: “We all deserve to know where our next meal is coming from, to feel safe in our homes and have faith for our future. But the stability of our food supply, the integrity of our homes and the future of organised society is in serious jeopardy due to the gouging of new fossil fuels out of our UK seas.

“With Rosebank and Jackdaw, Big Oil billionaires are pointing a flame thrower at our health and security and the government could soon pull the trigger.”

Andy Burnham is likely to approve the two fields according to recent speculation. Labour’s 2024 manifesto pledged to honour existing exploration licences but not issue new ones. So-called “tiebacks”, where offshore oil and gasfields are connected to already approved production facilities via pipelines, may provide a loophole. Despite suggestions from opposition politicians, the new fields are too small to reduce energy bills, increase energy security or provide many jobs.

Fahnbulleh has been a strong supporter of climate action and the drive to net zero emissions. In 2025, when energy minister, she told the Commons: “We will sprint to clean power, because that is the route by which we achieve energy security for the country … we are on the right side of history and on the side of consumers.”

UK inflation dropped by more than expected in June to 2.6%, in a boost for Burnham’s plans to reduce the cost of living.

The new prime minister has pledged to bring down the cost of living to give households more disposable income and improve the outlook for the economy, announcing a winter VAT cut on electricity bills and lowering the cap on bus fares in England.

Last month’s consumer prices index reading outdid economists’ forecasts of a slide from 2.8% in May to 2.7%, amid a fall in the prices of fuel, particularly diesel, helped by the unstable truce in the Middle East conflict.

The Office for National Statistics said clothing also dropped in price month on month along with the cost of transport and food, offsetting modest price increases in most other goods and services.

Grant Fitzner, the ONS chief economist, said:

double quotation markFood prices fell this month, driven by products including chocolate, margarine and beef. Clothing prices also fell with the start of summer sales, with bigger discounts than last year.

The cost of raw materials dipped for the first time since January, mainly due to the lower price of crude oil, while the increase in the costs of goods leaving factories slowed again.

However, analysts pointed out the reprieve is likely to be temporary, with the recent escalation of hostilities in the Middle East pushing the price of Brent crude back up above $90 a barrel this week.

Also this morning, Heidi Alexander declined to answer whether two men jailed for the death of PC Andrew Harper will be eligible for early release under Labour plans to tackle overcrowding in prisons.

Harper sustained fatal injuries while responding to a quad bike theft in Sulhamstead, Berkshire, in August 2019. He died after getting caught in a strap attached to the back of a car and dragged down a country road as three teenage suspects fled the scene of the burglary.

The transport secretary said ministers had to make “difficult but necessary decisions”, but added that the driver of the car which fatally dragged Harper along a road in 2019 would not be eligible for automatic release, although his two passengers could be eligible to get out halfway through their sentences.

Alexander told BBC Breakfast: “It was a horrific crime that happened. There were three individuals in the car at the time that that incident happened.

“The individual who was behind the wheel was given a longer sentence than the other two people in the car. That individual, the person behind the wheel, has been barred from any of these sentencing changes and he will have to face a parole board before he sees the light of day.

“This government did have to take some difficult decisions back in the summer of 2024. When we entered government, there was a day when there were about 100 prison spaces left because the previous government had failed to build the prison spaces that his country needs.”

My colleagues Kiran Stacey and Peter Walker take a closer look at the questions on how the government will finance its spending pledges:

double quotation markOn his first full day in power, the prime minister faced questions about how he would fund the removal of VAT from electricity bills and an expected uplift in defence spending – an issue that led his new chancellor, John Healey, to resign as Keir Starmer’s defence secretary last month.

Downing Street said on Tuesday that the tax cut was funded for the next financial year, but money would have to be found in future years from existing departmental budgets, suggesting spending cuts to come. No 10 has also ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending.

The Guardian has also learned Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for implementing his promise to roll out a national care service, a move that could cost further money before the election.

We have more comments from transport secretary Heidi Alexander who was on the media round this morning answering questions on the new £2 bus fare cap.

The government said this morning that the cap would be funded by reprioritising the energy budget, essentially money that was going to be given to third countries for climate projects as a grant will be now be given as a loan which has to be paid back.

When pressed for more details by Sky News, Alexander said they will “need to be worked out”.

She also said she was not sure whether developing countries will have to pay the UK interest on the loans.

Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of UK politics.

Andy Burnham’s new government is facing fresh questions on how it will fund its new policies aimed at easing the cost of living, after the prime minister announced a £2 cap on single bus fares this morning.

The cap on single bus fares in England will be in place from January, reducing the current cap of £3. The government said the measure is backed by £454m of funding, including money to allow devolved governments to take similar action. About £400m of that funding will come from switching investment in international climate finance to loans, with the rest from savings in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero budget.

It follows yesterday’s announcement of a cut to VAT on household electricity bills from 1 October, which the government said will be funded by cancelling the digital ID scheme. But former cabinet minister Darren Jones suggested the scheme was “unfunded” and said the government “will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget”.

When posed this question on the bus fare cap, transport secretary Heidi Alexander said she was “100% sure” the new policy was fully funded, saying the government was “reprioritising spending”.

“The prime minister has been absolutely clear that we’re going to observe the fiscal rules and that you won’t get any unfunded spending commitments from this government,” she told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme.

She also insisted the cut to VAT on domestic electricity bills was properly financed, telling BBC Breakfast that it was “not true to say the money isn’t there to implement the changes on electricity bills”.

She criticised Jones, who was sacked as the Duchy of Lancaster, for questioning how new government policies will be funded on social media, telling Sky News she was “not sure it was either the most timely or wise tweet that Darren has ever put out”.

Conservative shadow chancellor Mel Stride said the government could not answer the question: “Where is the money coming from?” Speaking to BBC Breakfast, he said there was not any money to be saved from scrapping the digital ID scheme because “it was never funded in the first place”.

title: Russia reports injuries after top online retailer Wildberries hit again
excerpt:
Ukrainian drones have again targeted warehouses belonging to Russia’s biggest online retailer, Wildberries, injuring eight people in the latest attack, the company says.

The CEO of Wildberries, Tatia...
body_text:
Ukrainian drones have again targeted warehouses belonging to Russia’s biggest online retailer, Wildberries, injuring eight people in the latest attack, the company says.

The CEO of Wildberries, Tatiana Kim, said on Wednesday morning that the company’s logistics centres in the southern cities of Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk had been evacuated following the attack.

Vladimir Vladimirov, the governor of Stavropol Krai, said on Russia’s state-run Max platform that a warehouse complex had caught fire after a drone attack on the outskirts of Nevinnomyssk. He added that five people had received medical treatment.

The Krasnodar Krai operational headquarters said later that three more people were wounded when drone debris fell on the warehouse in Krasnodar.

On Saturday, Ukraine had targeted two key Wildberries warehouses, killing eight night-shift workers and sparking fires.

Separately, local authorities in the southern city of Armavir said on Wednesday that one worker was killed when drone debris fell on one of the sites there.

Confirming Wednesday’s attacks, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said that the army had successfully targeted “important facilities in the Krasnodar and Stavropol regions”.

“Logistics centres involved in supplying the Russian army with components for drones, navigation equipment and supplies, as well as another oil depot,” he said.

“In the Black Sea and Azov Sea areas, a tanker and four cargo ships belonging to the Russian shadow fleet were hit. We thank all our soldiers of the Ukrainian Defence Forces for these results. We are justly returning the war to its source – to Russia,” Zelenskyy added.

The Commander of Ukraine’s Unmanned Systems Forces, Robert Brovdi, also said on Wednesday that Ukrainian drones struck 13 Russian vessels in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov in the past 48 hours.

Wednesday’s attacks come as Ukraine widens its attacks, as Kyiv pushes along with its strategy of using long-range drones to disrupt Russia’s war effort.

Reporting from Ukraine, Al Jazeera’s Audrey Macalpine said the attacks are part of what Ukraine calls its “40-day influence operations” to “influence Russia back to the negotiating table” through “long-range drone strikes deep inside Russia as well as on the Black Sea”.

On Wednesday, outgoing Ukrainian Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskii also said that Ukraine had recaptured 700 sq km (270 sq miles) of its territory this year.

So far, Russia has not commented on the Ukrainian attacks on its territory.

At the same time, the Ukrainian governor of the Kharkiv region, Oleh Syniehubov, said on Wednesday that over the past 24 hours, 15 settlements had been subjected to Russian attacks, killing three people and injuring 11 others, including two children.

title: Ossoff challenges Hegseth on cost and duration of Iran war
excerpt:
Ossoff challenges Hegseth on cost and duration of Iran war

Senator Jon Ossoff challenges Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth over past claims that Iran’s military had been ‘destroyed’ and rendered ‘combat...
body_text:
Ossoff challenges Hegseth on cost and duration of Iran war

Senator Jon Ossoff challenges Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth over past claims that Iran’s military had been ‘destroyed’ and rendered ‘combat ineffective,’ questioning whether those claims were accurate as US forces continue operations against Iran.

title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail for serving ants in dessert
excerpt:
Fine of 20m won (£10,108) could also be imposed for serving insect not approved for consumption under local law

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starr...
body_text:
Fine of 20m won (£10,108) could also be imposed for serving insect not approved for consumption under local law

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

The restaurant’s owner and the company were indicted on charges of violating the country’s food sanitation act. The ministry of food and drug safety launched its investigation after spotting mentions of the ant dishes in blog posts and social media, rather than through a routine inspection.

The restaurant, which holds two Michelin stars and is located in Seoul’s upmarket district of Gangnam, is reported to have served a sorbet dessert sprinkled with ants for customers who wanted the flavour.

Under South Korean law, only 10 insect species, including grasshoppers, mealworms and silkworm pupae, are approved for use as food ingredients. Ants are not among them.

Businesses wishing to use an unapproved insect must first obtain temporary approval, a process the restaurant did not follow, the ministry said. Violating the law carries a maximum penalty of five years in prison or a 50m won fine.

Prosecutors allege the restaurant imported dried ants from the United States and Thailand from 2021 and served them on dishes for around four years, selling the ant-topped dessert roughly 12,200 times for total revenue of about 120m won (£60,647). Prosecutors estimated about 49,000 ants were used in total.

The restaurant’s defence disputed that figure in court, arguing that only about 60% of customers accepted the ant topping when offered, meaning the prosecution’s estimate, based on all customers, was too high.

They also noted that only a small number of dishes on the restaurant’s 15-course menu included ants, and that restaurants in countries including Denmark, the UK and Australia use ants as an ingredient.

It was reportedly explained to prosecutors that the restaurant’s chef had used ants to add acidity to dishes while working in the US and Europe, and had not realised doing so was illegal in South Korea.

title: Wetherspoon’s issues fourth profit warning in seven months
excerpt:
Pub chain blames worse-than-expected sales on rising costs for workers, food, energy and property taxes

JD Wetherspoon has issued its fourth profit warning in seven months, with the pub chain blaming...
body_text:
Pub chain blames worse-than-expected sales on rising costs for workers, food, energy and property taxes

JD Wetherspoon has issued its fourth profit warning in seven months, with the pub chain blaming worse-than-expected sales, despite the World Cup, as it struggled with rising costs for food, workers, energy and property taxes.

Shares in the pub chain tumbled 10% on Wednesday morning as its chair, Tim Martin, warned that profits would fall short of forecasts when it reports full-year earnings in October.

“Profits for the year are likely to be below market expectations, with marginally lower sales than anticipated in the final quarter, combined with higher costs in the areas of food, labour, repairs, energy and business rates,” he said in a statement.

It marks the fourth profit warning this year from the company, which operates 793 pubs across the UK and Ireland.

In a short trading update on Wednesday, JD Wetherspoon said like-for-like sales rose only 4% over the 12 weeks to 19 July. That is despite hopes for an uptick in sales linked to customers heading off to pubs to watch Fifa World Cup games.

While pubs can usually count on the World Cup to boost demand for food and drinks, the late kick-off times dictated by the event having North American hosts made the tournament more challenging for some owners compared with previous years.

Richard Hunter, the head of markets at the investment platform Interactive Investor, said: “While others in the sector have been hailing a boost to sales from the impact of the World Cup and generally warm weather, Spoons has apparently not joined the party.”

Those lower-than-expected sales have compounded continuing pressures, including the recent rises in the UK minimum wage and business rates, which came into effect at the start of April. The hospitality sector has also been grappling with a jump in food and heating bills, resulting from higher energy prices pushed up by the US-Israel war on Iran.

However, Wetherspoon’s is now expecting its net debt to hit £720m, in line with the end of the last financial year, down from previous forecasts of £740m to £760m.

Hunter said: “Spoons has been dealt some difficult hands over the years, which, for the most part, it has been resolute in turning into profit. However, this year has compounded some earlier difficulties … The different tax treatment of alcohol sales in supermarkets is a case in point, alongside wrongly applied business rates.

“Wetherspoon’s dogged determination to fight its corner has won the brand many friends, but from an investment perspective the jury remains out on prospects.”

title: The battle for Congress gets a Wall Street-style scoreboard powered by real-money bets
excerpt:
Kalshi co-founder Luana Lopes Lara opens up about her company's success on 'Maria Bartiromo's Wall Street.'

EXCLUSIVE: As the race for control of Congress heats up, prediction markets are emerging as...
body_text:
Kalshi co-founder Luana Lopes Lara opens up about her company's success on 'Maria Bartiromo's Wall Street.'

EXCLUSIVE: As the race for control of Congress heats up, prediction markets are emerging as an increasingly influential complement to traditional polling, offering real-time election forecasts that shift as news breaks and campaigns evolve.

The launch reflects a broader shift in how elections are being tracked. Unlike traditional polls that capture voter sentiment at a single moment, prediction markets update continuously as traders react to debates, fundraising, endorsements and breaking news, giving campaigns and political observers a real-time read on how races are evolving.

Kalshi launched its new Midterms Hub on Tuesday, bringing together live market odds, polling averages, fundraising data, historical election results and political news into a single dashboard tracking House, Senate and gubernatorial races. Kalshi said it plans to expand the platform beyond the 2026 midterm elections, with the goal of making it a permanent destination for tracking U.S. political races.

PREDICTION MARKETS FAVOR DEMOCRATS IN NEW YORK, NEW JERSEY, VIRGINIA ELECTION DAY RACES

The 2026 midterms will determine control of the House and Senate, shaping President Donald Trump's legislative agenda during the final two years of his term. (Al Drago/Getty Images)

"Prediction markets don't care about spin or partisanship," Tarek Mansour, Kalshi's co-founder and CEO, said ahead of the launch. "They cut through polarization and show you what the wisdom of the crowds actually believes, backed by real money, not rhetoric."

The company argues that it gives users a more dynamic view of the campaign than traditional polling alone.

"You're able to see things happen in real time," a Kalshi spokesperson told Fox News Digital, arguing prediction markets can capture shifts in momentum that periodic polling may miss.

Kalshi says the platform has evolved beyond a trading venue, with roughly 75% of visitors using it to monitor election forecasts rather than place trades. A spokesperson said that trend suggests many users view the markets as an informational resource, even if they never place a wager.

FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS: VOTERS SAY THEY'RE IN ECONOMIC PAIN, BUT WILL DEMOCRATS GAIN?

Kalshi is a federally regulated U.S.-based prediction market exchange that allows users to trade on the outcomes of political, economic and other real-world events. (Laura Brett/Getty Images)

As election markets have grown in popularity, they've also sparked debate over whether traders with nonpublic political information could gain an advantage. Kalshi says it is federally regulated in the United States, where insider trading and market manipulation are illegal, and that it monitors its markets for suspicious activity.

The hub allows users to track races by competitiveness, projected party flips and trading volume while viewing polling averages, Federal Election Commission fundraising reports and historical voting results. Users can also see how election odds have changed over time following debates, fundraising reports and other major campaign developments.

The midterm elections heat up as voters return to the polls in 2026. (Aaron Schwartz/Bloomberg via Getty Images; Melissa Sue Gerrits/Getty Images)

Beyond voters and political observers, Kalshi says campaigns can use the hub to gauge whether debates, endorsements, fundraising hauls or breaking news are shifting expectations in real time instead of waiting days for new polling.

Kalshi says candidates favored by its markets have won about 90% of races since 2024, even three months before Election Day.

Amanda Macias covers the intersection of business, economics and politics, with a focus on how policy decisions shape markets, businesses and American workers.

title: SENATOR TIM SHEEHY: Crazy conspiracy theories are undermining America’s defense
excerpt:
Fox News chief congressional correspondent Chad Pergram reports on the growing divide within the Democratic Party over continued U.S. aid to Israel on 'Fox News Live.'

The National Defense Authorizat...
body_text:
Fox News chief congressional correspondent Chad Pergram reports on the growing divide within the Democratic Party over continued U.S. aid to Israel on 'Fox News Live.'

The National Defense Authorization Act has long been one of the few pieces of legislation that rise above politics, typically culminating in a bipartisan show of support for our military and national defense. Each year, Congress comes together to fund our military, equip our warfighters and make clear to our adversaries that the United States remains committed to peace through strength.

This year, however, that tradition came to an end.

Instead of debating how best to strengthen America's military, Democratic Socialists and online conspiracy theorists are choosing political theater over national security. One provision of the FY27 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) in particular – Section 1217, which establishes the United States-Israel Defense Technology Cooperation Initiative – became the target of a misinformation campaign fueled by rising antisemitism and growing domestic hostility toward American power and influence on the world stage.

THE US-ISRAEL STRATEGIC ALLIANCE – PARTNERSHIP, NOT CHARITY

According to the groypers and far-left socialists behind this delusional campaign, Section 1217 somehow "merges" the U.S. military with Israel's, places American troops under foreign command and subordinates America's national security to another country's interests.

President Donald Trump holds a press conference with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu (Left) at his Mar-a-Lago club on December 29, 2025, in Palm Beach, Florida. (Joe Raedle/Getty Images)

While this sounds like a great plot for a novel, none of it is even remotely true.

Section 1217 builds upon decades of intelligence sharing, defense research and technology cooperation between the United States and Israel to accelerate the development of capabilities that strengthen America's military.

"Section 1217 merges our militaries." No, it does not. Section 1217 does not alter America's constitutional chain of command. It does not place a single American service member under Israeli authority.

This claim isn’t simply inaccurate – it’s absurd. And frankly, it doesn’t make one iota of strategic sense.

Israel's security challenges are largely regional, as it fights to defend itself from hostile neighbors who seek every day to wipe the Jewish people off the map. America's military responsibilities span every continent and ocean, maintaining a global force capable of deterring China in the Indo-Pacific, defending NATO allies in Europe, protecting freedom of navigation and responding to terrorist threats wherever they may be.

MAMDANI’S ‘DERANGED’ ISRAEL TAKE RESURFACES AS HE TARGETS NETANYAHU

Neither nation has any interest in "merging" military commands because it would make both countries less effective, not more.

Section 1217 isn't some secret, nefarious scheme buried in the NDAA as the anti-Israel lobby would have you believe. It's a continuation of a defense partnership that has benefited our warfighters for decades.

For years, Israel has fought radical Islamic terrorists and hostile regimes while pioneering advances in missile defense, counter-drone technology, cyber defense, battlefield intelligence and autonomous systems. America benefits from this knowledge, just as Israel benefits from American industrial capacity, research and military leadership.

Fact: Section 1217 builds upon decades of intelligence sharing, defense research and technology cooperation between the United States and Israel to accelerate the development of capabilities that strengthen America's military.

As a combat veteran, I’m familiar with a concept every warfighter understands: on the battlefield, you use every advantage available to protect your troops and accomplish the mission. Pride and bad-faith political symbolism don’t win wars. Superior technology, trusted allies and military dominance do.

That is exactly why Congress included Section 1217 in this year's NDAA.

Artificial intelligence, cyber warfare, autonomous systems and electronic warfare are no longer tomorrow's technology; they are deciding battles today. In Ukraine, inexpensive drones have destroyed tanks worth millions of dollars. In the Middle East, missile defense systems have intercepted massive barrages that would otherwise devastate civilian populations.

MORNING GLORY: AMERICA IS WINNING ITS BATTLE WITH IRAN. IT’S ONLY A MATTER OF TIME

Our adversaries aren't working alone. China invests heavily in artificial intelligence, autonomous weapons, quantum computing and next-generation military technology with one goal: overtaking the United States. Russia continues adapting through battlefield experience.

In this age of modern warfare, America cannot afford to handicap itself by refusing to work with trusted allies while our enemies freely share tactics, technology and capabilities.

The anti-Israel crowd tries to suggest Section 1217 somehow places Israel's interests above America's. That could not be more backwards. The provision puts our interests first with every technological breakthrough, every missile defense improvement and every advance in counter-drone capabilities that saves American lives. That's not charity – it’s security.

As Montanans, we understand the importance of preparedness. Our state has long played a vital role in America's national defense, from Malmstrom Air Force Base to the generations of veterans who have worn our nation's uniform. We know deterrence isn't built with speeches, but with capability, credibility and a military so advanced that our enemies decide not to test it.

Americans should absolutely debate foreign policy. We should debate defense spending, military priorities and America's role on the world stage. But these days, legitimate policy disagreements have been replaced with tired antisemitic tropes dressed up as national security concerns.

Neither nation has any interest in "merging" military commands because it would make both countries less effective, not more.

Let’s be clear: conspiracies about Jewish control of America's military have no place in a serious debate about our nation's defense.

Our enemies don't care whether the technology that defeats them was developed in Washington, Tel Aviv, or on the far side of the moon. They care that it works. We have a responsibility to ensure American warfighters have every possible advantage when they go into harm's way – not to try and score political points by spreading disgusting conspiracy theories.

Section 1217 does just that by making America stronger, our military more capable, and our adversaries less likely to challenge us. That's peace through strength.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM SEN. TIM SHEEHY

Republican Tim Sheehy represents Montana in the United States Senate. He is a father, husband, former Navy SEAL team leader, aerial firefighter and entrepreneur. Sheehy completed several deployments and hundreds of missions as a Navy SEAL officer and team leader, earning the Bronze Star with Valor for Heroism in Combat and the Purple Heart.

title: China is building a massive ship that should scare Americans about the future
excerpt: Former Deputy National Security Advisor Steve Yates and Kurt ‘CyberGuy’ Knutsson discuss a New York school’s plans to introduce the first humanoid robot in a classroom and the state of the U.S.-China ...
body_text:
Former Deputy National Security Advisor Steve Yates and Kurt ‘CyberGuy’ Knutsson discuss a New York school’s plans to introduce the first humanoid robot in a classroom and the state of the U.S.-China AI race on ‘Fox & Friends.’

An enormous vessel taking shape near Guangzhou, China, appears designed to refuel, rearm and resupply Chinese carrier groups far from home. At roughly 885 feet long, it could become the world’s largest dedicated naval replenishment ship.

Every Army officer learns the same lesson: weapons attract attention, but logistics decides who can stay in the fight.

The ship does not prove that war is imminent. It does, however, fit a larger pattern. China is building the physical, industrial and technological capacity to absorb economic punishment, replace battlefield losses and project power long after a crisis begins. Taiwan remains the most dangerous flashpoint, but Beijing is preparing for a contest that could continue well beyond the opening shots.

CHINA’S IRAN TIES COMPLICATE TRUMP-XI SUMMIT AS TENSIONS TEST US LEVERAGE

Energy is the first requirement of endurance. By late February, China held an estimated 1.2 billion barrels of onshore crude, enough to cover roughly 109 days of seaborne imports. Those stocks gave Beijing more room to absorb the oil-market shock created by the Iran war.

China is also expanding its navy. Its latest warship is an amphibious ship capable of launching drones and aircraft, begins sea trials to test power and stability. (Zhang Liang/Xinhua via AP)

Beijing is extending its military reach as well. The Pentagon says China is developing a global logistics and basing network that could include permanent bases, shared facilities, military sites beside commercial infrastructure and temporary access to foreign ports. A joint logistics center opened at Cambodia’s Ream Naval Base in April 2025, while China continues to explore access along the sea lanes through the Malacca Strait, the Strait of Hormuz, Africa and the Middle East.

THREE KEY REASONS WHY AMERICA CANNOT AFFORD TO LOSE TAIWAN TO COMMUNIST CHINA

Beijing is building the ships that carry power and the access network that keeps them supplied.

China’s advantage is not confined to ships already afloat. It has built an industrial system able to manufacture and repair them. A U.S. Trade Representative investigation found that China’s share of global shipbuilding tonnage rose from less than 5% in 1999 to more than 50% in 2023. Commercial orders sustain the workers, steel mills, dry docks and suppliers that also strengthen the Chinese navy.

TIM SHEEHY EXPOSES A 'SCARY' SHIPBUILDING COLLAPSE THAT LEAVES THE US VULNERABLE TO CHINA

The same pattern is visible in the air. The Royal United Services Institute estimates that production of the upgraded J-20 stealth fighter reached about 120 aircraft a year by late 2025. An open-source assessment places the total delivered by mid-2026 at roughly 500. The exact number matters less than the lesson: China can mass-produce a sophisticated combat aircraft once dismissed as experimental.

On July 6, a Chinese nuclear-powered submarine launched a long-range ballistic missile into the Pacific. Whatever Beijing’s characterization, the test demonstrated greater sea-based nuclear reach and complicated any American calculation about intervening in a regional war.

China is not simply buying a military. It is building the means to replace one after losses begin.

Modern weapons depend on minerals, chips and data. The International Energy Agency reports that China is the leading refiner for 19 of 20 strategic minerals it tracks, with an average market share of about 70%. Those materials feed batteries, power grids, jet engines, electronics and defense systems. In a long confrontation, control over the ingredients needed to build the next missile may matter as much as the missile already in the launcher.

Beijing is shrinking its semiconductor vulnerability too. Chinese companies held 33% of global production capacity for foundational logic chips in 2023, up from 19% in 2015. These are not the most advanced chips, but they are workhorses inside vehicles, industrial equipment, communications systems and many weapons.

The ship does not prove that war is imminent. It does, however, fit a larger pattern. China is building the physical, industrial and technological capacity to absorb economic punishment, replace battlefield losses and project power long after a crisis begins.

Advanced computing power fits inside this strategy too. At the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Xi Jinping promoted Chinese open-source models, training for developing countries and a new coalition called the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization. Beijing wants Chinese technology and standards embedded across the Global South.

Space ties these systems together. The U.S. Space Force says China ended 2025 with more than 1,353 satellites in orbit, including more than 510 capable of intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance. Those systems improve Beijing’s ability to locate American carriers, track deployed forces and support long-range targeting.

China’s Cross-Border Interbank Payment System now has 210 direct participants and 1,619 indirect participants. It does not replace the dollar; the renminbi accounted for only 1.99% of reported global currency reserves in the first quarter of 2026. Its purpose is more practical: keep essential trade moving if Western financial channels are restricted.

The campaign also reaches inside the United States. In June, the Justice Department and FBI disabled 13 websites allegedly backed by Chinese intelligence personnel. The sites posed as consulting businesses and targeted current and former U.S. officials and military personnel, especially security-clearance holders. Investigators said the operation used computer-generated profile photographs, professional networking sites and online payments to appear legitimate.

China is preparing to sustain power abroad while seeking leverage inside the societies most capable of resisting it.

Officers and soldiers on the Chinese People's Liberation Army PLA Navy's guided-missile destroyer Nanning wave as the ship enters the waters of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region in south China, July 2, 2026. (Ji Peng/Xinhua via Getty Images)

Beijing has serious weaknesses. China’s population fell by 3.39 million in 2025. Its property slump continues to depress confidence and domestic demand. The People’s Liberation Army has not fought a war since 1979 and lacks extensive modern combat experience, and the renminbi remains far from challenging the dollar.

Those weaknesses do not disprove the strategy. They explain why Beijing is building buffers. Xi understands where China is vulnerable and is methodically reducing those vulnerabilities before a major crisis.

Washington still treats shipbuilding as a Navy issue, semiconductors as a Commerce Department issue, minerals as a trade issue, espionage as an FBI issue and Taiwan as an Indo-Pacific Command issue. Beijing sees one contest.

America must do the same. We need larger stockpiles of munitions and strategic materials, sustained investment in shipbuilding and repair, secure allied supply chains, hardened space and communications networks, and deeper coordination with Japan, Australia, South Korea, the Philippines, Taiwan and Europe. We must also warn current and former officials about Chinese recruitment methods without treating Chinese Americans as suspects.

The giant vessel near Guangzhou may never dominate a television broadcast. It lacks the drama of a missile launch or a stealth fighter crossing the Taiwan Strait.

But it may tell us more about China’s plans than either.

Xi is building a country capable of fueling its forces, replacing losses, protecting industry and operating beyond the reach of a quick American knockout. China is preparing not only for a battle, but for the long contest that follows.

The urgent question is whether America is doing the same.

Nations that lose that race rarely get to run it twice. Beijing is spending every year of American hesitation to build its advantage. Washington still has time to close the gap, but not much of it remains.

CLICK HERE TO READ MORE FROM ROBERT MAGINNIS

Robert Maginnis is a retired U.S. Army infantry officer, senior fellow for National Security at the Family Research Council, and the author of 14 books. His latest, "The New AI Cold War: Liberty vs. Tyranny in the Age of Machine Empires," examines the global contest for AI supremacy and its implications for freedom.

title: STEVE FORBES, STEPHEN MOORE: America must free its airwaves before China pulls ahead
excerpt:
FOX Business reporter Madison Alworth discusses China's rapid progress in artificial intelligence, revealing a new AI model outperforming U.S. counterparts at a lower cost.

Washington rarely discover...
body_text:
FOX Business reporter Madison Alworth discusses China's rapid progress in artificial intelligence, revealing a new AI model outperforming U.S. counterparts at a lower cost.

Washington rarely discovers an economic policy that promotes investment, strengthens national security, improves services for consumers and raises billions of dollars for the Treasury without imposing a new tax. Federal Communications Commission Chairman Brendan Carr’s newly announced "super band" spectrum auction manages to do all four.

Spectrum may be invisible, but it is among the most valuable resources in the modern economy. It carries phone calls, financial transactions, emergency communications, industrial data, streaming video and the growing volume of information moving between connected devices.

The FCC has moved to auction 160 megahertz of the Upper C-band — the valuable mid-band airwaves that provide the combination of speed, capacity and geographic reach required by modern wireless networks.

AMAZON LEO SATELLITE INTERNET TAKES ON STARLINK

Congress directed the commission to make at least 100 megahertz available. The FCC went further, adding another 60 megahertz and combining it with spectrum cleared in 2020. The result will be a contiguous block of more than 400 megahertz, creating one of the most commercially useful bands of wireless spectrum in the world.

This will be the first major federal spectrum auction in five years. The commission also moved from a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking to a final order faster than it has in any comparable proceeding during the 5G era.

STEVE FORBES: REGULATORS RISK DESTROYING LOCAL TV BY BLOCKING KEY MEDIA MERGER

Keeping usable spectrum idle — or trapped in inefficient government allocations — has real costs. Networks aren’t built. Jobs aren’t created. New services don’t reach consumers. American companies lose ground to foreign competitors.

Auctions offer the quintessentially American solution: Free up and use markets.

A properly designed auction gets spectrum to companies that value it most and are prepared to put it to productive use. It establishes clear, enforceable rights. It encourages private investment. And it generates revenue for the federal government without raising anyone’s income, payroll or capital-gains taxes.

COULD THE NEXT CHINESE THREAT WALK INTO YOUR KITCHEN ON TWO BATTERY-POWERED LEGS?

The economic potential is enormous. The FCC estimates that putting this spectrum to work could add as much as $264 billion to gross domestic product, support 1.5 million jobs and create nearly $390 billion in benefits for consumers.

Those estimates may sound ambitious, but history shows the value of these airwaves. The first C-band auction in 2000 raised roughly $81 billion for the federal government. Few government actions can promote economic growth, encourage lower consumer prices and reduce federal borrowing at the same time.

This one can because it relies on competition rather than command-and-control regulation.

The auction is also a national security imperative.

The country that leads in mid-band spectrum will have an enormous advantage in 5G, the emerging 6G economy and the artificial-intelligence, robotics, transportation and defense applications that depend on advanced connectivity.

Wireless leadership isn’t merely about downloading movies more quickly. It determines which country’s companies develop the next generation of network equipment, whose technical standards are adopted internationally and where the investment and high-paying jobs associated with these technologies are created.

China understands this. Beijing has long treated spectrum as a strategic national asset. It has deployed extensive mid-band capacity, built networks at enormous scale and used subsidized telecommunications equipment to increase its influence around the world.

America’s regulatory apparatus, by contrast, has often moved at the speed of an old rotary telephone.

The Upper C-band order is crucially important, but it can’t be a one-time event. America badly needs a long-term spectrum strategy, not the haphazard, hit-and-miss approach we’ve had up to now.

Wireless companies and technology innovators make investment decisions years in advance. They need a predictable pipeline of licensed spectrum, not occasional auctions that are interrupted by political disputes and bureaucratic delays. Washington should establish a multiyear calendar that identifies which bands will be studied, cleared and auctioned.

Congress should also make the FCC’s auction authority permanent. Allowing that authority to expire, and then reviving it through temporary extensions, creates uncertainty and delays investment. No successful business would manage one of its most valuable assets that way. Neither should the U.S. government.

Washington must also take a harder look at the enormous amount of spectrum controlled by its own agencies, including the Defense Department.

National security operations must be protected. But "national security" shouldn’t become a mantra that prevents officials from examining whether every allocation is being used efficiently. There are effective ways to preserve government capabilities while freeing additional spectrum for commercial use.

The big problem is that federal agencies have little incentive to surrender unused and/or unneeded airwaves. Congress should change that by allowing agencies to share in the savings or auction proceeds generated when they consolidate operations, upgrade equipment or relinquish underused spectrum.

The broader principle is straightforward. Government shouldn’t attempt to build wireless networks, dictate business models or select favored technologies. Its responsibility is to free scarce spectrum, establish clear rights, enforce the rules and then get out of the way.

Private capital and American ingenuity will do the rest.

Chairman Carr’s Upper C-band auction follows that formula.

But technological leadership isn’t secured with a single auction. Washington must adopt a sensible, long-term strategy so that America has the spectrum resources required to dominate the next technological era.

Stephen Moore is a co-founder of Unleash Prosperity and a senior fellow at the America First Policy Institute.

Steve Forbes is chairman and editor-in-chief of Forbes Media.

title: Americans far more likely to view politics as greatest threat to safety, study finds
excerpt:
New survey reveals one in 10 Americans identify politics as biggest risk they face in life – 10 times the global average

Americans are 10 times more likely than people elsewhere in the world to regar...
body_text:
New survey reveals one in 10 Americans identify politics as biggest risk they face in life – 10 times the global average

Americans are 10 times more likely than people elsewhere in the world to regard politics as the greatest threat to their personal safety, according to a global survey that suggests the country’s deep partisan divisions are poisoning everyday life.

The latest Lloyd’s Register Foundation World Risk Poll found that one in 10 Americans identified politics as the biggest risk they face in their daily lives when asked an open-ended question. The figure is up from 6% in both 2021 and 2023 and well above the global average of 1% across 140 countries.

Although Americans remain far more likely to cite road accidents and crime as the worst dangers they face, the survey found the US to be the only country in which more people named politics than personal health conditions as the biggest risk to their safety.

“The US figure of 10% is unprecedented,” said Benedict Vigers, a senior global news writer at Gallup, which partnered on the research. “No other country ranked higher. It’s 10 times the global average.

“It speaks to how charged the political environment is and has been for many years in the United States that people are now saying that the political situation is not just an issue nationally but is a seeable daily risk to their safety as they go about their normal lives.”

The US has ranked among the countries most likely to cite politics as a personal safety risk in all three waves of the World Risk Poll but 2025 marks the first time it has reached the top of the global ranking.

It was effectively tied with Hungary, which recorded 9%; polling was conducted months before the country’s recent election. Serbia, Latvia and Lithuania followed at 6% while Slovakia, Russia, Myanmar, Spain and Israel each recorded 5%.

But the US in particular has witnessed a surge in political violence over the past decade, including three assassination attempts against Donald Trump, the 2025 killing of Charlie Kirk, the targeting of other national and state officials and a deadly insurrection at the US Capitol in January 6, 2021. There has been a rise in localised harassment, death threats against election administrators and the use of doxing and “swatting” to intimidate and silence political opposition.

The concerns cut across demographic and ideological lines. There were no statistically significant differences by age, gender, education or political affiliation, with Republicans, Democrats and independents all similarly likely to identify politics as their greatest concern.

Respondents often blamed the opposing political camp for creating an atmosphere of insecurity, while others pointed more broadly to the country’s increasingly fractured politics.

“I would have to say the state of polarisation of the American people,” said one 54-year-old Republican man.

A 42-year-old independent male respondent said: “I would say political extremism. I don’t have a lot of personal risk, but I do feel the biggest risk is people that are fanatical about their position and that they could be violent.”

A 69-year-old Democratic woman added: “The political situation has a lot to do with the fact whether if I am safe or not. I am concerned where we’re going or might go.”

Vigers said: “Almost all of the respondents who mention that politics is the top risk to safety mention or refer to polarisation whether directly or indirectly. It’s either Republicans saying the Democrats or it’s Democratic voters saying Trump and the Republicans are a big risk to my safety.

“But we have several other people who call it out directly and say, the political environment makes me fearful for my safety or the heightened sense of polarisation makes me fear for my safety. It is quite a uniquely American phenomenon.”

The findings echo other Gallup polling that has highlighted Americans’ growing disillusionment with politics. In 2025 a third of US adults identified politics and government as the country’s most important problem, well above the global median and exceeded only by respondents in the Philippines and Taiwan.

In 2024, 5% of Americans named the US as its own greatest enemy, the highest figure on record.

At the same time, confidence in government appears to have deteriorated. In 2025, 58% of Americans said the government does not care about them at all, the highest figure recorded and 11 percentage points higher than in 2021. Among OECD member states, only Colombia and Greece recorded higher levels of public alienation from government.

excerpt:
Al Jazeera reports from anti-US protest outside ASEAN summit

Al Jazeera’s Barnaby Lo reported from an anti-US protest taking place outside the ASEAN summit in the Philippines, which US Secretary of S...

title: Iran, Gulf hit as Hormuz, Bab al-Mandeb close: What’s the latest?
excerpt:
US defence chief Hegseth says war on Iran has cost $37.5bn, seeks $87.6bn more

The conflict in the Middle East has widened as two oil tankers headed to Asia, carrying Saudi crude, turned around in th...
body_text:
US defence chief Hegseth says war on Iran has cost $37.5bn, seeks $87.6bn more

The conflict in the Middle East has widened as two oil tankers headed to Asia, carrying Saudi crude, turned around in the Red Sea on Tuesday after Yemen’s Houthis announced a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia.

The closure of the Bab al-Mandeb strait that connects the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean adds pressure on global trade routes, with ongoing disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz due to the ongoing US-Israel war on Iran.

Escalations continue, as the United States announced that it concluded its 11th round of attacks on Iran. Tehran retaliated, hitting US military targets in the Gulf.

Here is a recap of what happened on Tuesday night and Wednesday.

The US Central Command (CENTCOM) said that at 00:15 GMT on Wednesday, it concluded its latest round of strikes against Iran for the 11th consecutive night.

CENTCOM said it targeted Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities and military logistics infrastructure “to further degrade Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz”.

The CENTCOM statement claimed that over the past three months, Iran had attacked more than 30 commercial vessels traversing the Strait.

The deputy governor of Iran’s southwestern Khuzestan province told the Fars news agency in Iran that US missile attacks targeted areas near the cities of Behbahan and Omidiyeh in the province on Wednesday.

The agency also reported that early on Wednesday, US forces attacked a military site on the outskirts of Tabriz in northwestern Iran. Al Jazeera’s Tohid Assidi reported from Iranian capital Tehran that explosions were heard in Tabriz.

Assidi additionally reported that air defences were activated in Tehran and that two blasts were heard in Bushehr.

Iran’s IRIB state broadcaster said that the deputy governor of Sistan and Baluchestan province had confirmed attacks on the southeastern cities of Chabahar and Konarak.

IRIB additionally reported, citing the deputy governor of Hamadan province, that US forces launched air attacks on Kabudarahang County in west-central Hamadan province in the latest round of attacks.

The broadcaster also reported that air attacks hit the Chavar and Abdanan regions in Iran’s western Ilam province and that US attacks hit the western Kurdistan province.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said on Wednesday that “the operation to punish the aggressor continues”, IRIB reported.

Kuwait: Iran’s military says it launched drones at US military facilities at Camp Doha in western Kuwait on Wednesday, Iran’s semi-official Tasnim news agency reported.

Jordan: Iran’s military said in a statement on Wednesday that it targeted Jordan’s Muwaffaq Salti Airbase in Azraq with drones, according to the Mehr News Agency. The statement said: “The army targeted the ‘housing and welfare buildings’ and ‘equipment warehouses’ of the terrorist US army at the Azraq base in Jordan with drones in the morning.”

Bahrain: The statement, cited by Mehr news agency, added that Iran targeted Bahrain’s Isa Airbase with drones.

“[Drones] also targeted the ‘large equipment warehouses and sheds’ and the ‘heavy aircraft maintenance and repair sheds’ of the child-killing and criminal US army at the Sheikh Isa Airbase in Bahrain,” the statement said.

The US Department of Defense said in a statement on Wednesday that a third US soldier is believed to have been killed in action during an Iranian strike on Muwaffaq Salti Airbase in Jordan on July 17.

The department said the 28-year-old had previously been reported missing, but that the soldier’s status has now been updated to “Duty Status -Whereabouts Unknown, believed deceased”.

Two other soldiers were killed in the attack on the Jordan base. Another soldier was also killed in Iraq last week while attempting to detonate explosives on an Iranian drone. The latest death brings the total number of US soldiers killed to 18 since the war on Iran began on February 28.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that Washington is open-minded on the war on Iran and willing to engage, negotiate and settle differences.

“If they’re serious, we’re serious. If they’re not, then we will do what’s necessary to protect our interests and also the interests of our allies,” Rubio said in a meeting with Southeast Asian counterparts in Manila.

Meanwhile in Washington, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told senators during a hearing that the war had cost the country’s military $37.5bn so far, urging them to approve an additional $67bn to replenish depleted missile supplies.

title: Middle East crisis live: US renews strikes on Iran as Trump threatens to attack nuclear site ‘very heavily’
excerpt:
US military completes 11th night of strikes as attacks overshadow diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage interim ceasefire deal

Houthis threaten attacks on shipping tankers if they use Saudi ports...
body_text:
US military completes 11th night of strikes as attacks overshadow diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage interim ceasefire deal

Houthis threaten attacks on shipping tankers if they use Saudi ports on Red Sea

What is Pickaxe Mountain, Iran’s underground nuclear facility?

Welcome to our live coverage of the latest developments in the US-Iran war, and its regional and global ramifications.

The US military completed its 11th night of strikes on Iran, including on aircraft hangars and drone storage sites, as the renewed conflict continues to undermine diplomatic efforts to salvage an interim ceasefire deal.

In what could open up a potential new front in the war, Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis, who control the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia. With the strait of Hormuz blocked, Saudi Arabia has been relying on a pipeline to the Red Sea to get millions of barrels of oil out to market.

The latest US barrage came as Donald Trump warned strikes would intensify.

The US leader also declared the US would “soon” target “very heavily” the area near Pickaxe Mountain – a fortified underground site near one of Iran’s primary nuclear enrichment facilities, which Iran’s military leaders said would be considered a major escalation.

Before the latest strikes, Iranian interior minister Eskandar Momeni visited Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict. But it was unclear what new arrangement might be reached to end the war, which is now centered on a battle for control over the strait of Hormuz.

The US war in Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, US defence secretary Pete Hegseth said, an increase of nearly $8bn since the last public estimate.
