The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”.

House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.

title: US immigration agents arrested a record number of people in June, data shows
excerpt:
Guardian analysis shows officers arrested 43,138 people as quiet and steady operations ramp up across the country

The Trump administration arrested a record number of immigrants last month, according...
body_text:
Guardian analysis shows officers arrested 43,138 people as quiet and steady operations ramp up across the country

The Trump administration arrested a record number of immigrants last month, according to newly released government data that reveals how US immigration agents are quietly but steadily ramping up operations across the country.

A Guardian analysis of the latest data published by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) found that federal immigration agents arrested 43,138 people in June – the highest number since Donald Trump took office in January last year and promised a campaign of mass immigrant deportations.

The number of people in detention has also ticked up by 5,450 since early April, when ICE last provided a count of its detained population. There were a total of 65,765 people in ICE detention as of 11 July – slightly below a record 70,770 people who were in detention in January.

As has been the case since last summer, the number of people in ICE detention without any criminal history remains higher than the number who have pending charges or convictions.

In total, the Trump administration has deported more than 590,600 since January 2025, the latest figures show.

The new data matches what immigration advocates and attorneys have said they are seeing in communities across the US. Bolstered by a $70bn funding surge, the US Department of Homeland Security – which oversees ICE and border patrol – has been steadily ramping up arrests and detentions. The White House has reportedly pushed immigration officials to aim for 2,000 arrests every day.

Communities are once again on high alert for ICE activity after immigration officers killed two men – Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, 52, and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero, 26 – in a week, sparking protests against the US immigration enforcement system in cities around the country.

Last year, the Trump administration launched a number of militarized immigration operations in places including Chicago, Los Angeles and Minneapolis, but it has lately taken a quieter approach to enforcement. After immigration officers shot and killed two US citizens in Minneapolis – Renee Good and Alex Pretti – Trump replaced his DHS secretary, Kristi Noem, with Markwayne Mullin, who said he wanted to move away from high-profile operations.

The roving caravans of immigration officers raiding public parks and parking lots have become less common. Arrests are now happening routinely, at workplaces, outside homes or during traffic stops, according to advocates and legal aid groups. Many immigrants are being detained when they report to ICE offices for mandated check-in appointments. Others are being stopped by police, sheriffs and members of other state and local law enforcement agencies deputized by the federal government to conduct immigration sweeps.

Immigration advocates said because the arrests are generally happening routinely, and quietly, volunteers who had taken to keeping watch for ICE vehicles and officers in certain cities and blowing whistles when they spotted immigration agents, are lately having trouble seeing how and where ICE is operating.

“It’s just happening so quickly. So people can’t get there to observe, record and report what they saw,” said Gracie Willis, an attorney with the National Immigration Project, in a recent interview. “And often all that’s left is an empty car sitting there.”

Since 2019, Congress has required ICE to publish data on arrests, detentions and deportations every two weeks. But until Monday, the agency had failed to publish any updates since early April.

The delay had angered lawmakers across the political spectrum and multiple media organizations, academics and non-profit organizations had requested that the agency provide updates on how many immigrants it was arresting and detaining. In a statement to the Guardian, the DHS blamed the months-long delay in reporting data on a series of government shutdowns.

The number of arrests in June were the highest recorded since May 2019. At the time, a record number people – including families and unaccompanied children – were arriving at the US southern border. Most of the apprehensions then were made by the US Customs and Border Protection, whereas since Trump took office in 2025, most of the arrests have been conducted internally by ICE, according to the government’s data.

The best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods:

The Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.

If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.

Our guide at theguardian.com/tips lists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.

title: Slack messages released by Rand Paul appear to show authors of key COVID-19 origin paper held private doubts
excerpt: New documents reveal the FBI blocked Customs and Border Protection from questioning former President of EcoHealth Alliance Peter Daszak, a researcher linked to the Wuhan Institute of Virology and gain...
body_text:
New documents reveal the FBI blocked Customs and Border Protection from questioning former President of EcoHealth Alliance Peter Daszak, a researcher linked to the Wuhan Institute of Virology and gain-of-function research. Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky., and GOP lawmakers criticized the Biden administration's actions regarding the COVID-19 origins investigation.

Newly released messages among the authors of a widely cited paper regarding the origins of COVID-19 privately raised doubts that the virus originated from bats, as their paper stated.

Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky., on Tuesday released the internal Slack messages exchanged among the authors of "The Proximal Origin of SARS-CoV-2" — Kristian Andersen, Robert Garry, Eddie Holmes and Andrew Rambaut.

"The idea that it came from a lab is implausible, and, yet, privately they're saying the opposite. … This level of dishonesty, I think, needs to be exposed," Paul told Fox News. "These are people who get large, multimillion-dollar grants from our country, and we shouldn't be giving money to people who lied to us."

The paper was published in Nature Medicine in March 2020 and was widely cited by public health officials and the media as the scientific authority for ruling out a laboratory origin of SARS-CoV-2. However, the released messages appear to show that its authors were less certain than their public conclusions suggested.

LEGACY MEDIA IGNORES MASSIVE ANTHONY FAUCI DOCUMENT RELEASE ALLEGING HE MISLED CONGRESS ON COVID ORIGINS

Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky., on Tuesday released the internal Slack messages exchanged among the authors of "The Proximal Origin of SARS-CoV-2." (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc, File)

Some raised concerns that the virus originated in a lab.

Andersen assigned a 30% probability to a lab origin, while Holmes gave an estimate of 20%, though he later revised that to 10%. Either way, both figures contrast with the paper's public assertion that the virus had not been manipulated.

"The part I’m really struggling with is that, at the end of the day, we really don’t have any hard evidence one way or the other — and especially given some of the recent evidence, we also can’t rule out that somebody actually put it in there," Andersen wrote on June 11, 2020, according to the documents.

"That’s obviously not to say that somebody did, but we can’t rule it out. Our paper was pretty strong in saying ‘there’s no way,’ but I have less confidence in that statement at this stage."

RAND PAUL BRINGS CIA WHISTLEBLOWER TO SENATE HEARING ALLEGING 'DEEP STATE' COVID-19 CONSPIRACY

The P4 laboratory, center, on the campus of the Wuhan Institute of Virology in China. The lab has been at the center of alleged theories that COVID-19 was engineered in a lab. (Hector Retamal/AFP via Getty Images, File)

The documents also reveal internal debates over the furin cleavage site, the genetic feature at the heart of the lab-leak question. At one point, Andersen told colleagues that "some smoking(ish) guns" had emerged that made him "not comfortable" and that he had "less confidence ... at this stage," a news release from Paul's office states.

In addition, the authors coordinated with the U.S. intelligence community and National Institutes of Health (NIH) leadership as they were publicly defending their conclusions. Another topic of discussion among the authors was Ralph Baric, a prominent coronavirus researcher.

While they acknowledged his expertise, their internal messages revealed a highly contentious relationship with him, characterized by professional rivalry and suspicions that he actively tried to undermine them.

GABBARD SPOTLIGHTS FAUCI, COVID-ORIGIN QUESTIONS IN FINAL ACT AS INTELLIGENCE CHIEF AMID SUCCESSION FIGHT

Officials with U.S. Customs and Border Protection were allegedly prepared to question and search Peter Daszak, inset, upon his return to the U.S. from the World Health Organization’s investigation into the origins of COVID-19, before the FBI intervened and asked agents to stand down, according to documents released Monday by Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky. (Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs; Getty Images, File)

They also suspected Baric was the anonymous leaker, whom they dubbed "Deep Throat," who shared confidential peer-review information with a journalist. The leak suggested that the authors initially suspected a lab origin before changing their minds.

A World Health Organization (WHO) scientific advisory group released a report last year on the origins of COVID-19, concluding that the deadly pandemic was caused by "zoonotic spillover," either directly from bats or through an intermediate host.

On Monday, Paul released documents that revealed U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents were preparing to question British zoologist Peter Daszak upon his return to the United States from the World Health Organization’s investigation into the origins of COVID-19, before the FBI intervened and asked agents to stand down.

Dr. Anthony Fauci, the face of the U.S. response to the pandemic, is slated to testify before the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee on the origins of the virus this month after a subpoena was issued by Paul, the committee chairperson.

Fauci has been accused by some of manipulating intelligence on the origins of the pandemic and lying to Congress to cover up alleged wrongdoing.

Louis Casiano is a reporter for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to louis.casiano@fox.com.

title: WATCH: Smithsonian director pressed on woke exhibits: ‘Is Mickey Mouse racist?’
excerpt: National Museum of American History director Anthea Harting was pressed by Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, whether Mickey Mouse is a racist symbol and about 'objectivity' being labeled White supremacist i...
body_text:
National Museum of American History director Anthea Harting was pressed by Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, whether Mickey Mouse is a racist symbol and about 'objectivity' being labeled White supremacist in a toolkit promoted by the institution.

The director of the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History faced pointed questions from House Republicans Tuesday over exhibits and materials they characterized as woke, including a display linking Mickey Mouse to racism and a museum toolkit discussing "objectivity" as a feature of White supremacy.

Several Republican members questioned Director Anthea Hartig about sexually explicit imagery in exhibits, including "bondage," in the words of Chairman Tim Burchett, R-Tenn. Texas Rep.

Brandon Gill’s exchanges focused on one of America’s most iconic childhood characters.

"Is Mickey Mouse racist?" Gill asked Hartig about halfway through his allotted five minutes.

WATCH: SMITHSONIAN HISTORY MUSEUM DIRECTOR STUMBLES OVER BASIC QUESTIONS ON SEX: ‘I’M NOT SURE’

"Mickey Mouse is not racist," Hartig replied.

Gill responded by pointing to a Smithsonian Institution exhibit that described Steamboat Willie as representing "vestiges of long-standing traditions of blackface minstrelsy’."

"Would you describe that as racist?" Gill pressed.

OBAMA PRESIDENTIAL CENTER FOLLOWS WIDELY MOCKED 'STOLEN LAND' ACKNOWLEDGMENT WITH NATIVE AMERICAN DANCE SHOW

National American History Museum Director Anthea Hartig (left) was confronted by Rep. Brandon Gill (right), R-Texas, over the Smithsonian's alleged extreme political ideology in its displays. (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images; Al Drago/Getty Images)

Hartig began explaining the "historical trajectory" of Mickey Mouse before brief crosstalk. She then finished, saying, "[He’s] rooted in minstrelsy."

When Gill asked whether the purported racist overtones were "problematic," Hartig responded that it was not her role to make that determination.

Gill also cited the MASS Action Toolkit, a mid-2010s anthology for museums that references the 2014 Ferguson, Missouri, police shooting and subsequent Black Lives Matter protests, and asked Hartig whether it was important to be "polite."

MED SCHOOL DEANS COME UP EMPTY IN TENSE HEARING WHEN ASKED POINT BLANK IF MEN CAN HAVE BABIES: 'RIDICULOUS'

Gill then cited passages from the toolkit discussing "objectivity" and politeness in relation to White supremacy culture. He asked Hartig whether the guide's characterization implied she was a White supremacist.

"Can you help me square why your organization says that being polite is part of being a White supremacist?" Gill added. Hartig replied she could not immediately recall that passage.

Gill also asked Hartig whether it was important to be objective.

After Hartig answered largely in the affirmative, Gill pointed to the toolkit’s discussion of objectivity.

A 2017 copy of the toolkit available online stated that "Jones and Okun also list objectivity as a characteristic of a White supremacy culture. This encompasses repressing emotions in decision-making or group processes, thinking in a linear fashion, impatience with those who do not exhibit linear thinking or with thinking that does not appear logical."

National Museum of American History Director Anthea Hartig is sworn in during an Oversight and Government Reform subcommittee on Delivering on Government Efficiency hearing on Capitol Hill July 21, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Andrew Harnik/Getty Images)

"Museum staff have never been objective, but because dominant norms typically coincide with the worldview of the vast majority of museum staff, it has appeared to be objective to the dominant culture. Those with worldviews outside of the dominant culture are hyperaware of the fallacy in this pretense of museum objectivity," the toolkit went on.

When asked whether the characterization concerned her, Hartig replied, "It’s not our toolkit."

Gill said the Smithsonian had promoted the toolkit for years.

Born and raised in Allentown, Pennsylvania, worked in both the New York and Washington bureaus for Fox News since 2013.

title: New York Democrat hopes Mamdani won't be 'arresting anyone' after recent Netanyahu comments
excerpt:
New York Rep. Gregory Meeks told CNN's ‘The Situation Room’ Monday that New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani should not attempt to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu.

Rep. Gregory Meeks, D-N.Y., told CNN Monday...
body_text:
New York Rep. Gregory Meeks told CNN's ‘The Situation Room’ Monday that New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani should not attempt to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu.

Rep. Gregory Meeks, D-N.Y., told CNN Monday he hopes New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani does not make any effort to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu despite his past comments.

Meeks was asked by "The Situation Room" host Wolf Blitzer about reports that Mamdani is looking into the possibility of arresting Netanyahu if he visits New York City for the United Nations General Assembly in September.

Stepping away from Mamdani, Meeks emphasized that "all leaders" are welcome to speak at the United Nations and encouraged the New York City mayor to not try to disrupt the U.N.'s purpose.

CNN LEGAL ANALYST CALLS OUT 'UTTERLY RIDICULOUS' CLAIM BY MAMDANI ABOUT NETANYAHU ARREST

Rep. Gregory Meeks, D-N.Y., disagreed with the idea of arresting Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu if he came to New York City. (Tom Williams/CQ-Roll Call, Inc via Getty Images)

"Look, the United Nations is for all leaders to come to the United States, for them to have a chance to talk to the collective body, whether you agree with them or not," Meeks said.

"I have had, on other sides with other folks, to say that they should have had the right to come to the U.N., and they did, to speak to the United Nations in that regard. And people have the right to speak out loudly against what they may say and represent. That’s fine. But I think the mayor has also walked that back some.

"I saw a quote where he said that he does not intend to violate federal law, existing law and looking for other laws, as President [Donald] Trump sometimes does, you know, tries to go around the law, and that he understands that the U.N.’s functions need to continue to move forward, and let the UN takes care of its business. And, so, I think that the mayor should make sure that he’s not arresting anyone at this point."

NETANYAHU FIRES BACK AT NYC MAYOR MAMDANI'S VOW TO ENFORCE 'BOGUS ARREST WARRANT'

Fox News Digital reached out to Mamdani's office for comment.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said his administration is exploring whether the city has legal authority to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu if he visits for the U.N. General Assembly. (Getty Images)

In remarks first reported by The New York Times, Mamdani called Netanyahu a "war criminal" who "belongs in The Hague" and said his administration is in an "active conversation" with the city's Law Department over what legal authority New York City possesses should the Israeli prime minister travel to the Big Apple.

"Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do," Mamdani said, adding that his administration would not write new laws for that purpose.

MAMDANI’S ‘DERANGED’ ISRAEL TAKE RESURFACES AS HE TARGETS NETANYAHU

The International Criminal Court (ICC) originally issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and former Defense Minister Yoav Gallant in November 2024, over alleged war crimes and crimes against humanity tied to Israel's military campaign in Gaza.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani has said he will do "whatever the law allows [him]" to regarding Benjamin Netanyahu. (Angelina Katsanis/AP)

Israel has rejected the allegations and disputes the court's jurisdiction. In addition, the U.S. is not a party to the Rome Statute, the treaty that established the ICC, meaning it does not have the same arrest-and-surrender obligation that applies to ICC member states.

Fox News' Jasmine Baehr contributed to this report.

Lindsay Kornick is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to lindsay.kornick@fox.com and on Twitter: @lmkornick.

title: Trump administration attacks US left, alleging Cuba ties: Key takeaways
excerpt: The administration of United States President Donald Trump has released a 100-page report tying left-wing advocacy groups in the US to Cuba – often relying on circumstantial and ideological links, rat...
body_text:
The administration of United States President Donald Trump has released a 100-page report tying left-wing advocacy groups in the US to Cuba – often relying on circumstantial and ideological links, rather than direct connections.

The document, released by the State Department on Monday, portrays the beleaguered Caribbean nation as an ideological threat to the US and its interests, frequently using language and concepts harkening back to the Cold War. It argues that Cuba, which is led by a communist government, is trying to subvert the US from within.

“While Soviet communism was frequently driven by more practical great-power concerns – the preservation of the Soviet empire and the defence of its material interests around the globe – its Cuban successor was built around a near-singular obsession with the ‘liberation’ of the Global South from its Western colonial oppressors,” it reads.

“In the minds of the Cubans, there was only one path to this goal: the destruction of the Yankee imperialist regime.”

Left-wing activists have rejected the accusations that they have links to Cuba, saying the claims made in the report aim to increase pressure on Havana while intensifying the crackdown on dissent inside the US.

Here are five key takeaways from the report:

In a section titled “Front Groups and Fellow Travellers”, the State Department attempts to draw a link between left-wing groups in the US and across the world and Havana.

The Democratic Socialists of America (DSA), which has helped elect several progressive politicians to Congress, is the most prominent group on the list.

The Trump administration admits that the DSA is not a Cuban asset, but it says the group’s rise shows how “native and organic ideological commitments of the American far left now mirror the interests” of the Cuban government.

The DSA is an activist organisation whose stated mission is to support workers gaining power through democratic means.

The report hyped the DSA’s influence, calling it “a mass membership organisation operating openly in American electoral politics … a growing bench of elected officials, and real influence inside the Democratic Party coalition”.

“The DSA has built a national infrastructure dedicated to moving extreme-left politics from the margins into the mainstream – and with it, a deep and abiding devotion to the Cuban regime,” it said.

The Trump administration also singled out the antiwar group Code Pink, underscoring that one of the group’s donors is a US citizen who lives in China.

Code Pink was recently part of a humanitarian delegation to Cuba.

The group’s cofounder, Medea Benjamin, told Al Jazeera that the organisation “will not be intimidated” by the Trump administration.

She said the approach of labelling activists as communists is a “flashback” to the 1950s at the height of the Cold War.

“We are in an era of McCarthyism, where they’re not only labelling groups like us communists and trying to position us as enemies of the state, but also combining that with the efforts to crack down on protests,” Benjamin told Al Jazeera.

“So it’s a scary time for people, but it’s also a time that we can’t allow them to stop us. We’ve got to just stand up to these bullies.”

The document mentioned the New York mayor by name as it zeroed in on the case of Assata Shakur, a Black liberation activist who was convicted of killing a police officer in 1977 in New Jersey before fleeing to Cuba, where she was given asylum status. Shakur always insisted that she was innocent.

The State Department, led by Marco Rubio, who is of Cuban descent, said that Cuba pursued a “Shakur strategy” to gain a foothold in left-wing circles and African-American advocacy groups, including the Black Lives Matter movement.

“Figures like Shakur – and with them, Cuban influence – remain extraordinarily influential over left-wing politics in the United States today,” the report said.

It went on to cite US politicians who praised Shakur when she died in exile last year.

“Democratic congresswoman Ayanna Pressley tweeted ‘Rest in Power,’ and shared a video of herself leading a crowd in call-and-response recitation of one of Shakur’s slogans,” the State Department said.

“On the campaign trail in 2025, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani refused to denounce a Democratic Socialists of America tribute to Shakur.”

When Mamdani was asked about the DSA statement on Shakur, he said he was focused on affordability and lowering the cost of living for New Yorkers.

Antoni Kapcia, emeritus professor of Latin American history at the University of Nottingham, was surprised to see that an article he wrote in February for the left-wing magazine Jacobin was cited in Rubio’s report.

“The principal antagonist of world history was no longer ‘capital,’ but Western civilisation itself,” the report read.

“As the socialist magazine Jacobin approvingly recounted earlier this year, it was Cuba that ‘was seen by many in the Third World as the ideal site for radically rethinking anti-colonialism, anti-imperialism, and decolonisation.’ What Cuba was proposing, Jacobin writes, ‘was essentially a decolonisation of Marxist thought.'”

Kapcia stressed that his argument was that anti-colonialist movements in the Global South in the mid-1960s were taking a Marxist line in opposition to imperialism.

“The principal imperialist they often saw was the United States. But that again is not subverting the United States,” Kapcia told Al Jazeera in a phone interview.

He added that Cuba’s support for left-wing groups across the world has not been a consistent strategy; rather, it was a policy of the 1960s, when the island was under severe isolation imposed by the US.

“It’s complete nonsense,” Kapcia said of the claim that Cuba is sponsoring left-wing groups in the US.

The report is the latest move by the Trump administration against Cuba.

After US forces abducted Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro in January, Washington ordered the new authorities in Caracas to halt fuel shipments to Cuba, plunging the country into a humanitarian crisis.

In May, US authorities issued a criminal indictment against 95-year-old former Cuban politician Raul Castro over the downing of two planes in 1996 that killed four people, which Havana says crossed into the island’s airspace without authorisation.

“It looks as though it’s just setting up an invasion or some sort of intervention. Most of the rest of the world is totally opposed to that,” Kapcia said.

He added, however, that due to Cuba’s receding strategic importance and national resources and the rising antiwar sentiment in the US, Trump is unlikely to launch a military campaign against the island.

“It seems to be rhetoric, but that could be wishful thinking. We could be hoping it’s rhetoric,”  Kapcia told Al Jazeera. “I rule nothing out when it comes to Trump.”

Code Pink’s Benjamin said the report and other statements demonising Cuba aim to justify the “demonic policy of starvation”, referring to the US siege on the island.

“I think they’re getting ready for an invasion,” she told Al Jazeera. “I think they’re laying the groundwork, and this is part of the plan – because starving the people is not achieving the ends that they want, which is obviously regime change.”

Republican Congressman Carlos Gimenez, who represents a South Florida district home to a large Cuban-American community, cited the report to call for regime change in Cuba.

Both Benjamin and Kapcia ridiculed the idea that Cuba, an impoverished nation experiencing shortages and blackouts under US siege, is a threat to the US.

The report described Cuba as the “connective tissue of a broader anti-American coalition, a staging ground where the ambitions of Moscow, Beijing, and Tehran converge with the radical movements operating inside our own borders”.

“To mistake Cuba’s poverty for harmlessness is to misunderstand the threat entirely,” it said.

“Its material weakness was never the measure of its danger. Its power was always ideological, subversive, and parasitic – and it has proven remarkably durable, outlasting the Soviet empire that helped build it and grafting itself onto every new enemy of the United States that has arisen since.”

Benjamin told Al Jazeera the US administration “is painting a picture that doesn’t exist” about Cuba.

“To call Cuba a threat is just so absurd,” she said.

title: Moldova chooses new prime minister as it pushes for EU membership
excerpt:
Moldova’s parliament has elected financier Vasile Tofan as prime minister and approved his new government.

Tofan was approved by 53 votes to 21, with 21 abstentions, on Tuesday, ending three weeks of...
body_text:
Moldova’s parliament has elected financier Vasile Tofan as prime minister and approved his new government.

Tofan was approved by 53 votes to 21, with 21 abstentions, on Tuesday, ending three weeks of uncertainty and reaffirming the previous government’s pro-European Union mandate.

He replaces Alexandru Munteanu, who suddenly stepped down without a clear reason only eight months into his term, forcing his cabinet to resign.

“Our programme is called ‘The European Economy, an Effective State’. This is the commitment we will work towards, and it is by this commitment that we ask to be judged,” Tofan said in parliament.

Although Tofan is not affiliated with any party, he has received the backing of President Maia Sandu and her Party of Action and Solidarity. Sandu, who nominated Tofan, said Moldova needed a united government to reach EU membership.

The country is one of Europe’s poorest; its economy grew 2.4 percent last year. The new prime minister promises to revive the economy, cut budget deficits, sell off state-owned companies and press ahead with EU membership.

His roadmap seeks to advance Moldova’s EU membership talks this year, reach an accession deal by the end of 2028 and be ready to join the bloc by 2030.

Moldova won EU candidate status alongside Ukraine in 2022, and the two opened membership talks last month.

For decades, power in Moldova has shifted between pro-EU parties and those wanting closer ties with Russia, which the EU accuses of meddling in the country’s elections. Moldova has a Romanian-speaking majority, but a large minority of its citizens speak Russian.

Full membership in the EU is still years away and depends on prerequisite reforms that Moldova agreed to undertake when it accepted candidacy.

title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
excerpt:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential e...
body_text:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

UKHSA said the person was evacuated as a “highly precautionary measure”, adding: “The individual is not currently displaying any symptoms and remains well, but as a precautionary measure they have been evacuated on a flight chartered solely for the evacuation.

“They are now being assessed and monitored in isolation by infectious disease specialists at a London hospital.”

The agency said the incubation period of Ebola ranges from two to 21 days and the individual will be closely monitored and is required to isolate throughout this period.

There are no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK and the risk to the general public remains low.

Richard Pebody, the director of epidemic and emerging infections at the UKHSA, said: “This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution and we’re pleased they remain well.”

Last month, a patient suspected of having the Ebola virus at a Glasgow hospital tested negative.

Precautionary measures were taken after the patient was admitted to the Queen Elizabeth University hospital.

An outbreak of the disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda in May was declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO).

The virus is transmitted to people from wild animals and then spreads in the human population through direct contact with the blood, secretions, organs or other bodily fluids of infected people and with contaminated surfaces and materials such as bedding or clothing.

According to WHO, symptoms can include fever, fatigue, malaise, muscle, pain, headache and sore throat. These are followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rash, and symptoms of impaired kidney and liver functions.

title: Rabid bat found near zoo restaurant sparks urgent public health warning: 'Extremely serious'
excerpt:
San Antonio Zoo’s Brooke Chavez joins ‘Fox News Live’ to discuss the zoo’s Valentine’s Day program allowing visitors to name a cockroach after their ex and watch it get eaten.

Visitors to the San Die...
body_text:
San Antonio Zoo’s Brooke Chavez joins ‘Fox News Live’ to discuss the zoo’s Valentine’s Day program allowing visitors to name a cockroach after their ex and watch it get eaten.

Visitors to the San Diego Zoo Safari Park are being urged to come forward if they had contact with a wild bat after the animal tested positive for rabies.

A deceased wild bat was recovered outside the Oasis on the Rocks restaurant July 11. It later tested positive for rabies, according to the San Diego Zoo Safari Park.

"Safari Park visitors who had no physical contact with the bat are not at risk for rabies," the park told Fox News Digital.

'UNSUPERVISED' CHILD AT HERSHEYPARK ZOO INJURED BY WOLF AFTER CRAWLING UNDER SAFETY BARRIER

County health officials are asking anyone who may have had direct contact with the bat to come forward for evaluation.

The bat was not part of the Safari Park's animal collection, and officials said no human contact has been reported.

Visitors to the San Diego Zoo Safari Park (not pictured) are being urged to come forward if they had contact with a wild bat after the animal tested positive for rabies. (Smith Collection/Gado/Getty Images)

The bat was tested by the County Public Health Laboratory, which confirmed it was positive for rabies, San Diego County noted.

This marks the 19th bat to test positive for rabies in San Diego County this year. Twenty-one bats tested positive during all of 2025, according to county officials.

"Rabies is an extremely serious viral disease, one of the deadliest, with [a] nearly 100% fatality [rate] if not caught early enough," Dr. Ankita Kadakia, the county's deputy public health officer, said in a statement.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

"We are investigating to ensure anyone who came into contact with the bat receives care quickly. Once symptoms start, there is no treatment for rabies."

Wildlife experts say the incident serves as a reminder that people should never handle bats, even though they play an important role in the environment.

People should never handle bats, even though they play an important role in the environment, said experts.  (iStock)

"There are over 20 different species of bats living in San Diego County, and these animals are a huge benefit to our region's gardens and farms, eating up to 1,000 mosquito-sized bugs per hour," Dr. Alexis Wohl, wildlife veterinary manager at the San Diego Humane Society, told Fox News Digital.

"Although it's rare for bats to carry rabies, it is important to exercise caution and not handle them with bare hands," Wohl said.

Dr. Brian Collins, a veterinarian with the Cornell Riney Canine Health Center, based in New York, said bats are one of the primary carriers of rabies in the United States and account for most human rabies cases.

The bat was not part of the Safari Park's animal collection, and officials said no human contact has been reported. (Amy Sparwasser/iStock)

"If a bat is encountered in public, it is very important not to touch it," Collins told Fox News Digital.

Instead, he recommended keeping people and pets away from the animal and notifying property management, animal control or local law enforcement.

"If you know or suspect that you have had physical contact with the bat, you should wash the affected area with soap and water for 10 to 15 minutes," Collins said.

Kelly McGreal is a production assistant with the lifestyle team at Fox News Digital.

title: Maher mocks liberals for still panicking about Trump's antics, personality after 10 years
excerpt:
Bill Maher called out liberals who he says are still panicking about President Trump's hijinks after 10 years in the political limelight, arguing much of what he does doesn't really affect him.

Talk ...
body_text:
Bill Maher called out liberals who he says are still panicking about President Trump's hijinks after 10 years in the political limelight, arguing much of what he does doesn't really affect him.

Talk show host Bill Maher mocked liberals on Monday, saying many of them are constantly panicking about President Donald Trump’s surface-level antics and behavior that do not really impact their lives.

During a discussion on his "Club Random" podcast with British comedian Jack Whitehall, Maher jokingly pointed to a framed list of all of Trump’s insults toward him as a "great artifact of history." Maher had brought this list of nearly 60 insults to the White House and had Trump sign it when they met for dinner in March 2025.

Maher offered backhanded praise for Trump for having allegedly tried to prevent him from receiving the Mark Twain Prize at the Kennedy Center last month, suggesting he was flattered to be taken so seriously. Maher doubled down on Monday.

"I didn't think it was ever going to happen," Maher said of receiving the award. "Because first he tried to stop it, but then it was going through, and then they took his name off the building. He didn't like it. When they took his name off the building, he covered the building in drapery so you couldn't see anything on the building. I'm not kidding."

BILL MAHER RECOUNTS LARRY DAVID FALLING OUT, CELEBRITY BACKLASH AFTER TRUMP WHITE HOUSE DINNER

Comedian Bill Maher mocked liberal Americans who are still outraged by President Donald Trump's various surface-level hijinks after 10 years in the spotlight. (Real Time with Bill Maher/YouTube)

"I don't care," Maher said of Trump’s antics. "If, at this point, ten years into the Trump situation, you are still obsessing about his personality, my view is that's on you."

"Should we always be criticizing, as I do, what he actually does while he's in office? Absolutely," Maher continued. "But to keep just focusing on these personality things that we've talked about for ten years — they're not going to change. That's who he is. I don't care when he calls me names now. It's the way he does it. It's the way he talks. It's who he is."

"When he does it, I just got to go, ‘OK, yeah. This is what you do.’ And as long as it's something that doesn't really affect me or other people for real," he said.

TUNING OUT: WHY MANY AMERICANS ARE SICK OF THE NEWS – ESPECIALLY TRUMP NEWS

Mark Twain Prize recipient Bill Maher attends the 27th Mark Twain Prize For American Humor Gala at The Kennedy Center on June 28, 2026, in Washington, D.C. (Paul Morigi/Getty Images)

Maher went on to list a litany of other surface-level Trump controversies that ultimately do not impact the everyday lives of Americans like himself, arguing they are absurd topics for people to get worked up over.

"I don't care about your reflecting pool," he said. "I don't care about your ballroom. I don't care what you do on the lawn. You can have UFC fights. You can have sheep grazing there like one president did. You can do anything. Pickleball. I don't give a s--- what you do on the lawn."

"And if you're a person who does, you're just way too into Donald Trump as the person who controls how you think in life," Maher continued. "Everything is about him. I'm not going there with you."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

Bill Maher said that while he did not anticipate President Trump covering up how the Kennedy Center removed his name, in retrospect the move was not a surprising one from him at all. (Screenshot/HBO Max; Nathan Howard/Getty Images)

"I'm winning," Maher said of his mindset. "And I'm somebody he attacks personally, and I care less than you do. Your life is all about Donald Trump. Mine isn't."

Alexander Hall is an associate editor for Fox News Digital. Story tips can be sent to Alexander.hall@fox.com.

title: Texas Rep. Jasmine Crockett tried to call Argentina racist during the World Cup and embarrassed herself
excerpt:
Dan Dakich gives his reaction to Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN on Monday while he was live on air.

Leave it to Representative Jasmine Crockett to be so obsessed with race-baiting that she acciden...
body_text:
Dan Dakich gives his reaction to Ryan Clark being laid off by ESPN on Monday while he was live on air.

Leave it to Representative Jasmine Crockett to be so obsessed with race-baiting that she accidentally spent an afternoon carrying water for the Spanish Empire.

In her rush to lecture the world about racism, Crockett somehow landed on the side of the country that spent centuries colonizing much of Latin America.

During a House hearing on oversight of the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History, the Texas Democrat tried to turn World Cup fandom into a debate about racism.

SPAIN BEATS ARGENTINA TO WIN 2026 FIFA WORLD CUP BEHIND FERRAN TORRES' EXTRA-TIME GOAL

Rep. Jasmine Crockett speaks on the Global Black Economic Forum Stage during the 2026 ESSENCE Festival of CultureÂ® presented by Coca-ColaÂ® at Ernest N. Morial Convention Center on July 04, 2026 in New Orleans, Louisiana. (Arturo Holmes/Getty Images for ESSENCE)

Instead, Yale historian Dr. David Blight offered the explanation most sports fans would.

Rep. Jasmine Crockett: "The World Cup that captured the imaginations of the entire world when they saw all of these people of color that was playing for all these teams. And ultimately, if you recall, the last two teams, it was Argentina and Spain. And it seemed like the vast majority of the world was rooting for Argentina or actually against Argentina, right? And why was that? Just give me an explanation if you could guess..."

Dr. David Blight: "Spain had the best team, maybe that's why they were rooting for them."

Rep. Jasmine Crockett: "There's also a racist history that exists as it relates to Argentina."

Crockett saw racism. Dr. Blight saw soccer.

Players scuffle after a foul by Leandro Paredes of Argentina on Rodri of Spain during the FIFA World Cup 2026 final match between Spain and Argentina at New York New Jersey Stadium in East Rutherford, N.J., on July 19, 2026. (Maddie Meyer/FIFA)

Crockett was so determined to paint Argentina as uniquely racist that she ended up siding with Spain, the historical seat of Western European colonialism. It is the kind of historically illiterate self-own that would deeply upset the Democratic base, if they actually knew their history.

On one side was a world-renowned Ivy League historian stating the obvious: fans usually root for the better team.

FIFA LAUNCHES INVESTIGATION INTO BRAWL AFTER SPAIN DEFEATED ARGENTINA IN WORLD CUP FINAL

On the other was a sitting member of Congress suggesting the world's reaction to the World Cup final was driven, at least in part, by Argentina's racial history.

By Crockett's logic, even watching the World Cup is an act of political expression.

Rep. Jasmine Crockett, D-Texas, a U.S. Senate candidate, speaks during a campaign event at New Faith Church in Houston, Texas, on Monday, March 2, 2026. (Mark Felix/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Every penalty kick has to double as a political statement, even if it means pretending Spain was somehow the good guy.

It's the kind of performative political theater she's built her brand on. Watching her in Congress is like scrolling through the Facebook feed of the auntie who fires off unhinged political rants. The only difference is she has a congressional badge, a taxpayer-funded microphone, and far more influence than your aunt.

When a Yale historian has to remind a member of Congress that people usually root for the better team, you've probably overthought the World Cup.

It takes real ideological commitment to walk into a discussion about soccer and walk out carrying water for the Spanish Empire without noticing the irony.

Send us your thoughts: alejandro.avila@outkick.com / Follow along on X: @alejandroaveela

Alejandro Avila is a longtime writer at OutKick, living in Southern California.

title: LeAnn Rimes shares naked hot tub photo with Eddie Cibrian on road trip from California to Nashville
excerpt:
The "Blue" singer said she and Cibrian love each other and can be stupid together.

LeAnn Rimes is spending her road trip from California back home to Nashville naked in a hot tub with her husband, Ed...
body_text:
The "Blue" singer said she and Cibrian love each other and can be stupid together.

LeAnn Rimes is spending her road trip from California back home to Nashville naked in a hot tub with her husband, Eddie Cibrian.

On Tuesday, Rimes took to Instagram to document their journey. In the carousel of images, the singer and actress shared a photo at the end of her post, taken from behind. Cibrian had his arm around her while she sat naked in the hot tub, taking in the scenic view.

"The last picture is what its ALL about. @leannrimes ❤️," one user commented, referencing the hot tub photo. Another added, "The last photo with you and @eddiecibrian just warms my heart!"

LeAnn Rimes and Eddie Cibrian cuddled in the hot tub in one image the singer shared to her Instagram page. (LeAnn Rimes/Instagram)

"turns out, a cross country road trip with a horse trailer, a mystery tractor-ish thing, far too many bags and enough snacks to cross several countries was always going to produce some good footage," Rimes captioned her post. "there were questionable roadside sightings, car karaoke, a LOT of gas station potty breaks, incredible food and a few moments that probably should’ve stayed off camera… but didn’t."

LEANN RIMES POSES IN RED BIKINI IN CABO, SHARES EMOTIONAL MESSAGE ABOUT 'HEART-WRENCHING' FAMILY ISSUES

The "Blue" singer included a link to her substack, which had a more in-depth write-up of their road trip.

Rimes and Cibrian enjoyed the hot tub on their road trip from California to Nashville. (Monica Schipper/Getty Images)

Rimes and Cibrian have been married since April 2011, but their relationship began under intense public scrutiny.

LIKE WHAT YOU’RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

The pair met in 2008 while filming the Lifetime movie "Northern Lights," when Rimes was married to former backup dancer Dean Sheremet and Cibrian was married to "Real Housewives of Beverly Hills" alum Brandi Glanville.

Eddie Cibrian and LeAnn Rimes have been married since 2011. (Jacob Andrzejczak/Getty Images for MGM Resorts International)

News of their affair became public in 2009, leading to the end of both marriages and making the couple one of Hollywood's most talked-about scandals.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

LeAnn Rimes and Eddie Cibrian at the 60th Academy Of Country Music Awards in 2025. (Michael Buckner/Penske Media via Getty Images)

Despite years of tabloid attention and backlash over how their romance began, Rimes and Cibrian remained together, becoming engaged in December 2010 before tying the knot in a private California ceremony attended by about 40 guests, including Cibrian's two sons, Mason and Jake, from his marriage to Glanville.

This spring, the couple celebrated their 15th wedding anniversary.

Janelle Ash is an entertainment writer for Fox News Digital covering Hollywood, celebrity culture and breaking entertainment news through exclusive reporting, interviews and in-depth storytelling.

title: France first European country to ban social media access for under-15s
excerpt:
Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron

France’s parliament has approved a bill banning social media access for children under 15, making i...
body_text:
Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron

France’s parliament has approved a bill banning social media access for children under 15, making it the first European country to bar children from apps such as TikTok.

The president, Emmanuel Macron, has championed the ban as a major reform of his final term in office and pledged to enforce it by September.

“France is leading the way in Europe when it comes to protecting our children and teenagers,” Macron said in a video posted on social media, hailing “a major step forward”.

He thanked members of parliament for backing the legislation on Tuesday.

“The Constitutional Council must now rule on it, and then it will be time to take action to make this measure a reality and protect our children online,” he added on X.

After approval by the Senate earlier on Tuesday, members of the National Assembly passed the bill by 279 votes to 81.

A growing number of countries are taking steps to restrict social media access amid multiplying warnings over its harmful effects on children.

The ban was to be introduced in two stages, with under-15s blocked from creating new accounts from 1 September. The ban would apply to existing accounts from January 2027, according to the legislation.

The digital minister, Anne Le Hénanff, said before the vote that the timeline was realistic, “because age-verification tools already exist” and others are still in the works, and the onus was on the platforms to impose the rule.

“For four months, all of us in France will have to prove our age,” she told journalists. “If someone is under 15, the account will be closed.”

The minister also gave assurances that users’ personal data would be protected.

Enforcing the measure by September would ensure that one provision of the bill – a ban on mobile phones in high schools – takes effect at the beginning of the French school year.

France’s public health watchdog last year said platforms such as TikTok, Snapchat and Instagram were harmful to adolescents, particularly girls, though this was not the sole reason for their declining mental health.

Lawmakers agreed on the need for regulation but the two chambers had disagreed on the approach.

French senators had opted for a two-tier system distinguishing between blacklisted platforms flagged as harmful to a child’s development, and those that could still be accessed with parental consent.

But the lower house’s blanket ban prevailed, despite criticism from some on the left over the age verification process, the speed of its implementation and risks of circumvention as well as privacy concerns.

Exceptions to the ban are provided for sites such as online encyclopedias and educational platforms, and the text does not provide for any penalties for children or parents.

“The major platforms are, in my view, largely ready,” assembly member Laure Miller told AFP.

The blacklisting system would have taken more time, including fresh consultations with the European Commission on the criteria, and carried a “small risk” of non-compliance with European law, said centrist senator Catherine Morin-Desailly.

The European Commission has been considering a social media ban after a push by member states, including France, Greece and Spain, for limiting access.

Last week, Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the commission, said children should have “phased and gradual access” to social media.

The European Commission has also unveiled an age verification app that EU member states, including France, have begun testing in recent months.

After Macron’s government suspended major pensions reform last year, a social media ban could be his last major domestic change before he steps down next year.

France will be closely watched by other European states – it is one of 20 countries worldwide to have proposed or introduced such measures.

Last December, Australia became the first country in the world to require TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat and other top sites to remove accounts held by under-16s or face heavy fines.

title: Newsom admin faces questions over $6.2M diaper contract after touting 'competitive' process
excerpt:
Newsom unveiled the Baby2Baby partnership in May, promising 400 free diapers to families of newborns at participating California hospitals. (Credit: California Governor Gavin Newsom via YouTube)

Cali...
body_text:
Newsom unveiled the Baby2Baby partnership in May, promising 400 free diapers to families of newborns at participating California hospitals. (Credit: California Governor Gavin Newsom via YouTube)

California Gov. Gavin Newsom’s administration on Friday released the contract behind the state’s $6.2 million diaper program after questions about the deal’s noncompetitive designation and a reported delay in making the agreement public.

The agreement with nonprofit Baby2Baby will provide families of newborns with 400 free diapers at participating California hospitals.

Newsom said in May that the deal went through a "competitive bidding process." However, the state’s contract database lists it as "NON-COMPETITIVELY BID," according to CBS California Investigates.

CONSERVATIVES FLIP SCRIPT ON NEWSOM AFTER HE DEMANDED 25TH AMENDMENT FOR TRUMP: 'PROPPED UP A VEGETABLE'

Baby2Baby co-CEOs Kelly Sawyer Patricof and Norah Weinstein pose with California Gov. Gavin Newsom at the Golden State Start event in San Francisco on May 8, 2026. The agreement will provide 400 free diapers to families of newborns at participating California hospitals. (Miikka Skaffari/Getty Images for Baby2Baby)

Instead of seeking formal bids, the state asked interested groups to submit information through a "Request for Information." The document said it was "not a solicitation" and that prices submitted would not count as bids, CBS California Investigates reported.

Newsom’s office pushed back on the report, saying 15 groups responded and were reviewed based on cost and their ability to make and distribute diapers.

"The governor was referring to the process being competitive in nature, which remains accurate," Newsom's office told the outlet.

The records reportedly did not include scoring sheets, rankings or clear evaluation standards.

ACTING AG TODD BLANCHE SAYS NEWSOM'S DOJ CLAIMS ARE NOT 'GROUNDED IN FACT'

California Gov. Gavin Newsom and Baby2Baby leaders fill diaper boxes at the Golden State Start event in San Francisco on May 8, 2026. The state reportedly released the contract 66 days after the outlet filed a public records request. (Miikka Skaffari/Getty Images for Baby2Baby)

Several applicants said they only learned they were no longer being considered when Newsom announced the Baby2Baby partnership, CBS California Investigates reported.

California’s 2025 budget exempted the program from normal bidding rules and other state review requirements, according to the outlet.

CBS California Investigates found more than two dozen similar exemptions in the state budget, covering over $1 billion in spending. About two-thirds never expire.

The state released the contract on July 17, 66 days after the outlet filed a public records request.

The governor’s office said the extra time was needed to "grant our legal team adequate time to process and work on these requests," according to CBS Investigates.

NEWSOM BLASTED BY SHERIFF IN HIS OWN BACKYARD OVER CALIFORNIA CRIME AS VICTIMS CRY FOUL

Boxes of Baby2Baby diapers are displayed at the Golden State Start program launch in San Francisco on May 8, 2026. CBS reported that the agreement is at least the third time Baby2Baby has received California funding without a competitive bid. (Miikka Skaffari/Getty Images for Baby2Baby)

CBS California Investigates reported that the agreement marks at least the third time Baby2Baby has received California funding without a competitive bid.

The nonprofit also has a connection to the Newsom administration. Baby2Baby co-CEO Norah Weinstein serves on the board of the California Partners Project, which was founded by first partner Jennifer Siebel Newsom, according to the outlet.

Newsom's office and Baby2Baby could not immediately be reached by Fox News Digital for comment.

Sophia Compton is a Writer at Fox News Digital. Sophia was previously a business reporter covering finance, energy and tourism and has experience as a TV news producer. She graduated with a journalism degree in 2021 from the University of Hawaii at Manoa. Story tips can be sent to sophia.compton@fox.com.

title: ‘There’s no way they can survive’: Trump defends new 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods – US politics live
excerpt:
US president claims ‘Canada has been very tough on us over the years’ as Mark Carney says he has agreed to ‘intensify negotiations’ with US

In the Oval Office today, Donald Trump defended the new 50%...
body_text:
US president claims ‘Canada has been very tough on us over the years’ as Mark Carney says he has agreed to ‘intensify negotiations’ with US

In the Oval Office today, Donald Trump defended the new 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods.

“Canada has been very, very tough on us over the years, for many years, and no other president’s done anything about it,” Trump said. “In all fairness to them, they need us to survive. Without us, there’s no way they can survive.”

This week, the White House said that the new levies were because Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products. The tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible discussions between the two countries.

Mark Carney, Canada’s prime minister, said that he spoke with Trump earlier on Tuesday. “We agreed to intensify negotiations in the coming weeks, and the team and myself look forward to doing that,” Carney told reporters.

The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement.

Trump told members of the press that the latest round of tariffs was not in response to the Canadian wildfire smoke that spread across North America last week, and the president railed against.

On Friday, Trump blasted Canada for its response to the persistent wildfires and the smoke that affected about 109 million people across the midwest, mid-Atlantic and north-east. “The cost is incalculable!” Trump raged on social media. “This is Willful Negligence, and becoming a yearly occurrence, costing the United States Billions of Dollars, which cost of this pollution must of necessity be added to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying.”

However, the president told reporters on Tuesday that his administration is looking at those tariffs “separately”.

Pete Hegseth’s testimony before Senate lawmakers was quickly interrupted, on a number of occasions, by anti-war protesters in the hearing room.

Collins paused the proceedings as a result, and called on the Capitol police to remove those interrupting the hearing.

Patty Murray, the top Democratic senator on the appropriations committee, has slammed the latest White House funding request for the Iran war.

“Our country is no safer or better off than before the war. In fact, we are considerably less safe,” Murray said. “Our stockpiles have been depleted, and it will take years of production to fully replenish them … and we are now much further away from a long-term solution to halt Iran’s nuclear program than we were before Trump ripped up the deal that President Obama negotiated, which secured actual, measurable commitments and progress without ever starting a costly war.”

Murray noted that in the five months since the war began, the strait of Hormuz– which was open prior to the conflict – is still closed.

“This war is now spiraling out of control again. The president is threatening escalation and war crimes, and suggesting this could be another forever war,” she said. “[Trump] has told us over 40 times now that a deal is ‘near’ and the war will ‘be over soon’. But now he is asking for 70 billion dollars more, and for us to just trust him.”

The hearing where defense secretary Pete Hegseth and chairman of the joint chiefs of staff Dan Caine will testify before Senate appropriations lawmakers and explain their request for $80bn in supplemental funding for the Iran has begun.

The committee chair – Republican senator Susan Collins – kicked off by noting that “irrespective of our differing views about the military operations, the fact remains that there are must-pay bills and urgent requirements facing the department that affect the ability of our troops to be well trained, and more broadly, of the US military to deter and defend against potential aggressors worldwide”.

Collins – a noted moderate who is facing a reelection battle in her state of Maine – also pointed to “deeply troubling” reports that dozens of senior officers removed from promotion lists, many of whom appear to women and people of color. “This is likely to discourage well-qualified women and individuals of color from joining our armed forces, thus depriving us of their talent, dedication, and patriotism,” Collins said in her opening remarks. “The United States military is one of the greatest meritocracies in the free world. We need to make sure that it stays that way.”

Later today we can also expect a vote on Jay Clayton’s nomination to lead the national intelligence agencies.

A reminder that Clayton – who serves as the US attorney for the southern district of New York – testified before lawmakers on the Senate intelligence committee last week. During the hearing Trump’s pick to be the country’s next intelligence chief refused to say that Joe Biden won the 2020 election. He also evaded questions about his previous election integrity claims and whether or not a White House official asked him to subpoena a group of New York Times journalists as part of an alleged national security investigation.

On Sunday, the intelligence committee’s top Democrat, Mark Warner, said he would vote against Clayton as the nation’s top spy, to officially replace acting director Bill Pulte – who has received backlash from lawmakers on both sides of the aisle for his lack of experience in the intelligence community.

“I’ve known Jay for a long time. I like Jay. I worked well with him when he was at the SEC [Securities and Exchange Commission]. But I was bitterly disappointed by his performance in front of my committee,” Warner told CBS News.

The Trump administration has paused more than $1bn in Medicaid funding to California and Minnesota, citing “suspected fraud and noncompliance” – withholding of funding that follows a $243m pause in Medicaid payments to Minnesota earlier this year.

“If those states want that money, they need to provide documentation that these payments are legitimate,” said Robert F Kennedy Jr, secretary of the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), said at a press conference on Tuesday.

Officials relied on AI to identify potential fraud, Kennedy said. “We used AI, advanced analytics, and other cutting-edge tools to identify suspicious activity and potential fraud.”

The pause includes more than $867m for California and about $200m for Minnesota.

In California, most of the suspected fraud was in in-home services, he said. Kennedy has several times singled out in-home care provision, which can be a lifeline for disabled individuals – rhetoric that has worried families and angered lawmakers.

Vice-President JD Vance threatened in March to withhold funding to states if they did not cooperate with a new fraud taskforce, headed by Vance and established by a White House executive order.

A US appeals court has overturned a ruling that ordered the Donald Trump administration to release pro-Palestinian activist Mohsen Mahdawi from immigration detention.

The decision came from the 2nd US circuit court of appeals which found that the lower court did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning the 35-year old Palestinian green card holder.

“We conclude that the district court should not have reached this question,” US Circuit Judge Debra Ann Livingston wrote.

In response to the ruling, Michael Tan, deputy director of the ACLU’s Immigrants’ Rights Project, who argued on behalf of Mr. Mahdawi last year, said: “The government is claiming the unprecedented power to detain people for months or even years on end to censor their speech, with no ability to challenge their detention in court. This is as absurd as it is dangerous.”

We will continue to fight for our client and for his right to prompt judicial review,” Tan added.

Tuesday’s ruling follows a a decision earlier this year by an immigration judge who blocked the Trump administration from deporting Mahdawi, who secretary of state Marco Rubio has claimed “would have potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States.”

Mahdawi, a Columbia University student, was detained by federal agents in Vermont last year during his citizenship interview.

A software error in New Jersey’s motor vehicle system mistakenly registered about 6,600 people who identified themselves as non-US citizens to vote between June 2023 and June 2024, New Jersey governor Mikie Sherrill said on Tuesday.

Sherrill said that individuals “answered ‘no’ when asked on a keypad whether they were a US citizen when applying for drivers’ licenses and identification cards, but through no fault of their own, the system registered them anyway.”

She added that the error occured under the previous administration and that she has since ordered the removal of any residents from voter rolls that were mistakenly added during that time period.

“Our preliminary analysis shows fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted. They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state,” Sherrill said.

She went on to say that she is “appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time.”

“This failure didn’t occur under my watch, but accountability starts now. I am taking action to prevent anything like it from occurring in the future and make sure those responsible are held accountable,” she continued.

No part of the United States’ anti-HIV programme has escaped unscathed from Donald Trump’s funding cuts, despite pledges from the administration to protect care that saved lives around the world, a new report has found.

Funding cuts to the President’s Emergency Plan for Aids Relief (Pepfar) have caused “severe and devastating” disruption globally, with thousands of staff laid off and many clinics closed, according to research being presented next week at the 26th International Aids Conference in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. It warns that changes in US policy risk reversing progress in the fight against HIV/Aids.

Organisations still in receipt of US funding are also changing what they do to comply with the Trump administration’s policies targeting diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives, reproductive rights and the provision of gender-affirming care, researchers found.

Elise Lankiewicz, from the Aids research foundation Amfar and co-lead author of its Pepfar Pulse report, said: “Disruption was incredibly widespread. No part of the programme really escaped unscathed – and that includes areas of the programme that this administration intended to protect.”

In the Oval Office today, Donald Trump defended the new 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods.

“Canada has been very, very tough on us over the years, for many years, and no other president’s done anything about it,” Trump said. “In all fairness to them, they need us to survive. Without us, there’s no way they can survive.”

This week, the White House said that the new levies were because Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products. The tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible discussions between the two countries.

Mark Carney, Canada’s prime minister, said that he spoke with Trump earlier on Tuesday. “We agreed to intensify negotiations in the coming weeks, and the team and myself look forward to doing that,” Carney told reporters.

The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement.

Trump told members of the press that the latest round of tariffs was not in response to the Canadian wildfire smoke that spread across North America last week, and the president railed against.

On Friday, Trump blasted Canada for its response to the persistent wildfires and the smoke that affected about 109 million people across the midwest, mid-Atlantic and north-east. “The cost is incalculable!” Trump raged on social media. “This is Willful Negligence, and becoming a yearly occurrence, costing the United States Billions of Dollars, which cost of this pollution must of necessity be added to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying.”

However, the president told reporters on Tuesday that his administration is looking at those tariffs “separately”.

Trump, again offering mixed-messaging about the status of the Iran conflict, noted that the US has decimated Tehran’s military capabilities, but the conflict will not end soon.

“We have done damage that will take them 25 years to recover from. So, if we left tomorrow, we had a big success. But we’re not leaving tomorrow,” Trump said.

During his meeting with Joseph Aoun, Trump said Washington will “talk” with Beijing, after the president pushed misleading claims that China has interfered with US elections.

Last week, Trump delivered a televised address where he claimed that China obtained 220m voter records from the period of from 2020 to 2023. However, election experts note that voter data is some of the easiest to obtain because each state has a public voter file. Those can often be purchased, or even retrieved off the internet in some states. These files have existed since the beginning of voter registration in the US. The key allegation from the Trump administration that China could vote on behalf of people is false, according to experts.

“I think China is maybe a little bit different today than it was then,” Trump said in the Oval Office on Tuesday. “They do things and we do things to them. I’ll be honest, we do things to them too. It’s not a one-way street, but we’ll be talking to them.”

Trump repeated his claim that Tehran want to meet “desperately”, as strikes continue between the US and Iran.

As the fragile ceasefire collapsed this month, the president said that until Iran is “ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest in even meeting”.

As he begins his meeting with Lebanon’s president, Joseph Aoun, Trump said that the country has been “very mistreated country for a long period of time”.

Trump went on to say that Lebanon also has a “very importance place” in joining the Abraham Accords –the diplomatic agreements between Israel and several Arab states. Since the president returned to the White House, he’s expressed interest in expanding the Accords to include more countries.

“I think you’re going to see a lot of countries joining pretty soon,” Trump said today.

Trump has just welcomed Joseph Aoun, the president of Lebanon, to the White House. We’ll bring you the latest lines from their bilateral meeting.

As we reported yesterday, House speaker Mike Johnson has an ambitious legislative to-do list before the lower chamber breaks for August recess at the end of this week.

As my colleague Chris Stein notes, there are four bills in particular that the top Republican hopes to pass. This includes a measure that would extend government funding until 4 December; legislation which would curb lawmakers’ ability to trade stocks while in office; a $1.5tn Pentagon funding bill for the next fiscal year; and a third reconciliation package that would guarantee more defense funding and money for the Save America act, among other things.

Before any of this can happen, the Republican majority in the House would have to allow a procedural vote so that these key pieces of legislation can make their way to the floor for debate and, eventually, votes. It’s proving to be an uphill battle for Johnson, who is facing serious pushback from certain members of his own GOP conference.

title: Trump just expanded his tariff playbook with a powerful trade weapon no president has ever used
excerpt: David Clement, Policy Director at the Consumer Choice Center, says Canada's trade relationship with the U.S. is too strong to unravel, but shifting tariff policies are discouraging business investment...
body_text:
David Clement, Policy Director at the Consumer Choice Center, says Canada's trade relationship with the U.S. is too strong to unravel, but shifting tariff policies are discouraging business investment.

President Donald Trump is opening a new chapter in his playbook by invoking a tariff authority that no previous president has ever used — a move that could reshape future trade wars.

Trump used a little-known provision of the 1930 Tariff Act to impose 50% tariffs on a range of Canadian goods.

Known as Section 338, the nearly century-old law gives the president broad authority to impose duties on imports from countries deemed to discriminate against U.S. commerce. Its first-ever use by a U.S. president could establish a new legal blueprint for future trade disputes.

Canada, one of America's top trading partners, is the first country targeted under the long-dormant authority, with 50% tariffs covering exports ranging from wine and hockey sticks to cement, dairy products and furniture.

TRUMP BET TARIFFS WOULD BRING BACK AMERICAN FACTORY JOBS. NEW REPORT SAYS IT DIDN'T WORK

The latest tariffs add another point of friction in the already strained trade relationship between Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney. (Jim Watson/Getty Images)

In a statement, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney criticized the tariffs as the latest in a series of U.S. trade actions he said violate the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement while reaffirming Canada's commitment to negotiations.

The new duties are expected to take effect in the next 30 days.

"The significant thing is Trump uncorked a new tariff statute that has never been used and is pretty wide open in terms of the president's supposed power to impose tariffs," Scott Lincicome, vice president of general economics at the Cato Institute, told Fox News Digital.

"It's a lot more about what could be coming and the new uncertainty that just got injected into a multi-trillion-dollar trading environment."

The law has taken on added significance since the Supreme Court ruled against the administration's use of emergency powers for Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, prompting new interest in alternative legal authorities.

Against that backdrop, Lincicome said Section 338 could provide a broader legal avenue for imposing tariffs than authorities that have faced legal challenges or carry greater procedural constraints.

Tariffs are taxes on imported goods that U.S. importers generally pay upfront. Businesses can absorb the costs or pass them on to wholesalers, retailers and consumers through higher prices.

TRUMP DEFENDS TARIFFS, SAYS US HAS BEEN ‘THE KING OF BEING SCREWED’ BY TRADE IMBALANCE

Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, announced in April 2025, imposed broad new import duties on dozens of countries. (Photo by Brendan Smialowski/AFP/Getty Images)

Lincicome said the limited scope of the tariffs may reflect political realities ahead of the midterms.

"The president knows that the midterms are just a couple months away and that Americans generally don't like tariffs because they associate them with affordability issues," Lincicome said.

David Clement, policy director at the Consumer Choice Center, argued the administration's latest move appears designed to preserve Trump's broader tariff agenda despite recent legal setbacks while increasing uncertainty for businesses.

"I would actually view this as almost like a last-ditch effort to try and keep some tariffs on the table," Clement told Fox News Digital. "All this means is that for roughly $20 billion in imports, they're just going to pay more for them."

He said the bigger long-term risk is not the collapse of North American trade, but businesses delaying investment because of the uncertainty surrounding future tariff policy.

Monthly tariff collections have more than tripled since Trump returned to office, rising from roughly $9 billion to more than $30 billion. The government collected $30.4 billion in duties in January, a 275% increase from a year earlier, bringing fiscal-year collections to $254.8 billion.

Tariffs have been a defining feature of both of Trump's presidencies, with the president arguing they protect American industries, boost domestic manufacturing and strengthen the U.S. economy.

But with affordability remaining a defining issue ahead of the midterms, any tariff policy that raises consumer prices is likely to face renewed political scrutiny.

Amanda Macias covers the intersection of business, economics and politics, with a focus on how policy decisions shape markets, businesses and American workers.

title: Georgia coach sounds off: If Congress won't act, SEC should just break away from NCAA and govern themselves
excerpt:
Dan Dakich reacts to Greg Sankey saying the SEC is exploring options outside of the NCAA.

TAMPA, Florida - Kirby Smart took to the podium on Tuesday morning as the 'Dean of SEC coaches' who also is l...
body_text:
Dan Dakich reacts to Greg Sankey saying the SEC is exploring options outside of the NCAA.

TAMPA, Florida - Kirby Smart took to the podium on Tuesday morning as the 'Dean of SEC coaches' who also is looking to take his team back to the College Football Playoff mountaintop against a backdrop filled with parity overtaking the sport.

While it's been four years since the Bulldogs have raised a national championship trophy, that isn’t a part of the current landscape of college athletics that worries the Georgia coach.

And, while he has been tasked with being the vocal leader of the SEC, he's also not shying away from some of the hardest topics currently plaguing the sport.

Greg Sankey addresses SEC breakaway talk as Michigan tries to steal spotlight with AD Warde Manuel decision
