Georgia head coach Kirby Smart yells to his players during the first half of an NCAA college football game against Tennessee, Saturday, Nov. 13, 2021, in Knoxville, Tenn. (AP Photo/Wade Payne)

But, when it comes to parity, Smart is not going to complain. If anything, Kirby has welcomed the changing landscape, in his own subtle way.

"I think it's great for the sport," Kirby mentioned. "More guys can get invited to the party, especially with the transfer portal..It's not a one team dominate program."

If you thought he was going to complain about this part of the problem in college sports, you don't know Kirby Smart. While there can be plenty of excuses made about why Georgia hasn’t played for a championship over the past three years, they at least are thriving with talent during the most chaotic time in the sport.

Kirby Smart supports SEC leaving NCAA if there aren't major changes: 'I'm not afraid to break away'

But no, that argument is not going to sit well with the fans in Athens. The fan base knows that Georgia should be competing every season for titles, and Kirby Smart is not running away from the skeptics, even if it fits the current narrative that the Bulldogs can no longer stockpile talent on the roster.

And while he's now the Godfather of the SEC, I don't know if he wants the job of being the new Nick Saban.

You might not like him, or think he's soft on automobile incidents, but the Georgia coach has been around long enough for his opinions to actually mean something.

Kirby is also not a coach that is getting fired within the next decade, so his opinion actually means something to the school president and athletic director. And, when it comes to the current rule-structure, it's pretty easy to see how many problems need fixing.

The question is whether it will be Congress implementing the rules, or the SEC?

"Until we get governance and we have someone that actually has the power to make decisions, or we can make decisions within our own community, we're not going to have much control," Kirby Smart noted.

And, that's the Georgia head coach just speaking truth. While there are plenty of people that would rather see Kirby Smart just take care of his team, there has to be those willing to speak up on the current issues hurting college athletics.

SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey speaks during a roundtable discussion on college sports in the East Room of the White House on March 6, 2026, as Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick looks on. The Trump administration hosted the event titled "Saving College Sports" with leaders from the Power Four conferences, media executives and former coaches. (Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images)

This is also not a threat from Kirby Smart. If you don't think these coaches are speaking with each other every single day in some form or fashion, then you aren't living in reality. Whether it's the transfer portal, NIL, eligibility, or just standard everyday rulings across the sport, coaches in the SEC are filling up their group chat with questions.

Yes, that also includes Lane Kiffin stoking the fire every now and then.

But breaking away from the NCAA and creating their own rules might sound like a daunting task for a conference that some think are trying to buck the system. In reality, this is just the next move, if Congress cannot pass legislation to help curb the current issues overtaking athletics.

"Yeah, I've actually been saying that for a long time," Kirby Smart told reporters.

Can you blame the Big Ten or SEC for thinking this way? Better yet, what about the ACC and Big 12? How do you think some of those schools from other conferences outside the Big Ten or SEC felt on Monday when a report indicated Senators were discussing a 5-year period in which they'd have to go independent if they were changing conferences?

If Congress is going to filter out new provisions to the Protect College Sports Act on a daily basis to take the temperature of the fans, why can't conferences discuss enforcing their own rules. Yes, that's a rhetorical question.

Sitting in the corner of the Tampa Marriott ballroom, surrounded by a plethora of reporters, Georgia's starting quarterback was soft with his tone, but committed with his words.

The spotlight is not something Stockton runs from. He embraces the chance to prove people wrong, even if his tone of voice doesn’t fully correlate with the opinion. But, if there is one thing that Kirby Smart has had at Georgia over his tenure, it's mostly-consistent quarterback play with a few stars along the way.

In this case, it's Gunner. And, even though he's upgraded his Ford pickup truck and put his old-school ride in the garage, he's the same southern boy that just loves playing football. For that, Kirby Smart is thankful.

Georgia quarterback Gunner Stockton throws a pass during the first half against Notre Dame in the quarterfinals of a College Football Playoff, Thursday, Jan. 2, 2025, in New Orleans. (AP Photo/Gerald Herbert)

As for being a leader on this Georgia football team, making sure to leave egos at the door before entering the locker room is the only way in which a team might thrive in this era of college athletics.

"I think Coach Smart does a great job with that," Stockton said of the environment around NIL. "If you're getting paid, not getting paid, whatever, Coach Smart always says the best players play. I think people have lost the fact of if you're on the field it doesn’t matter how much or how less you're getting paid, it ain't going to help you when you're playing football.

"I think the fact that Coach Smart has built a good culture at Georgia and I think he's the best head coach, and that's for a reason."

Once again, expectations are on the Bulldogs to deliver.

In the case of Georgia, it's earned the pressure.

For Kirby Smart, he's earned a say in what the future of college athletics could at least look like.

Trey Wallace is the Sr. College Sports Reporter for OutKick.

title: America bashed as 'disgusting' fascist regime during progressive call with Abdul El-Sayed, Cori Bush
excerpt: California Congressman Ro Khanna (D) calls for a bipartisan commission to tackle political violence and rhetoric, citing recent incidents. Khanna defends his appearance on influencer Hasan Piker's con...
body_text:
California Congressman Ro Khanna (D) calls for a bipartisan commission to tackle political violence and rhetoric, citing recent incidents. Khanna defends his appearance on influencer Hasan Piker's controversial podcast, despite Piker's past remarks. He further debates the ongoing DHS funding battle, advocating for targeted funding for TSA and Border Patrol while pushing for immigration reform.

Several prominent Democrats were caught on camera participating in a progressive political video call which began with one speaker comparing America to a Nazi regime ruled by "dead-eye psychopaths" and "bloodthirsty imperialists."

Participants on the call, which was hosted by Progressive Victory in March, included Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., whose name has been floated as a possible presidential contender, and several Democratic Socialists of America-endorsed candidates, including Abdul El-Sayed, Cori Bush and Darializa Avila Chevalier. A spokesperson for Khanna has since disavowed the speaker's comments and said Khanna was not on the call at the time the remarks were made.

The virtual event, which also featured controversial streamer Hasan Piker, was titled "Choose Your Fighter" and was meant to highlight progressive politicians opposing the ongoing conflict in Iran.

At the start of the call, organizers introduced left-wing podcaster Kyle Kulinski, who they called an "up and comer" in the movement. Kulinski launched into a diatribe in which he accused the U.S. and Israel of "creating the fourth Reich."

WATCH: HOUSE DEMS DODGE QUESTIONS ON SOCIALIST AGENDA THAT THREATENS TO ABOLISH KEY INSTITUTIONS

Former Democratic Missouri congresswoman and current candidate Cori Bush (left), Democratic Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El Sayed (center) and Rep. Ro Khanna (right), D-Calif., participated in a Progressive Victory rally which began with a speaker bashing America as a Nazi-type regime. (Michael B. Thomas/Getty Images; Sarah Rice/Getty Images; Graeme Sloan/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

"These are genuinely repulsive, disgusting, wretched, depraved human beings, okay? They enjoy the pain and the violence and the suffering and the death and the destruction that they inflict on the rest of the world," Kulinski railed, adding, "It is absolutely accurate to compare these people to the worst criminals in history, to Mussolini, to Adolf Hitler."

Kulinski claimed that "we have descended into fascism" and that the U.S. is "governed by dead-eye psychopaths and sociopaths and bloodthirsty imperialists."

He asserted that "people need to come to terms with the fact that in this instance the U.S. government and the Israeli government, they are the terrorists … they are doing terrorism," adding that "if Iran is off the table as a regional power that means Hezbollah can’t be funded, Hamas can’t be funded, the Houthis can’t be funded, like the Shia crescent falls and then you have nothing but regional domination for Israel."

Kulinski also referred to the make America great again movement as "an imperialist fascist death cult," saying, "It’s time to take them down."

In response, a spokesperson for Canary Mission, an antisemitism watchdog, warned that Kulinski’s rhetoric evidences the growth of self-hatred for the U.S. on the part of the left.

"Kyle Kulinski's speech at Progressive Victory's online rally attended by prominent DSA and Democratic politicians indicates the hateful and anti-American ideas proliferating on the left," the spokesperson said.

"Support for terrorist regimes and historical revisionism has no place in American politics," the spokesperson continued, adding, "This level of extremism must be distanced from the Democratic Party."

Meanwhile, Benjamin Gilbert-Lif, deputy executive director of Progressive Victory, told Fox News Digital that "we stand by the comments made by Kyle Kulinski" and confirmed that "they reflect the views of our community and of the many Jewish members of our national Leadership."

DEM ‘EPSTEIN CLASS’ ATTACK BACKFIRES AS TOP CANDIDATES FUNDED BY EPSTEIN-TIED BILLIONAIRE

Democratic Socialists of America protest in solidarity with the Palestinians in New York City October 8, 2023. Per NYPD some 1000 protesters attended rally. (Peter Gerber for Fox News Digital)

"We also question why anyone would consider the idea of a ‘Jewish State’ to be anything other than an explicit desire for an ethno-state," Gilbert-Lif added. "PV stands against all ethno-states and at Choose Your Fighter sought to uplift voices that would push for the region to peacefully transition to a secular, one-state democracy where Israelis and Palestinians can coexist without artificially imposed borders or legal preferences toward one ethnicity dividing them."

Sarah Drory, a spokesperson for Khanna, told Fox News Digital that "Ro Khanna has always believed America is an exceptional country and was not on at the time these remarks were made."

She said that the congressman "joined for 5 minutes to speak and left" and added that "he obviously disagrees with them."

Fox News Digital reached out to Bush, Sayed, Chevalier and Piker for comment.

Oliver Larkin, a progressive Democrat running for Congress in Florida whose campaign helped to organize the call, told Fox News Digital that the virtual event raised $40,000 for 20 "progressive anti-war candidates" nationwide.

COLORADO'S SOCIALIST CANDIDATE CALLS FOR 'MORE IMMIGRANTS INTO OFFICE' TO STOP AMERICA'S 'EXPLOITATIVE' NATURE

A Houthi follower looks on, during a pro-Iran demonstration, as the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran continues, in Sanaa, Yemen April 6, 2026. (Khaled Abdullah/Reuters)

He also charged that "Congress must reassert its constitutional war powers and bring accountability to this dangerous, lawless Trump administration."

Asked whether he stands by Kulinski’s statements, Larkin remarked, "The crimes of the Epstein class are depraved."

He accused the Trump administration of "rather than enforcing U.S. and international law … continuing to fund an ongoing genocide."

Fox News Digital also reached out to the White House and DSA for comment.

Peter Pinedo is a politics writer for Fox News Digital.

title: Former Met police chief admits surveillance of Stephen Lawrence’s family was institutionally racist
excerpt:
Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses

A former Metropolitan police commissioner has admitted that the surveillance of the family ...
body_text:
Paul Condon tells spycops inquiry force failed to properly control unit which committed ‘horrendous’ abuses

A former Metropolitan police commissioner has admitted that the surveillance of the family of Stephen Lawrence by undercover officers was institutionally racist, the spycops inquiry has heard.

While Paul Condon was in command of the Metropolitan police in the 1990s, a controversial undercover unit spied on the family as they sought to persuade Scotland Yard to properly investigate Lawrence’s racist murder.

The officers reported information about the divorce of Lawrence’s parents, Doreen and Neville, to their superiors.

Giving evidence on Tuesday, Condon said the covert monitoring of the Lawrences and their campaign “meets very well the parameters of institutional racism”.

The failure to rigorously investigate the 1993 murder was one of the biggest and most contentious issues he had to handle, and caused enormous damage to the reputation of the UK’s biggest police force.

Speaking more widely about the undercover unit, Condon also said the Metropolitan police had failed over decades to control the special demonstration squad (SDS) which committed “horrendous” abuses.

He said he was appalled by the wrongdoing of the unit, which spent 40 years spying on tens of thousands of political activists. He told the inquiry there were “rogue officers who’ve done some bad things” but that the greater fault lay with the Met’s systemic failure to supervise the unit rigorously.

He said the undercover officers were told daily to “lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life”, but added that the Met should have realised the covert work “can be corrupting” if strong rules were not in place.

Condon, who became a member of the House of Lords after his police career, is the highest-ranked officer to have given evidence to the long-running public inquiry, which is headed by the retired judge Sir John Mitting.

The inquiry was set up after the Guardian revealed how the SDS spied on the Lawrences and their supporters in the 1990s.

Mitting is examining wide-ranging misconduct by the undercover officers. This includes how they monitored grieving parents while they sought justice for relatives or campaigned to expose police malpractice, regularly deceived women into long-term relationships and even fathered children, stole the identities of dead children and lied in court.

The SDS undercover officers adopted false identities to infiltrate mainly leftwing groups between 1968 and 2008 in deployments that typically lasted four years.

At the outset of his testimony, Condon said he wanted to “personally apologise for every single person who has been damaged by the actions of officers in the special demonstration squad over the decades, and I am appalled by so much of what I have learned throughout this inquiry and I sincerely apologise to every single person who has been damaged in this process”.

He said there were “horrendous things that went wrong” in the SDS and “appalling behaviour by individual officers”. He added: “Equally, I think the greater fault lies with the Metropolitan police as a whole over decades, that these officers were told daily as their job to lie, cheat and deceive as a way of life and what they’re asked to do.”

“The Metropolitan police should have realised that without limits, without ethical guidance, that this work in those circumstances can be corrupting and even someone with a strong moral compass will give in to baser instincts.”

However, Condon said he knew nothing of the abuses when he was in charge of the Met between 1993 and 2000. He said it was “unrealistic” to suggest that as the head of the Met, he would have been aware of the day-to-day running of a small and secretive unit.

Condon said he “never at any time commissioned, encouraged, authorised, acquiesced or was aware of any” of the surveillance that was carried out on the Lawrences and their supporters.

Asked by David Barr, the inquiry’s chief lawyer, if he would accept that the surveillance was “an expression” of institutional racism, Condon replied: “Yes.”

An inquiry by Sir William Macpherson into the killing said in 1999 that institutional racism within the Met was one of the reasons that allowed Lawrence’s murderers to escape justice. Police leaders accepted the findings, then later claimed to have reformed the service to the extent that it no longer applied.

title: Connecticut school board vice chair compares America's founding to Holocaust in viral July 4 post
excerpt:
MS NOW host Al Sharpton argued that America's upcoming 250th anniversary should not be considered a "celebration" for Black people who were enslaved at the time.

A Connecticut school board vice chair...
body_text:
MS NOW host Al Sharpton argued that America's upcoming 250th anniversary should not be considered a "celebration" for Black people who were enslaved at the time.

A Connecticut school board vice chair is facing backlash after a social media post comparing America's founding and its 250th anniversary celebration to the Holocaust went viral online.

Vice chair of the West Hartford Board of Education LaToya Yagaloff — also known as LaToya Fernandez — wrote that celebrating America's independence was akin to celebrating atrocities because slavery remained legal after 1776, asking readers, "Would you celebrate the historic beginning of the Holocaust or other genocides and atrocities?"

In a July 3 post on Facebook, Yagaloff wrote, "Slavery in the US started in 1619. In 1776, when America gained its independence, it did not free its slaves. Exactly 250 years ago in 1776 Chattel Slavery was the legal foundation of the American Colonies. So yes Americas 250th may feel like a celebration to you … But to me it’s like celebrating a nightmare that built the country that created the conditions for White privilege that allow you to turn a blind eye and be annoyed that I even mentioned it."

SPEAKER JOHNSON WARNS STUDENTS ARE 'INDOCTRINATED' TO SEE US THROUGH 'WRONG LENSES' AS NATION CELEBRATES 250TH

Vice chair of the West Hartford Board of Education LaToya Yagaloff is facing backlash for comparing America's founding to Holocaust. (iStock/Getty Images)

"Would you celebrate the historic beginning of the Holocaust or other Genocides and atrocities?!" Yagaloff continued. "Why can’t we all just be American on July 4th? Because we all were not even considered human on July 4th 17 76. Am I killing your buzz with my truth telling?? Good, time to sober up, cuz we need you conscious and well."

Her biography on West Hartford Public Schools' website says that her "educational leadership centers on closing opportunity gaps through culturally responsive teaching, community partnership, and academic excellence. She believes that when students see themselves represented, feel safe, and are encouraged to express their full identities, the entire community thrives."

On her personal website, Yagaloff says that she "designed and implemented a social justice based common core aligned curriculum for students in grades k-5, this curriculum-built awareness and called upon students to advocate for equity and justice in their neighborhoods." She also says she served as a Restorative Justice Coordinator for San Jose schools and worked in California for 13 years as a community activist and "Restorative Practices change agent."

FAR-LEFT ACTIVIST CONFERENCE AT TEACHERS UNION HQ RAISES CLASSROOM CONCERNS

In a July 3 Facebook post, Yagaloff asked, "Would you celebrate the historic beginning of the Holocaust or other genocides and atrocities?"

Yagaloff returned home to Connecticut in 2021 and became a Restorative Justice Coordinator within the state, "providing trainings in juvenile detention facilities and DCF group homes to staff and youth."

In her personal bio, she says that she became the City of Norwalk's first chief diversity, equity and inclusion officer, serves on the West Hartford Board of Education, and founded Beyond Circles, a restorative practices consulting organization.

The post, which was screenshotted and shared on X by Libs of TikTok, has drawn widespread criticism on social media as the nation just commemorated its 250th anniversary.

RHODE ISLAND TEACHER WHO CALLED SLAIN CHARLIE KIRK 'GARBAGE' HONORED IN HIGH SCHOOL YEARBOOK

The West Hartford GOP called out Yagaloff's post ahead of America's 250th celebration, while The West Hartford Democrats defended her.  (iStock)

One person said, "Sounds like like she needs God and should be teaching anyone anything."

"Someone should tell her who was actually selling Africans to the White slavers," another wrote. "Other Africans. Does she think that White men in wool suites went into the jungles to capture Africans? I think she needs to reread history."

On July 16, the West Hartford GOP shared a screenshot of Yagaloff's July 3 post and wrote, "The Democratic Vice Chair of the West Hartford Board of Education compared our country's 250th anniversary to a genocide and the Holocaust. These are the people entrusted with your children's education!"

In a Monday post on Facebook, West Hartford Democrats said in part, "We stand with LaToya Yagaloff. Full stop. The hateful campaign waged by the West Hartford GOP against BOE member LaToya Yagaloff has resulted in exactly what such targeted attacks invite: a flood of vile, racist, and misogynistic messages calling her the most degrading slurs imaginable. This is NOT acceptable political discourse. This is harassment. This is intimidation. Enough is enough."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

In a statement to Fox News Digital, LaToya Yagaloff said, "I stand by the heart of my message. My July 4th post was not written to diminish America, but to invite reflection on a historical truth that continues to shape how many Americans experience Independence Day."

"When the United States declared independence in 1776, chattel slavery remained legal," she continued. "Millions of enslaved Africans and their descendants were excluded from the freedoms being celebrated. That history is not an opinion — it is a fact. My post asked readers to consider why a day of national celebration can also evoke grief, reflection, and unresolved pain for many Black Americans whose ancestors were denied those very liberties."

"Some have interpreted one portion of my post as equating America's founding with the Holocaust," Yagaloff added. "That was not my intention. My point was to encourage reflection on how societies remember painful histories and to ask whether we can hold celebration and truth together. I believe we honor our nation not by avoiding difficult parts of our history, but by having the courage to confront them honestly."

"What has been especially heartbreaking is what followed," she said. "After being singled out publicly, I received a wave of racist and misogynistic harassment, including messages calling me the N-word and other degrading slurs. I welcome respectful disagreement and thoughtful debate. That is part of servant leadership. Racism, intimidation, and dehumanization are not."

"As a Marine Corps veteran, educator, Board of Education Vice Chair, and restorative justice practitioner, I have dedicated my life to bringing people together across differences," Yagaloff went on to say. "My work is rooted in the belief that truth and dignity must coexist. We cannot build reconciliation by denying history, nor can we create healing by dehumanizing those with whom we disagree."

"My faith teaches me that every person is made in the image of God and worthy of dignity," she continued. "My work in restorative justice teaches me that truth-telling is not the enemy of unity; it is often the beginning of it. Real reconciliation requires honesty, accountability, compassion, and the willingness to remain in conversation even when it is uncomfortable. I remain committed to serving my community with humility, integrity, and love."

"My hope is that this moment will encourage deeper conversations about our shared history and remind us that we can disagree without abandoning one another's humanity," Yagaloff added. "Patriotism is not the absence of criticism. It is loving your country enough to tell the truth about its past while believing in its capacity to grow."

Fox News Digital reached out to West Hartford Public Schools, the West Hartford GOP and the West Hartford Democrats for comment.

Rachel del Guidice is a culture reporter for Fox News covering the intersection of politics, faith, family, and American culture.

title: Trump's DNI pick clears key hurdle, reviving path for nation's top anti-terror surveillance program
excerpt: Chad Pergram reports from Capitol Hill, highlighting Democrats pressing Clayton on his stance regarding 2020 election claims and his delayed testimony, a move orchestrated by President Donald Trump ov...
body_text:
Chad Pergram reports from Capitol Hill, highlighting Democrats pressing Clayton on his stance regarding 2020 election claims and his delayed testimony, a move orchestrated by President Donald Trump over the SAVE America Act.

Senate Republicans quietly advanced President Donald Trump’s pick to run the nation’s intelligence agencies and placed Congress back on a path to restart a crucial counterterrorism tool.

Senate Intelligence Committee Chair Tom Cotton, R-Ark., revealed that the panel voted U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton out of committee nine to eight during a closed-door session. That tees up a later confirmation vote for Clayton to serve as the Director of National Intelligence (DNI).

"Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats," Cotton said. "I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence."

TRUMP'S DNI PICK JAY CLAYTON LOCKS HORNS WITH DEMOCRATIC SENATOR IN FIERY CONFIRMATION HEARING CLASH

Jay Clayton, U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York (SDNY), during the Bloomberg Global Credit Forum in New York, on June 3, 2026. (Michael Nagle/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

The role was previously vacated by former DNI Tulsi Gabbard and then hastily filled on an interim basis by Trump with Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) Director Bill Pulte, who Democrats railed against as an unqualified loyalist who would weaponize the intelligence community against the president’s enemies.

Clayton, who served as chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) during Trump’s first term, ended up becoming a victim of the political firestorm that surrounded Pulte.SENATE PUSH TO REAUTHORIZE NATION’S SPY POWERS STUMBLES OVER CONTROVERSIAL TRUMP DECISION

His first hearing was derailed by Trump, who demanded that the Senate confirm his replacement for the Southern District in New York and attach the SAVE America Act to must-pass legislation to reauthorize a key surveillance tool that has been inactive since June.

Clayton will now face a full confirmation vote in the Senate, where several Senate Democrats previously voted for him to be SEC chair.

TRUMP'S DNI PICK WILL HAVE TO WAIT FOR HIS CONFIRMATION HEARING AFTER TRUTH SOCIAL BOMB

Director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency Bill Pulte walks outside the White House, Sept. 2, 2025, in Washington. (Mark Schiefelbein/AP Photo)

But his hearing was dominated by questions about whether he would admit that former President Joe Biden won the 2020 election and Trump had lost, which Clayton shrugged off as "theater."

That could play out on the Senate floor in the coming days, and he will need Democratic votes to clinch the nomination.

Meanwhile, movement on his nomination could open up a path to reauthorizing the nation’s top counterterrorism tool, Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), which was notably used to thwart a terrorist attack at a Taylor Swift concert years ago.

Democrats derailed the process after Trump appointed Pulte, but with a new face leading the nation’s 18 intelligence agencies, Section 702 could soon see the lights come back on.

Alex Miller is a writer for Fox News Digital covering the U.S. Senate.

title: Tropical Storm Bertha strengthens as Gulf coast braces for flooding and surge
excerpt:
Storm warnings stretch from Florida to Louisiana as Bertha brings heavy rain, flooding risk and possible tornadoes

Tropical Storm Bertha strengthened in the Gulf of Mexico and was moving slowly towar...
body_text:
Storm warnings stretch from Florida to Louisiana as Bertha brings heavy rain, flooding risk and possible tornadoes

Tropical Storm Bertha strengthened in the Gulf of Mexico and was moving slowly toward the Florida Panhandle and Alabama, causing a tropical storm warning and storm surge watches, forecasters said.

The storm was about 125 miles (201km) south-east of Mobile, Alabama, the Miami-based National Hurricane Center (NHC) said on Tuesday afternoon. It was moving north-west at 5mph (8km/h) with maximum sustained winds of 60mph.

The wind speed increased by 20mph since Bertha formed on Monday night. A tropical storm warning, meaning the conditions are expected within the warning area within 36 hours, was in effect early on Tuesday from the Florida and Alabama border to the Jefferson and Plaquemines parish line in Louisiana.

A storm surge watch, indicating rising, possible life-threatening water moving inland from the coast within 48 hours, was issued from the Alabama and Florida border to the mouth of the Mississippi River, the NHC reported.

Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season after Tropical Storm Arthur brought heavy rainfall to the south-eastern US in June.

The storm was expected to stay near or along the northern Gulf coast, dropping heavy rain on coastal areas of Florida, Alabama, Mississippi and Louisiana while pushing west toward Texas.

Potentially life-threatening storm surges of up to 4ft (1.2 meters) are possible in portions of Louisiana, Alabama and Florida, the NHC said, warning Bertha could spin off an isolated tornado or two over the Florida Big Bend region.

Areas of flash flooding also were possible through Friday along the coast from western Florida to middle Texas, forecasters said, adding that rainfall could total 3in to 5in (7cm to 12cm) along the Louisiana coastline, with higher amounts in some scattered areas.

Red flags on the beach at Orange Beach, Alabama, cautioned swimmers about high surf and currents. In Escambia county, Florida, emergency officials offered sand for people living in flood-prone areas to use for sandbags.

Meanwhile, in the eastern Pacific Ocean, Hurricane Fausto churned far from Mexico’s Baja California peninsula and posed no threat to land, the NHC said.

Fausto gained hurricane status on Monday night. It was about 740 miles south-west of the southern tip of the Baja California peninsula, had top sustained winds of 80mph and was moving north-west at 12mph.

The storm was expected to strengthen on Tuesday and Wednesday. No coastal watches or warnings were in effect, though ocean swells generated by the storm could cause life-threatening surf and rip tide conditions on the Baja California coast, the NHC said.

title: What does a realistically successful first year look like for head coach Lane Kiffin at LSU?
excerpt:
Joel Klatt revealed his post-spring questions for his top 12 teams. He examined if the LSU Tigers could gel quickly with a new coaching staff and 40 transfers.

As SEC Media Days continue in Tampa, ea...
body_text:
Joel Klatt revealed his post-spring questions for his top 12 teams. He examined if the LSU Tigers could gel quickly with a new coaching staff and 40 transfers.

As SEC Media Days continue in Tampa, each program as well as their individual fanbases are starting to see a clearer picture about the expectations they will face for the 2026 season.

For schools like Texas and Georgia, the belief is that this year - as is the case with most years in Austin and Athens, respectively - is championship or bust, while other schools like Kentucky and Arkansas are breaking in first-year head coaches and are coming off lackluster campaigns in 2025.

One program that has a first-year head coach but is expected to compete for a national championship is LSU, who went out and acquired Lane Kiffin from SEC rivals Ole Miss.

BY ADDING ED ORGERON TO STAFF, LANE KIFFIN AND LSU ADDRESS KEY FLAW FROM PREVIOUS REGIME

Lane Kiffin speaks at a press conference as he is introduced as the new head football coach of the LSU Tigers at Tiger Stadium in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, on Dec. 1, 2025. (Tyler Kaufman/Getty Images)

To be fair, those expectations always exist at a place like LSU, but when you marry them with a coach who took an SEC compatriot with far less resources to the College Football Playoff just one season ago, the spotlight only gets brighter.

Kiffin has done his best to try and temper the aspirations of Tiger fans, requesting patience during spring practice, but the public pricetag of this roster - rumored to be around $40 million - means there isn't a lot of room for error in the court of public opinion.

Whether that's fair or not remains to be seen, but let's discuss what a realistic and successful season looks like for Kiffin and the Bayou Bengals in year one.

THE 5 MOST DISAPPOINTING PRESEASON TOP-10 COLLEGE FOOTBALL TEAMS OF THE 21ST CENTURY

The aforementioned $40 million roster is definitely a factor in all of this, and it cannot be ignored, but plenty of the most expensive rosters in college football have failed to win national titles in the past.

Everyone remembers Jimbo Fisher and Texas A&M's doomed 2022 season or Texas' lackluster campaign from just one year prior.

Those programs are both notorious for dumping big money into their football teams, so just having an expensive roster doesn't always mean immediate success.

INSIDE THE FIGHT: NIL ARMS RACE FUELING NEW PUSH FOR COLLEGE FOOTBALL PLAYOFF EXPANSION

LSU football head coach Lane Kiffin speaks at South Stadium Club at Tiger Stadium on Dec. 1, 2025 in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. (Matthew Hinton/Imagn Images)

We also have to take into account the simple fact that the Tigers haven't made the College Football Playoff since 2019, when Ed Orgeron guided a star-studded LSU team to a 15-0 record and a national championship.

We are coming up on seven years since that historic season, and the Tigers don't have anything to show for their efforts in the meantime.

With that being said, anything but a Playoff appearance should be considered a failure for Kiffin and LSU in year one.

Fans expecting a national championship need to pump their brakes a bit, but there is no excuse for this team and coaching staff not to at least make the big dance in 2026.

The Tigers may very well boast one of the best coordinator tandems in the SEC in offensive coordinator Charlie Weis Jr. and defensive coordinator Blake Baker.

When you couple that with all the talent that is littered across this roster, including a consensus top-ranked transfer portal class this offseason, it's fair to expect this team to at least compete for top status in the SEC.

Making the CFP should be the goal in year one, and once you make the postseason tournament, you can recalibrate your expectations accordingly from there.

While it's fair for Kiffin to ask for patience, he needs to be more specific.

Patience with regard to winning the whole thing is warranted, but if the first-year head coach thinks an 8-4 season and an invite to the Gator Bowl will earn him an "atta boy" from the fanbase, he is sorely mistaken.

Kiffin isn't a stranger to the SEC. He knows the expectations are high, especially at a conference blue-blood like LSU.

LSU Tigers CB Mansoor Delane celebrates a defensive stop against the Clemson Tigers at Memorial Stadium in South Carolina. (Ken Ruinard/USA TODAY Network)

The talent is abundant. The coaching staff is elite.

I expect the Tigers to compete for championships as long as Kiffin still resides in Baton Rouge, but I also don't expect a year one without any hiccups.

Even a demigod like Curt Cignetti needed a year to get his feet underneath him at Indiana.

Worry not, LSU fans. You hired the right guy.

The only one who can stop Kiffin and the Tigers once this thing gets rolling is Kiffin himself.

That's as scary for other SEC fans to hear as it is for LSU fans.

title: Row escalates in Ukraine over army chief's future
excerpt:
The role of Ukraine's commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, has been part of the country's rumour mill

Protesters in Ukraine have threatened nationwide demonstrations if Mykhailo Fedorov is not rein...
body_text:
The role of Ukraine's commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, has been part of the country's rumour mill

Protesters in Ukraine have threatened nationwide demonstrations if Mykhailo Fedorov is not reinstated as defence minister, as President Volodymyr Zelensky scrambles to quell outrage over the decision to oust him.

Over the past week, thousands in Kyiv and other cities have taken to the streets to protest against Fedorov's removal and demand the dismissal of Oleksandr Syrskyi, the commander of Ukraine's armed forces.

Army veteran Dmytro Koziatynskyi said in a social media post that if Syrskyi is not dismissed by Friday and Federov reinstated, "we will begin an open-ended nationwide protest".

Ukraine is waiting, anxiously, for a resolution. Over the weekend, Ukrainian media reported that Zelensky was consulting ministers and generals in an effort to resolve the crisis.

Amid a welter of rumours, the general staff was forced to deny reports that Gen Syrskyi was about to be – or even had already been – sacked. Another report suggested that Fedorov had refused an offer to return to a different position in government.

Kyrylo Budanov, head of the president's office, urged Ukrainians to remain calm.

"The situation surrounding the latest internal changes has caused strong emotions," he wrote on Tuesday.

With the noisy calls of angry protesters echoing around the tight confines of Ivana Franka Square, within earshot of the presidential palace, Budanov said the government was taking note.

"I understand people who express their position…..The position of society has been heard."

With large numbers of demonstrators directing their anger towards Gen Syrskyi – seen by many as representing outdated Soviet-era military doctrines, Budanov, a former head of military intelligence, warned people "not to hate anyone".

"The enemy is closely monitoring our every internal dispute and is waiting for us to start fighting among ourselves," he wrote.

"The work is under way. There will be results."

On Monday, the beleaguered but usually silent 60-year-old Gen Syrskyi hit back against his critics, publishing a long defence of his record and urging readers not to focus on his widely-reported rivalry with 35-year-old Mykhailo Fedorov.

Despite Zelensky's claim last week that the two men could not even bear to sit in the same room, Syrskyi said it had been a surprise to learn that he had a conflict with the minister.

Protesters hold signs which say 'Fedorov is the defence minister', 'Don't mess with what works', 'Hands off Fedorov' and 'Bring Fedorov back' in central Kyiv on Saturday

"I perceived our relationship as a working one," he wrote in an op-ed for the Militarny website.

"If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry."

The general said the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation currently being aired in the press and boiled down to pithy slogans on pieces of cardboard carried aloft by protestors were a great deal more complicated than most people realised.

"Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war."

Like the ousted defence minister, the commander-in-chief avoided directly personal attacks, but the object of much of his criticism was pretty clear.

"Presentations should not turn into self-representations," he wrote, in a thinly veiled swipe at Federov's long, detailed news conference last week.

"Behind each slide there should be a document, a budget and a procedure," the general went on. "Otherwise, instead of a decision the army gets disappointment."

Syrskyi's problem however is that most Ukrainians, especially the younger ones, seem to trust Federov's analysis of what needs to change in order for Ukraine to win the war, not the general's.

More than anyone, Federov is seen by many as driving innovation in the military, gradually improving Ukraine's chances of beating back its numerically superior enemy.

"Right now, the war is changing, so we need new ideas and a new structure," 44-year-old law company employee Tetiana Makarenko told me at one of the protests over the weekend.

"It's often said that a small Soviet army can't defeat a big Soviet army. It's time to change the mindset and start the new type of war."

Federov's six months at the helm in the defence ministry have coincided with a string of military successes for which the minister, not always fairly perhaps, is credited.

"We had this feeling because we saw the changing situation," Makarenko said. "We had the feeling that we can do this."

In his article, Syrskyi hit back at those accusing him of a lack of strategy, or that he had failed to embrace new technology.

"The strangest reproach that I have heard is that I supposedly do not want to fight with drones," he said, pointing out that he had presided over the creation of an unmanned systems force as a separate branch of the military.

A crowd of mostly young people gathered in Kyiv, pictured here, and other Ukrainian cities on Thursday morning

But drones, despite being responsible for the lion's share of Russian casualties, were not enough, he argued.

"I cannot transform a million-strong army to drones in two months and say: now we fight like this."

Syrskyi said he was not at war with the defence ministry but with Russia. But while he mounts his defence against a host of critics, speculation has fallen on who Zelensky might choose to replace him.

The candidates include Mykhailo Drapatyi, commander of the Joint Forces, Andriy Bilekskyi, commander of the Third Army Corps, Oleh Apostol, commander of the Airborne Assault Forces and Ihor Obolenskyi, commander of the National Guard Corps.

All have distinguished military records, but crucially all are from a much younger generation of officers. At 46, Biletskyi is the oldest while Apostol and Obolenskyi are both 39.

At just 35, the youngest possible candidate is Brig Gen Denys Prokopenko, another National guard commander, whose "Azov" Regiment, while politically controversial for its alleged far-right connections, achieved near mythic status during the bitter siege of the southern port city of Mariupol in 2022.

Held as a prisoner of war by Russia following the collapse of the city, Prokopenko returned to Ukraine as part of a prisoner exchange in July 2023, quickly resuming his command despite Russian demands that he remain outside the country until the war was over.

Zelensky says he is still working out "what adjustments need to be made to Ukraine's defence strategy" and has thanked everyone "for their openness, honesty and substantive contributions".

With Russia expected to resume its attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure this autumn, the president says "Ukraine must enter August as strong as possible".

As the protests continue for a sixth evening, the country waits for the president to make a series of possibly fateful decisions.

Many Ukrainian soldiers outraged over removal of defence minister, troops tell BBC

‘Everyone's afraid to die’: Struggle to recruit soldiers in Ukraine turns violent

Russia launches major ballistic missile attack on Ukrainian cities

title: Netherlands to sanction trade with illegal Israeli settlements
excerpt:
The Netherlands is set to ban imports of goods from illegal Israeli settlements in the occupied Palestinian territories.

The Dutch government announced the final approval of a decree on Tuesday. It w...
body_text:
The Netherlands is set to ban imports of goods from illegal Israeli settlements in the occupied Palestinian territories.

The Dutch government announced the final approval of a decree on Tuesday. It will see the Netherlands ban the “import, purchase, and sale of goods originating from unlawful Israeli settlements in the Palestinian Territories” from September 22.

With the move, the Netherlands will join a small but fast-growing group of European countries acting unilaterally on an issue that is focused mainly on illegal settlements in the occupied West Bank. European Union member states remain divided on instigating a bloc-wide ban, despite several governments pushing hard for a common regime.

The Netherlands’ Foreign Affairs Minister Tom Berendsen noted to parliament that the trade ban had already been planned, although the implementation date is new.

A majority in the Tweede Kamer, the lower house of the Dutch parliament, last year supported the measures.

Under the measure, it will also be illegal for Dutch companies to provide brokering services that facilitate trade with illegal Israeli settlements.

Favourable tax regulation has made the Netherlands a key location for many global corporations. The ban also applies to Dutch companies operating abroad.

The Dutch measures follow a similar move by Belgium, which approved its own import ban days earlier.

The Netherlands also joins Spain and the Republic of Ireland among EU states acting unilaterally, as pressure builds on the 27-nation bloc to adopt a coordinated response to Israel’s actions in Gaza and the occupied territories.

The Dutch Council of State raised no objection to the policy, though it flagged doubts over how enforceable it would be in practice, a concern that the government said it had already weighed when the measure was first announced two months ago.

The Dutch government has acknowledged that Israel’s settlements in the West Bank are unlawful under international law and has said the ban reflects its obligation not to contribute to that situation.

Dutch far-right politician Geert Wilders denounced the decision on X, saying he was “deeply ashamed” by the move.

Research released in June by the Global Echo group, which pursues legal cases for Palestinians against the occupation, found the Netherlands accounted for a third of agricultural exports from Israel’s illegal settlements and nearly half of those destined for the EU.

Imports from the settlements consist mainly of agricultural products, including avocados, dates, oranges, grapes and fresh herbs. The measure will also cover products from the occupied Golan Heights, a region known for wine production.

Annual trade involving the affected territories is estimated to be worth tens of millions of euros, although its exact value is difficult to determine.

title: Johnson scores commanding victory in fight to ram SAVE America Act through Democrat opposition
excerpt:
The Democratic strategist argued Americans should have seen Trump's address in full, saying exposure would have highlighted what he views as the president's erratic behavior.

Speaker Mike Johnson's b...
body_text:
The Democratic strategist argued Americans should have seen Trump's address in full, saying exposure would have highlighted what he views as the president's erratic behavior.

Speaker Mike Johnson's bid to end run Democrats' opposition to an election integrity bill backed by President Donald Trump survived a key House-wide vote on Tuesday.

It's a major victory for Republicans in a fight that Democrats have argued is about suppressing Americans' right to vote, while the GOP argues it is about enhancing Americans' confidence in the electoral process.

The Safeguarding American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act has faced significant headwinds in Congress despite having Trump’s full-throated support. While it passed the House, the legislation has struggled in the Senate, where at least several Democratic votes are needed to break a filibuster and advance to final passage.

JOHNSON'S STRATEGY TO BYPASS DEMOCRATS' SAVE ACT BLOCKADE SURVIVES KEY VOTE, HANDING GOP BIG WIN

Speaker Mike Johnson's plan to end run Democrats' opposition to the SAVE America Act survived a key hurdle. (Evan Vucci/AP Photo; J. Scott Applewhite/AP Photo)

But the new plan by Johnson, R-La., which would allow key parts of the bill to skirt Democrats entirely, survived a key test vote on the House floor Tuesday.

The House voted largely along party lines to allow for debate on a $95 billion budget bill that includes some $10 billion for states to implement voter ID laws and a proof-of-citizenship requirement in the voter registration process — both key points in the SAVE America Act.

It's part of a wider measure being passed via the budget reconciliation process.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

The reconciliation process allows the party in power to pass sweeping fiscal policy reforms without input from their opposition by lowering the Senate’s threshold for passage to line up with the House’s simple majority requirement.

President Donald Trump speaks to members of the media outside the Oval Office of the White House in Washington, D.C., on April 13, 2026. (Salwan Georges/Bloomberg via Getty Images)

But there are strict guidelines for what can be in a reconciliation bill. Such legislation must deal with yearly federal spending and revenues, meaning issues that do not directly affect fiscal policy are generally excluded.

The remainder of the budget bill includes $73 billion for the U.S. war against Iran and $12 billion in aid for American farmers.

GOP CIVIL WAR ERUPTS OVER TRUMP'S LATEST AGENDA PUSH AS KEY REPUBLICANS THREATEN TO SINK MEGABILL

The second chamber-wide step in the reconciliation process after Tuesday's successful vote is to pass a framework that directs the relevant committees to then craft details dealing with their allocated budgets. Those details are then put back into a single sweeping bill that must pass the House and Senate before getting to Trump’s desk for his signature.

That framework is set to get a vote on Wednesday afternoon.

Whether it passes, however, is unclear. In addition to Democratic opposition, some GOP fiscal hawks are wary of the legislation's lack of spending cuts to offset the increase in the federal budget.

Elizabeth Elkind is a politics reporter for Fox News Digital leading coverage of the House of Representatives. Previous digital bylines seen at Daily Mail and CBS News.

Follow on Twitter at @liz_elkind and send tips to elizabeth.elkind@fox.com

title: Saxophonist Plas Johnson, known for solo on The Pink Panther Theme, dies aged 94
excerpt:
Jazz saxophonist – who recorded with artists Frank Sinatra, BB King and more – died in Los Angeles, reports say

The US jazz saxophonist Plas Johnson, best known for his solo riff on the timeless clas...
body_text:
Jazz saxophonist – who recorded with artists Frank Sinatra, BB King and more – died in Los Angeles, reports say

The US jazz saxophonist Plas Johnson, best known for his solo riff on the timeless classic The Pink Panther Theme, has died at the age of 94, according to reports.

The native of Donaldsonville, Louisiana, died on 15 July, according to a report from the Los Angeles jazz radio station KKJZ.

Johnson’s hometown newspaper, the Donaldsonville Chief, added in a separate report that he died in Granada Hills, a neighborhood in Los Angeles. Social media posts from users identifying themselves as Louisiana family members of the musician also shared word of Johnson’s death.

“My cousin Mr Plas Johnson passed away,” one Donaldsonville-based Facebook user said on 17 July. The post added: “RIP Cuz.”

He was most famous for his performance on The Pink Panther Theme, which he recorded in 1963 alongside the late US composer Henry Mancini. The score to that movie was later ranked 20th on the American Film Institute’s list of greatest film scores ever.

It also won three Grammy awards and was nominated for an Oscar for best original score at the 37th Academy awards.

“We only did two takes, I think,” Johnson once said, according to the Donaldsonville Chief. “When we finished, everyone applauded, even the string players.”

Johnson, who performed the classic’s tenor saxophone solo, added jokingly: “And that’s saying something … They never applaud for anything.”

He began his musical career singing with his family before his father, who played both the banjo and saxophone, bought him a soprano saxophone.

Johnson made his recording debut in 1950 alongside his brother, Ray, for DeLuxe Records before touring with the singer and pianist Charles Brown.

After switching from soprano to alto and tenor saxophone, Johnson moved to Los Angeles in 1954, where he became a session musician, recording with artists who, beside Mancini, included BB King, Johnny Otis, Frank Sinatra, Marvin Gaye and Nat King Cole.

“My solos always seemed to bring the record up another notch,” Johnson said. “I could do that in eight bars, I could do that in 12 bars and I was used to maybe do[ing] fills behind the vocalist after that,” he added.

Johnson died six days shy of his 95th birthday, according to KKJZ and the Donaldsonville Chief.

title: Burnham cuts VAT on electricity bills in first major policy announcement – UK politics live
excerpt:
Move is expected to save households around £45 a year in effort to tackle cost of living

Burnham to cut VAT on electricity bills in first cost-of-living move

Andy Burnham promised to end rough sleep...
body_text:
Move is expected to save households around £45 a year in effort to tackle cost of living

Burnham to cut VAT on electricity bills in first cost-of-living move

Andy Burnham promised to end rough sleeping in England in his first pledge as prime minister, allocating an additional £340m over three years as he seeks to replicate the homelessness schemes he rolled out in Greater Manchester.

Speaking in front of Downing Street immediately after becoming prime minister on Monday, Burnham said he would try to get people off the streets “at the earliest opportunity”.

The announcement was welcomed by homelessness campaigners, who say the pledge can be met with enough money and political will.

The prime minister’s critics, however, greeted the promise with scepticism, pointing out that despite having set out a similar goal as Greater Manchester mayor, Burnham had overseen four years of rising street homelessness.

Officials said the government would now put an extra £340m into its homelessness strategy – equivalent to £113m a year – which would come from unused parts of the housing department’s budget.

The newly appointed business secretary, Jonathan Reynolds, has defended the policy, declaring the cut to VAT on electricity bills “a statement of priorities”.

Responding to Darren Jones’s claim that the cut remained unfunded, Reynolds told Radio 4’s Today programme:

double quotation markThey are part of the government’s pressure, it is a legitimate decision to remove that pressure and instead use the allocation of resources … It’s a straightforward switch spend, it’s the right decision to make, and one that will be made by the British people.

In our opening post we mentioned criticism of the tax cut announcement on household electricity bills from the Lib Dem leader, Ed Davey, who has told Times Radio that the policy is “coming apart at the seams” already.

“It’s a bit confusing isn’t it? And I don’t think it’s the best way to help people with their bills,” Davey added.

He said his party was calling for an “essential energy guarantee”, which the Lib Dems claim will cut energy bills by giving every household an essential amount of energy, equal to 50% of typical consumption, at a “discounted rate”.

Davey calls for Burnham to hold a budget as soon as possible so the figures can come under proper scrutiny. Sticking to the commitments not to raise the main rates of income tax, national insurance or VAT rule out the most obvious ways to raise revenue.

Also speaking on Times Radio, the shadow defence secretary, James Cartlidge, said that the Office for Budget Responsibility said last year that the digital ID card scheme had not been funded. He said: “They (the OBR) literally said that, so you can’t fund a tax cut… with savings from a programme that itself wasn’t funded.”

Good morning and welcome to our live coverage of UK politics. Andy Burnham has announced an £850m tax cut on electricity bills to help ease cost of living pressures in his first significant policy announcement as prime minister. It means that electricity bills will be reduced by an average of £45 a year from October.

Downing Street said the move will be partly funded by scrapping Keir Starmer’s digital ID scheme, which would have cost an estimated £1.8bn over three years.

The newly appointed chancellor, John Healey, said the cut will “bring down inflation while supporting households in every postcode”, signalling the political direction of the new cabinet. While welcomed as a positive initial step to help people save money off their bills, the policy has been criticised, including by Lib Dem leader Ed Davey, for not going far enough.

In an extraordinary intervention this morning, Darren Jones, Starmer’s former chief secretary, said the money had not been set aside for the digital ID scheme, which Burnham says will pay for the VAT tax cut. Jones, who was sacked as the Dunchy of Lancaster, wrote in an X post:

double quotation markGood news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills. It’s a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower. But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget.

Burnham will chair his first Cabinet meeting at 12.30pm after having removed Starmer loyalists from it yesterday. The ministers to lose their jobs included Rachel Reeves, David Lammy, Darren Jones, Steve Reed, Peter Kyle, Richard Hermer, Liz Kendall, Jo Stevens and Hilary Benn. But Reeves was understood to have turned down another job to return to the backbenches. We will bring you the latest throughout the day, which promises to be a busy one. Here is a non-exhaustive list of the members of the new cabinet:

Work and pensions secretary: Pat McFadden

Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster: Louise Haigh

title: Iran claims attacks on strait of Hormuz ships amid fresh US strikes – Middle East crisis live
excerpt:
Revolutionary Guard claims attack on oil tanker and two other ships as US carries out tenth consecutive night of strikes

Full report: Almost 100 troops injured in recent weeks, Pentagon admits, as Ir...
body_text:
Revolutionary Guard claims attack on oil tanker and two other ships as US carries out tenth consecutive night of strikes

Full report: Almost 100 troops injured in recent weeks, Pentagon admits, as Iran launches reprisals on US allies

Welcome to our live coverage of the latest developments in the Middle East crisis.

Iran attacked a tanker in the strait of Hormuz early on Tuesday, forcing its crew to abandon the ship, as Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthis announced they were imposing an immediate maritime blockade of Saudi Arabia in the Red Sea after the two sides traded fire last week for the first time in years.

A Houthi official said the Bab al-Mandeb strait – at the southern end of the Red Sea, through which about 12% of the world’s trade usually passes – would be closed to the Saudis in response to the kingdom’s “unjust blockade on Yemenis for over 10 years”.

Saudi Arabia said it would take “all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law”.

A 10th consecutive night of US airstrikes has not compelled Tehran to loosen its grip on the strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global energy supplies. But even as Iran’s president said the country had returned “full-scale war”, the Iranian interior minister travelled to Pakistan – a key mediator in the conflict – for talks.

The latest US strikes came hours after Donald Trump said Iran would pay “many times over” for killing US soldiers after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend.

A tanker came under attack early Tuesday in the Hormuz strait off Oman, forcing the crew to abandon the vessel, the British military’s UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) centre said. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed the attack as well as two other attacks on ships in the waterway on Monday.

The US military’s Central Command said its latest airstrikes were to “degrade” Iran’s ability to attack commercial shipping in the strait and included hitting military command centres and missile and drone launch sites.

Iranian media reported strikes in parts of the country including Bandar Abbas, Tabriz and Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power.

Iran launched retaliatory attacks against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, which all host US forces.

Oil prices softened after hitting their highest levels in more than a month in the previous session. Brent crude futures eased 0.4% to $88.87 ⁠a barrel by 0052 GMT on Tuesday while US West Texas Intermediate crude for September delivery was steady at $82.47 a barrel.

Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties.

In Lebanon, the Lebanese army began taking charge of security in three southern villages, the US said, as a deal to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Hezbollah faced its first test on the ground.

The Jordanian military said it shot down five drones that were launched from Iran, the official Petra news agency reported.

It added that the overnight attack did not cause casualties or damage.

Iran has repeatedly targeted Jordan in recent weeks as it continues to launch retaliatory attacks against countries in the region hosting US forces.

The Iranian army claimed to have launched a drone attack against US military targets at the Sheikh Isa airbase in Bahrain.

In a statement carried by Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency, the army said it launched a wave of drone strikes this morning at the base, which it claimed was being used as a launchpad for attacks against Tehran.

The Lebanese army has issued a statement confirming the deployment of troops to Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh, which had been occupied by the Israeli military.

In a statement shared online, it said: “Military units began deploying this morning in the town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyah, Nabatieh, following ongoing communications with the military coordination group for Lebanon.

“The army command renews its call for citizens not to go to the town until the security situation stabilises, and to abide by the instructions of the deployed military units in order to preserve their safety.”

Lebanon’s army has begun to deploy troops to a southern Lebanese village to take charge of security in the so-called “pilot zone”, according to Reuters, citing a security official.

Lebanese soldiers are heading to Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh as one of the first areas being trialled in the pilot zone programme as part of a US-brokered framework agreement between Lebanon and Israel. Two other villages – Froun and Srifa – will also trial the implementation of the programme, the US state department announced yesterday.

Under the framework, signed by Lebanon, Israel and the US on 26 June, the Israeli military will gradually retreat from the areas it occupies in southern Lebanon and hand over security to the Lebanese armed forces, which will also oversee the disarmament of the Hezbollah group.

The plan does not, however, set out a timeline for the withdrawal of Israeli troops. Hezbollah has also rejected the plan, insisting that any decisions on Lebanon should be linked to the wider negotiations between the US and Iran.

Nearly 100 US service members have been injured over the last two weeks of escalating strikes on Iran, the Pentagon has said, as Tehran launched a wave of reprisals to the tenth consecutive night of attacks.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said on Tuesday they had targeted US military sites including air defence systems and a radar installation in Bahrain and Kuwait, while a tanker in the strait of Hormuz reported being ⁠struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat.

Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan all said they had activated defences to repel the attacks, but the scale of US injuries at facilities across the region over recent week is likely to bring greater scrutiny to Iran’s reprisal attacks.

Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell said the vast majority of those injured only experienced “minor concussions” and 96% had since returned to work. Parnell was responding to a New York Times report that the Pentagon has withheld information about troop injuries from Iranian strikes. He denied that the Pentagon was hiding data about injuries.

In the last week three US soldiers have been killed in action in Jordan and Iraq.

The IRGC ⁠said it targeted ​infrastructure belonging ⁠to the US company ⁠Amazon in ​Bahrain, ‌according ‌to ‌a statements published by state media on ‌Tuesday.

There was no ​official confirmation from ⁠Bahrain. ​

Reuters, carrying the report, could not ​independently ​verify the ​incident.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) is saying it has launched an attack on a US military site in Jordan.

Earlier, Jordanian state TV reported that three missiles launched from Iran overnight had been intercepted, citing the armed forces, while sirens had sounded in the capital of Amman on Monday evening.

Two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian strike on an airbase in Jordan on Friday as Tehran targets Gulf region countries hosting American forces amid continuing US attacks.

Welcome to our live coverage of the latest developments in the Middle East crisis.

Iran attacked a tanker in the strait of Hormuz early on Tuesday, forcing its crew to abandon the ship, as Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthis announced they were imposing an immediate maritime blockade of Saudi Arabia in the Red Sea after the two sides traded fire last week for the first time in years.

A Houthi official said the Bab al-Mandeb strait – at the southern end of the Red Sea, through which about 12% of the world’s trade usually passes – would be closed to the Saudis in response to the kingdom’s “unjust blockade on Yemenis for over 10 years”.

Saudi Arabia said it would take “all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law”.

A 10th consecutive night of US airstrikes has not compelled Tehran to loosen its grip on the strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global energy supplies. But even as Iran’s president said the country had returned “full-scale war”, the Iranian interior minister travelled to Pakistan – a key mediator in the conflict – for talks.

The latest US strikes came hours after Donald Trump said Iran would pay “many times over” for killing US soldiers after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in ⁠action ​over the ​weekend.

A tanker came under attack early Tuesday in the Hormuz strait off Oman, forcing the crew to abandon the vessel, the British military’s UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) centre said. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed the attack as well as two other attacks on ships in the waterway on Monday.

The US military’s Central Command said its latest airstrikes were to “degrade” Iran’s ability to attack commercial shipping in the strait and included hitting military command centres and missile and drone launch sites.

Iranian media reported strikes in parts of the country including Bandar Abbas, Tabriz and Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power.

Iran launched retaliatory attacks against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, which all host US forces.

Oil prices softened after hitting their highest levels in more than a month in the previous session. Brent crude futures eased 0.4% to $88.87 ⁠a barrel by 0052 GMT on Tuesday while US West Texas Intermediate crude for September delivery was steady at $82.47 a barrel.

Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties.

In Lebanon, the Lebanese army began taking charge of security in three southern villages, the US said, as a deal to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Hezbollah faced its first test on the ground.

Five Palestinians die in fire after Israeli attack in Gaza

An Israeli air attack on an apartment in Gaza City’s Sabra neighbourhood set the building on fire, killing five people, including three chil...

An Israeli air attack on an apartment in Gaza City’s Sabra neighbourhood set the building on fire, killing five people, including three children who couldn’t be saved from the flames. Israel’s near-daily bombing of Gaza is in violation of the October 2025 ceasefire.

title: Defence stocks rally as John Healey appointed chancellor; UK borrows less than expected in June – business live
excerpt:
Rolling coverage of the latest economic and financial news

The UK’s blue chip FTSE 100 index has opened slightly lower this morning, down 0.3% – but some of its biggest defence stocks are rallying am...
body_text:
Rolling coverage of the latest economic and financial news

The UK’s blue chip FTSE 100 index has opened slightly lower this morning, down 0.3% – but some of its biggest defence stocks are rallying amid excitement that a former defence secretary now occupies No 11.

Babcock International is up 4%, while BAE Systems is up 2.4%. Over in the FTSE 250, Qinetiq is up 3.5%.

Investors are hoping that chancellor John Healey will use his new position to increase defence spending – possibly though issuing “defence bonds”, a form of borrowing allocated only for the military which he has previously advocated for in government.

But Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at the broker IG, warns that Healey’s appointment does not necessarily lead to an immediate windfall for defence.

double quotation markAs chancellor, he will have many competing demands, and won’t just be the MoD’s man in No 11. His experience made him an obvious candidate for the role, and he represents a middle way between Miliband and Mahmood, but it will not be easy to find lots more cash for defence, especially when the new PM is so busy making broad spending commitments in other areas.

The group of investors pursuing a rescue bid for Thames Water have offered the government a “golden share” in a last-ditch attempt to convince Andy Burnham not to nationalise Britain’s biggest water company.

London & Valley Water (L&VW), a consortium of 100 institutional investors that hold £17bn of the company’s £21bn debt, said it recognised that the new prime minister wants greater public control and to strengthen accountability of the water company.

The creditors said on Tuesday they were working on a revised rescue package for Thames, including the “golden share”, which would give the government a veto over important decisions and hostile takeovers.

double quotation markL&VW agrees that there is an opportunity to enhance the way local government, regulators and Thames Water work together, granting greater public controls on critical business issues to improve delivery of complex, essential infrastructure projects, and demonstrate shared accountability.

The consortium is willing to make significant new commitments to grant greater controls and oversight to ministers, implemented through a ‘golden share’.

And in case you missed it overnight: Donald Trump has said he will impose 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods in response to the country retaliating against previous US tariffs.

The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement.

The new tariffs will exclude energy products, fish, critical minerals and potash. They also exclude products already subject to tariffs aimed at protecting national security, such as steel and aluminium.

Read the full story by Mollie Reilly here:

Elsewhere on the corporate front – the outsourcer Mitie has agreed to a £3.1bn takeover by its private-equity owned rival OCS Group, making it the latest London-listed company to be acquired this year.

Its board has recommended that shareholders accept a cash offer of 221.6p a share, a 44.7% premium to Monday’s closing price.

Mitie, which was founded in 1987 and employs 84,000 staff, specialises in facilities management, such as engineering maintenance and other services such as hygiene and security.

The news comes after its chief executive Phil Bentley announced last month that he would leave in March 2027 after more than 10 years in the top job.

double quotation markThis is an important milestone for both organisations and an exciting opportunity to bring together two highly complementary businesses with a shared commitment to delivering the best outcomes for colleagues and customers.

Subject to completion, we would build a British facilities management group that is better positioned to support the organisations that keep the country running. Together, we can better support existing and new customers, help more people into work and strengthen our contribution to getting Britain moving.

While there is a long process ahead, both businesses remain focused on supporting customers and delivering the high standards they expect every day. Together, we can build something remarkable for our colleagues, our customers and the country.

OCS is owned by the private equity firm ​Clayton, Dubilier & Rice.

The deal, which is expected to complete in the first quarter of 2027, is the latest in a long string of takeovers on London’s stock market – Intertek, easyJet, Beazley and Schroders have all agreed to takeovers this year, while the US property group Prologis has made repeated attempts to buy its FTSE 100 rival Segro.

Mitie shares have shot up 41% this morning to a record high of 213.6p.

The UK’s blue chip FTSE 100 index has opened slightly lower this morning, down 0.3% – but some of its biggest defence stocks are rallying amid excitement that a former defence secretary now occupies No 11.

Babcock International is up 4%, while BAE Systems is up 2.4%. Over in the FTSE 250, Qinetiq is up 3.5%.

Investors are hoping that chancellor John Healey will use his new position to increase defence spending – possibly though issuing “defence bonds”, a form of borrowing allocated only for the military which he has previously advocated for in government.

But Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at the broker IG, warns that Healey’s appointment does not necessarily lead to an immediate windfall for defence.

double quotation markAs chancellor, he will have many competing demands, and won’t just be the MoD’s man in No 11. His experience made him an obvious candidate for the role, and he represents a middle way between Miliband and Mahmood, but it will not be easy to find lots more cash for defence, especially when the new PM is so busy making broad spending commitments in other areas.

Gilt yields are down slightly this morning, after rising late yesterday following comments from Andy Burnham that he will use “flexibility” in the fiscal rules to bolster public investment.

The yield on the 10-year gilt is about 1 basis point lower, though crucially still above the 5% level at 5.03%.

Bond markets are highly sensitive to comments from Burnham, amid expectations he could run a looser approach to public finances than Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves. However investors may have taken comfort this morning from official figures which showed that the government borrowed less than expected in June.

But Burnham has also promised more cost of living support, which will not be cheap. The VAT cut on household electricity bills announced today is expected to cost £850m and Healey is expected to outline more long-term action to bring down living costs at the budget later this year. There has been speculation around reducing the cap on bus fares and temporarily freezing rents in the private sector.

Sterling is still standing firm against the US dollar, up by 0.13% to around $1.34.

Paul Nowak, general secretary of the TUC, says that the economic data released today show that Burnham is “doing the right thing by pledging action on the cost of living”.

double quotation markWorking people are up against it with stagnant real pay, over a million people stuck on insecure zero-hour contracts, and a million young people not in employment, education, or training.

Cutting VAT on energy bills will provide some welcome relief. But with Donald Trump’s illegal war in Iran continuing to drive up bills, the government will need to go further. The new prime minister could raise up to £60bn over four years by hiking taxes on banks – and use it to bring down bills further for households across the country.

Andy Burnham is sending the right signals by pledging to help young people into work. In the months ahead, he will need to expand the youth jobs guarantee and double down on his pledge to reindustrialise Britain and deliver good growth in every postcode.

Meanwhile the boss of JP Morgan, Jamie Dimon, has warned Burnham against raising tax charges on banks, suggesting it could threaten plans to build its £3bn headquarters in London and drive investment away from Britain.

The chief executive of the world’s biggest bank, who has a track record of criticising the UK’s bank tax surcharge, does not want to see the UK’s new prime minister targeting the banking industry to raise extra revenue. Read more here:

Elsewhere this morning, new figures from the ONS show that unemployment remained at the same level as in April, at 4.9% – highlighting one of the many tough tasks ahead for Andy Burnham.

Employers cut the number of job vacancies down to 712,000, almost half the level in 2022, as employers put off hiring new staff in the three months to May.

The new prime minister has promised to raise living standards across all regions as part of a 10-year economic plan, but the latest pay data showed private sector earnings growth dropped to 2.9% to leave the average rise in earnings, including bonuses, at 4.3%.

Economists had forecast a rise in average pay with bonuses in the three months to May of 4.5%, up from 4.4% for the three months to the end of April.
